topics.title: Y-Bus Formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Y-Bus Formulation # ⚡ Module Experiment 2: Y-Bus Formulation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCEEL609 — Power System Lab):** > The **Bus Admittance Matrix (Y-Bus)** is a symmetrical, sparse, square matrix of order $(n \times n)$, where $n$ is the number of buses in the power network. It mathematically describes the nodal admittance interconnections of the entire power system and is fundamental to performing **load flow analysis**, **fault analysis**, and **transient stability studies**. Formally, the Y-bus relates the **injected bus currents** to the **bus voltages** through the nodal admittance equation: $$\mathbf{I}_{bus} = \mathbf{Y}_{bus} \cdot \mathbf{V}_{bus}$$ where: - $\mathbf{I}_{bus}$ is the vector of net injected currents at each bus (in per-unit, pu), - $\mathbf{V}_{bus}$ is the vector of bus voltages with respect to the reference (slack) bus, - $\mathbf{Y}_{bus}$ is the bus admittance matrix of order $n \times n$. --- ### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electrical Plumbing Network" Imagine a city water distribution network: | Water Network Element | Power System Equivalent | Symbol | |---|---|---| | Water reservoir tanks | Generating stations / Loads | Buses (nodes) | | Connecting pipes of different diameters | Transmission lines | Branches | | Pipe conductance (how easily water flows) | Line admittance | $y_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{z_{ij}}$ | | Pressure at each tank | Voltage at each bus | $V_i$ | | Net water inflow to each tank | Injected current | $I_i$ | > [!NOTE] > Just as a water engineer can predict pressures at every tank if he knows the pipe conductances and inflows, a power engineer can compute bus voltages if he knows the **Y-bus** and the injected currents. **Y-bus is, therefore, the "DNA of the power network"** — once formed, it captures the entire topology and electrical connectivity in a single compact algebraic object. ### Physical & Standard Metrics Used - All values are expressed in the **per-unit (pu)** system on a common MVA base. - Frequency of operation in India: **$f = 50$ Hz**. - Admittance units: **per-unit (pu)** or in **Siemens (℧)**. - Line charging susceptance $B$ is significant for lines exceeding **80 km** (long lines). > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Examiners love asking the *properties* of the Y-bus matrix. Memorize that it is **symmetric** ($Y_{ij} = Y_{ji}$), **sparse** (most off-diagonals are zero for large systems), and in most cases **complex-valued** (because of line charging). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Do We Need the Y-Bus? In any modern interconnected grid (the Indian grid operates as "ONE NATION — ONE GRID" since 2013), the network may contain **hundreds of buses and thousands of lines**. Solving the network by writing KCL/KVL branch-by-branch is impossible manually. The Y-bus compresses this complexity into a single matrix that a computer can invert in milliseconds. The Y-bus is the **gateway to almost every power system study**: | Study Type | Role of Y-Bus | |---|---| | Newton–Raphson Load Flow | Forms the Jacobian sub-matrix | | Gauss–Seidel Load Flow | Directly inverted: $V = Y^{-1} I$ | | Z-Bus Formation | $Z = Y^{-1}$ | | Symmetrical / Unsymmetrical Fault Analysis | Admittance matrix used in Thevenin/Z-bus methods | | Transient Stability | Network reduction at each time step | | Optimal Power Dispatch | Sensitivity analysis | --- ### 2.2 Two Standard Methods of Y-Bus Formulation #### **Method 1: Singular Transformation Method (Bus Impedance Method)** This is the **classical, mathematically rigorous** approach using Kirchhoff's laws and primitive network matrices. $$\boxed{\;\mathbf{Y}_{bus} = \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{y} \cdot \mathbf{A}^{T}\;}$$ where: - $\mathbf{A}$ — Bus incidence matrix (also called element-node incidence matrix), size $(n \times b)$, where $n$ = number of buses, $b$ = number of branches. - $\mathbf{y}$ — Primitive admittance matrix (diagonal), size $(b \times b)$. - $\mathbf{A}^{T}$ — Transpose of incidence matrix. #### **Method 2: Direct Inspection Method (Branch-Oriented)** Much faster and **the most popular for KTU lab exams**. By inspection of the one-line diagram: - **Diagonal element** $Y_{ii}$ = sum of all admittances connected to bus $i$ (including shunt admittances). - **Off-diagonal element** $Y_{ij}$ = negative of the admittance directly connecting bus $i$ to bus $j$. $$\boxed{\;Y_{ii} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} y_{ij}\;}\quad \text{(including shunt admittances at bus } i\text{)}$$ $$\boxed{\;Y_{ij} = Y_{ji} = -\,y_{ij}\;}\quad \text{(if buses } i \text{ and } j \text{ are directly connected)}$$ $$Y_{ij} = 0 \quad \text{(if buses } i \text{ and } j \text{ are not directly connected)}$$ --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table > [!NOTE] > The table below is the **single most important cheat sheet** for the Y-bus experiment in KTU University Practical Lab Examinations. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $y_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{z_{ij}} = \dfrac{1}{R_{ij} + jX_{ij}}$ | Series admittance of line $i\text{–}j$ | pu (or ℧) | | 2 | $Y_{ii} = \displaystyle\sum_{j \neq i} y_{ij} + y_{i}^{sh}$ | Diagonal element (self admittance) | pu | | 3 | $Y_{ij} = -\,y_{ij}$ | Off-diagonal (mutual admittance) | pu | | 4 | $\mathbf{I}_{bus} = \mathbf{Y}_{bus}\,\mathbf{V}_{bus}$ | Nodal admittance equation | pu | | 5 | $I_{i} = \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij}\,V_{j}$ | KCL at bus $i$ | pu | | 6 | $\mathbf{Y}_{bus} = \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{y}\,\mathbf{A}^{T}$ | Singular transformation | pu | | 7 | $Y_{bus}^{shunt} = \dfrac{Y_{ii}^{shunt}}{2}$ (at each end) | Half-line charging for $\pi$-model | pu | | 8 | $y_{ij}^{shunt} = jB_{ij}$ | Line charging susceptance (purely reactive) | pu | | 9 | $I_{i}^{net} = I_{Gi} - I_{Li}$ | Net injected current at bus $i$ | pu | | 10 | Base MVA: $Z_{pu} = Z_{\Omega}\cdot\dfrac{\text{MVA}_{B}}{(\text{kV}_{B})^{2}}$ | Conversion from physical to per-unit | pu | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** Line charging $B/2$ must be added **individually** to **each end** of the line in the diagonal entry. Adding the **total** $B$ to one bus is the most common error — and it costs full marks. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power System Simulators** (PSCAD, ETAP, PSS®E, MATPOWER, DigSilent PowerFactory) construct Y-bus as their **first internal step** before any analysis. - **Smart-grid state estimators** operate on the Y-bus to clean noisy SCADA measurements. - **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)** compare measured voltage phasors with Y-bus-predicted values for real-time monitoring. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Singular Transformation Method ($\mathbf{Y}_{bus} = \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{y}\,\mathbf{A}^{T}$) Consider a power network with $b$ branches and $n$ independent buses (excluding the reference/slack bus, which is grounded). We define the **primitive network**: **Step 1 — Define the primitive admittance matrix $\mathbf{y}$.** The primitive network represents each branch **in isolation** with its own admittance. For passive, mutually-coupled-free branches: $$\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & y_2 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & y_3 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & y_b \end{bmatrix}$$ > **Explanation:** $\mathbf{y}$ is **diagonal** because we assume no mutual coupling between branches. Each $y_k = \dfrac{1}{z_k}$ is the series admittance of the $k^{th}$ branch. **Step 2 — Define the incidence matrix $\mathbf{A}$.** The incidence matrix describes the **topological connection** of every branch to every bus. Each row corresponds to a bus; each column to a branch. $$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} & \cdots & a_{1b} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} & \cdots & a_{2b} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ a_{n1} & a_{n2} & \cdots & a_{nb} \end{bmatrix}$$ The entry $a_{ij}$ is defined as: $$a_{ij} = \begin{cases} +1, & \text{if current in branch } j \text{ leaves bus } i \\ -1, & \text{if current in branch } j \text{ enters bus } i \\ \;\;\,0, & \text{if branch } j \text{ is not connected to bus } i \end{cases}$$ **Step 3 — Relate bus currents and bus voltages using the incidence matrix.** Branch currents can be expressed in terms of bus voltages as: $$\mathbf{I}_{branch} = \mathbf{y}\cdot\mathbf{A}^{T}\cdot\mathbf{V}_{bus}$$ Bus currents (KCL injection) are related to branch currents by: $$\mathbf{I}_{bus} = \mathbf{A}\cdot\mathbf{I}_{branch}$$ Substituting the first equation into the second: $$\mathbf{I}_{bus} = \mathbf{A}\cdot\mathbf{y}\cdot\mathbf{A}^{T}\cdot\mathbf{V}_{bus}$$ **Step 4 — Identify the Y-bus.** Comparing with the defining equation $\mathbf{I}_{bus} = \mathbf{Y}_{bus}\,\mathbf{V}_{bus}$, we directly obtain: $$\boxed{\;\mathbf{Y}_{bus} = \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{y}\,\mathbf{A}^{T}\;}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Direct Inspection Method — From Nodal KCL Apply KCL at any arbitrary bus $i$ in the network. The net current injected at bus $i$ equals the sum of currents leaving bus $i$ through all branches: $$I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} I_{ij} \quad \text{(sum over all buses connected to } i\text{)}$$ For a single branch connecting buses $i$ and $j$ with series admittance $y_{ij}$: $$I_{ij} = y_{ij}\,(V_i - V_j)$$ Therefore, the total injected current at bus $i$ becomes: $$I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} y_{ij}\,(V_i - V_j)$$ Expanding the sum: $$I_i = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_{ij}\right)V_i - \sum_{j=1}^{n} y_{ij}\,V_j$$ **Step 1 — Identify the diagonal element** $Y_{ii}$: The coefficient of $V_i$ in the expression above is: $$\boxed{\;Y_{ii} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} y_{ij} + y_{i}^{shunt}\;}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the off-diagonal element** $Y_{ij}$: The coefficient of $V_j$ (for $i \neq j$) is: $$\boxed{\;Y_{ij} = -\,y_{ij}\;}$$ If buses $i$ and $j$ are not directly connected, then $y_{ij} = 0$, hence $Y_{ij} = 0$. This gives the Y-bus its characteristic **sparsity**. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A 4-Bus System Let us consider a typical KTU-style problem. > **Given:** A 4-bus system with the following line data (all in pu on 100 MVA base): | Line | From–To | Series Impedance $z$ | Half Line Charging $B/2$ | |------|---------|----------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | 1–2 | $0.02 + j0.06$ | $j0.030$ | | 2 | 1–3 | $0.08 + j0.24$ | $j0.025$ | | 3 | 2–3 | $0.06 + j0.18$ | $j0.020$ | | 4 | 2–4 | $0.04 + j0.12$ | $j0.015$ | | 5 | 3–4 | $0.01 + j0.03$ | $j0.010$ | **Step 1 — Compute series admittance of each line: $y_k = \dfrac{1}{z_k}$.** For line 1–2: $$y_{12} = \frac{1}{0.02 + j0.06} = \frac{0.02 - j0.06}{(0.02)^2 + (0.06)^2} = \frac{0.02 - j0.06}{0.0040} = 5.0 - j15.0$$ For line 1–3: $$y_{13} = \frac{1}{0.08 + j0.24} = \frac{0.08 - j0.24}{0.0640} = 1.25 - j3.75$$ For line 2–3: $$y_{23} = \frac{1}{0.06 + j0.18} = \frac{0.06 - j0.18}{0.0360} = 1.6667 - j5.0$$ For line 2–4: $$y_{24} = \frac{1}{0.04 + j0.12} = \frac{0.04 - j0.12}{0.0160} = 2.5 - j7.5$$ For line 3–4: $$y_{34} = \frac{1}{0.01 + j0.03} = \frac{0.01 - j0.03}{0.0010} = 10.0 - j30.0$$ **Step 2 — Compute the diagonal elements $Y_{ii}$.** $$Y_{11} = y_{12} + y_{13} + \frac{B_{12}}{2} + \frac{B_{13}}{2}$$ $$Y_{11} = (5.0 - j15.0) + (1.25 - j3.75) + j0.030 + j0.025$$ $$Y_{11} = 6.25 - j18.695$$ $$Y_{22} = y_{12} + y_{23} + y_{24} + \frac{B_{12}}{2} + \frac{B_{23}}{2} + \frac{B_{24}}{2}$$ $$Y_{22} = (5.0 - j15.0) + (1.6667 - j5.0) + (2.5 - j7.5) + j0.030 + j0.020 + j0.015$$ $$Y_{22} = 9.1667 - j27.435$$ $$Y_{33} = y_{13} + y_{23} + y_{34} + \frac{B_{13}}{2} + \frac{B_{23}}{2} + \frac{B_{34}}{2}$$ $$Y_{33} = (1.25 - j3.75) + (1.6667 - j5.0) + (10.0 - j30.0) + j0.025 + j0.020 + j0.010$$ $$Y_{33} = 12.9167 - j38.695$$ $$Y_{44} = y_{24} + y_{34} + \frac{B_{24}}{2} + \frac{B_{34}}{2}$$ $$Y_{44} = (2.5 - j7.5) + (10.0 - j30.0) + j0.015 + j0.010$$ $$Y_{44} = 12.5 - j37.475$$ **Step 3 — Compute the off-diagonal elements $Y_{ij} = -y_{ij}$.** $$Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -(5.0 - j15.0) = -5.0 + j15.0$$ $$Y_{13} = Y_{31} = -(1.25 - j3.75) = -1.25 + j3.75$$ $$Y_{23} = Y_{32} = -(1.6667 - j5.0) = -1.6667 + j5.0$$ $$Y_{24} = Y_{42} = -(2.5 - j7.5) = -2.5 + j7.5$$ $$Y_{34} = Y_{43} = -(10.0 - j30.0) = -10.0 + j30.0$$ **Step 4 — Assemble the complete Y-bus matrix.** $$\mathbf{Y}_{bus} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.250 & -5.000 & -1.250 & 0.000 \\ -5.000 & 9.167 & -1.667 & -2.500 \\ -1.250 & -1.667 & 12.917 & -10.000 \\ 0.000 & -2.500 & -10.000 & 12.500 \end{bmatrix} - j \begin{bmatrix} 18.695 & 15.000 & 3.750 & 0.000 \\ 15.000 & 27.435 & 5.000 & 7.500 \\ 3.750 & 5.000 & 38.695 & 30.000 \\ 0.000 & 7.500 & 30.000 & 37.475 \end{bmatrix}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** Each row sum equals the sum of shunts at the buses connected to it, which is consistent with the physics of nodal injection (Kirchhoff's Current Law). The matrix is **symmetric** about its main diagonal. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Y-Bus Formulation Pipeline The following block diagram maps the **end-to-end logical flow** of Y-bus formation as performed in a MATLAB/Python lab environment (e.g., the official KTU PCEEL609 lab manual). ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Read Network Data] --> B[Parse Bus Count n and Branch Count b] B --> C[Read Line Impedance z_ij for each branch] C --> D[Read Line Charging B/2 for each branch] D --> E[Initialize Y_bus as n x n Zero Matrix] E --> F{Loop over each branch i-j} F --> G[Compute Series Admittance: y_ij = 1 / z_ij] G --> H[Update Diagonal: Y_ii += y_ij + jB_ij/2] H --> I[Update Diagonal: Y_jj += y_ij + jB_ij/2] I --> J[Update Off-Diagonal: Y_ij -= y_ij] J --> K[Update Off-Diagonal: Y_ji -= y_ij] K --> F F -- All branches processed --> L[Apply Transformer Tap Ratios if any] L --> M[Apply Bus Shunt Admittances y_shunt] M --> N[Symmetrize: Y_ij = Y_ji] N --> O[Display Final Y_bus Matrix] O --> P[End: Save Workspace Variables] style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style P fill:#065f46,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style G fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style H fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style J fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style N fill:#581c87,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff ``` --- ### 4.2 Schematic Representation — Sample 4-Bus Network This Mermaid diagram represents the **sample network** used in the numerical example (Section 3.3), with buses as nodes and transmission lines as labeled edges. ```mermaid graph LR bus1((BUS 1
Generator)) bus2((BUS 2
Load)) bus3((BUS 3
Load)) bus4((BUS 4
Generator)) bus1 ---|L1: 0.02+j0.06
B/2=j0.030| bus2 bus1 ---|L2: 0.08+j0.24
B/2=j0.025| bus3 bus2 ---|L3: 0.06+j0.18
B/2=j0.020| bus3 bus2 ---|L4: 0.04+j0.12
B/2=j0.015| bus4 bus3 ---|L5: 0.01+j0.03
B/2=j0.010| bus4 style bus1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px style bus2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px style bus3 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px style bus4 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Role of Y-Bus in Power System Studies ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input I1[Network Topology] I2[Line Parameters] I3[Transformer Data] I4[Bus Shunts] end subgraph Processing YMAT[Y-Bus Matrix
Formation] end subgraph Applications A1[Newton Raphson
Load Flow] A2[Fault Analysis
Z-Bus Method] A3[Transient Stability
Simulation] A4[State Estimation
in EMS] A5[Contingency
Analysis] end I1 --> YMAT I2 --> YMAT I3 --> YMAT I4 --> YMAT YMAT --> A1 YMAT --> A2 YMAT --> A3 YMAT --> A4 YMAT --> A5 style YMAT fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** As the diagram illustrates, the Y-bus matrix acts as the **central processing kernel** for every power-system analytical tool. Mastery of Y-bus formation is therefore not just an academic exercise — it is the **foundational skill** for any career in power-system engineering. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]** *State any three properties of the Y-bus matrix.* **Model Answer:** The Y-bus matrix possesses the following essential properties: 1. **Square and Symmetric:** The Y-bus is a square matrix of order $n \times n$ where $n$ is the number of buses. It is symmetric about its main diagonal, i.e., $Y_{ij} = Y_{ji}$, *provided there is no phase-shifting transformer*. 2. **Sparse Nature:** For large power networks, the Y-bus is highly sparse because each bus is physically connected to only a few other buses. Off-diagonal entries corresponding to non-adjacent buses are exactly zero. 3. **Diagonal Dominance:** The magnitude of each diagonal element $Y_{ii}$ is greater than or equal to the sum of magnitudes of all off-diagonal elements in the same row, i.e., $\vert Y_{ii}\vert \geq \sum_{j \neq i}\vert Y_{ij}\vert$. This guarantees invertibility. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *Define the terms (i) self-admittance and (ii) mutual admittance with respect to Y-bus.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Self-Admittance ($Y_{ii}$):** It is the diagonal element of the Y-bus matrix and represents the **sum of all admittances connected to bus $i$**, including the half line-charging capacitances of all lines incident on bus $i$ and any shunt admittance at bus $i$. 2. **Mutual Admittance ($Y_{ij}$):** It is the off-diagonal element of the Y-bus matrix and represents the **negative of the admittance of the line directly connecting buses $i$ and $j$**. If buses $i$ and $j$ are not directly connected by a transmission line, then $Y_{ij} = 0$. --- ### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]** > **(a)** For the 3-bus system whose one-line diagram and parameters are shown below, form the Y-bus matrix using the **direct inspection method**. Each line has a series impedance of $0.02 + j0.08$ pu and total line charging of $j0.02$ pu. **(7 marks)** | Line | From–To | |------|---------| | 1 | 1–2 | | 2 | 2–3 | | 3 | 1–3 | > **(b)** Explain the **singular transformation method** of Y-bus formulation with a suitable mathematical derivation. List the conditions under which it is preferred over direct inspection. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(a) **Step 1 — Calculate the series admittance of each line.** $$y_{12} = y_{23} = y_{13} = \frac{1}{0.02 + j0.08}$$ Rationalizing by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate: $$y_{12} = \frac{0.02 - j0.08}{(0.02)^2 + (0.08)^2} = \frac{0.02 - j0.08}{0.0068} = 2.941 - j11.765 \;\; \text{pu}$$ The total line charging of each line is $j0.02$ pu; therefore, **half-line charging at each end** is: $$\frac{B_{ij}}{2} = \frac{j0.02}{2} = j0.01 \;\; \text{pu}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the diagonal elements.** Each bus is connected to all three lines (1–2, 2–3, 1–3), so: $$Y_{11} = y_{12} + y_{13} + 2 \times j0.01$$ $$Y_{11} = (2.941 - j11.765) + (2.941 - j11.765) + j0.02 = 5.882 - j23.510 \;\; \text{pu}$$ By symmetry of the network parameters: $$Y_{22} = Y_{33} = Y_{11} = 5.882 - j23.510 \;\; \text{pu}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the off-diagonal elements.** $$Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -y_{12} = -(2.941 - j11.765) = -2.941 + j11.765 \;\; \text{pu}$$ $$Y_{13} = Y_{31} = -y_{13} = -2.941 + j11.765 \;\; \text{pu}$$ $$Y_{23} = Y_{32} = -y_{23} = -2.941 + j11.765 \;\; \text{pu}$$ **Step 4 — Assemble the Y-bus.** $$\mathbf{Y}_{bus} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.882 & -2.941 & -2.941 \\ -2.941 & 5.882 & -2.941 \\ -2.941 & -2.941 & 5.882 \end{bmatrix} - j \begin{bmatrix} 23.510 & 11.765 & 11.765 \\ 11.765 & 23.510 & 11.765 \\ 11.765 & 11.765 & 23.510 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{pu}$$ #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(b) The singular transformation method uses the **bus incidence matrix $\mathbf{A}$** and **primitive admittance matrix $\mathbf{y}$**. The primitive admittance matrix $\mathbf{y}$ is a diagonal matrix whose entries are the series admittances of each branch: $$\mathbf{y} = \text{diag}(y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_b)$$ The incidence matrix $\mathbf{A}$ has entries $a_{ij}$ equal to $+1$ if branch $j$ leaves bus $i$, $-1$ if branch $j$ enters bus $i$, and $0$ otherwise. Using KCL: $\mathbf{I}_{bus} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{I}_{branch}$ and Ohm's law: $\mathbf{I}_{branch} = \mathbf{y}\mathbf{V}_{branch} = \mathbf{y}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{V}_{bus}$, we combine: $$\mathbf{I}_{bus} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{y}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{V}_{bus}$$ Comparing with the defining Y-bus equation gives: $$\boxed{\;\mathbf{Y}_{bus} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{y}\mathbf{A}^{T}\;}$$ **Conditions when singular transformation is preferred:** 1. The system contains **mutually coupled lines**, where the off-diagonal elements of $\mathbf{y}$ are non-zero. 2. **Phase-shifting transformers** are present (asymmetric Y-bus elements). 3. A **systematic, automated matrix-build** is needed (computer implementation). 4. **Large networks with many branches** — programming the incidence matrix is faster than manual KCL. --- #### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternate Choice]** > **(a)** For the 5-bus power system given below, compute the Y-bus matrix using direct inspection. Lines 1–2, 2–3, 3–4, 4–5, 1–5 have impedances of $j0.4, j0.2, j0.3, j0.25, j0.5$ pu respectively. Neglect line charging. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A single line-to-ground fault occurs at bus 2 of the Y-bus computed in part (a). Using the **Z-bus method**, explain how you would compute the fault current. State the relevant formula. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(a) **Step 1 — Compute the series admittances.** $$y_{12} = \frac{1}{j0.4} = -j2.5, \quad y_{23} = -j5.0, \quad y_{34} = -j3.333, \quad y_{45} = -j4.0, \quad y_{15} = -j2.0$$ **Step 2 — Form the diagonal elements.** $$Y_{11} = y_{12} + y_{15} = -j2.5 - j2.0 = -j4.5 \;\; \text{pu}$$ $$Y_{22} = y_{12} + y_{23} = -j2.5 - j5.0 = -j7.5 \;\; \text{pu}$$ $$Y_{33} = y_{23} + y_{34} = -j5.0 - j3.333 = -j8.333 \;\; \text{pu}$$ $$Y_{44} = y_{34} + y_{45} = -j3.333 - j4.0 = -j7.333 \;\; \text{pu}$$ $$Y_{55} = y_{45} + y_{15} = -j4.0 - j2.0 = -j6.0 \;\; \text{pu}$$ **Step 3 — Form the off-diagonal elements.** $$Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -y_{12} = j2.5; \quad Y_{23} = Y_{32} = j5.0$$ $$Y_{34} = Y_{43} = j3.333; \quad Y_{45} = Y_{54} = j4.0; \quad Y_{15} = Y_{51} = j2.0$$ All other entries are zero. **Step 4 — Y-bus matrix.** $$\mathbf{Y}_{bus} = \begin{bmatrix} -j4.5 & j2.5 & 0 & 0 & j2.0 \\ j2.5 & -j7.5 & j5.0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & j5.0 & -j8.333 & j3.333 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & j3.333 & -j7.333 & j4.0 \\ j2.0 & 0 & 0 & j4.0 & -j6.0 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{pu}$$ #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(b) For a single line-to-ground (SLG) fault at bus $k$ with fault impedance $Z_f$, the fault current is computed using the Z-bus (which is the inverse of Y-bus: $\mathbf{Z}_{bus} = \mathbf{Y}_{bus}^{-1}$): $$I_f = \frac{V_f^{(0)}}{Z_{kk} + Z_f}$$ where: - $V_f^{(0)}$ is the **pre-fault voltage** at the faulted bus $k$ (usually taken as $1.0 \angle 0°$ pu), - $Z_{kk}$ is the **$k^{th}$ diagonal element** of the Z-bus matrix, - $Z_f$ is the **fault impedance** (zero for a bolted fault). The corresponding voltage at any other bus $i$ during the fault is: $$V_i = V_i^{(0)} - Z_{ik}\,I_f$$ --- > [!WARNING] > 🚨 **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting half-line charging:** Always add $B/2$ at **each end** of every transmission line. Most students add the full $B$ to one bus, losing 1 to 2 marks per occurrence. > 2. **Sign error in off-diagonal elements:** Off-diagonal elements must be **negative** of the line admittance. Writing $+y_{ij}$ instead of $-y_{ij}$ is the most common sign error. > 3. **Asymmetry trap:** Phase-shifting transformers make the Y-bus **asymmetric**. In such cases, $Y_{ij} \neq Y_{ji}$. Students often lose marks for assuming symmetry unconditionally. > 4. **Unit mismatch:** Failing to convert impedance to **per-unit** before forming the Y-bus. > 5. **Not symmetrizing:** After populating the matrix, always enforce $Y_{ij} = Y_{ji}$ for passive networks — the examiner verifies this. > 6. **Skipping the "zero entries"** for non-adjacent buses: explicitly mark them as $0$ in the answer, otherwise the examiner may deduct for incompleteness. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > Use this high-density checklist as your **last-hour revision sheet** before the KTU Power System Lab examination. - ⭐ **Y-bus is symmetric, sparse, complex, and square** of order $n \times n$. - ⭐ **Diagonal element** $Y_{ii}$ = sum of all admittances connected to bus $i$ **plus** any shunt at bus $i$. - ⭐ **Off-diagonal element** $Y_{ij} = -y_{ij}$ (negative of direct line admittance) or **zero** (if no direct connection). - ⭐ Always add **half line-charging** $B/2$ at each end of the line — never the full $B$. - ⭐ The defining equation is $\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{Y}\mathbf{V}$; equivalently, $I_i = \sum_{j} Y_{ij} V_j$. - ⭐ Two methods: **Direct Inspection** (faster, manual, KTU preferred) and **Singular Transformation** ($Y = A y A^T$, used for mutually coupled lines and computer implementation). - ⭐ For a network with **mutually coupled lines**, the primitive matrix $\mathbf{y}$ is no longer diagonal — it contains off-diagonal terms representing mutual admittances. - ⭐ For **phase-shifting transformers**, the off-diagonal terms become asymmetric ($Y_{ij} \neq Y_{ji}$). - ⭐ The Y-bus is **invertible** (and hence the Z-bus exists) because it is **diagonally dominant**. - ⭐ Real-world software tools: **MATPOWER**, **PSCAD**, **ETAP**, **PSS®E**, **PowerWorld** all begin their analysis pipeline with Y-bus formation. - ⭐ KTU Lab Viva favorite questions: 1. *"Why is Y-bus symmetric?"* → Because line admittances are reciprocal ($y_{ij} = y_{ji}$). 2. *"When does Y-bus become asymmetric?"* → In the presence of phase-shifting transformers. 3. *"What is the role of Y-bus in load flow?"* → It forms the linear admittance network equation $I = YV$; the bus admittance matrix is then iteratively solved for voltage phasors. 4. *"Why prefer Y-bus over Z-bus for large systems?"* → Y-bus is sparse (easy to store), Z-bus is dense (computationally expensive to build for large networks). --- > 📌 **Final Note:** Mastering Y-bus formation unlocks the door to **load flow, fault analysis, and stability studies** — the three pillars of every KTU Power Systems examination paper. Practice at least three hand-computed examples and one MATPOWER/Python implementation before the lab record evaluation.

topics.title: Transmission Line Modelling: ABCD Constants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Transmission Line Modelling: ABCD Constants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Transmission Line** in a power system is a conductor used to transmit bulk electrical energy from generating stations to load centres. To analyse the steady-state performance of medium and long transmission lines, the line is represented as a **two-port network** characterised by the **Generalised Circuit Constants (A, B, C, D)**, also called **ABCD Parameters** or **Generalised Line Constants**.

The two-port network is mathematically expressed as:

$$V_S = A \cdot V_R + B \cdot I_R$$

$$I_S = C \cdot V_R + D \cdot I_R$$

In matrix form, this is written as:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_S \\ I_S \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_R \\ I_R \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- $V_S, I_S$ = Sending-end voltage and current
- $V_R, I_R$ = Receiving-end voltage and current
- $A, B, C, D$ = Complex dimensionless/ohmic/mho quantities called the **Generalised Circuit Constants**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property of ABCD Constants:** For any passive, linear, bilateral two-port network representing a transmission line, the **determinant condition** must be satisfied: $AD - BC = 1$. For a *symmetrical* line, $A = D$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a long water pipeline carrying water from a reservoir (sending end) to a city (receiving end). As water flows through the pipe:
- The **pressure** (analogous to **voltage**) drops along the pipe due to friction (resistance).
- Some water **leaks out** through tiny side openings along the pipe (analogous to the **shunt capacitance** of the line).
- The pipe itself causes a delay and opposition to flow (analogous to **series inductance and resistance**).

The **ABCD constants** act like a *translator* — they take the measurements at the city end (receiving end) and tell you exactly what is happening at the reservoir end (sending end). They package the entire behaviour of the pipeline (or transmission line) into just four numbers.

For **short lines**, the leakage is so small we can ignore it. For **medium lines**, we model the leakage as lumped capacitors at the ends. For **long lines**, the leakage is distributed uniformly and must be modelled using **hyperbolic functions** (cosh and sinh).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "A, B, C, D" and not other letters?** These letters were standardised in early power system textbooks by pioneers like Charles Proteus Steinmetz. They have been retained in KTU 2024 Scheme to maintain consistency with international power engineering literature.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Frequency:** $f = 50$ Hz (Indian standard, **bolded** as it is fixed in KTU problems unless otherwise stated)
- **Angular Frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159$ rad/s
- **Line length classification**:
  - **Short line:** Length $< 80$ km
  - **Medium line:** Length $80$ to $250$ km
  - **Long line:** Length $> 250$ km
- **Per-unit-length parameters**: $z$ (series impedance/km), $y$ (shunt admittance/km)
- **Propagation constant**: $\gamma = \sqrt{zy}$ (per unit length)
- **Characteristic impedance**: $Z_c = \sqrt{z/y}$ (ohms)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Transmission Line Models

The ABCD constants are derived differently for each class of line:

### (a) Short Transmission Line (Negligible Shunt Capacitance)
- Total series impedance: $Z = R + jX_L$
- Shunt admittance $Y = 0$
- $A = 1$, $B = Z$, $C = 0$, $D = 1$
- Determinant: $AD - BC = (1)(1) - (Z)(0) = 1$ ✓

### (b) Medium Transmission Line — **Nominal T Model**
The total shunt admittance $Y$ is lumped at the **centre** of the line, and half the series impedance is placed on each side.

### (c) Medium Transmission Line — **Nominal π Model**
The total shunt admittance $Y$ is split equally at the **two ends** of the line, with the full series impedance in the middle.

### (d) Long Transmission Line (Distributed Parameters)
The line parameters $z$ and $y$ are **uniformly distributed**. Solutions to the differential equations give rise to **hyperbolic functions**.

## 2.2 Why and How — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Why use two-port parameters?** Because power engineers are typically interested in the receiving-end conditions (load voltage, power factor) and need to determine the sending-end quantities (which is what the generator must supply).
- **How are A and D dimensionless?** They are voltage ratios with no units.
- **How is B measured?** In ohms (Ω) — it represents the impedance "seen" by the current.
- **How is C measured?** In siemens (S) — it is a transfer admittance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in every KTU board exam question on transmission lines.**

| # | Line Model | A | B | C | D | Key Equation |
|---|------------|---|---|---|---|--------------|
| 1 | Short Line | $1$ | $Z$ | $0$ | $1$ | $V_S = V_R + I_R Z$ |
| 2 | Nominal **T** | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | $Z\left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$ | $Y$ | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | — |
| 3 | Nominal **π** | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | $Z$ | $Y\left(1 + \dfrac{YZ}{4}\right)$ | $1 + \dfrac{YZ}{2}$ | — |
| 4 | Long Line | $\cosh(\gamma \ell)$ | $Z_c \sinh(\gamma \ell)$ | $\dfrac{\sinh(\gamma \ell)}{Z_c}$ | $\cosh(\gamma \ell)$ | $\gamma = \sqrt{zy}$, $Z_c = \sqrt{z/y}$ |

### Additional Important Performance Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Units |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Sending-end power factor | $\cos\phi_S = \dfrac{\text{Re}\{V_S I_S^*\}}{\vert V_S \vert \cdot \vert I_S \vert}$ | dimensionless |
| Voltage Regulation | $VR\% = \dfrac{\vert V_{R,NL} \vert - \vert V_{R,FL} \vert}{\vert V_{R,FL} \vert} \times 100$ | % |
| Transmission Efficiency | $\eta\% = \dfrac{P_R}{P_S} \times 100 = \dfrac{V_R I_R \cos\phi_R}{V_S I_S \cos\phi_S} \times 100$ | % |
| Surge Impedance Loading | $SIL = \dfrac{\vert V_L \vert^2}{Z_c}$ | MW |
| Propagation Constant | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ | per km |
| Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi}{\beta}$ | km |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Substitution:** Whenever you need to write $\vert x \vert$ (modulus) inside a table, use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Load Flow Studies:** ABCD parameters form the building blocks of the **ABCD model of the network** used in the Newton-Raphson and Gauss-Seidel methods.
- **FACTS Devices:** Flexible AC Transmission Systems (like SSSC, UPFC) use ABCD parameter modulation to control power flow.
- **Relay Coordination:** Distance relays use line impedance ($B$) to determine fault location.
- **Renewable Integration:** Long HVAC submarine cables (offshore wind) are analysed exclusively with the **distributed-parameter (long line) model** using hyperbolic functions.
- **EHV Transmission:** India's 765 kV and 1200 kV lines always require the long-line model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nominal T Model ABCD Constants

### Circuit Description
For the Nominal T model, the half-line series impedance is $Z/2$ on each side and the total shunt admittance $Y$ is at the midpoint.

**Step 1: Express the voltage at the midpoint ($V_M$) using KVL**

Applying KVL across the receiving-end half:

$$V_M = V_R + I_R \cdot \frac{Z}{2}$$

**Step 2: Express the current at the sending end using KCL**

The current through the shunt branch at midpoint is $V_M \cdot Y$. The current flowing through the series half from the sending end is therefore $(I_R + V_M Y)$. Applying KVL at the sending end:

$$V_S = V_M + \frac{Z}{2}(I_R + V_M Y)$$

**Step 3: Substitute $V_M$ into the $V_S$ equation**

$$V_S = V_R + I_R \frac{Z}{2} + \frac{Z}{2}I_R + \frac{Z}{2} V_M Y$$

$$V_S = V_R + I_R Z + \frac{ZY}{2}\left(V_R + I_R \frac{Z}{2}\right)$$

$$V_S = V_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R \cdot Z\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)$$

**Step 4: Compare with the standard form $V_S = A V_R + B I_R$**

$$\boxed{A_T = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}, \quad B_T = Z\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)}$$

**Step 5: Derive the sending-end current expression**

$$I_S = I_R + V_M Y = I_R + \left(V_R + I_R \frac{Z}{2}\right)Y$$

$$I_S = V_R \cdot Y + I_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)$$

**Step 6: Compare with $I_S = C V_R + D I_R$**

$$\boxed{C_T = Y, \quad D_T = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}}$$

**Step 7: Verify the determinant condition**

$$AD - BC = \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)^2 - Z\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)(Y) = 1 + YZ + \frac{Y^2Z^2}{4} - YZ - \frac{Y^2Z^2}{4} = 1 \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Nominal π Model ABCD Constants

### Circuit Description
The Nominal π model has $Y/2$ at each end and total series impedance $Z$ in the middle.

**Step 1: Define the voltage at the receiving-end node (after the first $Y/2$ shunt branch)**

Let $V_1$ be the voltage at the receiving terminal. The current $I_R$ splits: one part flows through the series $Z$, another through $Y/2$. The current entering the series branch is $I_R + V_R \cdot (Y/2)$.

**Step 2: Apply KVL across the series branch**

The voltage at the sending-end terminal is:

$$V_S = V_R + Z\left(I_R + V_R \cdot \frac{Y}{2}\right)$$

$$V_S = V_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R \cdot Z$$

**Step 3: Compare with standard form**

$$\boxed{A_\pi = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}, \quad B_\pi = Z}$$

**Step 4: Apply KCL at the sending-end node**

The sending-end current equals the current through the series branch plus the current through the sending-end shunt:

$$I_S = I_R + V_R \frac{Y}{2} + V_S \frac{Y}{2}$$

Substituting $V_S$:

$$I_S = I_R + \frac{Y}{2}V_R + \frac{Y}{2}\left[V_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + I_R Z\right]$$

$$I_S = I_R\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right) + V_R Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)$$

**Step 5: Compare with standard form**

$$\boxed{C_\pi = Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right), \quad D_\pi = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2}}$$

**Step 6: Verify the determinant condition**

$$AD - BC = \left(1 + \frac{YZ}{2}\right)^2 - Z \cdot Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) = 1 \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Long Transmission Line (Distributed Parameters)

### Starting Assumptions
For a long line of length $\ell$, the per-unit-length series impedance is $z$ and the per-unit-length shunt admittance is $y$. Consider an infinitesimal element $dx$ at distance $x$ from the receiving end.

### Step 1: Write the Telegrapher's Equations
Across the infinitesimal element:

$$dV(x) = I(x) \cdot z \, dx$$

$$dI(x) = V(x) \cdot y \, dx$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dV(x)}{dx} = z \cdot I(x) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$\frac{dI(x)}{dx} = y \cdot V(x) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 2: Decouple the Equations
Differentiate equation (i) with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{d^2V(x)}{dx^2} = z \cdot \frac{dI(x)}{dx}$$

Substitute (ii):

$$\frac{d^2V(x)}{dx^2} = zy \cdot V(x)$$

Define the **propagation constant** $\gamma^2 = zy$:

$$\frac{d^2V(x)}{dx^2} = \gamma^2 V(x) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

Similarly:

$$\frac{d^2I(x)}{dx^2} = \gamma^2 I(x) \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

### Step 3: Solve the Second-Order ODE
The general solution of equation (iii) is:

$$V(x) = k_1 e^{\gamma x} + k_2 e^{-\gamma x}$$

Using boundary condition at $x = 0$ (receiving end): $V(0) = V_R$, so $k_1 + k_2 = V_R$.

Using $I(x) = \dfrac{1}{z}\dfrac{dV}{dx} = \dfrac{\gamma}{z}(k_1 e^{\gamma x} - k_2 e^{-\gamma x})$ and $I(0) = I_R$:

$$k_1 - k_2 = \frac{z I_R}{\gamma} = \sqrt{\frac{z}{y}} \cdot I_R = Z_c \cdot I_R$$

where $Z_c = \sqrt{z/y}$ is the **characteristic (surge) impedance**.

Solving:

$$k_1 = \frac{1}{2}(V_R + Z_c I_R), \quad k_2 = \frac{1}{2}(V_R - Z_c I_R)$$

### Step 4: Express in Hyperbolic Form
Using the identities $e^{\gamma x} = \cosh(\gamma x) + \sinh(\gamma x)$ and $e^{-\gamma x} = \cosh(\gamma x) - \sinh(\gamma x)$:

$$V(x) = V_R \cosh(\gamma x) + Z_c I_R \sinh(\gamma x)$$

$$I(x) = I_R \cosh(\gamma x) + \frac{V_R}{Z_c} \sinh(\gamma x)$$

### Step 5: Apply $x = \ell$ (Sending End)
$$V_S = V_R \cosh(\gamma \ell) + Z_c I_R \sinh(\gamma \ell)$$

$$I_S = \frac{V_R}{Z_c} \sinh(\gamma \ell) + I_R \cosh(\gamma \ell)$$

### Step 6: Read the ABCD Constants

$$\boxed{A = D = \cosh(\gamma \ell), \quad B = Z_c \sinh(\gamma \ell), \quad C = \frac{\sinh(\gamma \ell)}{Z_c}}$$

### Step 7: Verify Determinant
$$AD - BC = \cosh^2(\gamma \ell) - \sinh^2(\gamma \ell) = 1 \checkmark$$

(Using the hyperbolic identity $\cosh^2\theta - \sinh^2\theta = 1$.)

### Step 8: Useful Hyperbolic Series Expansions
For shorter segments, the hyperbolic functions can be expanded into polynomials (similar to a Taylor series):

$$\cosh(\gamma \ell) = 1 + \frac{(\gamma \ell)^2}{2!} + \frac{(\gamma \ell)^4}{4!} + \cdots$$

$$\sinh(\gamma \ell) = \gamma \ell + \frac{(\gamma \ell)^3}{3!} + \frac{(\gamma \ell)^5}{5!} + \cdots$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Truncating these series** at the second term actually **recovers the Nominal T and Nominal π** results, demonstrating that the medium-line models are first-order approximations of the distributed-parameter long-line model. This is a frequently-asked conceptual question in KTU viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Two-Port Representation of a Transmission Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SENDING END GENERATOR BUS] -->|V_S, I_S| B[TRANSMISSION LINE NETWORK]
    B -->|ABCD PARAMETERS| C[RECEIVING END LOAD BUS]
    C --> D[LOAD]
    B --> E[SERIES IMPEDANCE Z]
    B --> F[SHUNT ADMITTANCE Y]
    E --> G[ABC = 1 + YZ/2]
    E --> H[B = Z]
    F --> I[C = Y]
    F --> J[AD = 1 + YZ/2]
    G --> K[VALIDATE AD - BC = 1]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification of Transmission Line Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TRANSMISSION LINE] --> B{LENGTH}
    B -->|LESS THAN 80 KM| C[SHORT LINE]
    B -->|80 TO 250 KM| D[MEDIUM LINE]
    B -->|GREATER THAN 250 KM| E[LONG LINE]
    C --> F[A=1 B=Z C=0 D=1]
    D --> G[NOMINAL T MODEL]
    D --> H[NOMINAL PI MODEL]
    E --> I[ABC = cosh gamma L]
    E --> J[B = Zc sinh gamma L]
    E --> K[C = sinh gamma L divided by Zc]
    E --> L[D = cosh gamma L]
    G --> M[A = D = 1 + YZ/2]
    H --> N[A = D = 1 + YZ/2]
    M --> O[VERIFY AD - BC = 1]
    N --> O
    I --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    L --> O
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Nominal T Model — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[SENDING END] -->|Z/2| M[MIDPOINT NODE]
    M -->|Z/2| R[RECEIVING END]
    M -->|Y| G[GROUND]
    S --> VS[VOLTAGE V_S]
    M --> VM[VOLTAGE V_M]
    R --> VR[VOLTAGE V_R]
    R --> IR[CURRENT I_R]
    S --> IS[CURRENT I_S]
    VM --> EQ1[V_M = V_R + I_R Z/2]
    EQ1 --> A1[A = 1 + YZ/2]
    EQ1 --> B1[B = Z(1 + YZ/4)]
    G --> Y1[Y = 1/2 ADMITTANCE]
    Y1 --> C1[C = Y]
    Y1 --> D1[D = 1 + YZ/2]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Nominal π Model — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[SENDING END] -->|Z| R[RECEIVING END]
    S -->|Y/2| G1[GROUND NODE 1]
    R -->|Y/2| G2[GROUND NODE 2]
    S --> VS[VOLTAGE V_S]
    R --> VR[VOLTAGE V_R]
    R --> IR[CURRENT I_R]
    S --> IS[CURRENT I_S]
    VS --> AP[A = 1 + YZ/2]
    VS --> BP[B = Z]
    G2 --> CP[C = Y(1 + YZ/4)]
    G1 --> DP[D = 1 + YZ/2]
    AP --> CK[CHECK AD - BC = 1]
    BP --> CK
    CP --> CK
    DP --> CK
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Long Line Solution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT z, y, L] --> B[COMPUTE gamma = sqrt zy]
    A --> C[COMPUTE Z_c = sqrt z/y]
    B --> D[COMPUTE gamma L]
    C --> E[COMPUTE A = D = cosh gamma L]
    C --> F[COMPUTE B = Z_c sinh gamma L]
    C --> G[COMPUTE C = sinh gamma L / Z_c]
    D --> E
    D --> F
    D --> G
    E --> H[VALIDATE AD - BC = 1]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[OUTPUT ABCD MATRIX]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the ABCD constants of a transmission line. State the condition that these constants must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

The ABCD constants (also called Generalised Circuit Constants) are the parameters of a two-port network that represent the relationship between the sending-end and receiving-end voltages and currents of a transmission line:

$$V_S = A \cdot V_R + B \cdot I_R$$

$$I_S = C \cdot V_R + D \cdot I_R$$

**Condition:** For any passive, linear, bilateral two-port network (such as a transmission line), the determinant of the ABCD matrix must equal unity:

$$AD - BC = 1$$

Additionally, for a *symmetrical* line, $A = D$.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equations, 1 for the condition)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the Nominal T and Nominal π models of a medium transmission line.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Nominal T Model | Nominal π Model |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| Shunt admittance placement | Lumped at the **centre** of the line | Split equally at the **two ends** |
| Series impedance placement | Split as $Z/2$ on either side of the centre | Lump sum $Z$ in the middle |
| Parameter $B$ | $Z(1 + YZ/4)$ | $Z$ |
| Parameter $C$ | $Y$ | $Y(1 + YZ/4)$ |
| Easier to compute | $C$ (simpler) | $B$ (simpler) |

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for T model description, 1.5 marks for π model description)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 200 km transmission line has a resistance of 0.15 Ω/km/phase, an inductance of 1.2 mH/km/phase, and a shunt capacitance of 0.01 μF/km/phase. Using the **Nominal π model**, determine the ABCD constants. Also calculate the sending-end voltage if the receiving-end voltage is 110 kV (line-to-line) at no load.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 50 Hz transmission line of length 300 km has the following per-phase per-km parameters: $z = 0.1 + j0.5$ Ω/km, $y = j3.0 \times 10^{-6}$ S/km. Calculate the ABCD constants using the **long-line (distributed parameter) model**. Take receiving-end voltage as 220 kV (line) and receiving-end current as 200 A at 0.9 power factor lagging.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a): Nominal π Model

**Step 1: Compute total series impedance Z per phase**

$$R = 0.15 \times 200 = 30 \text{ Ω}$$

$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2\pi \times 50 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 = 75.398 \text{ Ω}$$

$$\boxed{Z = 30 + j75.398 \text{ Ω/phase}}$$

**Step 2: Compute total shunt admittance Y per phase**

$$Y = j \omega C \ell = j \times 2\pi \times 50 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \times 200$$

$$Y = j \times 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S/phase}$$

**Step 3: Compute the product $YZ$**

$$YZ = j 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \times (30 + j75.398)$$

$$YZ = j 0.01885 - 0.04738 = -0.04738 + j0.01885$$

**Step 4: Compute the ABCD constants for Nominal π**

$$A = D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2} = 1 + \frac{-0.04738 + j0.01885}{2}$$

$$\boxed{A = D = 0.9763 + j0.00943}$$

$$B = Z = 30 + j75.398 \text{ Ω}$$

$$C = Y\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right) = j6.2832 \times 10^{-4}\left(1 + \frac{-0.04738 + j0.01885}{4}\right)$$

$$C = j6.2832 \times 10^{-4} (0.98815 + j0.00471)$$

$$C = -2.96 \times 10^{-6} + j6.21 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S}$$

**Step 5: No-load sending-end voltage**

At no-load, $I_R = 0$. Therefore $V_S = A \cdot V_R$.

Per phase: $V_R = 110 \times 10^3 / \sqrt{3} = 63508.5$ V

$$V_S = (0.9763 + j0.00943) \times 63508.5$$

$$V_S = 62000 + j598.7 \text{ V/phase}$$

$$\vert V_S \vert = 63510 \text{ V/phase}$$

Line-to-line: $V_S (L-L) = 110.003$ kV

**Voltage Regulation** at no load is essentially zero by definition; however, the **no-load receiving-end voltage** (Ferranti effect check) is the sending voltage as computed.

*(7 marks: 2 for Z, 2 for Y, 2 for ABCD, 1 for V_S)*

#### Part (b): Long-Line Model

**Step 1: Per-unit-length parameters**

$$z = 0.1 + j0.5 \text{ Ω/km}$$

$$y = j 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ S/km}$$

**Step 2: Compute propagation constant $\gamma$**

$$zy = (0.1 + j0.5)(j3.0 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$zy = j3.0 \times 10^{-7} - 1.5 \times 10^{-6} = -1.5 \times 10^{-6} + j3.0 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{zy} = \sqrt{-1.5 \times 10^{-6} + j3.0 \times 10^{-7}}$$

Computing the square root using polar form:

$$\vert zy \vert = \sqrt{(1.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 + (3.0 \times 10^{-7})^2} = 1.5297 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\angle zy = \arctan\left(\frac{3.0 \times 10^{-7}}{-1.5 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 180° - 11.31° = 168.69°$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{1.5297 \times 10^{-6}} \angle 84.345° = 1.237 \times 10^{-3} \angle 84.345°$$

$$\gamma = 1.221 \times 10^{-4} + j1.230 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per km}$$

**Step 3: Compute characteristic impedance $Z_c$**

$$Z_c = \sqrt{\frac{z}{y}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1 + j0.5}{j3.0 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$\frac{z}{y} = \frac{0.1 + j0.5}{j3.0 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{(0.1 + j0.5)(-j)}{3.0 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.5 - j0.1}{3.0 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\frac{z}{y} = 1.667 \times 10^{5} - j3.333 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\left\vert \frac{z}{y} \right\vert = 1.6997 \times 10^5, \quad \angle = -11.31°$$

$$Z_c = \sqrt{1.6997 \times 10^5} \angle -5.655° = 412.27 \angle -5.655° \text{ Ω}$$

$$Z_c = 410.14 - j40.62 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 4: Compute $\gamma \ell$ for $\ell = 300$ km**

$$\gamma \ell = (1.221 \times 10^{-4} + j1.230 \times 10^{-3}) \times 300$$

$$\gamma \ell = 0.03663 + j0.3690 \text{ radians}$$

**Step 5: Compute $\cosh(\gamma \ell)$ and $\sinh(\gamma \ell)$**

For a complex argument $\gamma \ell = a + jb$:

$$\cosh(a + jb) = \cosh(a)\cos(b) + j\sinh(a)\sin(b)$$

$$\cosh(0.03663) = 1.00067, \quad \cos(0.3690) = 0.9323$$

$$\sinh(0.03663) = 0.03664, \quad \sin(0.3690) = 0.3615$$

$$\cosh(\gamma \ell) = (1.00067)(0.9323) + j(0.03664)(0.3615)$$

$$\boxed{A = D = 0.9330 + j0.01325}$$

$$\sinh(\gamma \ell) = \sinh(a)\cos(b) + j\cosh(a)\sin(b)$$

$$\sinh(\gamma \ell) = (0.03664)(0.9323) + j(1.00067)(0.3615)$$

$$\sinh(\gamma \ell) = 0.03416 + j0.3618$$

**Step 6: Compute B and C**

$$B = Z_c \sinh(\gamma \ell)$$

$$B = (410.14 - j40.62)(0.03416 + j0.3618)$$

$$B = (410.14)(0.03416) - (-40.62)(0.3618) + j[(410.14)(0.3618) + (-40.62)(0.03416)]$$

$$B = 14.01 + 14.70 + j(148.39 - 1.39)$$

$$\boxed{B = 28.71 + j147.0 \text{ Ω}}$$

$$C = \frac{\sinh(\gamma \ell)}{Z_c} = \frac{0.03416 + j0.3618}{410.14 - j40.62}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate:

$$C = \frac{(0.03416 + j0.3618)(410.14 + j40.62)}{(410.14)^2 + (40.62)^2}$$

$$C = \frac{(0.03416 \times 410.14 - 0.3618 \times 40.62) + j(0.03416 \times 40.62 + 0.3618 \times 410.14)}{(168215 + 1650)}$$

$$C = \frac{(14.01 - 14.70) + j(1.388 + 148.39)}{169865}$$

$$C = \frac{-0.69 + j149.78}{169865}$$

$$\boxed{C = -4.06 \times 10^{-6} + j8.816 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S}}$$

**Step 7: Verify $AD - BC = 1$**

$$AD = (0.9330 + j0.01325)^2 = 0.8704 + j0.02472 - 0.000176 = 0.8702 + j0.02472$$

$$BC = (28.71 + j147.0)(-4.06 \times 10^{-6} + j8.816 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$BC = -1.166 \times 10^{-4} + j0.02532 - 5.968 \times 10^{-4} - 0.1296$$

$$BC = -0.1297 + j0.02473$$

$$AD - BC = (0.8702 + 0.1297) + j(0.02472 - 0.02473) = 1.0 + j0.0 \approx 1 \checkmark$$

*(7 marks: 1 for γ, 1 for Z_c, 1 for γℓ, 1.5 for cosh & sinh, 1.5 for B and C, 1 for verification)*

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat phasor diagrams and derivations, obtain the ABCD constants of a **medium transmission line** represented by (i) Nominal T model, and (ii) Nominal π model. Verify the determinant condition for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-circuit 3-phase, 50 Hz, 250 km transmission line has the following per-km per-phase parameters: $R = 0.12$ Ω, $L = 1.0$ mH, $C = 0.008$ μF. Using the **Nominal T model**, compute:
- (i) The ABCD constants.
- (ii) The sending-end voltage when the line delivers a load of 50 MVA at 0.85 p.f. lagging and 110 kV (line-to-line) at the receiving end.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a): Derivations of T and π Models

*(Refer to Sections 3.1 and 3.2 above for the complete step-by-step derivations. The model answer for a 7-mark derivation question should include:)*

- **Nominal T Model:**
  - Circuit diagram (1 mark)
  - Expression for $V_M$ in terms of $V_R$ and $I_R$ (1 mark)
  - Derivation of $V_S$ and $I_S$ (2 marks)
  - Final constants $A_T, B_T, C_T, D_T$ with determinant verification (1 mark)
  - Determinant check showing $AD - BC = 1$ (1 mark)
  - Note: $C_T = Y$ is simpler than $B_T$

- **Nominal π Model:**
  - Circuit diagram (1 mark)
  - KCL at the receiving-end node (1 mark)
  - KVL across the series branch (1 mark)
  - Final constants $A_\pi, B_\pi, C_\pi, D_\pi$ (1 mark)
  - Determinant check (1 mark)
  - Note: $B_\pi = Z$ is simpler than $C_\pi$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake in derivation questions:** Many students forget to include the **determinant check** $AD - BC = 1$. KTU examiners specifically allocate marks (typically 1 mark) for this verification. Skipping it results in avoidable mark loss.

#### Part (b): Numerical Problem with Nominal T Model

**Step 1: Compute per-phase impedance Z**

$$R = 0.12 \times 250 = 30 \text{ Ω}$$

$$X_L = 2\pi \times 50 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 = 78.54 \text{ Ω}$$

$$Z = 30 + j78.54 \text{ Ω/phase}$$

$$|Z| = \sqrt{30^2 + 78.54^2} = 84.07 \text{ Ω}, \quad \angle Z = 69.09°$$

**Step 2: Compute per-phase shunt admittance Y**

$$Y = j 2\pi \times 50 \times 0.008 \times 10^{-6} \times 250$$

$$Y = j 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S/phase}$$

**Step 3: Compute $YZ$**

$$YZ = j 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \times (30 + j78.54)$$

$$YZ = -0.04934 + j 0.01885$$

**Step 4: Compute the ABCD constants for Nominal T**

$$A = D = 1 + \frac{YZ}{2} = 1 + \frac{-0.04934 + j 0.01885}{2}$$

$$\boxed{A = D = 0.9753 + j 0.00943}$$

$$B = Z\left(1 + \frac{YZ}{4}\right)$$

$$1 + \frac{YZ}{4} = 1 + \frac{-0.04934 + j 0.01885}{4} = 0.9877 + j 0.00471$$

$$B = (30 + j78.54)(0.9877 + j 0.00471)$$

$$B = 30 \times 0.9877 - 78.54 \times 0.00471 + j(30 \times 0.00471 + 78.54 \times 0.9877)$$

$$\boxed{B = 29.26 + j77.90 \text{ Ω}}$$

$$C = Y = j 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \text{ S}$$

**Step 5: Compute receiving-end voltage per phase and current**

$$V_R = \frac{110 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3}} = 63508.5 \text{ V/phase} \angle 0°$$

Receiving-end apparent power $S_R = 50$ MVA, $p.f. = 0.85$ lagging:

$$I_R = \frac{S_R}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{L-L}} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 110 \times 10^3} = 262.43 \text{ A}$$

$$\phi_R = \cos^{-1}(0.85) = 31.79°$$

$$I_R = 262.43 \angle -31.79° \text{ A} = 223.07 - j138.30 \text{ A}$$

**Step 6: Compute sending-end voltage**

$$V_S = A \cdot V_R + B \cdot I_R$$

$$V_S = (0.9753 + j 0.00943)(63508.5) + (29.26 + j77.90)(223.07 - j138.30)$$

$$V_S = (61937 + j598.8) + [(29.26 \times 223.07 + 77.90 \times 138.30) + j(77.90 \times 223.07 - 29.26 \times 138.30)]$$

$$V_S = (61937 + j598.8) + [(6527.6 + 10775.5) + j(17375.7 - 4046.7)]$$

$$V_S = (61937 + j598.8) + (17303.1 + j13329.0)$$

$$V_S = 79240.1 + j13927.8 \text{ V/phase}$$

$$|V_S| = \sqrt{79240.1^2 + 13927.8^2} = 80455 \text{ V/phase}$$

Line-to-line: $V_S (L-L) = 80455 \times \sqrt{3} = 139.36$ kV

**Step 7: Compute sending-end current and power factor**

$$I_S = C \cdot V_R + D \cdot I_R$$

$$I_S = j6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \times 63508.5 + (0.9753 + j0.00943)(223.07 - j138.30)$$

$$I_S = j39.91 + [223.07 \times 0.9753 + 138.30 \times 0.00943 + j(-223.07 \times 0.00943 + 0.9753 \times (-138.30))]$$

$$I_S = j39.91 + [217.57 + 1.304 + j(-2.103 - 134.88)]$$

$$I_S = 218.87 + j(39.91 - 136.98) = 218.87 - j97.07 \text{ A}$$

$$|I_S| = \sqrt{218.87^2 + 97.07^2} = 239.51 \text{ A}, \quad \angle I_S = -23.91°$$

**Step 8: Compute sending-end power factor**

$$V_S \angle = \arctan(13927.8/79240.1) = 9.98°$$

$$\phi_S = 9.98° - (-23.91°) = 33.89°$$

$$\cos\phi_S = \cos(33.89°) = 0.830 \text{ lagging}$$

**Step 9: Compute Transmission Efficiency**

$$P_R = \sqrt{3} \times V_{L-L} \times I_R \times \cos\phi_R = \sqrt{3} \times 110 \times 10^3 \times 262.43 \times 0.85 = 42.5 \text{ MW}$$

$$P_S = \sqrt{3} \times V_S (L-L) \times I_S \times \cos\phi_S = \sqrt{3} \times 139.36 \times 10^3 \times 239.51 \times 0.830$$

$$P_S = 47.31 \text{ MW}$$

$$\eta = \frac{42.5}{47.31} \times 100 = 89.83\%$$

*(7 marks: 1 for Z, 1 for Y, 1 for ABCD, 1.5 for V_S, 1.5 for I_S and efficiency, 1 for power factor)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in ABCD-constant questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the √3 conversion** between phase and line values. KTU problems often quote receiving-end voltage as line-to-line, but ABCD constants are always computed **per phase**. Deduct 1 mark for every missed conversion.
> 2. **Ignoring the determinant check.** Examiners allocate 1 dedicated mark for verifying $AD - BC = 1$. If you skip this, you lose a free mark.
> 3. **Sign error in $YZ$** when $Y$ is purely imaginary and $Z$ is complex. The real part of $YZ$ becomes **negative**, which surprises many students. Practice this repeatedly.
> 4. **Long-line questions: Using degrees instead of radians** for $\cos$ and $\sin$ of the imaginary part. Always convert $\beta \ell$ to radians.
> 5. **Misinterpreting** $I_R$ as the line current rather than the phase current in 3-phase problems.
> 6. **Forgetting to state the units** of $B$ (ohms) and $C$ (siemens) in the answer.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ABCD Constants**

- ✅ **Two-port representation** is the foundation: $V_S = A V_R + B I_R$ and $I_S = C V_R + D I_R$.
- ✅ **Determinant condition:** $AD - BC = 1$ — always verify.
- ✅ **Symmetric line:** $A = D$ holds.
- ✅ **Short line:** $A = D = 1$, $B = Z$, $C = 0$.
- ✅ **Nominal T model:** $A = D = 1 + YZ/2$, $B = Z(1 + YZ/4)$, $C = Y$ — easier to compute $C$.
- ✅ **Nominal π model:** $A = D = 1 + YZ/2$, $B = Z$, $C = Y(1 + YZ/4)$ — easier to compute $B$.
- ✅ **Long-line model:** Use hyperbolic functions: $A = D = \cosh(\gamma \ell)$, $B = Z_c \sinh(\gamma \ell)$, $C = \sinh(\gamma \ell)/Z_c$.
- ✅ **Propagation constant:** $\gamma = \sqrt{zy}$ where $z, y$ are per-km values.
- ✅ **Characteristic impedance:** $Z_c = \sqrt{z/y}$.
- ✅ **Hyperbolic identities:** $\cosh^2\theta - \sinh^2\theta = 1$ (this is what makes the determinant check work).
- ✅ **Voltage regulation** uses the no-load receiving-end voltage: $VR\% = (V_{R,NL} - V_{R,FL})/V_{R,FL} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Efficiency** uses active powers: $\eta = P_R/P_S \times 100$.
- ✅ **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL):** $SIL = V_L^2/Z_c$ — the natural loading of a long line.
- ✅ **Ferranti Effect** (open-circuit receiving-end voltage rises): a direct consequence of $C$ being non-zero and capacitive charging current.
- ✅ Always convert **line-to-line** quantities to **per phase** before applying ABCD analysis.
- ✅ In the long-line model, the **real part of $\gamma$** is the attenuation constant $\alpha$ (nepers/km); the **imaginary part** is the phase constant $\beta$ (radians/km).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Flow Analysis: Gauss-Seidel Method, Newton-Raphson Method, and Fast Decoupled Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load Flow Analysis: Gauss-Seidel Method, Newton-Raphson Method, and Fast Decoupled Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Load Flow Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Load Flow Analysis?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Flow Analysis (LFA)**, also called **Power Flow Analysis**, is the numerical steady-state analysis of an electric power system. It determines the voltage magnitude $\vert V_i \vert$, voltage phase angle $\delta_i$, real power $P_i$, and reactive power $Q_i$ at every bus in the system under balanced three-phase steady-state operating conditions.

The fundamental objective of LFA is to solve the following **nonlinear algebraic power balance equations** at every bus $i$ of the network:

$$P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cos(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

$$Q_i = -\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

where $\theta_{ij}$ is the angle of the $Y_{ij}$ element, $\delta_i$ and $\delta_j$ are voltage angles at buses $i$ and $j$, and $n$ is the total number of buses.

### 1.2 Bus Classification in Power Flow

> [!NOTE]
> Each bus in the power system has **four quantities** — $\vert V_i \vert$, $\delta_i$, $P_i$, $Q_i$. Two are specified and two are to be computed. This gives rise to the three bus types.

| Bus Type | Specified (Known) | Unknown (To Be Computed) | Symbol |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------------|--------|
| **Slack / Swing / Reference Bus** | $\vert V \vert$ and $\delta$ | $P$ and $Q$ | Bus 1 |
| **PV Bus (Generator Bus)** | $P$ and $\vert V \vert$ | $Q$ and $\delta$ | Bus 2, 4, ... |
| **PQ Bus (Load Bus)** | $P$ and $Q$ | $\vert V \vert$ and $\delta$ | Bus 3, 5, ... |

The **Slack Bus** is mandatory because the system's total losses are unknown before the solution; it absorbs the $P-Q$ mismatch.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Plumbing System

> [!TIP]
> Imagine a complex network of water pipes connecting a few pumps (generators) and several taps (loads). Before opening the taps, an engineer must calculate the **pressure (voltage magnitude)** and **flow rate (power)** at every junction.
>
> - **Gauss-Seidel** is like solving pipe equations by **trial and adjustment**: guess a pressure, measure the error, then correct the guess using the measured value.
> - **Newton-Raphson** is like a **GPS recalculation**: based on the current error and its rate of change, it plots the slope and jumps to the best estimate instantly.
> - **Fast Decoupled** is the **simplified GPS** that uses only the road's main direction, ignoring side slopes — much faster, accurate enough for flat terrain.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **System Frequency:** $\mathbf{50\ Hz}$ (India) or $60\ Hz$ (US)
- **Base Power:** $\mathbf{100\ MVA}$ (standard convention)
- **Convergence Tolerance:** $\mathbf{10^{-5}\ p.u.}$ (industry standard) or $10^{-4}$
- **Maximum Iterations:** $\mathbf{50}$ for Gauss-Seidel, $\mathbf{10\text{--}15}$ for Newton-Raphson
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Power Flow Problem — General Formulation

The nodal equations of a power system in admittance form are:

$$I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

The complex power injected at bus $i$ is:

$$S_i = P_i + jQ_i = V_i I_i^{*} = V_i \left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j\right)^{*}$$

Separating real and imaginary parts yields the **power flow equations** (called the **P-δ and Q-V equations**):

$$P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cos(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

$$Q_i = -\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

## 2.2 Method 1 — Gauss-Seidel (GS) Method

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle
The GS method is an **iterative technique** that solves the nonlinear power flow equations by successive substitution. It uses the diagonal elements of the Y-bus matrix to update one variable at a time using the most recent values.

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Formulation

For a **PQ bus** $i$, the voltage update equation is:

$$V_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i - jQ_i}{(V_i^{(k)})^{*}} - \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> For PQ buses, $\vert V_i \vert$ is updated separately using the **Q-limit check** to ensure reactive power limits are not violated.

For a **PV bus** $i$, the voltage magnitude is fixed; only the angle $\delta_i$ is updated. The reactive power is computed as:

$$Q_i^{(k)} = -\text{Im}\left\{ V_i^{*(k)} \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right\}$$

### 2.2.3 Acceleration Factor
To speed up convergence, an **acceleration factor** $\alpha$ is applied to the voltage correction:

$$V_i^{(k+1)_{\text{acc}}} = V_i^{(k)} + \alpha \left( V_i^{(k+1)} - V_i^{(k)} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Optimal values: $\alpha = \mathbf{1.6}$ for voltage magnitude, $\alpha = \mathbf{1.0}$ for angle. Higher $\alpha$ may cause divergence.

### 2.2.4 Convergence Criterion
The iteration stops when the maximum voltage mismatch is less than a specified tolerance $\epsilon$:

$$\max_i \left\vert V_i^{(k+1)} - V_i^{(k)} \right\vert \leq \epsilon$$

### 2.2.5 Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** Form the $\mathbf{Y_{bus}}$ matrix using the network data.
- **Step 2:** Set initial voltages: $V_i^{(0)} = 1.0 + j0.0$ p.u. for all PQ buses; $V_i = \vert V_i \vert \angle \delta_i$ for PV buses (using flat start).
- **Step 3:** Compute the scheduled $P_i^{sch}$ and $Q_i^{sch}$ at each bus.
- **Step 4:** Update $V_i$ for each PQ bus using the GS equation.
- **Step 5:** Compute $Q_i$ for each PV bus and verify Q-limits.
- **Step 6:** Check convergence; if not converged, go to Step 4.

---

## 2.3 Method 2 — Newton-Raphson (NR) Method

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
The NR method uses **Taylor series expansion** truncated to first order, forming the **Jacobian matrix** $J$. It achieves **quadratic convergence** — the error squares with every iteration, giving rapid convergence in 3–5 iterations.

### 2.3.2 Mathematical Formulation

Define the power mismatches:

$$\Delta P_i = P_i^{sch} - P_i^{calc} = P_i^{sch} - \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cos(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

$$\Delta Q_i = Q_i^{sch} - Q_i^{calc} = Q_i^{sch} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

The **Jacobian matrix** relates the mismatches to the corrections:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} J_1 & J_2 \\ J_3 & J_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert / \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.3.3 Jacobian Sub-Matrices

$$
J_1 = \frac{\partial P}{\partial \delta}, \quad
J_2 = \frac{\partial P}{\partial \vert V \vert} \vert V \vert, \quad
J_3 = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial \delta}, \quad
J_4 = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial \vert V \vert} \vert V \vert
$$

The diagonal elements are:

$$J_{1,ii} = \frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \delta_i} = -Q_i - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

$$J_{4,ii} = \frac{\partial Q_i}{\partial \vert V_i \vert} \vert V_i \vert = Q_i - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

### 2.3.4 Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** Form $\mathbf{Y_{bus}}$ and set initial estimates $V_i^{(0)}, \delta_i^{(0)}$.
- **Step 2:** Calculate $P_i^{calc}$ and $Q_i^{calc}$ at all buses.
- **Step 3:** Compute mismatches $\Delta P_i$ and $\Delta Q_i$ for all non-slack buses.
- **Step 4:** Form the **Jacobian matrix** $J$ at the current operating point.
- **Step 5:** Solve $\mathbf{J} \cdot \mathbf{\Delta x} = \mathbf{\Delta S}$ for the correction vector $\mathbf{\Delta x} = [\Delta \delta, \Delta \vert V \vert / \vert V \vert]^T$.
- **Step 6:** Update the state variables and check convergence.

---

## 2.4 Method 3 — Fast Decoupled Load Flow (FDLF)

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle
The **Stott-Alsac Fast Decoupled Load Flow (FDLF)** method simplifies the NR method using two physical approximations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approximation 1:** $\cos(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j) \approx 1$ and $\sin(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j) \approx \theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j$ (valid when $\delta_i - \delta_j$ is small).
>
> **Approximation 2:** $\vert V_i \vert \approx 1.0$ p.u. (near-nominal voltage).

These decouple the $P\text{-}\delta$ and $Q\text{-}\vert V \vert$ sub-problems, and the Jacobian reduces to **constant matrices** $B'$ and $B''$.

### 2.4.2 Decoupled Equations

The FDLF algorithm uses two decoupled linear systems:

$$\Delta P / \vert V \vert = B' \cdot \Delta \delta$$

$$\Delta Q / \vert V \vert = B'' \cdot \Delta \vert V \vert$$

where $B'$ is the imaginary part of $Y_{bus}$ (with certain modifications) and $B''$ is the imaginary part of $Y_{bus}$ excluding the column/row of the slack bus and shunts.

### 2.4.3 Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** Form $B'$ and $B''$ matrices (constant).
- **Step 2:** Initialize $V_i^{(0)}, \delta_i^{(0)}$.
- **Step 3:** Compute $\Delta P_i$ and $\Delta Q_i$ mismatches.
- **Step 4:** Solve $\Delta \delta = B'^{-1} (\Delta P / \vert V \vert)$.
- **Step 5:** Update angles; recompute $\Delta Q$.
- **Step 6:** Solve $\Delta \vert V \vert = B''^{-1} (\Delta Q / \vert V \vert)$.
- **Step 7:** Update voltages; check convergence.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formula Compilation** — These are the equations you must memorize for the lab exam and viva.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|------------------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Nodal Current Equation | $I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j$ | KCL at bus $i$ |
| 2 | Complex Power Injection | $S_i = V_i I_i^{*}$ | $S_i = P_i + jQ_i$ |
| 3 | Real Power Equation | $P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cos(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$ | Nonlinear |
| 4 | Reactive Power Equation | $Q_i = -\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$ | Nonlinear |
| 5 | GS Voltage Update | $V_i^{(k+1)} = \dfrac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \dfrac{P_i - jQ_i}{(V_i^{(k)})^{*}} - \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right]$ | PQ bus |
| 6 | Acceleration Factor | $V_i^{acc} = V_i^{old} + \alpha (V_i^{new} - V_i^{old})$ | $\alpha \approx 1.6$ |
| 7 | NR Mismatch Vector | $\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix} = J \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert / \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$ | Jacobian form |
| 8 | Jacobian Diagonal $J_1$ | $-Q_i - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$ | $J_{1,ii}$ |
| 9 | Jacobian Diagonal $J_4$ | $Q_i - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$ | $J_{4,ii}$ |
| 10 | FDLF P-δ Decoupling | $\Delta P / \vert V \vert = B' \cdot \Delta \delta$ | Constant $B'$ |
| 11 | FDLF Q-V Decoupling | $\Delta Q / \vert V \vert = B'' \cdot \Delta \vert V \vert$ | Constant $B''$ |
| 12 | Convergence Tolerance | $\max \vert \Delta V \vert \leq \epsilon = 10^{-5}$ p.u. | Standard |

---

## 2.6 Comparison of the Three Methods

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently asked KTU exam question** — be prepared.

| Feature | Gauss-Seidel | Newton-Raphson | Fast Decoupled |
|---------|--------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Convergence** | Linear | Quadratic | Quasi-quadratic |
| **Iterations Required** | 20–100 | 3–5 | 5–20 |
| **Computation per Iteration** | Low | High | Lowest |
| **Memory Required** | Low | High (Jacobian) | Lowest |
| **Convergence Reliability** | Poor (diverges for ill-conditioned systems) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Best Suited For** | Small systems, educational use | Large systems, all sizes | Real-time / on-line applications |
| **Acceleration** | Yes ($\alpha = 1.6$) | Not needed | Not needed |
| **Time per Iteration** | Linear with $n$ | $n^2$ to $n^3$ | Linear with $n$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Planning Studies:** Voltage profile and reactive power planning for transmission expansion.
- **Operational Studies:** Real-time security assessment, contingency analysis.
- **Economic Dispatch:** Optimal power flow for minimum cost generation.
- **Stability Studies:** Initial operating point for transient stability simulations.
- **Industry Tools:** **MATPOWER** (MATLAB), **PSAT**, **PSS®E**, **DIgSILENT PowerFactory** all use NR/FDLF as the core solver.
