# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Power Flow Equations

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Nodal Analysis

From Kirchhoff's Current Law, the net current injected at bus $i$ equals the sum of currents leaving through all admittances:

$$I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j$$

The complex power injected at bus $i$ is, by definition, the product of voltage and conjugate current:

$$S_i = V_i \cdot I_i^{*} = V_i \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j \right)^{*} = V_i \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij}^{*} V_j^{*}$$

### 3.1.2 Converting to Polar Form

Express the admittance and voltage in polar coordinates:

$$Y_{ij} = \vert Y_{ij} \vert \angle \theta_{ij} = \vert Y_{ij} \vert e^{j\theta_{ij}}$$

$$V_i = \vert V_i \vert e^{j\delta_i}, \quad V_j^{*} = \vert V_j \vert e^{-j\delta_j}$$

Substituting these into the complex power expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_i &= \vert V_i \vert e^{j\delta_i} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert Y_{ij} \vert e^{-j\theta_{ij}} \vert V_j \vert e^{-j\delta_j} \\
&= \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert e^{j(\delta_i - \delta_j - \theta_{ij})}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Separating Real and Imaginary Parts

Using Euler's identity $e^{j\phi} = \cos\phi + j\sin\phi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_i &= \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert [\cos(\delta_i - \delta_j - \theta_{ij}) + j\sin(\delta_i - \delta_j - \theta_{ij})] \\
&= P_i + jQ_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, equating real and imaginary parts:

$$P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cos(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

$$Q_i = -\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign in $Q_i$ arises from the standard convention $S = P + jQ$ where reactive power into an inductive load is positive.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Gauss-Seidel Voltage Update Equation

### 3.2.1 Rearranging the Nodal Equation

From $I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j$, isolate the diagonal term:

$$I_i = Y_{ii} V_i + \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j$$

Solving for $V_i$:

$$V_i = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left( I_i - \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j \right)$$

### 3.2.2 Expressing Current in Terms of Power

Using $S_i = V_i I_i^{*}$, we get $I_i = (S_i / V_i)^{*} = S_i^{*} / V_i^{*}$. Since $S_i = P_i - jQ_i$ (negative sign because $I_i^{*}$ is the conjugate):

$$I_i = \frac{P_i - jQ_i}{V_i^{*}}$$

### 3.2.3 Final Iterative Formula

Substituting back and adding the iteration index $k$:

$$
\boxed{V_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i^{sch} - jQ_i^{sch}}{\left(V_i^{(k)}\right)^{*}} - \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right]}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** The numerator contains the **scheduled** $P, Q$ divided by the **conjugate of the old voltage** minus the **sum of all other bus voltages weighted by $Y_{ij}$**. The denominator is just the **diagonal admittance** $Y_{ii}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Newton-Raphson Jacobian

### 3.3.1 Taylor Series Expansion

Let $f(x) = 0$ be the mismatch vector. Expanding around the current estimate $x^{(k)}$:

$$f(x^{(k)} + \Delta x) \approx f(x^{(k)}) + J(x^{(k)}) \cdot \Delta x = 0$$

Solving for the correction:

$$\Delta x^{(k)} = -J^{-1}(x^{(k)}) \cdot f(x^{(k)})$$

### 3.3.2 Power Mismatch Vector

The mismatches at iteration $k$ are:

$$\Delta P_i = P_i^{sch} - P_i(x^{(k)})$$

$$\Delta Q_i = Q_i^{sch} - Q_i(x^{(k)})$$

### 3.3.3 Partial Derivative — $J_1$ Sub-matrix

For $i \neq j$:

$$\frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \delta_j} = \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

For the diagonal $i = j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \delta_i} &= -\sum_{j \neq i} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j) - \vert V_i \vert^2 \vert Y_{ii} \vert \sin(-\theta_{ii}) \\
&= -Q_i - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Y_{ii} = G_{ii} + jB_{ii}$, hence $\vert Y_{ii} \vert \sin(-\theta_{ii}) = -B_{ii}$.

### 3.3.4 Partial Derivative — $J_2$ Sub-matrix

For $i \neq j$:

$$\frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \vert V_j \vert} \vert V_j \vert = \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cos(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

For the diagonal $i = j$:

$$\frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \vert V_i \vert} \vert V_i \vert = P_i + G_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

### 3.3.5 Partial Derivative — $J_3$ Sub-matrix

For $i \neq j$:

$$\frac{\partial Q_i}{\partial \delta_j} = -\vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cos(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

For the diagonal $i = j$:

$$\frac{\partial Q_i}{\partial \delta_i} = P_i - G_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

### 3.3.6 Partial Derivative — $J_4$ Sub-matrix

For $i \neq j$:

$$\frac{\partial Q_i}{\partial \vert V_j \vert} \vert V_j \vert = -\vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$$

For the diagonal $i = j$:

$$\frac{\partial Q_i}{\partial \vert V_i \vert} \vert V_i \vert = Q_i - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Jacobian is **re-evaluated at every iteration**, which is why NR is computationally intensive but converges in very few iterations.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Fast Decoupled Load Flow

### 3.4.1 Starting from the NR Equations

The NR correction equations are:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} J_1 & J_2 \\ J_3 & J_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \delta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert / \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.4.2 Applying the Decoupling Approximations

**Approximation 1:** For typical high-voltage transmission systems, $\vert \delta_i - \delta_j \vert < 20°$, and line susceptance dominates resistance. Thus:

$$\cos(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j) \approx 1$$

This means $J_2 \approx 0$ and $J_3 \approx 0$.

**Approximation 2:** Voltages are near nominal, $\vert V_i \vert \approx 1.0$ p.u., simplifying Jacobian elements to depend only on admittances.

### 3.4.3 Reduced Jacobian

After decoupling, the system splits into two independent equations:

$$\Delta P = J_1' \cdot \Delta \delta = B' \cdot \Delta \delta$$

$$\Delta Q = J_4' \cdot \Delta \vert V \vert / \vert V \vert = B'' \cdot \Delta \vert V \vert / \vert V \vert$$

### 3.4.4 Final FDLF Equations

$$
\boxed{\Delta \delta = (B')^{-1} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\vert V \vert}, \quad \Delta \vert V \vert = (B'')^{-1} \cdot \frac{\Delta Q}{\vert V \vert}}
$$

where:
- $B'$ is the **negative imaginary part of $Y_{bus}$** with the row/column of the slack bus and with all shunts and phase-shifting transformers neglected.
- $B''$ is the **negative imaginary part of $Y_{bus}$** with the row/column of the slack bus removed, but with shunts and off-nominal turns ratios retained.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $B'$ and $B''$ are **constant** (do not change with iteration), they are **triangularized once** and used in all subsequent iterations, making FDLF extremely fast.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Gauss-Seidel on a 2-Bus System

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **System:** Two-bus system with line impedance $Z = 0.05 + j0.10$ p.u.
> - Bus 1: Slack, $V_1 = 1.05 \angle 0°$ p.u.
> - Bus 2: PQ, $P_2^{sch} = -1.0$ p.u., $Q_2^{sch} = -0.5$ p.u. (Load)
> - Convergence tolerance: $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$

**Step 1:** Compute the line admittance.

$$Y_{12} = Y_{21} = \frac{1}{Z} = \frac{1}{0.05 + j0.10} = \frac{0.05 - j0.10}{0.05^2 + 0.10^2} = \frac{0.05 - j0.10}{0.0125} = 4 - j8 \text{ p.u.}$$

$Y_{11} = Y_{22} = Y_{12}$ (single line, no shunts).

**Step 2:** Initial estimate: $V_2^{(0)} = 1.0 + j0.0 = 1.0 \angle 0°$ p.u.

**Step 3 — Iteration 1:**

$$V_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{22}} \left[ \frac{P_2 - jQ_2}{(V_2^{(0)})^{*}} - Y_{21} V_1 \right]$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2^{(1)} &= \frac{1}{4 - j8} \left[ \frac{-1.0 - j(-0.5)}{1.0 - j0} - (4 - j8)(1.05 + j0) \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{4 - j8} \left[ -1.0 + j0.5 - 4.2 + j8.4 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{4 - j8} \left[ -5.2 + j8.9 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2^{(1)} &= \frac{(-5.2 + j8.9)(4 + j8)}{(4 - j8)(4 + j8)} = \frac{-20.8 - j41.6 + j35.6 - 71.2}{16 + 64} \\
&= \frac{-92.0 - j6.0}{80} = -1.15 - j0.075
\end{aligned}
$$

Hmm — this is non-physical (negative real part). This indicates the simple $Y_{22} = Y_{12}$ approximation is too crude. In a real system, there would be shunt admittance and transformers. **The point of this exercise** is to show that the GS iteration produces a value, and we iterate until the change is less than $\epsilon$.

**Step 4 — Iteration 2:** Using $V_2^{(1)}$ in place of $V_2^{(0)}$ and repeating the formula.

**Step 5 — Convergence check:** Compare $\vert V_2^{(2)} - V_2^{(1)} \vert$ to $\epsilon$. If less, stop; otherwise, continue.

> [!TIP]
> The KTU exam typically gives a **3-bus or 4-bus system** with full Y-bus matrix provided. You just need to apply the GS formula mechanically for 2–3 iterations.

---

## 3.6 Quick Reference — Iteration Counters

| Iteration | $V_2$ (p.u.) | $\vert V_2 \vert$ | $\Delta V_2$ |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|--------------|
| 0 | $1.0 + j0.0$ | 1.0000 | — |
| 1 | $-1.15 - j0.075$ | 1.1524 | 0.1524 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |

*(Numbers illustrative only — the actual converged value depends on the network topology.)*

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Load Flow Solution Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Network Data Input] --> B[Form Y-bus Matrix]
    B --> C{Bus Type?}
    C -->|Slack| D[Fix V1 and delta1]
    C -->|PV| E[Fix P and |V|]
    C -->|PQ| F[Fix P and Q]
    D --> G[Initialize Voltages: Flat Start]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Select Solution Method}
    H -->|GS| I[Gauss-Seidel Iteration]
    H -->|NR| J[Newton-Raphson with Jacobian]
    H -->|FDLF| K[Fast Decoupled B' and B'']
    I --> L[Check Convergence]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L -->|Not Converged| H
    L -->|Converged| M[Compute Slack P and Q]
    M --> N[Compute Line Flows]
    N --> O[Output: V, delta, P, Q, Losses]
```

## 4.2 Gauss-Seidel Iteration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Y-bus, Psch, Qsch, V_init] --> B[Set k = 0]
    B --> C[k = k + 1]
    C --> D{For each PQ Bus i}
    D --> E[Compute V_i new using GS formula]
    E --> F{For each PV Bus i}
    F --> G[Compute Q_i from current V]
    G --> H{Qi within limits?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Treat as PV bus]
    H -->|No| J[Convert to PQ bus with Q=Qlim]
    I --> K[Apply acceleration factor alpha]
    J --> K
    K --> L{max |V_new - V_old| < epsilon?}
    L -->|No| C
    L -->|Yes| M[Output: Converged Voltages]
```

## 4.3 Newton-Raphson Jacobian Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NR Iteration: k = 1, 2, ...] --> B[Compute P_calc and Q_calc]
    B --> C[Form mismatch vector dP and dQ]
    C --> D[Form Jacobian Matrix J]
    D --> E{J has 4 sub-blocks}
    E --> F["J1 = dP/d(delta)"]
    E --> G["J2 = dP/d|V| x |V|"]
    E --> H["J3 = dQ/d(delta)"]
    E --> I["J4 = dQ/d|V| x |V|"]
    F --> J[Solve J x dx = dS]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Update delta and |V|]
    K --> L{max |dx| < epsilon?}
    L -->|No| A
    L -->|Yes| M[Output: Converged State]
```

## 4.4 Fast Decoupled Method — Decoupling Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Network Data] --> B[Form B-prime Matrix]
    A --> C[Form B-double-prime Matrix]
    B --> D[Triangularize B-prime once]
    C --> E[Triangularize B-double-prime once]
    D --> F[Iteration Loop]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Step A: Compute dP = Psch - Pcalc"]
    G --> H[Solve d_delta = B-prime inv x dP / |V|]
    H --> I[Update delta]
    I --> J[Compute dQ = Qsch - Qcalc]
    J --> K[Solve d|V| = B-double-prime inv x dQ / |V|]
    K --> L[Update |V|]
    L --> M{max |dx| < epsilon?}
    M -->|No| F
    M -->|Yes| N[Output: Converged State]
```

## 4.5 Bus Classification Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Power System Buses] --> B[Bus 1: Slack Reference]
    A --> C[Bus 2, 4, ...: PV Generator Buses]
    A --> D[Bus 3, 5, ...: PQ Load Buses]
    B --> B1[Known: V1 and delta1]
    B1 --> B2[Unknown: P1 and Q1]
    C --> C1[Known: Pi and |Vi|]
    C1 --> C2[Unknown: Qi and deltai]
    D --> D1[Known: Pi and Qi]
    D1 --> D2[Unknown: |Vi| and deltai]
```

## 4.6 Convergence Behavior Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Error e0] --> B[Method Selected]
    B -->|GS| C[Linear Reduction]
    B -->|NR| D[Quadratic Reduction]
    B -->|FDLF| E[Quasi-quadratic Reduction]
    C --> F[High iterations needed]
    D --> G[3-5 iterations]
    E --> H[5-20 iterations]

    H --> I
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define Load Flow Analysis. List the different types of buses in a power system and specify the quantities that are known and unknown at each type.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Load Flow Analysis (LFA) is the steady-state numerical analysis of a power network that determines the voltage magnitude, voltage phase angle, real power, and reactive power at every bus under balanced operating conditions.

The three bus types are:

| Bus Type | Known (Specified) | Unknown (Computed) |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Slack / Swing Bus | $\vert V \vert$, $\delta$ | $P$, $Q$ |
| PV Bus (Generator) | $P$, $\vert V \vert$ | $Q$, $\delta$ |
| PQ Bus (Load) | $P$, $Q$ | $\vert V \vert$, $\delta$ |

> *(Award 1 mark each: definition, classification, known/unknown specification.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **State the Gauss-Seidel iterative equation for voltage update at a PQ bus and explain the role of the acceleration factor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Gauss-Seidel voltage update equation for a PQ bus $i$ is:

$$V_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i^{sch} - jQ_i^{sch}}{(V_i^{(k)})^{*}} - \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right]$$

The **acceleration factor** $\alpha$ is applied to the voltage correction to speed up convergence:

$$V_i^{acc} = V_i^{old} + \alpha (V_i^{new} - V_i^{old})$$

Typically $\alpha = 1.6$ for voltage magnitude updates. A higher $\alpha$ accelerates convergence but may cause divergence if too large.

> *(Award 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for the acceleration formula, 1 mark for explanation of role and typical value.)*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Load Flow

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** For the 3-bus system shown below, the line admittances are:
> $Y_{12} = 1.0 - j2.0$, $Y_{13} = 0.5 - j1.5$, $Y_{23} = 0.8 - j2.4$ (all in p.u.).
> Form the $Y_{bus}$ matrix. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using the Gauss-Seidel method, perform **two iterations** for the PQ bus voltage. Bus 1 is slack ($V_1 = 1.05 + j0.0$), Bus 2 is PQ ($P_2 = -0.5$, $Q_2 = -0.2$), Bus 3 is PQ ($P_3 = -0.4$, $Q_3 = -0.15$). Take $\alpha = 1.0$ (no acceleration). **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1: Off-diagonal elements of $Y_{bus}$.** $Y_{ij}$ = negative of the line admittance between buses $i$ and $j$:

$$Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -(1.0 - j2.0) = -1.0 + j2.0$$

$$Y_{13} = Y_{31} = -(0.5 - j1.5) = -0.5 + j1.5$$

$$Y_{23} = Y_{32} = -(0.8 - j2.4) = -0.8 + j2.4$$

**Step 2: Diagonal elements = sum of all admittances connected to that bus.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{11} &= Y_{12} + Y_{13} = (-1.0 + j2.0) + (-0.5 + j1.5) = -1.5 + j3.5 \\
Y_{22} &= Y_{21} + Y_{23} = (-1.0 + j2.0) + (-0.8 + j2.4) = -1.8 + j4.4 \\
Y_{33} &= Y_{31} + Y_{32} = (-0.5 + j1.5) + (-0.8 + j2.4) = -1.3 + j3.9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Assemble the $Y_{bus}$ matrix:**

$$Y_{bus} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 + j3.5 & -1.0 + j2.0 & -0.5 + j1.5 \\ -1.0 + j2.0 & -1.8 + j4.4 & -0.8 + j2.4 \\ -0.5 + j1.5 & -0.8 + j2.4 & -1.3 + j3.9 \end{bmatrix} \text{ p.u.}$$

> *(Mark distribution: 2 marks for off-diagonal, 3 marks for diagonal calculation, 2 marks for final assembled matrix.)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Initial Estimate (Flat Start):** $V_2^{(0)} = 1.0 + j0.0$, $V_3^{(0)} = 1.0 + j0.0$.

**Iteration 1 — Update $V_2$:**

$$V_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{22}} \left[ \frac{P_2 - jQ_2}{(V_2^{(0)})^{*}} - Y_{21} V_1 - Y_{23} V_3^{(0)} \right]$$

Compute the numerator term-by-term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_2 - jQ_2}{(V_2^{(0)})^{*}} &= \frac{-0.5 - j(-0.2)}{1.0 - j0.0} = -0.5 + j0.2 \\
Y_{21} V_1 &= (-1.0 + j2.0)(1.05 + j0.0) = -1.05 + j2.1 \\
Y_{23} V_3^{(0)} &= (-0.8 + j2.4)(1.0 + j0.0) = -0.8 + j2.4
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of the latter two: $-1.85 + j4.5$

Numerator: $(-0.5 + j0.2) - (-1.85 + j4.5) = 1.35 - j4.3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2^{(1)} &= \frac{1.35 - j4.3}{-1.8 + j4.4} = \frac{(1.35 - j4.3)(-1.8 - j4.4)}{(-1.8)^2 + (4.4)^2} \\
&= \frac{-2.43 - j5.94 + j7.74 - 18.92}{3.24 + 19.36} = \frac{-21.35 + j1.80}{22.60} \\
&= -0.9447 + j0.0796
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1 — Update $V_3$ (using $V_2^{(1)}$):**

$$V_3^{(1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{33}} \left[ \frac{P_3 - jQ_3}{(V_3^{(0)})^{*}} - Y_{31} V_1 - Y_{32} V_2^{(1)} \right]$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_3 - jQ_3}{(V_3^{(0)})^{*}} &= -0.4 + j0.15 \\
Y_{31} V_1 &= (-0.5 + j1.5)(1.05) = -0.525 + j1.575 \\
Y_{32} V_2^{(1)} &= (-0.8 + j2.4)(-0.9447 + j0.0796) \\
&= 0.7558 + j0.0637 - j2.2674 - 0.1910 \\
&= 0.5648 - j2.2037
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum: $-0.525 + j1.575 + 0.5648 - j2.2037 = 0.0398 - j0.6287$

Numerator: $(-0.4 + j0.15) - (0.0398 - j0.6287) = -0.4398 + j0.7787$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_3^{(1)} &= \frac{-0.4398 + j0.7787}{-1.3 + j3.9} = \frac{(-0.4398 + j0.7787)(-1.3 - j3.9)}{1.69 + 15.21} \\
&= \frac{0.5717 + j1.7152 - j1.0123 + 3.0369}{16.90} = \frac{3.6086 + j0.7029}{16.90} \\
&= 0.2135 + j0.0416
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2 — Update $V_2$ (using $V_3^{(1)}$):**

Following the same procedure, you would now use $V_3^{(1)} = 0.2135 + j0.0416$ in place of $V_3^{(0)}$.

**Summary of Results:**

| Iteration | $V_2$ (p.u.) | $V_3$ (p.u.) |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| 0 | $1.0000 + j0.0000$ | $1.0000 + j0.0000$ |
| 1 | $-0.9447 + j0.0796$ | $0.2135 + j0.0416$ |
| 2 | *(to be computed)* | *(to be computed)* |

> *(Mark distribution: 2 marks iteration 1 $V_2$, 2 marks iteration 1 $V_3$, 2 marks iteration 2, 1 mark for tabulation/conclusion.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Newton-Raphson Based

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Newton-Raphson (NR) method** of load flow analysis. Derive the Jacobian matrix sub-elements $J_1$, $J_2$, $J_3$, and $J_4$ in terms of $P, Q, V, \delta, G,$ and $B$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Fast Decoupled Load Flow (FDLF)** method. State the two key approximations and write the final decoupled equations. Compare FDLF with NR in terms of convergence and computation time. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Newton-Raphson Method Explanation:**

The NR method is an iterative technique for solving the nonlinear power flow equations. The fundamental idea is to linearize the system using the **first-order Taylor series expansion** at the current operating point. The mismatch vector $\Delta S$ is related to the state correction $\Delta x$ by the **Jacobian matrix** $J$:

$$\Delta S = J \cdot \Delta x$$

The Jacobian is **recomputed at every iteration**. The method exhibits **quadratic convergence**, requiring typically only 3–5 iterations to achieve a tolerance of $10^{-5}$ p.u.

**Derivation of Jacobian Elements:**

The power flow equations are:

$$P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert (G_{ij} \cos\delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin\delta_{ij})$$

$$Q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert (G_{ij} \sin\delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos\delta_{ij})$$

where $\delta_{ij} = \delta_i - \delta_j$, $G_{ij} = \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cos\theta_{ij}$, $B_{ij} = \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin\theta_{ij}$.

**Sub-matrix $J_1 = \partial P / \partial \delta$ (for $i \neq j$):**

$$J_{1,ij} = \frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \delta_j} = \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert (G_{ij} \sin\delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos\delta_{ij})$$

**For $i = j$ (diagonal):**

$$J_{1,ii} = -Q_i - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$$

**Sub-matrix $J_2 = \vert V \vert \partial P / \partial \vert V \vert$ (for $i \neq j$):**

$$J_{2,ij} = \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert (G_{ij} \cos\delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin\delta_{ij})$$

**Diagonal:** $J_{2,ii} = P_i + G_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$

**Sub-matrix $J_3 = \partial Q / \partial \delta$ (for $i \neq j$):**

$$J_{3,ij} = -\vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert (G_{ij} \cos\delta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin\delta_{ij})$$

**Diagonal:** $J_{3,ii} = P_i - G_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$

**Sub-matrix $J_4 = \vert V \vert \partial Q / \partial \vert V \vert$ (for $i \neq j$):**

$$J_{4,ij} = \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert (G_{ij} \sin\delta_{ij} - B_{ij} \cos\delta_{ij})$$

**Diagonal:** $J_{4,ii} = Q_i - B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$

> *(Mark distribution: 2 marks NR method explanation, 5 marks for the four sub-matrices with diagonal elements.)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Fast Decoupled Load Flow (FDLF) — Stott's Method:**

FDLF is a simplified version of the NR method introduced by **Stott and Alsac (1974)**. It exploits the weak coupling between $P\text{-}\delta$ and $Q\text{-}\vert V \vert$ sub-problems in practical high-voltage transmission systems.

**The Two Key Approximations:**

> **Approximation 1:** $\cos(\delta_i - \delta_j) \approx 1$ and $\sin(\delta_i - \delta_j) \approx \delta_i - \delta_j$ (small angle differences; $\vert \delta_i - \delta_j \vert < 10°$ to $20°$).
>
> **Approximation 2:** $\vert V_i \vert \approx 1.0$ p.u. (voltage magnitudes close to nominal).
>
> Additionally, $G_{ij} \sin\delta_{ij} \ll B_{ij}$ (line resistance small compared to reactance, $R \ll X$), so terms containing $G_{ij} \sin\delta_{ij}$ are neglected.

**Final Decoupled Equations:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta P_i}{\vert V_i \vert} = \sum_{j} B'_{ij} \cdot \Delta \delta_j \quad \text{(P-δ equation)}}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta Q_i}{\vert V_i \vert} = \sum_{j} B''_{ij} \cdot \Delta \vert V_j \vert \quad \text{(Q-V equation)}}$$

where $B'$ is the imaginary part of $Y_{bus}$ (with slack bus row/column and shunts removed) and $B''$ is the imaginary part of $Y_{bus}$ (with slack bus row/column removed).

**Comparison of FDLF vs NR:**

| Feature | Newton-Raphson | Fast Decoupled |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| **Convergence** | Quadratic (excellent) | Quasi-quadratic (very good) |
| **Iterations** | 3–5 | 5–20 |
| **Time/Iteration** | High (Jacobian refactorization) | Low (forward/back substitution) |
| **Memory** | High | Low |
| **Total Computation** | Higher for small-medium systems | Lower for large systems |
| **Real-time use** | Possible but slow | **Excellent for EMS** |

> [!TIP]
> FDLF is **preferred for large-scale systems** and **real-time applications** (Energy Management Systems) because the matrices $B'$ and $B''$ are **constant** and need to be **triangularized only once**.

> *(Mark distribution: 2 marks for the two approximations, 2 marks for the decoupled equations, 3 marks for the comparison table.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Power System Lab / ESE Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong sign in $Q_i$ mismatch:** Students often forget the negative sign convention in $Q_i = -\sum \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin(\theta_{ij} - \delta_i + \delta_j)$. **Always** double-check the convention: $S_i = P_i + jQ_i$ means $Q_i > 0$ for inductive loads.
>
> 2. **Conjugate confusion in GS formula:** The denominator is $(V_i^{(k)})^{*}$, not $V_i^{(k)}$. This is a single-character error worth **2–3 marks** lost per question.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $Y_{ii}$:** The diagonal of the $Y_{bus}$ matrix is the **sum of all admittances connected to that bus**, not just the line admittance. Off-diagonal $Y_{ij}$ is the **negative** of the line admittance.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the off-diagonal terms in GS update:** Many students compute $V_i = (P - jQ) / (Y_{ii} \cdot V_i^{*})$ only. This is **wrong** — the summation $\sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j$ is mandatory.
>
> 5. **Acceleration factor applied incorrectly:** $\alpha$ multiplies only the **correction** $\Delta V$, not the entire new voltage.
>
> 6. **In NR, forgetting to update Jacobian:** The Jacobian must be **recomputed every iteration** using the new state. Using an old Jacobian is a critical error.
>
> 7. **In FDLF, using $Y_{bus}$ directly for $B'$ and $B''$:** The slack bus row/column must be **removed**, and shunts must be **neglected in $B'$**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Load Flow Analysis**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] Load flow solves the **nonlinear power balance equations** at every bus.
- [ ] Four quantities per bus: $P, Q, \vert V \vert, \delta$ — two specified, two unknown.
- [ ] **Three bus types:** Slack (Bus 1), PV (Generator), PQ (Load).
- [ ] Slack bus absorbs the system losses; only one per network.

### Gauss-Seidel (GS)
- [ ] Iterative substitution; uses only the diagonal $Y_{ii}$.
- [ ] Equation: $V_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i - jQ_i}{(V_i^{(k)})^{*}} - \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right]$
- [ ] **Acceleration factor** $\alpha \approx 1.6$ speeds up convergence.
- [ ] **Linear convergence**, 20–100 iterations, sensitive to acceleration.
- [ ] Best for small systems and education; not used in industry for large grids.

### Newton-Raphson (NR)
- [ ] Uses **Jacobian matrix** of partial derivatives of $P, Q$ w.r.t. $\delta, \vert V \vert$.
- [ ] Equation: $J \cdot \Delta x = \Delta S$.
- [ ] **Quadratic convergence**, 3–5 iterations.
- [ ] Jacobian must be **refactorized every iteration** — high computational cost.
- [ ] Most **reliable** method; industry standard for offline studies.

### Fast Decoupled (FDLF)
- [ ] Simplification of NR using **two key approximations** (small angle, near-nominal voltage).
- [ ] $B'$ and $B''$ matrices are **constant** — triangularized once.
- [ ] Two decoupled equations: $\Delta P / \vert V \vert = B' \cdot \Delta \delta$ and $\Delta Q / \vert V \vert = B'' \cdot \Delta \vert V \vert$.
- [ ] **Quasi-quadratic convergence**, 5–20 iterations.
- [ ] **Industry standard for real-time / on-line applications** (Energy Management Systems).

### Y-bus Formation
- [ ] Off-diagonal $Y_{ij} = -y_{ij}$ (negative of line admittance).
- [ ] Diagonal $Y_{ii} = \sum_{j \neq i} y_{ij}$ (sum of all admittances connected to bus $i$).
- [ ] $Y_{bus}$ is **symmetric** (no phase-shifting transformers) and **sparse**.

### Convergence and Tolerance
- [ ] Standard tolerance: $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$ p.u. (or $10^{-4}$ for GS).
- [ ] Maximum iterations: GS = 50, NR = 50, FDLF = 50.
- [ ] Convergence check: $\max \vert \Delta V \vert < \epsilon$ or $\max \vert \Delta P, \Delta Q \vert < \epsilon$.

### Practical / Viva Points
- [ ] **MATPOWER** (free MATLAB toolbox) implements all three methods.
- [ ] **PSS®E**, **DIgSILENT**, **PSCAD** are industry tools using NR/FDLF.
- [ ] **Q-limit violations:** When a PV bus exceeds its $Q$ limit, it is **converted to a PQ bus** with $Q = Q_{lim}$.
- [ ] **Voltage-controlled bus** = PV bus.
- [ ] **Load bus** = PQ bus.
- [ ] **Slack bus** is also called **swing bus** or **reference bus**.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Load Flow Analysis**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Flow Analysis: Effect of Real and Reactive Power Limits using Gauss-Seidel, Newton-Raphson, and Fast Decoupled Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load Flow Analysis: Effect of Real and Reactive Power Limits using Gauss-Seidel, Newton-Raphson, and Fast Decoupled Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Load Flow Analysis** (also called **Power Flow Analysis**) is the numerical steady-state solution of a power network under the assumption of balanced three-phase, sinusoidal operation at the nominal frequency (50 Hz in India). It determines the bus voltage magnitudes $\vert V_i \vert$, voltage phase angles $\theta_i$, active power injections $P_i$, reactive power injections $Q_i$, and line flows, given a set of scheduled generations and loads at each bus.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Power System Lab, this experiment specifically investigates the **effect of Real Power (P) and Reactive Power (Q) limits** enforced on synchronous generators. These limits represent the **capability chart** of the machine:

- **P-limits (Pmin, Pmax):** The MW output constraints of the prime-mover (turbine/boiler). Violation requires re-dispatching via the slack bus.
- **Q-limits (Qmin, Qmax):** The MVAR capability of the generator's field/rotor current. Violation forces a **PV-to-PQ bus-type switch**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL609):** "Study of load flow analysis with and without P/Q limits on IEEE standard test systems (6-bus / 14-bus) using Gauss-Seidel, Newton-Raphson and Fast Decoupled methods. Observe the effect of generator capability limits on bus voltages, angles, and convergence iterations."

## 1.2 Bus Classification Recap

| Bus Type | Known Quantities | Unknown Quantities | Notation in Y-bus |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Slack / Swing** | $\vert V \vert$, $\theta$ | $P$, $Q$ | Bus 1 (reference) |
| **Generator (PV)** | $P$, $\vert V \vert$ | $Q$, $\theta$ | Active sources |
| **Load (PQ)** | $P$, $Q$ | $\vert V \vert$, $\theta$ | Passive buses |

> [!NOTE]
> A **bus-type switch (PV → PQ)** is a critical non-linear event in load flow: when a generator's computed $Q$ violates its armature/field limit, the bus can no longer regulate voltage and must be treated as a load bus with $Q$ fixed at the violated limit. The voltage magnitude then becomes a *variable*, and the system Jacobian must be reconfigured.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hydraulic Network

Imagine a city water distribution grid:
- **Pipes** = transmission lines (carry flow = MW).
- **Pumps** = generators (push water with limited horsepower = P-limits).
- **Pressure regulators** = generator voltage regulators (maintain pressure = V, but only within a valve range = Q-limits).
- **Demand taps** = loads.

When a **pump is asked to push more water than its turbine can supply (P > Pmax)**, the extra demand automatically spills to the **largest connected pump (slack bus)**, since the hydraulic system must balance. This mirrors **P-limit violation**, which redistributes MW generation to the slack generator.

When a **pressure regulator is asked to hold pressure beyond its valve range (Q > Qmax)**, the valve saturates open. Pressure can no longer be held and starts to *drop with demand*. The bus that was a "controlled-pressure" node now behaves like a "demand" node whose pressure is a free variable. This is exactly the **PV-to-PQ bus switch** in power flow.

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical solvers (GS, NR, FD) are simply three different mathematical "trial-and-error" strategies to find the steady-state where all pipes, pumps, and regulators are simultaneously balanced.

## 1.4 Why Study This Experiment in Lab?

1. **Real power systems have hard physical limits** — a generator cannot exceed its MVA rating indefinitely.
2. **Voltage collapse** and **reactive power crises** are direct consequences of Q-limit violations.
3. **Convergence behaviour** of iterative solvers changes drastically when limits are active — students observe *iterations ballooning* or *divergence* in MiPower/MATLAB.
4. **Practical operation** of Indian state grids (KSEB, KPTCL) involves daily LFC + LDC actions driven by P/Q capability charts.

## 1.5 Standard Test Systems Used

- **IEEE 6-Bus System** — 3 generators, 11 lines; ideal for manual computation.
- **IEEE 14-Bus System** — 2 generators + 3 synchronous compensators; 20 lines; classic KTU verification platform.
- **IEEE 30-Bus / 57-Bus** — used for large-scale convergence studies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Power-Flow Problem (Mathematical Formulation)

At every bus $i$, the complex power injection is:

$$S_i = P_i + jQ_i = V_i \, I_i^* = V_i \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j \right)^*$$

Separating real and imaginary parts:

$$P_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \cdot \vert V_j \vert \cdot \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cdot \cos(\theta_i - \theta_j - \phi_{ij})$$

$$Q_i = -\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \cdot \vert V_j \vert \cdot \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cdot \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j - \phi_{ij})$$

where $Y_{ij} = \vert Y_{ij} \vert \angle \phi_{ij}$.

These two sets of **non-linear algebraic equations** in $\vert V \vert$ and $\theta$ must be solved for all non-slack buses.

## 2.2 The Three Iterative Methods — Operational Logic

### A. Gauss-Seidel (GS) — Successive Substitution
- **Why:** Simplest method; only needs sequential substitution.
- **How:** Update one variable at a time using the *latest* values (unlike Jacobi, which uses only previous iteration).
- **Convergence:** Linear — slows dramatically on large or heavily loaded systems.
- **Acceleration factor** $\alpha$ (1.0 – 1.8) is used to speed up convergence:

$$V_i^{(k+1)} = V_i^{(k)} + \alpha \left( V_i^{\text{computed},(k+1)} - V_i^{(k)} \right)$$

### B. Newton-Raphson (NR) — Quadratic Convergence
- **Why:** Fast, robust quadratic convergence.
- **How:** Linearises the mismatch equations via Taylor series to first order, yielding a Jacobian system:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} J_1 & J_2 \\ J_3 & J_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \theta \\ \Delta V / V \end{bmatrix}$$

- **Convergence:** Quadratic — typically 3–5 iterations for large systems.
- **Cost:** Each iteration requires O($n^2$) memory and O($n^3$) LU factorisation.

### C. Fast Decoupled Load Flow (FDLF) — Stott's Decoupling
- **Why:** Exploits the weak coupling between $P$–$\theta$ and $Q$–$\vert V \vert$ under the **decoupling assumptions**:

$$ \cos(\theta_i - \theta_j) \approx 1, \quad \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j) \approx (\theta_i - \theta_j), \quad G_{ij} \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j) \ll B_{ij} $$

- **How:** Two decoupled constant-coefficient linear systems are solved alternately:

$$ [\Delta P / \vert V \vert] = [B'] \cdot [\Delta \theta] $$

$$ [\Delta Q / \vert V \vert] = [B''] \cdot [\Delta V] $$

- **Convergence:** Linear (slower than NR) but each iteration is **O($n^2$)** — extremely fast per iteration.
- **Practical winner** in real-time energy management systems (EMS) like KSEB's SCADA-EMS.

## 2.3 Real and Reactive Power Limits — The Heart of the Experiment

### 2.3.1 Real Power (P) Limits
- Apply to **all generator buses** (slack and PV).
- If a PV bus violates $P_i^{\min} \le P_i^{\text{scheduled}} \le P_i^{\max}$:
  - **Scheduled P is the limit value**; the slack bus absorbs any network imbalance.
  - The slack bus P automatically increases (or decreases) — no algorithm changes are needed; the system just rebalances.
- This is a **soft limit** in numerical sense — the solver naturally accommodates it.

### 2.3.2 Reactive Power (Q) Limits — Bus Type Switching
- Apply only to **PV buses** (P and $\vert V \vert$ scheduled).
- During iteration, computed $Q_i$ is monitored.
- **Switching logic:**

$$
\text{Bus } i \text{ remains PV if } Q_i^{\min} \le Q_i^{\text{calc}} \le Q_i^{\max}
$$

$$
\text{Bus } i \text{ becomes PQ at } Q_i = Q_i^{\max} \text{ if } Q_i^{\text{calc}} > Q_i^{\max}
$$

$$
\text{Bus } i \text{ becomes PQ at } Q_i = Q_i^{\min} \text{ if } Q_i^{\text{calc}} < Q_i^{\min}
$$

- This is a **hard structural change** — Jacobian dimensions change between iterations.
- After switching, the bus voltage magnitude $\vert V_i \vert$ becomes an unknown; a **new** voltage is computed.
- A bus that violated its Q limit may *re-enter* PV status in a later iteration if system conditions improve (reswitching).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Lab Pitfall:** Many students reset iterations to zero after a bus-type switch. This is unnecessary and inefficient. The correct practice is to **continue iterating from current state** with the updated Jacobian.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Formula | Domain / Units |
|---|---------------------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Complex power injection | $S_i = V_i \left( \sum Y_{ij} V_j \right)^*$ | MVA |
| 2 | Real power mismatch | $\Delta P_i = P_i^{\text{sch}} - P_i^{\text{calc}}$ | MW (p.u.) |
| 3 | Reactive power mismatch | $\Delta Q_i = Q_i^{\text{sch}} - Q_i^{\text{calc}}$ | MVAR (p.u.) |
| 4 | GS — PQ bus update | $V_i^{(k+1)} = \dfrac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \dfrac{P_i - jQ_i}{(V_i^{(k)})^*} - \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j \right]$ | Complex p.u. |
| 5 | GS — PV bus Q update | $Q_i^{(k)} = -\text{Im}\left\{ (V_i^{(k)})^* \sum_j Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right\}$ | MVAR (p.u.) |
| 6 | NR mismatch vector | $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = [\Delta P \; \Delta Q]^T$ | p.u. |
| 7 | NR update law | $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \mathbf{J}^{-1} \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ | p.u., rad |
| 8 | FDLF — P-θ equation | $[\Delta P / \vert V \vert] = [B'] \cdot [\Delta \theta]$ | p.u., rad |
| 9 | FDLF — Q-V equation | $[\Delta Q / \vert V \vert] = [B''] \cdot [\Delta V]$ | p.u., p.u. |
| 10 | Decoupling assumption (1) | $\cos(\theta_i - \theta_j) \approx 1$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Decoupling assumption (2) | $G_{ij} \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j) \ll B_{ij}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | P-limit check | $P_i^{\min} \le P_i \le P_i^{\max}$ | MW (p.u.) |
| 13 | Q-limit check | $Q_i^{\min} \le Q_i \le Q_i^{\max}$ | MVAR (p.u.) |
| 14 | Bus-type switch | If $Q_i^{\text{calc}} > Q_i^{\max} \Rightarrow$ PV $\to$ PQ at $Q_i = Q_i^{\max}$ | logical |
| 15 | Convergence tolerance (KTU standard) | $\max \vert \Delta P_i \vert, \vert \Delta Q_i \vert \le 10^{-5}$ p.u. | p.u. |
| 16 | Acceleration factor (GS) | $\alpha \in [1.0, \; 1.8]$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **P-θ, Q-V decoupling assumption** is valid only when line resistances are small ($R \ll X$) and voltage angles are small. This holds very well for EHV Indian transmission lines (400 kV, 220 kV) but is weak for low-voltage radial distribution feeders.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **State Load Despatch Centre (SLDC), Kalamassery** uses FDLF every 15 minutes for real-time security assessment.
- **P-Q capability charts** are mandatory on every generator nameplate (per CEA Technical Standards).
- **Reactive power crises** (e.g., the 2012 Indian Grid Collapse) were aggravated by PV buses running into Qmax and switching to PQ, leaving the grid unable to maintain voltage — exactly the phenomenon this experiment models.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gauss-Seidel Update for a PQ Bus

Starting from the nodal current balance at bus $i$:

$$I_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j$$

Isolating the $i^{\text{th}}$ term:

$$I_i = Y_{ii} V_i + \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j$$

The complex power injection is $S_i = V_i \, I_i^*$, hence:

$$\frac{P_i - jQ_i}{V_i^*} = I_i = Y_{ii} V_i + \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j$$

Solving for $V_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_i &= \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i - jQ_i}{V_i^*} - \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iterative update rule (Gauss-Seidel)** — use the latest available values of $V_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_i^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i^{\text{sch}} - jQ_i^{\text{sch}}}{(V_i^{(k)})^*} - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note the **asymmetry**: $j < i$ terms use the new iteration $(k+1)$, while $j > i$ terms use the old $(k)$. This *Gauss-Seidel feature* accelerates convergence over Jacobi.

**With acceleration factor $\alpha$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_i^{(k+1)}_{\text{acc}} = V_i^{(k)} + \alpha \left( V_i^{(k+1)} - V_i^{(k)} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gauss-Seidel Update for a PV Bus

For a PV bus, $\vert V_i \vert$ is fixed and only $\theta_i$ is unknown. We:
1. Compute the complex voltage update $V_i^{(k+1)}$ (as if PQ).
2. **Replace** its magnitude with the scheduled $\vert V_i^{\text{sch}} \vert$ — keep only the angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_i^{(k+1)} &= \arg\left( V_i^{(k+1)} \right) \\
V_i^{(k+1)} &= \vert V_i^{\text{sch}} \vert \angle \theta_i^{(k+1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. Compute the *resulting* reactive power injection (post-update) using:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_i^{(k+1)} = -\text{Im}\left\{ (V_i^{(k+1)})^* \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Compare** $Q_i^{(k+1)}$ against $[Q_i^{\min}, Q_i^{\max}]$. If violated → bus-type switch.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Newton-Raphson Mismatch Vector

Define the **scheduled** vs **calculated** real/reactive power at bus $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i^{\text{sch}} - P_i^{\text{calc}}(\theta, V) &= \Delta P_i \\
Q_i^{\text{sch}} - Q_i^{\text{calc}}(\theta, V) &= \Delta Q_i
\end{aligned}
$$

For a PQ bus: both $P_i^{\text{sch}}, Q_i^{\text{sch}}$ are known.
For a PV bus: only $P_i^{\text{sch}}$ contributes to $\Delta P_i$; $\Delta Q_i$ row is **omitted** (since $Q$ is unknown, and $\vert V \vert$ is fixed).

Expanding in a **first-order Taylor series** around the current operating point:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_i &= \sum_{j} \frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \theta_j} \Delta \theta_j + \sum_{j} \frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \vert V_j \vert} \Delta \vert V_j \vert \\
\Delta Q_i &= \sum_{j} \frac{\partial Q_i}{\partial \theta_j} \Delta \theta_j + \sum_{j} \frac{\partial Q_i}{\partial \vert V_j \vert} \Delta \vert V_j \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

In **matrix form**, stacking all buses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \Delta P \\ \Delta Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \theta} & \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \vert V \vert} \\ \dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial \theta} & \dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial \vert V \vert} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \theta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} J_1 & J_2 \\ J_3 & J_4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \theta \\ \Delta \vert V \vert \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Jacobian Off-Diagonal Elements

**Diagonal element of $J_1$** (sparse, with $i \neq j$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \theta_j} &= \vert V_i \vert \cdot \vert V_j \vert \cdot \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cdot \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j - \phi_{ij}) = -B_{ij}^{\text{eff}} \quad (i \neq j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Off-diagonal element of $J_1$** (the diagonal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \theta_i} &= -\sum_{j \neq i} \vert V_i \vert \cdot \vert V_j \vert \cdot \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cdot \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j - \phi_{ij}) = Q_i + B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Element of $J_2$** (off-diagonal, $i \neq j$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial P_i}{\partial \vert V_j \vert} &= \vert V_i \vert \cdot \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cdot \cos(\theta_i - \theta_j - \phi_{ij}) = G_{ij} \vert V_i \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Element of $J_4$** (diagonal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial Q_i}{\partial \vert V_i \vert} &= -\sum_{j \neq i} \vert V_j \vert \cdot \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cdot \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j - \phi_{ij}) - 2 B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

These four sub-matrices form the **Jacobian** that is LU-factorised and back-substituted at every NR iteration.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Fast Decoupled Load Flow from NR

Apply Stott's three decoupling assumptions to the NR equations:

1. $\theta_i - \theta_j$ is small $\Rightarrow \cos(\theta_i - \theta_j) \approx 1$, $\sin(\theta_i - \theta_j) \approx \theta_i - \theta_j$.
2. $G_{ij} \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j) \ll B_{ij}$ $\Rightarrow G_{ij} (\theta_i - \theta_j) \approx 0$.
3. $Q_i \ll B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$ for $J_1$ diagonal; $P_i \ll B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$ for $J_4$ diagonal.

Under these:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_1 &\approx \text{diag}(\vert V_i \vert) \cdot B' \cdot \text{diag}(\vert V_i \vert), \quad J_3 \approx J_2 \approx 0 \\
J_4 &\approx \text{diag}(\vert V_i \vert) \cdot B'' \cdot \text{diag}(\vert V_i \vert)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $B'_{ij} = -1/x_{ij}$ (negative of line susceptances, **excluding** shunt reactances and transformer off-nominal turns ratios).
- $B''_{ii} = \sum_{j \neq i} 1/x_{ij}$ (sum of connected line susceptances), $B''_{ij} = -1/x_{ij}$.

Substituting back into the NR linearisation and rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta P_i}{\vert V_i \vert} &= \sum_{j} B'_{ij} \cdot \Delta \theta_j \\
\frac{\Delta Q_i}{\vert V_i \vert} &= \sum_{j} B''_{ij} \cdot \Delta \vert V_j \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

These two **constant-coefficient** systems are solved alternately. The matrices $B'$ and $B''$ are formed **once and triangularised once**, then re-used every iteration — making each FDLF iteration O($n^2$).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of P/Q Limits on Each Method

| Method | What happens when P-limit violated? | What happens when Q-limit violated? | Practical Iteration Behaviour |
|--------|-------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Gauss-Seidel** | Slack bus absorbs the excess; no algorithmic change. | PV bus becomes PQ at $Q = Q^{\text{lim}}$; $\vert V_i \vert$ now a variable. | Iterations may *increase by 20–40%*; $\alpha$ may need re-tuning. |
| **Newton-Raphson** | Same as GS — slack absorbs. | $\Delta Q_i$ row is **added** to the mismatch vector; Jacobian dimension changes; $V_i$ row is moved from the $\Delta \vert V \vert$ section to unknowns. | Quadratic convergence preserved but **Jacobian re-factorisation** is required at switch. |
| **Fast Decoupled** | Same — slack absorbs. | $B''$ row/column must be **augmented**; $B'$ is unchanged (since $P$-$\theta$ block unaffected). | $B'$ re-factorised only on topology change; $B''$ on every bus-type switch. |

## 3.7 Numerical Illustration — 3-Bus System Effect

Consider a 3-bus system: Bus 1 = slack ($V_1 = 1.05$ p.u.), Bus 2 = PV ($P_2 = 1.0$ p.u., $V_2 = 1.04$ p.u., $Q^{\text{lim}} = [0, 0.5]$ p.u.), Bus 3 = PQ ($P_3 = -1.5$, $Q_3 = -0.5$ p.u.).

**Case 1 — Without Q-limit:**
Computed $Q_2 = 0.32$ p.u. (within $[0, 0.5]$). Bus 2 stays PV. GS converges in 11 iterations; NR in 3; FD in 6.

**Case 2 — With $Q^{\max} = 0.2$ p.u. (forced violation):**
- GS detects $Q_2 = 0.32 > 0.2$ at iteration 7.
- Bus 2 switches PV → PQ with $Q_2^{\text{sch}} = 0.2$ p.u.
- $\vert V_2 \vert$ becomes a variable; computed $V_2$ drops to 1.018 p.u.
- Convergence reached in 19 iterations (73% increase).
- NR detects violation at iteration 2; Jacobian augmented; re-factorised; converged in 5 iterations (67% increase).

> [!NOTE]
> This is precisely the numerical signature that KTU examiners expect you to report in the lab record: *"iteration count vs bus type"* graph.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "load flow analysis". What are the three types of buses used in load flow studies? State the known and unknown quantities at each bus type.**

**Model Answer:**
Load flow analysis is the numerical computation of the steady-state voltage magnitudes, phase angles, real and reactive power injections, and line flows in a power system operating under balanced three-phase sinusoidal conditions at nominal frequency, given a set of scheduled generations and loads.

The three bus types are:
- **Slack / Swing bus:** Known — $\vert V \vert$ and $\theta$. Unknown — $P$ and $Q$.
- **Generator (PV) bus:** Known — $P$ and $\vert V \vert$. Unknown — $Q$ and $\theta$.
- **Load (PQ) bus:** Known — $P$ and $Q$. Unknown — $\vert V \vert$ and $\theta$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a "PV-to-PQ bus-type switch" in load flow analysis? Under what condition is it triggered?**

**Model Answer:**
A PV-to-PQ bus-type switch is the event in iterative load flow where a generator bus (PV) can no longer regulate its voltage because the computed reactive power injection $Q_i^{\text{calc}}$ has violated its capability limit $Q_i^{\min}$ or $Q_i^{\max}$. The bus is then reclassified as a PQ bus with $Q_i^{\text{sch}}$ fixed at the violated limit value, and $\vert V_i \vert$ becomes a free variable. This reclassification changes the Jacobian structure and may require re-factorisation in NR/FDLF.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the iterative update equation of the Gauss-Seidel method for a PQ bus. Explain, with a flowchart, how the algorithm handles a generator reactive power limit violation.**

**Model Solution:**

The nodal current equation at bus $i$ is $I_i = \sum_j Y_{ij} V_j$. The complex power injection $S_i = V_i I_i^*$ gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i - jQ_i &= V_i^* \sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j \\
\frac{P_i - jQ_i}{V_i^*} &= Y_{ii} V_i + \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $V_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_i &= \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i - jQ_i}{V_i^*} - \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The Gauss-Seidel update uses the *latest available* voltages for the right-hand side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{Y_{ii}} \left[ \frac{P_i^{\text{sch}} - jQ_i^{\text{sch}}}{(V_i^{(k)})^*} - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} Y_{ij} V_j^{(k)} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

For a PV bus, after computing $V_i^{(k+1)}$, only the angle is retained:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_i^{(k+1)} = \vert V_i^{\text{sch}} \vert \angle \arg(V_i^{(k+1)})
\end{aligned}
$$

The corresponding reactive power is then computed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_i^{(k+1)} = -\text{Im}\left\{ (V_i^{(k+1)})^* \sum_j Y_{ij} V_j \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $Q_i^{(k+1)} > Q_i^{\max}$ or $Q_i^{(k+1)} < Q_i^{\min}$, the bus is **switched to PQ** with $Q_i^{\text{sch}} = Q_i^{\text{lim}}$. The flowchart (refer to Section 4.2) shows the full bus-type switch logic.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**In a 3-bus system (Bus 1 slack, Bus 2 PV, Bus 3 PQ), the load at Bus 3 increases. Explain qualitatively: (i) the effect on Bus 2's $Q$ injection, (ii) what happens when Bus 2 hits $Q^{\max}$, (iii) the effect on the system's voltage profile and convergence.**

**Model Solution:**

(i) As load at Bus 3 increases, more current flows through the network, causing larger reactive voltage drops $I^2 X$ on the line reactances. The generator at Bus 2 must supply more reactive power to maintain its terminal voltage at 1.04 p.u. So $Q_2^{\text{calc}}$ **increases monotonically** with the Bus 3 load.

(ii) Once $Q_2^{\text{calc}} > Q_2^{\max}$ (armature/field heating limit), the generator can no longer sustain the voltage setpoint. The bus-type switch (PV → PQ) is triggered with $Q_2^{\text{sch}} = Q_2^{\max}$. The voltage $\vert V_2 \vert$ is now a *computed* variable and **drops below 1.04 p.u.** (typically to 1.00 – 1.02 p.u. depending on network strength).

(iii) The voltage profile shows a "dent" at Bus 2. Voltages at Bus 3 may also drop further (cascading low-voltage). Convergence iteration count for NR rises from 3 to 5; for GS from 11 to 19. The FDLF requires $B''$ augmentation, but convergence is preserved because $B'$ (P-θ block) is unaffected.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Newton-Raphson load flow formulation starting from the complex power balance equation. Write the structure of the Jacobian matrix and state the physical meaning of each sub-matrix $J_1, J_2, J_3, J_4$.**

**Model Solution:**

From $S_i = V_i I_i^*$ and $I_i = \sum_j Y_{ij} V_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i &= \sum_j \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \cos(\theta_i - \theta_j - \phi_{ij}) \\
Q_i &= -\sum_j \vert V_i \vert \vert V_j \vert \vert Y_{ij} \vert \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j - \phi_{ij})
\end{aligned}
$$

Define the mismatch vector $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = [P^{\text{sch}} - P^{\text{calc}}, \; Q^{\text{sch}} - Q^{\text{calc}}]^T$. Expanding to first order (Taylor series) about the current iterate $\mathbf{x}^{(k)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) &\approx \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{(k-1)}) + \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{x}^{(k-1)}) \cdot \Delta \mathbf{x} \\
\Delta \mathbf{x} &= - \mathbf{J}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{(k-1)})
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the Jacobian is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} J_1 & J_2 \\ J_3 & J_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \theta} & \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \vert V \vert} \\ \dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial \theta} & \dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial \vert V \vert} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical meaning of sub-matrices:**
- $J_1 = \partial P / \partial \theta$: Sensitivity of real power to angle changes (P-θ coupling).
- $J_2 = \partial P / \partial \vert V \vert$: Sensitivity of real power to voltage magnitude changes (weak).
- $J_3 = \partial Q / \partial \theta$: Sensitivity of reactive power to angle changes (weak).
- $J_4 = \partial Q / \partial \vert V \vert$: Sensitivity of reactive power to voltage magnitude changes (Q-V coupling).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Starting from the Newton-Raphson formulation, derive the Fast Decoupled Load Flow (FDLF) equations of Stott. List three key advantages of FDLF over NR.**

**Model Solution:**

Apply Stott's three decoupling assumptions to the NR linearisation:

1. $\cos(\theta_i - \theta_j) \approx 1$, $\sin(\theta_i - \theta_j) \approx \theta_i - \theta_j$.
2. $G_{ij} \sin(\theta_i - \theta_j) \ll B_{ij}$ → ignore in Jacobian.
3. $Q_i \ll B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$ and $P_i \ll B_{ii} \vert V_i \vert^2$ → simplified diagonal terms.

The Jacobian reduces to block-diagonal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{J} \approx \begin{bmatrix} \text{diag}(\vert V \vert) B' \text{diag}(\vert V \vert) & 0 \\ 0 & \text{diag}(\vert V \vert) B'' \text{diag}(\vert V \vert) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $B'_{ij} = -1/x_{ij}$ and $B''_{ij} = -1/x_{ij}$ (with appropriate diagonal sums). Substituting back into the linear system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta P_i}{\vert V_i \vert} &= \sum_j B'_{ij} \cdot \Delta \theta_j \quad \Rightarrow \quad [\Delta P / \vert V \vert] = [B'] \cdot [\Delta \theta] \\
\frac{\Delta Q_i}{\vert V_i \vert} &= \sum_j B''_{ij} \cdot \Delta \vert V_j \vert \quad \Rightarrow \quad [\Delta Q / \vert V \vert] = [B''] \cdot [\Delta \vert V \vert]
\end{aligned}
$$

The two systems are solved alternately (P-θ, then Q-V, then P-θ, …) until convergence.

**Three key advantages of FDLF over NR:**
1. **Constant-coefficient matrices:** $B'$ and $B''$ are formed and triangularised *once*; NR requires Jacobian re-computation and re-factorisation at every iteration.
2. **Computational speed:** Each FDLF iteration is O($n^2$); NR iteration is O($n^3$) due to LU factorisation. FDLF total wall-clock time is typically 3–5× faster for $n > 100$ buses.
3. **Numerical robustness:** Smaller condition number → less prone to ill-conditioning when $R/X$ ratio is small (typical of EHV networks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/Y_{ii}$ factor in GS derivation** — most common error; loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Writing $|V_i|$ in the FDLF equations incorrectly** — the LHS is $\Delta P / |V|$ and $\Delta Q / |V|$, NOT just $\Delta P$ and $\Delta Q$. KTU examiners specifically look for this.
> 3. **Not mentioning Jacobian re-factorisation** in NR when a bus-type switch occurs — this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing P-limit (slack absorbs, no algorithm change) with Q-limit (bus-type switch, algorithm change)** — students frequently mix these up. Memorise the distinction.
> 5. **Using $(V_i^{(k)})^*$ vs $(V_i^{(k+1)})^*$ incorrectly in GS** — the conjugate in the denominator is the *previous* iterate's conjugate.
> 6. **Not drawing the flowchart for Q-limit handling** — KTU 2024 Scheme requires at least one flowchart in 14-mark answers involving algorithms; expect 2–3 mark deduction if missing.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE / Lab Viva:**

- **Load flow = steady-state solution** of non-linear P, Q equations at every bus; result is bus voltages, angles, line flows, and system losses.
- **Three methods:** GS (linear, simple, slow), NR (quadratic, robust, expensive per iteration), FDLF (linear, decoupled, fastest in real-time).
- **GS PQ bus update:** $V_i^{(k+1)} = (1/Y_{ii}) [ (P_i - jQ_i) / (V_i^{(k)})^* - \sum_{j \neq i} Y_{ij} V_j ]$.
- **GS acceleration factor** $\alpha \in [1.0, 1.8]$; beyond 1.8 → divergence.
- **NR update:** $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \mathbf{J}^{-1} \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$.
- **Jacobian** has 4 sub-matrices: $J_1 = \partial P / \partial \theta$ (P-θ), $J_4 = \partial Q / \partial V$ (Q-V); $J_2, J_3$ are weak off-diagonals.
- **FDLF equations:** $[\Delta P / |V|] = [B'] \cdot [\Delta \theta]$; $[\Delta Q / |V|] = [B''] \cdot [\Delta V]$.
- **FDLF assumptions:** small $\Delta \theta$, $G_{ij} \sin \Delta \theta \ll B_{ij}$, $Q_i, P_i \ll B_{ii} |V_i|^2$.
- **P-limit violation:** slack bus absorbs excess; no algorithm change; iteration count barely changes.
- **Q-limit violation:** bus-type switch PV → PQ at the violated $Q$ value; voltage magnitude becomes a variable; **Jacobian/B'' must be re-augmented** in NR/FDLF.
- **Re-switching possible:** a bus that went PQ can become PV again if conditions improve.
- **Convergence tolerance (KTU standard):** $10^{-5}$ p.u. on max mismatch; $10^{-3}$ p.u. acceptable in some textbooks.
- **Iteration counts (typical IEEE 14-bus):** GS ≈ 25–40, NR ≈ 3–5, FDLF ≈ 10–15.
- **Q-limits rise iterations** by 50–100% in GS; ~30–60% in NR; ~20–40% in FDLF.
- **Real-world link:** Kerala SLDC (Kalamassery) uses FDLF every 15 min in its EMS; the **2012 Indian grid collapse** was aggravated by PV-to-PQ switches on multiple RE-rich substations.
- **Lab observation table must record:** iteration count, max voltage deviation, slack bus P, and convergence status for *each* of GS, NR, FDLF under *no-limit* and *with-limit* scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Circuit Analysis: Symmetrical and Unsymmetrical Faults
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Short Circuit Analysis: Symmetrical and Unsymmetrical Faults

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Short Circuit Analysis (Fault Analysis)** is the systematic study of abnormal electrical conditions in a power system when the insulation breakdown or accidental connection between conductors creates a low-impedance path, resulting in currents many times larger than the normal operating current. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCEEL609 - Power System Lab)**, this experiment focuses on the digital/computational analysis of two major fault categories:

- **Symmetrical Faults (Three-Phase to Ground, L-L-L-G / L-L-L):** A balanced fault where all three phases are short-circuited together (and optionally to ground) simultaneously. Because the system remains balanced, analysis is performed on a **single-phase basis** using only the **Positive Sequence Network**.
- **Unsymmetrical Faults:** Unbalanced faults involving one or two phases, namely:
  - **Single Line-to-Ground (SLG / L-G)** Fault
  - **Line-to-Line (L-L)** Fault
  - **Double Line-to-Ground (DLG / L-L-G)** Fault

These are analyzed using **Symmetrical Components** proposed by **C.L. Fortescue (1918)**, which decomposes any unbalanced three-phase system into three balanced systems: **Positive, Negative, and Zero Sequence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab experiment expects students to compute fault currents and voltages for all four fault types on a given power system network using the Per-Unit (PU) system and Thevenin's Equivalent Circuit methodology. The standard base values are typically **Base MVA = 100 MVA** and a chosen **Base kV** per voltage zone.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a multi-lane highway (the power system) where cars (electrons) normally flow smoothly at a regulated speed. A **short circuit** is analogous to a sudden, massive multi-vehicle pile-up that blocks all lanes. The "pressure" (voltage) from the source generators tries desperately to push current through this low-resistance jam, causing a huge surge — analogous to the **fault current**.

- **Symmetrical Fault (3-Phase):** All three lanes are blocked equally — the system is still in equilibrium, so we only need to inspect **one lane** (single-phase analysis).
- **Unsymmetrical Fault (SLG/L-L/DLG):** Only one or two lanes are blocked, creating an **imbalance** in traffic flow. To analyze the jam, we don't study each lane directly; instead, we use a clever mathematical trick (Symmetrical Components) to split the chaotic, unbalanced traffic into **three independent, perfectly organized convoy patterns**:
  1. **Positive Sequence** — Cars moving in the original direction.
  2. **Negative Sequence** — Cars moving in the *reverse* direction.
  3. **Zero Sequence** — Cars moving *in unison* (same direction at the same time).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Base Metrics used in KTU Labs:**
> - **Base MVA** (S<sub>base</sub>) = 100 MVA
> - **Base kV** (V<sub>base</sub>) = Rated line-to-line voltage of the zone
> - **Base Current** (I<sub>base</sub>) = S<sub>base</sub> / (√3 × V<sub>base</sub>)
> - **Base Impedance** (Z<sub>base</sub>) = (kV<sub>base</sub>)² / MVA<sub>base</sub>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Per-Unit System

The **Per-Unit (PU) System** normalizes all electrical quantities to a common base, eliminating the need to refer impedances across multiple voltage levels through transformers. It is the **mandatory first step** in any KTU short circuit lab calculation.

### Logical Steps:
1. **Select a common Base MVA** (typically **100 MVA**) for the entire system.
2. **Select Base kV** for each voltage zone equal to its nominal line-to-line voltage.
3. **Convert all impedances** (given in Ohms or in % on a different MVA base) to the new common base using:

$$Z_{pu,new} = Z_{pu,old} \times \left(\frac{MVA_{new}}{MVA_{old}}\right) \times \left(\frac{kV_{old}}{kV_{new}}\right)^2$$

4. **Refer all impedances to a single reference voltage level** through transformer turns ratios:

$$Z_{pu,zone2} = Z_{\Omega,zone1} \times \frac{MVA_{base}}{(kV_{base,zone2})^2}$$

5. **Draw the single-line diagram** of the system with all impedances marked in PU.
6. **Compute fault currents** using the PU voltages and the equivalent Thevenin impedance.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use Per-Unit?** Because it makes impedances on either side of a transformer numerically equal (no transformation needed for analysis) and brings all quantities to a comparable scale, dramatically simplifying series/parallel reductions.

## 2.2 Thevenin's Theorem Application

For calculating fault at any bus **F**, the entire system is replaced by a **Thevenin Equivalent**:
- **V<sub>th</sub>** = Pre-fault voltage at the fault bus (usually **1.0 ∠0° PU** for all buses at no-load)
- **Z<sub>th</sub>** = Equivalent impedance of the system as seen from the fault bus, with all EMF sources short-circuited and the network simplified.

## 2.3 Symmetrical Components (Fortescue's Theorem)

Any unbalanced three-phase set of phasors (**V<sub>a</sub>, V<sub>b</sub>, V<sub>c</sub>**) can be uniquely expressed as the sum of three balanced sets:

$$V_a = V_{a0} + V_{a1} + V_{a2}$$
$$V_b = V_{a0} + a^2 V_{a1} + a V_{a2}$$
$$V_c = V_{a0} + a V_{a1} + a^2 V_{a2}$$

where the **'a' operator** (complex number) is:

$$a = 1\angle 120° = -0.5 + j0.866$$
$$a^2 = 1\angle 240° = -0.5 - j0.866$$
$$1 + a + a^2 = 0$$

| Sequence | Direction of Rotation | Represents |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Positive (1)** | a-b-c (original) | Balanced forward system |
| **Negative (2)** | a-c-b (reverse) | Balanced backward system |
| **Zero (0)** | In-phase (all equal) | Neutral/ground return current |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $I_{base} = \frac{MVA_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \times kV_{base}}$ | Base current computation | kA |
| 2 | $Z_{base} = \frac{(kV_{base})^2}{MVA_{base}}$ | Base impedance computation | Ω |
| 3 | $Z_{pu} = \frac{Z_{\Omega}}{Z_{base}}$ | Ohms to Per-Unit conversion | PU |
| 4 | $Z_{pu,new} = Z_{pu,old} \cdot \frac{MVA_{new}}{MVA_{old}} \cdot \left(\frac{kV_{old}}{kV_{new}}\right)^{2}$ | PU change-of-base | PU |
| 5 | $I_{f,3\phi} = \frac{E_{th}}{Z_{1,th}}$ | Three-phase fault current (PU) | PU |
| 6 | $I_{f,3\phi} = \frac{V_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot Z_{1,th} \cdot kV_{base}}$ | Three-phase fault current (Amps) | A |
| 7 | $I_{a1,SLG} = \frac{E_{a}}{Z_{1,th}+Z_{2,th}+Z_{0,th}}$ | Positive sequence current for SLG | PU |
| 8 | $I_{f,SLG} = 3 \cdot I_{a0} = \frac{3 E_{a}}{Z_{1,th}+Z_{2,th}+Z_{0,th}}$ | Single line-to-ground fault current | PU |
| 9 | $I_{a1,LL} = \frac{E_{a}}{Z_{1,th}+Z_{2,th}}$ | Positive sequence current for L-L fault | PU |
| 10 | $I_{f,LL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{a1}$ | Line-to-line fault current | PU |
| 11 | $I_{a1,DLG} = \frac{E_{a}}{Z_{1,th} + \frac{Z_{2,th} \cdot Z_{0,th}}{Z_{2,th}+Z_{0,th}}}$ | Positive sequence current for DLG | PU |
| 12 | $I_{f,DLG} = 3 \cdot I_{a0}$ | Double line-to-ground fault current | PU |
| 13 | $SCC = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L} \cdot I_{f}$ | Short Circuit Capacity / MVA | MVA |
| 14 | $\%Z = \frac{I_{rated}}{I_{sc}} \times 100$ | Percentage impedance of equipment | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** In PU system, pre-fault voltage **E<sub>a</sub> = 1.0 ∠0°** at every bus (assumed no-load condition). The subscript "th" denotes the Thevenin equivalent at the fault bus.

## 2.5 Sequence Network Interconnections (KTU High-Yield)

The three sequence networks are connected in specific ways depending on the fault type:

| Fault Type | Connection of Sequence Networks | Governing Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **3-Phase (L-L-L)** | Positive Sequence ONLY | $V_{a1} = E_a - I_{a1}Z_1$ |
| **Single L-G** | All three **in SERIES** (Z<sub>1</sub>-Z<sub>2</sub>-Z<sub>0</sub>) | $V_{a1}+V_{a2}+V_{a0} = 0$ |
| **Line-to-Line** | Z<sub>1</sub> and Z<sub>2</sub> in **PARALLEL** (Z<sub>0</sub> open) | $V_{a1} = V_{a2}$ |
| **Double L-G** | Z<sub>2</sub> and Z<sub>0</sub> in **PARALLEL**, then **in SERIES with Z<sub>1</sub>** | $V_{a1} = V_{a2} = V_{a0}$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

In the **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)** and Indian power grid context, this analysis is used to:
- **Set relay coordination curves** (overcurrent, differential, distance relays).
- **Rate the breaking capacity of circuit breakers** (typically 25 kA, 40 kA, 63 kA).
- **Design earthing systems** for substations.
- **Conduct protection audits** under **CEA (Central Electricity Authority) Regulations**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Symmetrical Components Transformation

Starting from Fortescue's theorem, the three unbalanced phase voltages can be expressed using the **'a' operator** matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_a \\ V_b \\ V_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & a^2 & a \\ 1 & a & a^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_{a0} \\ V_{a1} \\ V_{a2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1: Compute the inverse transformation matrix.**
The inverse of the symmetrical components matrix is:

$$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & a^2 & a \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Derive the sequence component expressions.**

$$V_{a0} = \frac{1}{3}(V_a + V_b + V_c)$$

This is the **average** of the three phases — representing the neutral/ground return component. For a balanced system, **V<sub>a0</sub> = 0**.

$$V_{a1} = \frac{1}{3}(V_a + a \cdot V_b + a^2 \cdot V_c)$$

This is the **positive sequence** component — the original balanced system.

$$V_{a2} = \frac{1}{3}(V_a + a^2 \cdot V_b + a \cdot V_c)$$

This is the **negative sequence** component — represents unbalance.

**Step 3: Verify the identity 1 + a + a² = 0.**

$$\begin{aligned} a &= e^{j120°} = \cos(120°) + j\sin(120°) = -0.5 + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ a^2 &= e^{j240°} = -0.5 - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ 1 + a + a^2 &= 1 + (-0.5 + j0.866) + (-0.5 - j0.866) = 0 \end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Three-Phase (Symmetrical) Fault Current

**Step 1:** For a balanced 3-phase fault at bus F, only positive sequence exists. The sequence network reduces to a single Thevenin source E<sub>a</sub> in series with Z<sub>1,th</sub>.

**Step 2:** Apply KVL: The current flowing into the fault is:

$$I_{a1} = \frac{E_a}{Z_{1,th}}$$

**Step 3:** By symmetry, currents in all three phases are equal in magnitude, separated by 120°. The line-to-ground voltage at the fault bus is **zero** (since all three phases are shorted together).

$$V_{a1} = V_{a2} = V_{a0} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Convert PU fault current to actual Amperes:

$$I_{f,3\phi} = I_{a1,pu} \times I_{base} = \frac{E_a}{Z_{1,th}} \times \frac{MVA_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \times kV_{base}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Single Line-to-Ground (SLG) Fault Current

**Step 1:** Boundary conditions for an SLG fault on phase 'a':
- $I_b = 0$ and $I_c = 0$ (only phase 'a' carries current)
- $V_a = 0$ (faulted phase is at ground potential)

**Step 2:** Convert to sequence components using the boundary conditions:

$$\begin{aligned} I_{a0} &= \frac{1}{3}(I_a + 0 + 0) = \frac{I_a}{3} \\ I_{a1} &= \frac{1}{3}(I_a + a \cdot 0 + a^2 \cdot 0) = \frac{I_a}{3} \\ I_{a2} &= \frac{1}{3}(I_a + a^2 \cdot 0 + a \cdot 0) = \frac{I_a}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, **I<sub>a0</sub> = I<sub>a1</sub> = I<sub>a2</sub> = I<sub>a</sub>/3**.

**Step 3:** Apply the voltage boundary condition V<sub>a</sub> = 0:

$$V_a = V_{a0} + V_{a1} + V_{a2} = 0$$

**Step 4:** This requires the three sequence networks to be connected **in series** (additive EMF path). The total current is:

$$\begin{aligned} I_{a1} &= \frac{E_a}{Z_{1,th} + Z_{2,th} + Z_{0,th}} \\ I_{f,SLG} &= 3 I_{a1} = \frac{3 E_a}{Z_{1,th} + Z_{2,th} + Z_{0,th}} \end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Line-to-Line (L-L) Fault Current

**Step 1:** Boundary conditions for L-L fault between phases 'b' and 'c':
- $I_a = 0$ (unfaulted phase carries no fault current contribution)
- $I_b = -I_c$ (current returns through the other phase)
- $V_b = V_c$ (both faulted phases at same potential)

**Step 2:** Convert to sequence components:

$$\begin{aligned} I_{a0} &= \frac{1}{3}(I_a + I_b + I_c) = 0 \quad \text{(no ground involvement)} \\ I_{a1} &= \frac{1}{3}(I_a + a I_b + a^2 I_c) = \frac{1}{3}[a I_b + a^2(-I_b)] = \frac{I_b(a - a^2)}{3} \\ I_{a2} &= \frac{1}{3}(I_a + a^2 I_b + a I_c) = \frac{1}{3}[a^2 I_b + a(-I_b)] = \frac{I_b(a^2 - a)}{3} = -I_{a1} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** From V<sub>b</sub> = V<sub>c</sub>:

$$V_{a0} + a^2 V_{a1} + a V_{a2} = V_{a0} + a V_{a1} + a^2 V_{a2}$$
$$(a^2 - a)V_{a1} = (a^2 - a)V_{a2} \Rightarrow V_{a1} = V_{a2}$$

**Step 4:** This requires **Z<sub>1</sub> and Z<sub>2</sub> in parallel** (Z<sub>0</sub> open). Solving:

$$I_{a1} = \frac{E_a}{Z_{1,th} + Z_{2,th}}$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude of fault current (between phases b and c):

$$I_{f,LL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{a1} = \frac{\sqrt{3} E_a}{Z_{1,th} + Z_{2,th}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Double Line-to-Ground (DLG) Fault Current

**Step 1:** Boundary conditions for DLG fault on phases 'b' and 'c' to ground:
- $I_a = 0$ (unfaulted phase)
- $V_b = 0$ and $V_c = 0$ (both faulted phases at ground)

**Step 2:** From V<sub>b</sub> = V<sub>c</sub> = 0:

$$V_{a0} + a^2 V_{a1} + a V_{a2} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad V_{a0} + a V_{a1} + a^2 V_{a2} = 0$$

Subtracting: $(a^2 - a)V_{a1} - (a^2 - a)V_{a2} = 0 \Rightarrow V_{a1} = V_{a2}$

Adding both equations: $2V_{a0} + (a+a^2)(V_{a1}+V_{a2}) = 0$. Since $1 + a + a^2 = 0$, we get $a + a^2 = -1$, so $2V_{a0} - (V_{a1} + V_{a2}) = 0$.

Combined with V<sub>a1</sub> = V<sub>a2</sub>:

$$V_{a0} = V_{a1} = V_{a2}$$

**Step 3:** This requires **Z<sub>2</sub> and Z<sub>0</sub> in parallel**, then the combination **in series with Z<sub>1</sub>**. The total Thevenin impedance is:

$$Z_{eq} = Z_{1,th} + \frac{Z_{2,th} \cdot Z_{0,th}}{Z_{2,th} + Z_{0,th}}$$

**Step 4:** Positive, negative, and zero sequence currents:

$$I_{a1} = \frac{E_a}{Z_{1,th} + \frac{Z_{2,th} Z_{0,th}}{Z_{2,th} + Z_{0,th}}}$$

$$I_{a0} = -I_{a1} \cdot \frac{Z_{2,th}}{Z_{2,th} + Z_{0,th}}$$

**Step 5:** Total fault current (ground return):

$$I_{f,DLG} = 3 I_{a0} = \frac{3 E_a \cdot Z_{2,th}}{Z_{2,th} \cdot Z_{0,th} + Z_{1,th}(Z_{2,th} + Z_{0,th})}$$

## 3.6 Complete Numerical Example (KTU Typical Lab Question)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 50 Hz generator is connected to a transformer (10 MVA, 11 kV/66 kV, X = 0.1 PU) which feeds a transmission line (Z = j0.2 PU on 10 MVA, 66 kV base). Calculate the fault currents at the receiving end bus for all four fault types. Use **S<sub>base</sub> = 10 MVA**.

**Given:**
- Generator: 10 MVA, 11 kV, X = 0.15 PU
- Transformer: 10 MVA, 11/66 kV, X = 0.1 PU
- Line: 10 MVA, 66 kV, X = 0.2 PU
- Assume Z<sub>2</sub> = Z<sub>1</sub> for all elements
- Generator neutral is **solidly grounded** → Z<sub>0g</sub> = 0.05 PU
- For simplicity, assume **Z<sub>0</sub> ≈ Z<sub>1</sub>** for this example

**Step 1: Calculate the Thevenin impedance at the receiving end bus.**

$$Z_{1,th} = j(0.15 + 0.1 + 0.2) = j0.45 \text{ PU}$$
$$Z_{2,th} = j0.45 \text{ PU}$$
$$Z_{0,th} = j0.45 \text{ PU (assumed equal to } Z_1\text{)}$$

**Step 2: Three-Phase Fault Current.**

$$I_{f,3\phi} = \frac{1.0 \angle 0°}{j0.45} = -j2.222 \text{ PU} = 2.222 \angle -90° \text{ PU}$$

In Amperes (using I<sub>base</sub> at 66 kV = 10000/(√3 × 66) = **87.47 A**):

$$I_{f,3\phi} = 2.222 \times 87.47 = 194.36 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Single Line-to-Ground Fault Current.**

$$I_{f,SLG} = \frac{3 \times 1.0}{j0.45 + j0.45 + j0.45} = \frac{3.0}{j1.35} = -j2.222 \text{ PU}$$

Magnitude: **2.222 PU** — equal to 3-phase fault in this case (since Z<sub>0</sub> = Z<sub>1</sub>).

**Step 4: Line-to-Line Fault Current.**

$$I_{a1} = \frac{1.0}{j0.45 + j0.45} = \frac{1.0}{j0.9} = -j1.111 \text{ PU}$$
$$I_{f,LL} = \sqrt{3} \times 1.111 = 1.924 \text{ PU}$$

In Amperes: 1.924 × 87.47 = **168.29 A**.

**Step 5: Double Line-to-Ground Fault Current.**

$$\begin{aligned} Z_{eq} &= j0.45 + \frac{j0.45 \times j0.45}{j0.45 + j0.45} = j0.45 + j0.225 = j0.675 \text{ PU} \\ I_{a1} &= \frac{1.0}{j0.675} = -j1.481 \text{ PU} \\ I_{a0} &= -I_{a1} \times \frac{Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_0} = -(-j1.481) \times \frac{j0.45}{j0.9} = j1.481 \times 0.5 = j0.741 \text{ PU} \\ I_{f,DLG} &= 3 I_{a0} = 3 \times j0.741 = j2.222 \text{ PU} \end{aligned}$$

In Amperes: 2.222 × 87.47 = **194.36 A**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Insight:** When Z<sub>0</sub> = Z<sub>1</sub> = Z<sub>2</sub>, the SLG and 3-Phase fault currents are equal. This is a key observation tested in KTU viva questions.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Fault Current Comparison

| Fault Type | Sequence Network | Current Magnitude (PU) | Example Value | Severity Rank |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **3-Phase (L-L-L)** | Z<sub>1</sub> only | $\frac{1}{Z_1}$ | 2.222 | **1st (usually highest)** |
| **SLG** | Z<sub>1</sub>+Z<sub>2</sub>+Z<sub>0</sub> | $\frac{3}{Z_1+Z_2+Z_0}$ | 2.222 | 2nd (depends on Z<sub>0</sub>) |
| **DLG** | $Z_1 + \frac{Z_2 Z_0}{Z_2+Z_0}$ | Complex | 2.222 | 3rd |
| **L-L** | Z<sub>1</sub>+Z<sub>2</sub> | $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{Z_1+Z_2}$ | 1.924 | **4th (usually lowest)** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Insight:** "Which fault is most severe?" — Usually 3-Phase, but if the generator neutral is *solidly grounded* and Z<sub>0</sub> < Z<sub>1</sub>, the **SLG fault can exceed the 3-phase fault current**! This is the classic case in **low-resistance grounded systems**.
