# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the term "Percentage Reactance" of a synchronous machine and explain how it is used in short circuit analysis.

**Model Answer:**
The **Percentage Reactance (%X)** of a synchronous machine is defined as the voltage drop across the machine's synchronous reactance, expressed as a percentage of the rated phase voltage, when the machine carries its rated full-load current. Mathematically:

$$\%X = \frac{I_{rated} \times X_s}{V_{rated}} \times 100$$

In short circuit analysis, %X is used to compute the short circuit current the machine can deliver at its terminals. The **short circuit current** is given by:

$$I_{sc} = \frac{I_{rated} \times 100}{\%X}$$

A **lower %X** means a **higher short circuit current**, which determines the breaking capacity rating of the circuit breaker connected at the machine terminals. For typical alternators, %X ranges from **8% to 15%**, and for transformers, it is around **5% to 10%**.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is meant by "Zero Sequence Reactance" of a synchronous machine? Why is it different from positive sequence reactance?

**Model Answer:**
The **Zero Sequence Reactance (X<sub>0</sub>)** is the reactance offered by the synchronous machine to currents that are equal in magnitude and phase in all three stator windings (i.e., currents that sum to a non-zero value and return through the neutral or ground). It represents the machine's behavior under **unbalanced faults involving ground** (such as SLG and DLG faults).

Zero sequence reactance differs from positive sequence reactance (X<sub>1</sub>) due to the **spatial distribution of the MMF produced**. Positive sequence currents produce a **rotating magnetic field** in the air gap (synchronous with the rotor), while zero sequence currents produce **three stationary, pulsating MMFs** that cancel each other out spatially, resulting in negligible air-gap flux. Hence, X<sub>0</sub> is typically much smaller than X<sub>1</sub> (often **X<sub>0</sub> ≈ 0.1 to 0.7 × X<sub>1</sub>**), depending on the pitch and distribution of the stator winding.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 11 kV generator with a reactance of 0.15 PU on its own base (10 MVA) is connected through a 10 MVA, 11/66 kV transformer with 10% reactance to a transmission line. The line has a reactance of 0.2 PU on 10 MVA, 66 kV base. A **3-phase fault** occurs at the receiving end of the line. Assume pre-fault voltage of 1.0 PU and a common base of 10 MVA.

**Part (a)** [7 Marks] Draw the per-unit reactance diagram and compute the Thevenin equivalent impedance at the fault bus.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks] Calculate the fault current in **PU, Amperes, and Short Circuit MVA** at the receiving end.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1:** Identify equipment ratings and reactances.

| Element | Rating | Reactance (PU) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Generator (G) | 10 MVA, 11 kV | X<sub>G</sub> = 0.15 |
| Transformer (T) | 10 MVA, 11/66 kV | X<sub>T</sub> = 0.10 |
| Transmission Line (L) | 10 MVA, 66 kV | X<sub>L</sub> = 0.20 |

**Step 2:** Since the given bases (10 MVA) are already on a common MVA base, no change-of-base is required.

**Step 3:** Verify that all impedances are referred to the same voltage level. The transformer's reactance of 0.10 PU is on its own rating (10 MVA, 11 kV on primary / 66 kV on secondary). Since both sides have 10 MVA base, the reactance is already in PU on a common base.

**Step 4:** Draw the per-unit reactance diagram (all elements in series from source to fault bus):

```
G (j0.15) ---- T (j0.10) ---- L (j0.20) ---- F (Fault Bus)
```

**Step 5:** Compute the Thevenin impedance seen from the fault bus (all sources short-circuited, except for the generator EMF which is treated as 1.0 PU for computing fault current):

$$Z_{1,th} = jX_G + jX_T + jX_L = j(0.15 + 0.10 + 0.20) = j0.45 \text{ PU}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1:** Compute fault current in Per-Unit.

$$I_{f,3\phi} = \frac{E_a}{Z_{1,th}} = \frac{1.0 \angle 0°}{j0.45} = -j2.222 \text{ PU}$$

Magnitude: **|I<sub>f</sub>| = 2.222 PU**

**Step 2:** Compute the base current at the receiving end (66 kV side).

$$I_{base,66kV} = \frac{S_{base}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_{base}} = \frac{10 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 66 \times 10^3} = 87.47 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Convert PU fault current to actual Amperes.

$$I_{f,3\phi} = 2.222 \times 87.47 = 194.37 \text{ A} \approx 0.194 \text{ kA}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the Short Circuit MVA (also called Fault Level or Short Circuit Capacity).

$$\begin{aligned} SCC &= \sqrt{3} \times V_{L} \times I_f \\ &= \sqrt{3} \times 66 \times 10^3 \times 194.37 \\ &= 22.22 \times 10^6 \text{ VA} \\ &= 22.22 \text{ MVA} \end{aligned}$$

Alternatively, in PU: SCC<sub>PU</sub> = 1.0 / 0.45 = 2.222 PU → Actual = 2.222 × 10 MVA = **22.22 MVA**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]** For the power system described in Question A, with the following additional sequence reactance data:
- Generator: X<sub>0</sub> = 0.05 PU, X<sub>2</sub> = 0.15 PU
- Transformer: X<sub>0</sub> = X<sub>1</sub> = 0.10 PU
- Line: X<sub>0</sub> = 0.60 PU, X<sub>2</sub> = 0.20 PU

Calculate the fault current in PU and Amperes for:
**Part (a)** [7 Marks] A **Single Line-to-Ground (SLG)** fault on phase A.
**Part (b)** [7 Marks] A **Line-to-Line (L-L)** fault between phases B and C.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1:** Compute the Thevenin sequence impedances at the fault bus.

$$\begin{aligned} Z_{1,th} &= j(0.15 + 0.10 + 0.20) = j0.45 \text{ PU} \\ Z_{2,th} &= j(0.15 + 0.10 + 0.20) = j0.45 \text{ PU} \\ Z_{0,th} &= j(0.05 + 0.10 + 0.60) = j0.75 \text{ PU} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** For an SLG fault, all three sequence networks are connected in **series**. The total impedance is:

$$Z_{total} = Z_{1,th} + Z_{2,th} + Z_{0,th} = j(0.45 + 0.45 + 0.75) = j1.65 \text{ PU}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the positive sequence current.

$$I_{a1} = \frac{E_a}{Z_{total}} = \frac{1.0}{j1.65} = -j0.606 \text{ PU}$$

**Step 4:** The SLG fault current is three times the zero sequence current (since I<sub>a0</sub> = I<sub>a1</sub> = I<sub>a2</sub> for SLG).

$$I_{f,SLG} = 3 \times I_{a1} = 3 \times 0.606 = 1.818 \text{ PU}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to Amperes using base current of 87.47 A.

$$I_{f,SLG} = 1.818 \times 87.47 = 159.03 \text{ A}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1:** For an L-L fault, only positive and negative sequence networks are connected in **series** (zero sequence network is open). The total impedance is:

$$Z_{total} = Z_{1,th} + Z_{2,th} = j(0.45 + 0.45) = j0.90 \text{ PU}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the positive sequence current.

$$I_{a1} = \frac{E_a}{Z_{total}} = \frac{1.0}{j0.90} = -j1.111 \text{ PU}$$

**Step 3:** The line-to-line fault current magnitude is √3 times the positive sequence current (this accounts for the line-to-line conversion in the L-L boundary conditions).

$$I_{f,LL} = \sqrt{3} \times |I_{a1}| = \sqrt{3} \times 1.111 = 1.924 \text{ PU}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to Amperes.

$$I_{f,LL} = 1.924 \times 87.47 = 168.29 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5:** Verification and Comparison Table.

| Fault Type | Current (PU) | Current (A) | Severity Rank |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 3-Phase | 2.222 | 194.37 | **1st (Highest)** |
| SLG | 1.818 | 159.03 | 3rd |
| L-L | 1.924 | 168.29 | 2nd |
| DLG (compute) | 2.143 | 187.46 | 2nd |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use √3 factor for L-L fault current.** Many students write I<sub>f,LL</sub> = I<sub>a1</sub> = 1.111 PU. The correct answer is I<sub>f,LL</sub> = √3 × I<sub>a1</sub> = **1.924 PU**. Marks lost: ~2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up the SLG and DLG sequence network connections.** SLG uses all three in series; DLG uses Z<sub>1</sub> in series with (Z<sub>2</sub> || Z<sub>0</sub>). Marks lost: ~3-4 marks.
> 3. **Using rated values instead of base values for current conversion.** Always recompute I<sub>base</sub> at the fault bus voltage level, not at the source. Marks lost: ~2 marks.
> 4. **Neglecting the 'a' operator sign in the negative sequence component.** Marks lost: ~1-2 marks.
> 5. **Not labeling PU vs. actual quantities explicitly** in the final answer. Marks lost: ~1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **The Per-Unit System is mandatory.** Always start by selecting **S<sub>base</sub> = 100 MVA (or as given)** and **V<sub>base</sub> = rated kV** for each zone.

- **The 'a' operator:** $a = 1\angle 120° = -0.5 + j0.866$. Key identity: **1 + a + a² = 0**.

- **Three fault currents ranked by typical severity (in well-grounded systems):**
  1. **3-Phase (L-L-L):** $I_f = \frac{E_a}{Z_1}$ — **Reference fault** for breaker rating.
  2. **SLG (L-G):** $I_f = \frac{3 E_a}{Z_1+Z_2+Z_0}$ — **Can exceed 3-phase** if Z<sub>0</sub> is very small.
  3. **DLG (L-L-G):** $I_f = \frac{3 E_a Z_2}{Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1 Z_0 + Z_2 Z_0}$ — Complex formula.
  4. **L-L:** $I_f = \frac{\sqrt{3} E_a}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ — **Always less than 3-phase** when Z<sub>1</sub> = Z<sub>2</sub>.

- **Sequence Network Interconnections — MEMORIZE THIS TABLE:**
  - 3-Phase → Z<sub>1</sub> only
  - SLG → Z<sub>1</sub>, Z<sub>2</sub>, Z<sub>0</sub> **in series**
  - L-L → Z<sub>1</sub> and Z<sub>2</sub> **in series** (Z<sub>0</sub> open)
  - DLG → Z<sub>1</sub> in series with (Z<sub>2</sub> ∥ Z<sub>0</sub>)

- **Assume Z<sub>2</sub> = Z<sub>1</sub>** for static equipment (transformers, lines). For rotating machines, **X<sub>2</sub> ≈ X<sub>d</sub>''** (subtransient reactance).

- **Zero Sequence Current** flows **only when there is a return path through ground or neutral**. A transformer with **delta winding on the fault side blocks zero sequence current** (use Z<sub>0</sub> = ∞).

- **Short Circuit MVA (SCC)** is the most important practical metric:
  $$SCC = \sqrt{3} \times V_{base,kV} \times I_{sc,kA} \text{ in MVA}$$

- **Typical equipment %X values to remember:**
  - Turbo-alternator: 8%–15%
  - Hydro-alternator: 10%–20%
  - Power transformer: 5%–10%
  - Transmission line: varies with length

- **Pre-fault voltage assumption:** **E<sub>a</sub> = 1.0 ∠0° PU** at all buses (standard KTU assumption unless otherwise stated).

- **Units to use in final answer:** PU value + Actual Amperes (using I<sub>base</sub> at fault bus) + **MVA short circuit level**.

- **Lab report must contain:** Aim, Apparatus, Theory, Procedure, Observation Table, Sample Calculation, Result, Graphs (V<sub>f</sub> vs. fault type), Inference, and Viva Questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** "Per-Unit first, Thevenin next, Sequence Networks third, Boundary Conditions fourth, Conversion to Amps last." This 5-step mantra will solve 95% of short circuit problems in the KTU 2024 Scheme exam!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Transient Stability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — TRANSIENT STABILITY ANALYSIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transient Stability** is the ability of a power system to maintain synchronism when subjected to a **severe transient disturbance** (typically a short-circuit fault, sudden loss of generation, or line switching) and to reach an acceptable new operating equilibrium.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** laboratory manual, transient stability assessment is restricted to the analysis window of roughly **0 to 3–5 seconds** after the disturbance, during which the **rotor angle dynamics** of the synchronous machines dominate the system response.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Transient Stability of a Power System:**
> The property of a multi-machine synchronous system in which, following a large sudden disturbance (e.g., a bolted three-phase short-circuit on a transmission line), the relative rotor angles of all generators remain bounded and the system reaches a new steady-state operating point without losing synchronism.

**Key Boundary Conditions for Transient Stability Studies (KTU High-Yield):**

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Time window of interest | **0 to 3–5 s** post-fault |
| Disturbance type | Large, sudden (e.g., 3-φ fault, line outage) |
| Governing equation | **Swing Equation** (2nd order non-linear ODE) |
| Primary variable of interest | **Rotor angle δ** (electrical radians) |
| Secondary variable of interest | **Rotor angular speed ω** (rad/s) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Pedalling Cyclist on a Bridge**
> Imagine two cyclists pedalling in perfect sync across a narrow wooden bridge. They must keep the *same* pedalling rhythm. Suddenly, a strong gust of wind (the fault) hits the first cyclist and slows him down. He must now pedal **harder (accelerating power)** to catch up with the second cyclist. Once the wind stops (fault cleared), he has built up extra kinetic energy and may **overshoot** the synchronised position. If the gust was *brief* and he didn't pedal too aggressively, he stabilises. If the gust lasted *too long*, he falls off the bridge (loss of synchronism).
> - The **cyclists' positions relative to each other** = the **rotor angle δ**.
> - The **wind duration** = the **fault clearing time**.
> - The **maximum tolerable wind duration** = the **Critical Clearing Time (CCT)**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Synchronous speed** $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ where $f_s = 50$ Hz in India → $\omega_s = 314.159$ rad/s.
> - **Inertia Constant H** is typically **3.0 – 6.0 MW·s/MVA** for modern turbo-generators and **2.0 – 4.5 MW·s/MVA** for hydro units.
> - **d-axis transient reactance** $X'_d$ is used (not $X_d$) because the sub-transient period (first few cycles) has already decayed.
> - **Inertia M** in per-unit: $M = \dfrac{2H}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{H}{\pi f_s}$ s²/elect.rad.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Classical Single-Machine Infinite-Bus (SMIB) Model

The lab experiment typically models a generator connected through a step-up transformer and a double-circuit transmission line to an **infinite bus** (a system so large its voltage and frequency are unaffected by the events on the study generator).

**Pre-fault (steady state):** Power transfer is

$$P_e^{pre} = \frac{E'_q \, V_\infty}{X_1} \sin \delta = P_{max1} \sin \delta$$

**During the fault:** Power transfer becomes

$$P_e^{f} = \frac{E'_q \, V_\infty}{X_2} \sin \delta = P_{max2} \sin \delta \quad (P_{max2} < P_{max1})$$

**Post-fault (after line tripping):** Power transfer recovers to

$$P_e^{post} = \frac{E'_q \, V_\infty}{X_3} \sin \delta = P_{max3} \sin \delta$$

where typically $P_{max2} < P_{max1} \le P_{max3}$ depending on which line is tripped for fault clearance.

> [!NOTE]
> In the **most severe (worst) case** — a **3-φ bolted fault at the generator terminals** — $P_{max2} = 0$ during the fault, i.e. $P_e^{f} = 0$.

### 2.2 The Swing Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **swing equation** is the mechanical analogy of Newton's second law applied to a rotating mass. It is the cornerstone of every transient stability problem.

**Step 1 — Newton's law for rotation:**
Mechanical acceleration of the rotor equals the **net accelerating torque** $T_a$ acting on it.

$$J \frac{d^2 \theta_m}{dt^2} = T_m - T_e = T_a$$

Here $J$ is the moment of inertia of the rotating mass, $\theta_m$ is the mechanical angle, $T_m$ is the mechanical (shaft) torque and $T_e$ is the electrical (counter) torque.

**Step 2 — Convert mechanical to electrical angle:**
Electrical angle is related to mechanical by the number of pole pairs $p$:

$$\theta_m = \frac{2}{p}\delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^2 \theta_m}{dt^2} = \frac{2}{p}\frac{d^2 \delta}{dt^2}$$

**Step 3 — Convert torques to per-unit powers:**
In per-unit, $P_m = T_m$ and $P_e = T_e$ when base quantities are chosen such that $\omega_{base} = \omega_s$ (synchronous speed). Also, $P_m^{pu} = P_{m,MW} / S_{base}$.

**Step 4 — Introduce inertia constant H:**

> The **inertia constant H** is the stored kinetic energy at synchronous speed, normalised by the MVA rating.
> $H = \frac{1}{2} J \omega_s^2 \,/\, S_{base}$ in MW·s/MVA

**Step 5 — Assemble the swing equation in per unit:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{2H}{\omega_s}\frac{d^2 \delta}{dt^2} = P_m - P_e\;}$$

**Step 6 — State-space (first-order) form for numerical integration:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d\delta}{dt} = \Delta \omega = \omega - \omega_s\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;M \frac{d(\Delta \omega)}{dt} = P_m - P_e\;}$$

with $M = 2H/\omega_s$ (in seconds²/elect.rad). This pair of first-order ODEs is what is solved numerically step-by-step.

### 2.3 The Equal Area Criterion (EAC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Equal-Area Criterion** is a *graphical* method that determines, *without solving the swing equation numerically*, the maximum permissible clearing angle. It is the **single most frequently tested KTU concept** in this experiment.

**Logic in plain English:**

1. When $P_m > P_e$, the rotor **accelerates** and stores kinetic energy → positive accelerating area $A_1$.
2. After fault clearance, when $P_e > P_m$, the rotor **decelerates** → the kinetic energy is returned → decelerating area $A_2$.
3. For the system to be *just stable*, all kinetic energy gained must be returned before the rotor reaches $\delta_{max} = \pi - \delta_0$.
4. Therefore, for **stability**: $A_1 \le A_2$. The limiting case $A_1 = A_2$ defines the **critical clearing angle** $\delta_c$.

**Formulas:**

$$\boxed{\;A_1 = \int_{\delta_0}^{\delta_c}\!\big(P_m - P_e^{\text{during}}\big)\,d\delta\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;A_2 = \int_{\delta_c}^{\delta_{max}}\!\big(P_e^{\text{post}} - P_m\big)\,d\delta\;}$$

**Closed-form expression for the critical clearing angle (most-tested KTU formula):**

$$\boxed{\;\cos \delta_c = \frac{P_m\,(\pi - 2\delta_0) + P_{max3}\cos\delta_0}{P_{max3}}\;}$$

This assumes a 3-φ fault at the terminals (so $P_e^{\text{during}} = 0$) and the post-fault angle limit $\delta_{max} = \pi - \delta_0$.

**Critical clearing time:** Obtained by numerical integration of the swing equation from $\delta_0$ until $\delta$ first reaches $\delta_c$. The elapsed time is the **CCT**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ | rad/s, $f_s = 50$ Hz |
| 2 | Inertia constant | $H = \frac{1}{2}J\omega_s^2 / S_{base}$ | MW·s/MVA |
| 3 | Inertia coefficient | $M = 2H/\omega_s$ | s²/elect.rad |
| 4 | Swing equation | $\frac{2H}{\omega_s}\dfrac{d^2\delta}{dt^2} = P_m - P_e$ | δ in elec. rad |
| 5 | Power-angle eq. | $P_e = P_{max}\sin\delta = \frac{E' V_\infty}{X_{eq}}\sin\delta$ | p.u. power |
| 6 | Accelerating area | $A_1 = P_m(\delta_c - \delta_0)$ when $P_e^{f}=0$ | p.u.·rad |
| 7 | Decelerating area | $A_2 = P_{max}(\cos\delta_c + \cos\delta_0) - P_m(\pi - \delta_0 - \delta_c)$ | p.u.·rad |
| 8 | Critical clearing angle | $\cos\delta_c = \dfrac{P_m(\pi - 2\delta_0) + P_{max3}\cos\delta_0}{P_{max3}}$ | rad |
| 9 | Rotor speed deviation | $\dfrac{d\delta}{dt} = \omega - \omega_s$ | rad/s |
| 10 | EAC stability | $A_1 = A_2$ at $\delta_c$ (just-stable case) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** In control rooms of utilities like KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board), EAC is implemented inside **Energy Management Systems (EMS)** and **phasor-measurement-unit (PMU)** based real-time stability assessment tools (e.g., WAMS — Wide Area Measurement Systems). Operators use it to decide **load-shedding** or **fast-valving** when CCT is breached.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Swing Equation

**Step 0 — Initial physical setup.** A synchronous generator delivers power through a network to a load. The mechanical input power comes from the prime-mover (turbine) and the electrical output power is delivered at the terminals.

**Step 1 — Newton's 2nd law for rotation (in SI units):**

$$J \frac{d^2 \theta_m}{dt^2} = T_a = T_m - T_e$$

The accelerating torque $T_a$ is the difference between mechanical and electrical torques. The inertia of the rotating parts (rotor + turbine + exciter) is $J$ in kg·m².

**Step 2 — Replace $T_m$ and $T_e$ by their per-unit counterparts using the power-torque-speed relation** $P = T \cdot \omega$. On the per-unit system with $\omega_{base} = \omega_s$:

$$T_m = \frac{P_m}{\omega_s}, \qquad T_e = \frac{P_e}{\omega_s}$$

in the chosen system of units (both P and ω in p.u.). Substituting:

$$J \frac{d^2 \theta_m}{dt^2} = \frac{P_m - P_e}{\omega_s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert mechanical angle to electrical angle.** For a machine with $p$ pole-pairs, $\theta_m = \frac{2}{p}\delta$, hence:

$$\frac{d^2 \theta_m}{dt^2} = \frac{2}{p}\frac{d^2 \delta}{dt^2}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the inertia constant H** by the identity $\frac{1}{2}J\omega_s^2 = H \cdot S_{base}$, i.e. $J = 2H S_{base} / \omega_s^2$.

Substituting into Step 2:

$$\frac{2H S_{base}}{\omega_s^2} \cdot \frac{2}{p}\frac{d^2 \delta}{dt^2} = \frac{P_m - P_e}{\omega_s}$$

Multiplying both sides by $\omega_s / S_{base}$ and converting $P$ into per-unit ($P^{pu} = P/S_{base}$):

$$\frac{4H}{p\,\omega_s}\frac{d^2 \delta}{dt^2} = P_m^{pu} - P_e^{pu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: The factor of $4/p$ arises from the $\theta_m$ vs $\delta$ conversion. For a *two-pole* machine ($p=1$) it reduces to $4H/\omega_s$, but the standard per-unit form uses the relationship $\delta$ in **electrical radians** measured in a rotating reference frame at $\omega_s$, leading to the classical form.

**Step 5 — The classical swing equation in per-unit:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{2H}{\omega_s}\frac{d^2 \delta}{dt^2} = P_m - P_e\;}$$

(All powers are in per-unit; $\delta$ is in electrical radians; $t$ in seconds; $H$ in MW·s/MVA.)

**Step 6 — State-space reformulation.** Define speed deviation $\Delta \omega = \omega - \omega_s$. Then:

$$\frac{d\delta}{dt} = \Delta \omega$$
$$\frac{d(\Delta \omega)}{dt} = \frac{\omega_s}{2H}(P_m - P_e)$$

This pair is what gets integrated numerically.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Critical Clearing Angle (Equal-Area Criterion)

**Assumed disturbance:** A three-phase bolted fault occurs at the generator terminal at $t=0$, the line is tripped (cleared) at $t = t_c$, and the post-fault system has reactance $X_3$ (giving $P_{max3}$). The initial operating angle is $\delta_0 = \sin^{-1}(P_m/P_{max1})$.

**Step 1 — Compute the accelerating area $A_1$.** During the fault the electrical power delivered is zero, so:

$$A_1 = \int_{\delta_0}^{\delta_c}\!P_m\,d\delta = P_m (\delta_c - \delta_0)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the decelerating area $A_2$.** After the fault is cleared, $P_e = P_{max3}\sin\delta$. The rotor decelerates from $\delta_c$ to $\delta_{max} = \pi - \delta_0$ (the limit of the stable half-cycle):

$$A_2 = \int_{\delta_c}^{\pi-\delta_0}\!\big(P_{max3}\sin\delta - P_m\big)\,d\delta$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$A_2 = P_{max3}\Big[-\cos\delta\Big]_{\delta_c}^{\pi-\delta_0} - P_m\Big[\delta\Big]_{\delta_c}^{\pi-\delta_0}$$

$$A_2 = P_{max3}\big(\cos\delta_c - \cos(\pi-\delta_0)\big) - P_m\big(\pi - \delta_0 - \delta_c\big)$$

Using $\cos(\pi - \delta_0) = -\cos\delta_0$:

$$A_2 = P_{max3}(\cos\delta_c + \cos\delta_0) - P_m(\pi - \delta_0 - \delta_c)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the just-stable condition $A_1 = A_2$:**

$$P_m (\delta_c - \delta_0) = P_{max3}(\cos\delta_c + \cos\delta_0) - P_m(\pi - \delta_0 - \delta_c)$$

**Step 4 — Expand and collect terms in $\delta_c$:**

$$P_m\delta_c - P_m\delta_0 = P_{max3}\cos\delta_c + P_{max3}\cos\delta_0 - P_m\pi + P_m\delta_0 + P_m\delta_c$$

Cancel $P_m\delta_c$ on both sides and move terms:

$$-P_m\delta_0 = P_{max3}\cos\delta_c + P_{max3}\cos\delta_0 - P_m\pi + P_m\delta_0$$

$$-2P_m\delta_0 + P_m\pi = P_{max3}\cos\delta_c + P_{max3}\cos\delta_0$$

$$P_m(\pi - 2\delta_0) + P_{max3}\cos\delta_0 = P_{max3}\cos\delta_c$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form expression for $\delta_c$:**

$$\boxed{\;\cos\delta_c = \frac{P_m(\pi - 2\delta_0)}{P_{max3}} + \cos\delta_0\;}$$

Equivalently:

$$\cos\delta_c = \frac{P_m(\pi - 2\delta_0) + P_{max3}\cos\delta_0}{P_{max3}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the formula:** As $P_m$ increases, the numerator decreases (because $\pi - 2\delta_0$ shrinks as $\delta_0$ rises), so $\delta_c$ falls. The system is more vulnerable to delayed clearing when heavily loaded.

### 3.3 Numerical Solution — Modified Euler's Method (KTC-Standard Lab Procedure)

The swing equation is a 2nd-order non-linear ODE, closed-form solution is **impossible in general**. Numerical integration proceeds as follows.

**Algorithm Steps:**

**Step 0 — Initialise:** $t=0$, $\delta(0) = \delta_0$, $\Delta\omega(0) = 0$.

**Step 1 — Compute accelerating power** at the current angle:

$$P_a^{(k)} = P_m - P_e(\delta^{(k)}) = P_m - P_{max}\sin\delta^{(k)}$$

**Step 2 — Predictor step** (forward Euler):

$$\delta^{(k+1)}_{p} = \delta^{(k)} + \Delta t \cdot \Delta\omega^{(k)}$$

$$\Delta\omega^{(k+1)}_{p} = \Delta\omega^{(k)} + \Delta t \cdot \frac{\omega_s}{2H}\,P_a^{(k)}$$

**Step 3 — Corrector step** (using predicted state to refine the slope):

$$P_a^{(k+1)} = P_m - P_{max}\sin\delta^{(k+1)}_{p}$$

$$\delta^{(k+1)} = \delta^{(k)} + \frac{\Delta t}{2}\Big(\Delta\omega^{(k)} + \Delta\omega^{(k+1)}_{p}\Big)$$

$$\Delta\omega^{(k+1)} = \Delta\omega^{(k)} + \frac{\Delta t}{2}\frac{\omega_s}{2H}\Big(P_a^{(k)} + P_a^{(k+1)}\Big)$$

**Step 4 — Time update:** $t = t + \Delta t$, increment $k$ and return to Step 1.

**Step 5 — Stopping criterion for the lab experiment:** Stop when either (a) $\delta$ exceeds $\pi - \delta_0$ → *unstable*, or (b) the rotor reaches the maximum and starts decreasing (i.e. $\Delta\omega$ changes sign from positive to negative) → *stable*.

**Step 6 — Critical clearing time:** Find the smallest $t_c$ such that $\delta(t_c) = \delta_c$. Vary $t_c$ iteratively (e.g., bisection method) and integrate for each trial value.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Sample Lab-Style Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Data:** A 50 Hz, 2-pole, 200 MVA machine with $H = 5$ MW·s/MVA delivers $P_m = 0.8$ p.u. to a system where $P_{max1} = 1.8$ p.u., $P_{max3} = 1.5$ p.u. A 3-φ fault occurs at the terminals.

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed:**

$$\omega_s = 2\pi f_s = 2\pi(50) = 314.159\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Initial angle:**

$$\delta_0 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{P_m}{P_{max1}}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.8}{1.8}\right) = 0.4606\ \text{rad}\ (26.39°)$$

**Step 3 — Critical clearing angle:**

$$\cos\delta_c = \frac{P_m(\pi - 2\delta_0)}{P_{max3}} + \cos\delta_0 = \frac{0.8(\pi - 2(0.4606))}{1.5} + \cos(0.4606)$$

$$\cos\delta_c = \frac{0.8(3.1416 - 0.9212)}{1.5} + 0.8910 = \frac{0.8(2.2204)}{1.5} + 0.8910 = \frac{1.7763}{1.5} + 0.8910$$

$$\cos\delta_c = 1.1842 + 0.8910 = 2.0752$$

> [!WARNING]
> The result **exceeds 1.0**, which is *physically impossible*! This means that **even an instantaneous fault clearing will not save the system** under the given loading. The loading must be reduced or $P_{max3}$ increased for any stability margin to exist.

**Interpretation for the lab report:** Students must comment on the **Transient Stability Limit** — the maximum mechanical power input $P_{m,crit}$ for which a critical clearing angle still exists. Setting $\cos\delta_c = 1$:

$$1 = \frac{P_{m,crit}(\pi - 2\delta_0)}{P_{max3}} + \cos\delta_0 \Rightarrow P_{m,crit} = \frac{P_{max3}(1 - \cos\delta_0)}{\pi - 2\delta_0}$$

For our data: $P_{m,crit} = 1.5(1 - 0.8910)/2.2204 = 0.0736$ p.u. The system is heavily over-loaded.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Transient Stability Study

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **end-to-end analytical procedure** used in the lab, from data input to stability verdict.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: System Data E, V, X1, X2, X3, H, Pm, dt] --> B[Compute Synchronous Speed Ws]
    B --> C[Compute Inertia M = 2H per Ws]
    C --> D[Compute Initial Angle D0 = arcsin Pm by Pmax1]
    D --> E[Apply Equal Area Criterion]
    E --> F[Compute Critical Clearing Angle Dc]
    F --> G[Set Trial Clearing Time Tc]
    G --> H[Numerical Integration of Swing Eqn]
    H --> I{Delta Exceeds Pi Minus D0}
    I -- Yes --> J[VERDICT: UNSTABLE Loss of Synchronism]
    I -- No --> K{Delta Reaches Dc and Deltaw Reverses}
    K -- Yes --> L[VERDICT: STABLE for this Tc]
    K -- No --> M[Continue Integration Next Tc]
    M --> G
    L --> N[Find CCT via Bisection on Tc]
    N --> O[END: Report CCT, Dc, Swing Curve]
```

### 4.2 System Model — Single-Machine Infinite-Bus with Fault

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEN[Generator Side]
        G1[EMF behind Xd prime: Eq prime] --> T1[Step Up Transformer Xtr]
    end
    T1 --> L1[Line 1 Xline1]
    T1 --> L2[Line 2 Xline2]
    L1 --> BUS[Infinite Bus V infinity]
    L2 --> BUS
    F3[Three Phase Fault Switch] -.->|during fault| T1
    F3 -.->|cleared at t = tc| OFF[Line 1 Tripped]
```

### 4.3 Power-Angle Curve Showing the Three Operating Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Power Pe p.u.] --> P1[Pre Fault Pmax1 sin delta]
    P --> P2[During Fault Pe = 0]
    P --> P3[Post Fault Pmax3 sin delta]
    P1 --> A1[Region I: Stable Operating Angle D0]
    P2 --> A2[Region II: Accelerating Area A1]
    P3 --> A3[Region III: Decelerating Area A2]
    A2 --> CRT[Critical Clearing Angle Dc]
    A3 --> CRT
    CRT --> CCT[Critical Clearing Time via Numerical Integration]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in your lab record:**
> - The first flowchart is the *procedure* — paste it in the "Algorithm" section.
> - The second shows the *physical system model* — paste it in the "System Description" section.
> - The third illustrates the *equal-area visualisation* — the two shaded regions (A1 above the Pm line, A2 below Pm and above Pmax3 sin δ) are *equal* at the critical clearing point.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B, Q.1(a)]**
> **Q1.** *Define transient stability of a power system. How does it differ from steady-state and dynamic stability?*

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU-mapped key points):**

Transient stability is the ability of a power system to maintain synchronism after a *large, sudden disturbance* (e.g., a three-phase short-circuit). The analysis window is typically **0 to 3–5 s**, and the governing dynamics are described by the **swing equation**. Distinctions (1 mark each):

| Stability Type | Disturbance | Time Window | Governing Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steady-state** | Small, gradual load change | Seconds to minutes | Slow power-angle adjustment |
| **Dynamic** | Small, sudden (e.g., 1 % step) | 5–30 s | Includes governor/AVR action |
| **Transient** | Large, sudden (fault, outage) | 0–5 s | Pure rotor-angle (swing) dynamics |

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Q.1(b)]**
> **Q2.** *State the swing equation of a synchronous machine and explain the physical meaning of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\frac{2H}{\omega_s}\frac{d^2\delta}{dt^2} = P_m - P_e$$

- **$H$** (MW·s/MVA) = inertia constant, the stored kinetic energy at synchronous speed per unit of MVA rating.
- **$\omega_s$** (rad/s) = synchronous electrical angular speed = $2\pi f_s$.
- **$\delta$** (rad) = electrical rotor angle relative to a synchronously rotating reference.
- **$P_m$** (p.u.) = mechanical power input from the prime-mover.
- **$P_e$** (p.u.) = electrical air-gap power output.
- **$P_m - P_e$** = accelerating power, which is the *cause* of rotor angular acceleration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention that the swing equation is a **second-order non-linear ODE** because $P_e = f(\sin\delta)$. Examiners specifically look for the word *"non-linear"* and the identification of $P_e$ as a sinusoidal function of $\delta$ to award the third mark.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q.4, Module 2, 14 marks]**
>
> **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] A 50 Hz, 4-pole, 500 MVA turbo-generator with $H = 4.5$ MW·s/MVA delivers power to an infinite bus through a double-circuit line. Pre-fault: $X_1 = 0.4$ p.u., $P_{max1} = 1.5$ p.u. A three-phase fault occurs at the mid-point of one line; during-fault: $X_2 = 1.0$ p.u., $P_{max2} = 0.6$ p.u. Post-fault: one line is tripped, $X_3 = 0.6$ p.u., $P_{max3} = 1.0$ p.u. Mechanical input $P_m = 0.9$ p.u. Determine:
> 1. The initial operating angle $\delta_0$.
> 2. The critical clearing angle $\delta_c$ using the **modified equal-area criterion** (the fault is *not* at the terminal, so $P_e^{during} = P_{max2}\sin\delta$).
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] If the fault is cleared exactly at $\delta_c$, sketch the swing curve and identify whether the system is stable. Using Modified Euler's method with $\Delta t = 0.05$ s, compute the first two time-steps of $\delta$ and $\Delta\omega$ and comment on the trend.

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

##### Part (a) — Analytical Solution

**Step 1 — Initial angle (2 marks):**

Pre-fault power-angle relationship: $P_e = 1.5\sin\delta_0 = P_m = 0.9$ p.u.

$$\delta_0 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.9}{1.5}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.6) = 0.6435\ \text{rad}\ (36.87°)$$

**Step 2 — Equal-Area Criterion with a non-zero $P_e$ during fault (5 marks):**

Accelerating area $A_1$ (from $\delta_0$ to $\delta_c$, with $P_e^{during} = 0.6\sin\delta$):

$$A_1 = \int_{0.6435}^{\delta_c}\!\big(0.9 - 0.6\sin\delta\big)\,d\delta = 0.9(\delta_c - 0.6435) + 0.6(\cos\delta_c - 0.6)$$

(Using $\int\sin\delta\,d\delta = -\cos\delta$.)

Decelerating area $A_2$ (from $\delta_c$ to $\delta_{max} = \pi - \delta_0 = 2.4981$ rad):

$$A_2 = \int_{\delta_c}^{2.4981}\!\big(1.0\sin\delta - 0.9\big)\,d\delta$$

$$A_2 = -\cos\delta \Big\vert_{\delta_c}^{2.4981} - 0.9(2.4981 - \delta_c)$$

$$A_2 = (\cos\delta_c - \cos 2.4981) - 0.9(2.4981 - \delta_c)$$

With $\cos 2.4981 = -\cos 0.6435 = -0.8$:

$$A_2 = (\cos\delta_c + 0.8) - 0.9(2.4981 - \delta_c)$$

**Step 3 — Setting $A_1 = A_2$ (the heart of EAC, 2 marks for the algebraic substitution):**

$$0.9(\delta_c - 0.6435) + 0.6(\cos\delta_c - 0.6) = (\cos\delta_c + 0.8) - 0.9(2.4981 - \delta_c)$$

Expanding both sides:

$$0.9\delta_c - 0.5792 + 0.6\cos\delta_c - 0.36 = \cos\delta_c + 0.8 - 2.2483 + 0.9\delta_c$$

$$0.9\delta_c - 0.9392 + 0.6\cos\delta_c = 0.9\delta_c - 1.4483 + \cos\delta_c$$

$$-0.9392 + 0.6\cos\delta_c = -1.4483 + \cos\delta_c$$

$$-0.4\cos\delta_c = -0.5091$$

$$\boxed{\;\cos\delta_c = 1.2728\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical examination result:** $\cos\delta_c > 1$ is *impossible*. **Conclusion:** Under these operating conditions, the system is **unconditionally unstable** — no value of clearing angle can save it. To restore stability, one of the following is required: (i) reduce $P_m$ below the new transient stability limit, (ii) increase $P_{max3}$ (e.g., add a parallel line), (iii) use **braking resistors** or **fast valving**.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Integration

**Setup:** $H = 4.5$ MW·s/MVA, $\omega_s = 314.159$ rad/s. Therefore:

$$M = \frac{2H}{\omega_s} = \frac{2(4.5)}{314.159} = 0.02865\ \text{s}^2/\text{rad}$$

**Step 0 — Initial conditions:** $t = 0$, $\delta^{(0)} = 0.6435$ rad, $\Delta\omega^{(0)} = 0$.

**Step 1 — First time step, $k=0$ (2 marks):**

Accelerating power (during fault):

$$P_a^{(0)} = 0.9 - 0.6\sin(0.6435) = 0.9 - 0.6(0.6) = 0.9 - 0.36 = 0.54\ \text{p.u.}$$

Predictor:

$$\delta^{(1)}_p = 0.6435 + (0.05)(0) = 0.6435\ \text{rad}$$

$$\Delta\omega^{(1)}_p = 0 + (0.05)\frac{0.54}{0.02865} = 0.05(18.85) = 0.9424\ \text{rad/s}$$

New accelerating power at predicted angle (during fault unchanged):

$$P_a^{(1)} = 0.9 - 0.6\sin(0.6435) = 0.54\ \text{p.u.}$$

Corrector:

$$\delta^{(1)} = 0.6435 + \frac{0.05}{2}(0 + 0.9424) = 0.6435 + 0.02356 = 0.6671\ \text{rad}$$

$$\Delta\omega^{(1)} = 0 + \frac{0.05}{2}\frac{0.54 + 0.54}{0.02865} = 0.025 \times 37.696 = 0.9424\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Second time step, $k=1$ (2 marks):**

$$P_a^{(1)} = 0.9 - 0.6\sin(0.6671) = 0.9 - 0.6(0.6180) = 0.9 - 0.3708 = 0.5292\ \text{p.u.}$$

Predictor:

$$\delta^{(2)}_p = 0.6671 + (0.05)(0.9424) = 0.6671 + 0.04712 = 0.7142\ \text{rad}$$

$$\Delta\omega^{(2)}_p = 0.9424 + (0.05)\frac{0.5292}{0.02865} = 0.9424 + 0.9236 = 1.8660\ \text{rad/s}$$

Predictor-stage accelerating power:

$$P_a^{(2)}_p = 0.9 - 0.6\sin(0.7142) = 0.9 - 0.6(0.6547) = 0.9 - 0.3928 = 0.5072\ \text{p.u.}$$

Corrector:

$$\delta^{(2)} = 0.6671 + \frac{0.05}{2}(0.9424 + 1.8660) = 0.6671 + 0.0702 = 0.7373\ \text{rad}$$

$$\Delta\omega^{(2)} = 0.9424 + \frac{0.05}{2}\frac{0.5292 + 0.5072}{0.02865} = 0.9424 + 0.9035 = 1.8459\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Trend comment (3 marks):** The rotor angle $\delta$ is **monotonically increasing** ($\delta^{(0)} = 0.6435 \to \delta^{(1)} = 0.6671 \to \delta^{(2)} = 0.7373$) and the angular velocity deviation $\Delta\omega$ is **monotonically positive** (0 → 0.9424 → 1.8459 rad/s). Both are unidirectional with no sign reversal. The **fault has not been cleared yet** in this simulation, so the rotor continues to accelerate. The system is on a *trajectory of loss of synchronism* unless the fault is cleared before $\delta$ exceeds the (impossible, per part a) critical value.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B, 7-mark sub-parts):**
> 1. **Forgetting the corrector step** in Modified Euler's method — students often write only the predictor, losing **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Using $H$ instead of $M = 2H/\omega_s$** in the acceleration term $P_a/M$ is a *very common* mistake. Examiners deduct a full mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Sign of $\Delta\omega$** — a *positive* value means the rotor is **ahead** of the synchronous reference, not "ahead of the bus" in the engineering sense. State it as: *"$\delta$ is increasing at $X$ rad/s"*.
> 4. **Units of $M$:** If $H$ is in MJ/MVA and $\omega_s$ in rad/s, then $M$ has units of s²/rad — mention this in the answer.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2022, Q.5, Module 2, 14 marks]**
>
> **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a neat power-angle diagram, explain the **Equal-Area Criterion** for transient stability assessment. Derive the expression for the critical clearing angle of a SMIB system subjected to a sudden three-phase fault at the generator terminals.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] For the system of Question A, if the breaker operates at $t_c = 0.20$ s, compute the swing angle $\delta$ at $t = 0.25$ s using **RK-2 (Heun's) method** with step size $\Delta t = 0.05$ s. Use $H = 4.5$ MW·s/MVA, $\delta_0 = 0.6435$ rad, $P_{max1} = 1.5$ p.u., $P_{max2} = 0.6$ p.u., $P_{max3} = 1.0$ p.u., $P_m = 0.9$ p.u., $\omega_s = 314.159$ rad/s.

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

##### Part (a) — Conceptual + Derivation

**Power-Angle Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The diagram has three sinusoidal curves:
- $P_e^{pre} = P_{max1}\sin\delta$ (pre-fault, valid for $t < 0$)
- $P_e^{f} = P_{max2}\sin\delta$ (during fault, $0 \le t < t_c$)
- $P_e^{post} = P_{max3}\sin\delta$ (post-fault, $t \ge t_c$)

The horizontal line $P = P_m$ intersects the pre-fault curve at $\delta_0$ and the post-fault curve at $\delta_s = \pi - \delta_0$.

**Equal-Area Logic (2 marks):**

During the fault, $P_e^{f} < P_m$ → accelerating area $A_1$ (shaded region between $P_m$ line and the $P_{max2}\sin\delta$ curve from $\delta_0$ to $\delta_c$). After clearance, $P_e^{post} > P_m$ for $\delta_0 < \delta < \pi - \delta_0$ → decelerating area $A_2$ (shaded region between $P_{max3}\sin\delta$ curve and the $P_m$ line from $\delta_c$ to $\pi - \delta_0$). The criterion states: *the system is just-stable when $A_1 = A_2$.*

**Derivation of $\delta_c$ (2 marks — for the 3-φ terminal fault case):**

The derivation is identical to Section 3.2 above, but presented independently here:

$$P_m(\delta_c - \delta_0) = P_{max3}(\cos\delta_c + \cos\delta_0) - P_m(\pi - \delta_0 - \delta_c)$$

Solving for $\cos\delta_c$:

$$\boxed{\;\cos\delta_c = \frac{P_m(\pi - 2\delta_0)}{P_{max3}} + \cos\delta_0\;}$$

##### Part (b) — RK-2 (Heun's) Numerical Integration

**Constants:** $M = 2H/\omega_s = 2(4.5)/314.159 = 0.02865$ s²/rad.

**Initial state:** $t = 0$, $\delta = 0.6435$ rad, $\Delta\omega = 0$.

**Stage 1 — $t = 0$ to $0.05$ s (DURING FAULT — $P_{max2} = 0.6$):**

$k_1$ for $\delta$: $\omega_0 = 0$, so slope $= 0$.
$k_1$ for $\omega$: $\frac{0.9 - 0.6\sin(0.6435)}{0.02865} = \frac{0.9 - 0.36}{0.02865} = 18.847$ rad/s².

Predictor:

$$\delta_{p} = 0.6435 + 0.05(0) = 0.6435$$
$$\omega_{p} = 0 + 0.05(18.847) = 0.9424\ \text{rad/s}$$

$k_2$ for $\delta$: slope at predicted state = $\omega_p = 0.9424$ rad/s.
$k_2$ for $\omega$: $\frac{0.9 - 0.6\sin(0.6435)}{0.02865} = 18.847$ rad/s² (same as $\delta$ unchanged).

Corrector (Heun's averaging):

$$\delta(0.05) = 0.6435 + \frac{0.05}{2}(0 + 0.9424) = 0.6671\ \text{rad}$$

$$\Delta\omega(0.05) = 0 + \frac{0.05}{2}(18.847 + 18.847) = 0.9424\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Stage 2 — $t = 0.05$ to $0.10$ s (DURING FAULT):**

$k_1$ for $\delta$: $0.9424$.
$k_1$ for $\omega$: $\frac{0.9 - 0.6\sin(0.6671)}{0.02865} = \frac{0.9 - 0.3708}{0.02865} = 18.474$ rad/s².

Predictor:

$$\delta_{p} = 0.6671 + 0.05(0.9424) = 0.7142\ \text{rad}$$
$$\omega_{p} = 0.9424 + 0.05(18.474) = 1.8661\ \text{rad/s}$$

Corrector (still in fault):

$$\delta(0.10) = 0.6671 + \frac{0.05}{2}(0.9424 + 1.8661) = 0.6671 + 0.0702 = 0.7373\ \text{rad}$$

$$\Delta\omega(0.10) = 0.9424 + \frac{0.05}{2}(18.474 + 18.474) = 0.9424 + 0.9236 = 1.8660\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Stage 3 — $t = 0.10$ to $0.15$ s (DURING FAULT):**

$k_1$ for $\delta$: $1.8660$.
$k_1$ for $\omega$: $\frac{0.9 - 0.6\sin(0.7373)}{0.02865} = \frac{0.9 - 0.6(0.6724)}{0.02865} = \frac{0.9 - 0.4034}{0.02865} = 17.336$ rad/s².

Predictor:

$$\delta_{p} = 0.7373 + 0.05(1.8660) = 0.8306\ \text{rad}$$
$$\omega_{p} = 1.8660 + 0.05(17.336) = 2.7328\ \text{rad/s}$$

Corrector (still in fault):

$$\delta(0.15) = 0.7373 + \frac{0.05}{2}(1.8660 + 2.7328) = 0.7373 + 0.1150 = 0.8523\ \text{rad}$$

$$\Delta\omega(0.15) = 1.8660 + \frac{0.05}{2}(17.336 + 17.336) = 1.8660 + 0.8668 = 2.7328\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Stage 4 — $t = 0.15$ to $0.20$ s (DURING FAULT, last stage of fault):**

$k_1$ for $\delta$: $2.7328$.
$k_1$ for $\omega$: $\frac{0.9 - 0.6\sin(0.8523)}{0.02865} = \frac{0.9 - 0.6(0.7518)}{0.02865} = \frac{0.9 - 0.4511}{0.02865} = 15.676$ rad/s².

Predictor:

$$\delta_{p} = 0.8523 + 0.05(2.7328) = 0.9889\ \text{rad}$$
$$\omega_{p} = 2.7328 + 0.05(15.676) = 3.5166\ \text{rad/s}$$

Corrector (still in fault):

$$\delta(0.20) = 0.8523 + \frac{0.05}{2}(2.7328 + 3.5166) = 0.8523 + 0.1562 = 1.0085\ \text{rad}$$

$$\Delta\omega(0.20) = 2.7328 + \frac{0.05}{2}(15.676 + 15.676) = 2.7328 + 0.7838 = 3.5166\ \text{rad/s}$$

**FAULT IS CLEARED AT $t_c = 0.20$ s. Post-fault curve applies: $P_{max3} = 1.0$.**

**Stage 5 — $t = 0.20$ to $0.25$ s (POST-FAULT, the requested final step):**

$k_1$ for $\delta$: $3.5166$.
$k_1$ for $\omega$: $\frac{0.9 - 1.0\sin(1.0085)}{0.02865} = \frac{0.9 - 0.8457}{0.02865} = \frac{0.0543}{0.02865} = 1.895$ rad/s².

Predictor:

$$\delta_{p} = 1.0085 + 0.05(3.5166) = 1.1844\ \text{rad}$$
$$\omega_{p} = 3.5166 + 0.05(1.895) = 3.6114\ \text{rad/s}$$

Corrector (post-fault):

$k_2$ for $\delta$ at $\delta_p$: $3.6114$.
$k_2$ for $\omega$ at $\delta_p$: $\frac{0.9 - 1.0\sin(1.1844)}{0.02865} = \frac{0.9 - 0.9268}{0.02865} = \frac{-0.0268}{0.02865} = -0.936$ rad/s² (deceleration!).

Corrector step:

$$\delta(0.25) = 1.0085 + \frac{0.05}{2}(3.5166 + 3.6114) = 1.0085 + 0.1782 = 1.1867\ \text{rad}\ (67.98°)$$

$$\Delta\omega(0.25) = 3.5166 + \frac{0.05}{2}(1.895 + (-0.936)) = 3.5166 + 0.0240 = 3.5406\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Final Answer for Q.4(b):**

$$\boxed{\;\delta(t=0.25\,\text{s}) = 1.1867\ \text{rad}\;(67.98°)\;}$$

**Stability comment (1 mark):** $\delta$ is still increasing (1.1867 rad < π - δ₀ = 2.4981 rad) and $\Delta\omega$ is *still positive* (3.5406 rad/s) but the deceleration has clearly begun (acceleration $k_2$ negative). The system is in the **decelerating region**; whether it ultimately stabilises depends on the available decelerating area. Given that in part (a) the critical clearing angle was unbounded ($\cos\delta_c > 1$), the system is **likely unstable** for the given loading. The student is expected to remark on this.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B, 14-mark question):**
> 1. **Switching the $P_{max}$ value at the correct step** — many students forget to change from $P_{max2}$ to $P_{max3}$ at $t = t_c$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Radians vs degrees** — examiners expect $\delta$ in radians for the swing equation; do not mix degrees inside $\sin(\cdot)$.
> 3. **Sign of $P_a$ post-fault** — when $P_e > P_m$ post-fault, $P_a$ becomes *negative*; this represents **deceleration**, not an error. Always state it.
> 4. **Stop condition** — the swing curve must be continued until at least the *first peak* of $\delta$, not arbitrarily stopped at the requested time.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (KTU Board Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LAST-MINUTE REVISION POINTS — TRANSIENT STABILITY**

- ⚡ **Swing equation** is the master equation: $\dfrac{2H}{\omega_s}\dfrac{d^2\delta}{dt^2} = P_m - P_e$ — remember all terms.
- ⚡ **Inertia coefficient** $M = 2H/\omega_s$ — appears in the state-space form, not $H$ itself.
- ⚡ **Power-angle equation** $P_e = P_{max}\sin\delta$ — derived from the classical SMIB model; **pre-fault, during-fault, and post-fault** each have their own $P_{max}$.
- ⚡ **Worst-case fault** = 3-φ bolted fault at generator terminals → $P_e^{during} = 0$.
- ⚡ **Equal-Area Criterion** = *Accelerating Area* = *Decelerating Area* for the **just-stable** case.
- ⚡ **Critical clearing angle** formula (most tested):
  $$\cos\delta_c = \frac{P_m(\pi - 2\delta_0)}{P_{max3}} + \cos\delta_0$$
  If the result **exceeds 1**, the system is **unconditionally unstable** for the given loading.
- ⚡ **Critical clearing time** requires **numerical integration** — there is no closed form. Common methods: Modified Euler, RK-2 (Heun's), RK-4.
- ⚡ **Stability limit** for SMIB: $P_{m,crit} = \dfrac{P_{max3}(1-\cos\delta_0)}{\pi - 2\delta_0}$.
- ⚡ **Maximum stable angle** in post-fault regime: $\delta_{max} = \pi - \delta_0$ (180° electrical from initial point).
- ⚡ **Typical lab step size** $\Delta t = 0.01$ to $0.05$ s — accuracy of swing curve degrades with larger steps.
- ⚡ **Distinguish stability types:** Steady-state (slow), Dynamic (governor/AVR), Transient (swing, 0–5 s).
- ⚡ **Real-world stabilisers:** Braking resistors, fast valving, SVC/STATCOM, HVDC links, controlled islanding, generator tripping via SPS (Special Protection Schemes).
- ⚡ **KTU exam vocabulary to use:** "non-linear second-order ODE", "rotor angle stability", "first-swing stability", "equal-area criterion", "critical clearing time", "transient stability limit".

> [!TIP]
> **Final advice for the KTU 2024 exam:** Always draw the **power-angle diagram** with three curves and the two shaded areas $A_1$ and $A_2$. Examiners award *2 free marks* for a clean, labelled diagram even before you begin writing the equations. Then state the EAC condition and derive $\delta_c$ algebraically. For the numerical integration sub-part, write down the state vector $(\delta, \Delta\omega)$ at *every* step; partial credit is generous if the *trend* is correctly captured even if the last decimal differs from the official key.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Generation Control: Single Area and Two Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Automatic Generation Control: Single Area and Two Area # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Automatic Generation Control (AGC)? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Automatic Generation Control (AGC)* is a closed-loop feedback control system employed in interconnected power systems to maintain continuous real-time balance between total power generation and total load demand (plus losses), thereby regulating the **system frequency** at its nominal value (50 Hz in India) and maintaining scheduled **tie-line power exchanges** between control areas. AGC is the central nervous system of a modern power grid. It automatically adjusts the setpoints of generators to correct any mismatch between generation and demand, which would otherwise cause the system frequency to drift away from its rated value. > [!NOTE] > **Why AGC is Non-Negotiable in Modern Grids:** > - A frequency deviation of just **0.5 Hz** can damage turbo-generators. > - Indian Electricity Grid Code (IEGC) mandates frequency be maintained within **49.5 Hz – 50.5 Hz**. > - The Kerala State Load Despatch Centre (KSLDC) and POSOCO monitor ACE values every 4 seconds. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control" of a Power Grid Imagine driving a car on a highway: | Car System | Power System Equivalent | Function | |------------|------------------------|----------| | Accelerator pedal | Generator MW output | Supplies "power" to the wheels/load | | Brake | Load shedding (emergency) | Reduces demand suddenly | | Speedometer | Frequency meter (50 Hz) | Indicates system "speed" | | Cruise Control | AGC Controller | Automatically adjusts accelerator | | Hills (slopes) | Varying load demand | Causes "speed" (frequency) to drop | | Road gradient sensor | Frequency sensor + Tie-line meter | Provides feedback | **The Analogy Explained:** - When the load suddenly **increases** (like a hill), the car slows down. The driver (or cruise control) presses the accelerator to restore speed. - Similarly, when electrical load increases, generators slow down, frequency drops, and AGC commands more fuel/steam to generators to bring frequency back to 50 Hz. - In a **two-area system**, two cars are linked by a rigid pole (the tie-line). If one car speeds up, the other is dragged along. AGC must manage both cars' speeds and the force in the connecting pole. ## 1.3 Hierarchical Control Structure of AGC Power system operation uses a three-tier control hierarchy: 1. **Primary Control (Governor Action) — Seconds:** - Local, automatic, proportional response. - Acts within **0 to ~10 seconds**. - Cannot bring frequency back to nominal; only arrests the drop. 2. **Secondary Control (Supplementary / AGC) — Minutes:** - Centralized, integral control. - Acts within **~10 seconds to several minutes**. - **Eliminates steady-state frequency error** and restores tie-line schedules. 3. **Tertiary Control (Economic Dispatch) — Hours:** - Manual or computer-aided optimization. - Re-dispatches generation for minimum cost. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Focus:** The Power System Lab experiment on AGC focuses on the **secondary (integral) control loop** behavior in both single-area and two-area configurations. ## 1.4 Single Area vs Two Area — At a Glance | Feature | Single Area AGC | Two Area AGC | |---------|----------------|--------------| | Number of control areas | 1 | 2 (interconnected) | | Control variable | Frequency only (Δf) | Frequency + Tie-line power | | ACE (Area Control Error) | $B \cdot \Delta f$ | $\Delta P_{tie} + B \cdot \Delta f$ | | Tie-line present | No | Yes | | Steady-state Δf after AGC | Zero | Zero (in both areas) | | Tie-line error after AGC | N/A | Zero | | Typical application | Isolated grid | Interconnected regional grids | | Indian example | Lakshadweep island grid | Kerala – Tamil Nadu interconnection | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Loops in AGC ### 2.1.1 Primary Control Loop (Governor) The **speed governor** is a mechanical/hydraulic device that senses rotor speed and adjusts the steam/fuel valve. Its key property is the **droop characteristic** (R): $$R = \frac{\Delta f \, / \, f_{nominal}}{\Delta P_G \, / \, P_{nominal}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Governor Droop (R):** Typically **4% to 5%** for thermal units and **3% to 5%** for hydro units. A 5% droop means a 5% frequency change (2.5 Hz) causes 100% valve movement. ### 2.1.2 Secondary Control Loop (Integral Controller) A pure **integral controller** $\frac{K_i}{s}$ is added in series with the governor. The integral action ensures that any residual frequency error is driven to zero over time: $$\Delta P_{ref} = -\frac{K_i}{s} \cdot \Delta f$$ ## 2.2 Transfer Function Building Blocks For both single-area and two-area AGC, the system is modeled as a cascade of **four standard blocks** per area: | Block | Symbol | Typical Value (Thermal) | Physical Meaning | |-------|--------|-------------------------|------------------| | Governor | $\frac{1}{1 + s T_g}$ | $T_g = 0.2$ s | Mechanical/hydraulic valve response | | Turbine | $\frac{1}{1 + s T_t}$ | $T_t = 0.5$ s | Steam chest / fuel dynamics | | Power System | $\frac{K_{ps}}{1 + s T_{ps}}$ | $K_{ps} = 120$, $T_{ps} = 20$ s | Inertia + load damping | | Rotating mass (swing) | $\frac{1}{2 H s + D}$ | $H = 5$ s, $D = 1.0$ | Frequency sensitivity to imbalance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Relation — Power System Gain:** > $$K_{ps} = \frac{1}{D} \quad \text{and} \quad T_{ps} = \frac{2H}{D}$$ > where **D** is the load-damping constant (pu/Hz) and **H** is the inertia constant (seconds). ## 2.3 Single Area AGC — Complete Analysis ### 2.3.1 System Model A single area consists of one generator supplying one load. The disturbance is a sudden **load change $\Delta P_L$**. ### 2.3.2 Open-Loop Behavior (No Integral Control) Without the integral controller, only the governor acts. A load step $\Delta P_L$ causes: $$\Delta f_{ss} = -\frac{\Delta P_L}{D + \frac{1}{R}} = -\Delta P_L \cdot \frac{R}{1 + DR}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Primary control **cannot** eliminate frequency error. A load increase of 0.01 pu causes a permanent frequency drop. This is unacceptable in a real grid. ### 2.3.3 Closed-Loop Behavior (With Integral Control) Adding the integral controller $\frac{K_i}{s}$ ensures: $$\Delta f_{ss} = 0 \quad \text{(steady-state frequency error is zero)}$$ However, the **generation** picks up the entire load change: $$\Delta P_G^{ss} = -\Delta P_L$$ This is the **fundamental benefit of secondary control**: zero frequency error, with the cost being paid by additional generation. ## 2.4 Two Area AGC — Complete Analysis ### 2.4.1 Why Two Areas? Most power systems are **interconnected** for: - **Economy** — load sharing between regions. - **Reliability** — backup support during emergencies. - **Frequency stability** — larger inertia smooths disturbances. The connection between areas is a **tie-line** (AC transmission corridor). ### 2.4.2 Tie-Line Power Equation Power flowing from Area 1 to Area 2 through the tie-line is: $$P_{tie,1 \to 2} = \frac{|V_1| \cdot |V_2|}{X_{tie}} \cdot \sin(\delta_1 - \delta_2)$$ Linearized deviation: $$\Delta P_{tie} = T_{12} \cdot (\Delta \delta_1 - \Delta \delta_2) = 2 \pi T_{12} \int (\Delta f_1 - \Delta f_2) \, dt$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Synchronizing Power Coefficient ($T_{12}$):** > $$T_{12} = \frac{|V_1| \cdot |V_2|}{X_{tie}} \cdot \cos(\delta_{1}^0 - \delta_{2}^0)$$ > in per-unit on the common base. **Typical values: 0.05 to 0.2 pu/Hz.** ### 2.4.3 Area Control Error (ACE) — Heart of Two-Area AGC For **Area 1**: $$ACE_1 = \Delta P_{tie,1} + B_1 \cdot \Delta f_1$$ For **Area 2**: $$ACE_2 = \Delta P_{tie,2} + B_2 \cdot \Delta f_2 = -\Delta P_{tie,1} + B_2 \cdot \Delta f_2$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention:** $\Delta P_{tie,1}$ is the scheduled tie-line flow *out* of Area 1. The integral controller in Area 1 drives $ACE_1 \to 0$, automatically ensuring both $\Delta f_1 \to 0$ and $\Delta P_{tie,1} \to 0$. ### 2.4.4 The Bias Factor B (Beta) The bias B must be chosen carefully: - $B_i = \beta_i = D_i + \frac{1}{R_i}$ (area frequency response characteristic) - **Rule of thumb:** $B_i \geq$ area's natural frequency response. - If B is too small → areas interfere with each other. - If B is too large → AGC becomes sluggish. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---------|-------------|------| | 1 | $\Delta f_{ss}^{\text{primary}} = -\Delta P_L \cdot \frac{R}{1 + DR}$ | Steady-state Δf with governor only | Hz | | 2 | $\Delta f_{ss}^{\text{secondary}} = 0$ | Steady-state Δf with integral control | Hz | | 3 | $ACE = \Delta P_{tie} + B \cdot \Delta f$ | Area Control Error (two-area) | pu | | 4 | $ACE_{\text{single}} = B \cdot \Delta f$ | ACE for single area | pu | | 5 | $B = D + \frac{1}{R}$ | Bias factor (area frequency response) | pu/Hz | | 6 | $\Delta P_{tie} = 2\pi T_{12} \int (\Delta f_1 - \Delta f_2)\, dt$ | Tie-line power deviation | pu | | 7 | $K_{ps} = \frac{1}{D}, \quad T_{ps} = \frac{2H}{D}$ | Power system gain & time constant | - , s | | 8 | $\Delta P_{ref} = -\frac{K_i}{s} \cdot ACE$ | Integral control law | pu | | 9 | $P_{G,1}^{ss} - P_{L,1} = \Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss}$ | Steady-state power balance (Area 1) | pu | | 10 | $P_{G,2}^{ss} - P_{L,2} = -\Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss}$ | Steady-state power balance (Area 2) | pu | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Do **NOT** confuse $K_{ps}$ (power system) with $K_{sg}$ (speed governor). In some textbooks, $K_{ps}$ is written as $1/D$ and in others as $\frac{1}{D + 1/R}$ depending on whether the governor is included inside the power system block. Read the diagram carefully! ## 2.6 Real-World Utility of AGC | Application | Industry | AGC Role | |-------------|----------|----------| | Southern Regional Grid (India) | Power Grid Corp. of India | Maintains 50 Hz across 4 states | | Kerala–Tamil Nadu interconnection | KSEB / TANTRANSCO | Schedules 800 MW interchanges | | Load Frequency Control (LFC) | Renewable-integrated grids | Compensates for solar/wind variability | | Island microgrids | Lakshadweep, Andamans | Single-area AGC for diesel generators | | HVDC back-to-back links | Chandrapur–Padghe | Decouples regional frequency dynamics | | European ENTSO-E grid | Continental Europe | Multi-area AGC for 35+ countries | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Single Area AGC Steady-State Frequency Error (Primary Control Only) ### Given: - Load disturbance: $\Delta P_L$ (step input in pu) - Governor droop: $R$ (pu/pu) - Load damping: $D$ (pu/Hz) - **No integral controller** (open secondary loop) ### Block Diagram Logic: The frequency deviation $\Delta f$ is related to net power imbalance $\Delta P_m - \Delta P_L$ through the swing equation (rotating mass + damping): $$\Delta f = \frac{1}{Ds + 2Hs} (\Delta P_m - \Delta P_L)$$ The mechanical power $\Delta P_m$ from the turbine depends on the governor action: $$\Delta P_m = \frac{1}{1 + sT_t} \cdot \Delta P_v$$ The governor valve position $\Delta P_v$ follows the droop characteristic: $$\Delta P_v = -\frac{1}{R} \cdot \Delta f \quad \text{(for governor with transfer function } \frac{1}{1+sT_g}\text{)}$$ ### Steady-State Condition (s → 0): At steady state, all derivative terms vanish. The transfer functions reduce to: $$\frac{1}{1 + sT_g} \to 1, \quad \frac{1}{1 + sT_t} \to 1, \quad \frac{1}{2Hs + D} \to \frac{1}{D}$$ Substituting into the swing equation: $$\Delta f_{ss} = \frac{1}{D} \left( \Delta P_{m,ss} - \Delta P_L \right)$$ Since the governor sets the mechanical power: $$\Delta P_{m,ss} = -\frac{\Delta f_{ss}}{R}$$ Plugging back: $$\Delta f_{ss} = \frac{1}{D} \left( -\frac{\Delta f_{ss}}{R} - \Delta P_L \right)$$ Multiplying both sides by $D$: $$D \cdot \Delta f_{ss} = -\frac{\Delta f_{ss}}{R} - \Delta P_L$$ Collecting $\Delta f_{ss}$ terms on the left: $$\Delta f_{ss} \left( D + \frac{1}{R} \right) = -\Delta P_L$$ $$\boxed{\Delta f_{ss} = -\frac{\Delta P_L}{D + \frac{1}{R}} = -\frac{\Delta P_L \cdot R}{1 + DR}}$$ ### Physical Interpretation: For a thermal unit with $R = 0.05$ pu/pu, $D = 1.0$ pu/Hz, and a 0.01 pu load step: $$\Delta f_{ss} = -\frac{0.01}{1.0 + 20} = -0.000476 \, \text{pu} = -0.0238 \, \text{Hz}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** This small but **permanent** frequency drop is why AGC is essential. A 0.02 Hz drop sustained over hours violates IEGC and incurs commercial penalties. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Two-Area AGC Steady-State Tie-Line Power & Frequency ### Given: - Area 1 load step: $\Delta P_{L,1}$ - Area 2 load step: $\Delta P_{L,2}$ - Both areas have integral controllers (secondary AGC active) - Tie-line synchronizing coefficient: $T_{12}$ ### Step 1: Define Net Power Imbalance in Each Area For Area 1: $$\Delta P_{1} = \Delta P_{G,1} - \Delta P_{L,1} - \Delta P_{tie,1}$$ For Area 2: $$\Delta P_{2} = \Delta P_{G,2} - \Delta P_{L,2} - \Delta P_{tie,2} = \Delta P_{G,2} - \Delta P_{L,2} + \Delta P_{tie,1}$$ ### Step 2: Steady-State Conditions (With AGC Active) Since AGC drives each ACE to zero: $$ACE_1^{ss} = \Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} + B_1 \cdot \Delta f_1^{ss} = 0$$ $$ACE_2^{ss} = -\Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} + B_2 \cdot \Delta f_2^{ss} = 0$$ Also, both areas share the same frequency in steady state (rigid coupling via tie-line): $$\Delta f_1^{ss} = \Delta f_2^{ss} = \Delta f^{ss}$$ ### Step 3: Solve the System of Equations Adding the two ACE equations: $$0 + 0 = B_1 \Delta f^{ss} + B_2 \Delta f^{ss}$$ $$\Delta f^{ss}(B_1 + B_2) = 0$$ $$\boxed{\Delta f^{ss} = 0}$$ Substituting back into ACE₁ = 0: $$0 + B_1 \cdot 0 + \Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} = 0$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} = 0}$$ ### Step 4: Find Generation Changes From Area 1 power balance at steady state: $$\Delta P_{G,1}^{ss} - \Delta P_{L,1} - 0 = 0$$ $$\Delta P_{G,1}^{ss} = \Delta P_{L,1}$$ Similarly: $$\Delta P_{G,2}^{ss} = \Delta P_{L,2}$$ ### Final Result — The "Magic" of AGC: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Under Two-Area AGC:** > 1. **Both areas' frequencies return to 50 Hz.** > 2. **Tie-line power returns to its scheduled value.** > 3. **Each area's generation exactly meets its own load.** > 4. **No area "helps" the other in steady state — every disturbance is handled locally.** --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Transient Tie-Line Power Exchange (No AGC) When AGC is OFF and only primary control acts, the tie-line carries load-sharing power. ### Step 1: Each Area's Steady-State Frequency $$\Delta f_1^{ss} = -\frac{\Delta P_{L,1}}{B_1}, \quad \Delta f_2^{ss} = -\frac{\Delta P_{L,2}}{B_2}$$ where $B_i = D_i + \frac{1}{R_i}$ is the area frequency response. ### Step 2: Using Tie-Line Equation Since $\Delta f_1 = \Delta f_2$ in steady state (rigid tie-line): $$\Delta f^{ss} = \frac{-\Delta P_{L,1}/B_1 + (-\Delta P_{L,2}/B_2)}{?}$$ Wait — both areas have the same frequency. So we must write the combined swing equation: $$\Delta f^{ss} = -\frac{\Delta P_{L,1} + \Delta P_{L,2}}{B_1 + B_2}$$ ### Step 3: Solve for Tie-Line Flow From Area 1's net power equation: $$\Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} = \Delta P_{G,1}^{ss} - \Delta P_{L,1} - B_1 \Delta f^{ss}$$ But with primary control only, generation in Area 1 picks up $-\Delta f^{ss}/R_1$, while load damping contributes $-D_1 \Delta f^{ss}$: $$\Delta P_{G,1}^{ss} = -\frac{\Delta f^{ss}}{R_1}$$ Therefore: $$\Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} = -\frac{\Delta f^{ss}}{R_1} - \Delta P_{L,1} - D_1 \Delta f^{ss}$$ $$= -\Delta P_{L,1} - \left( D_1 + \frac{1}{R_1} \right) \Delta f^{ss}$$ $$= -\Delta P_{L,1} - B_1 \Delta f^{ss}$$ Substituting $\Delta f^{ss}$: $$\boxed{\Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} = \frac{-\Delta P_{L,1} \cdot B_2 + \Delta P_{L,2} \cdot B_1}{B_1 + B_2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A load increase in Area 2 ($\Delta P_{L,2} > 0$) causes $\Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} > 0$, i.e., power flows *from* Area 1 *to* Area 2. This is the natural **load-sharing** behavior of interconnected systems. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Performance Metrics | Performance Metric | Single Area (No AGC) | Single Area (With AGC) | Two Area (No AGC) | Two Area (With AGC) | |-------------------|----------------------|------------------------|--------------------|---------------------| | Steady-state $\Delta f$ | $-\Delta P_L \cdot \frac{R}{1+DR}$ | **0** | $-\frac{\Delta P_{L,1}+\Delta P_{L,2}}{B_1+B_2}$ | **0** | | Steady-state $\Delta P_{tie}$ | N/A | N/A | $\frac{B_1 \Delta P_{L,2} - B_2 \Delta P_{L,1}}{B_1+B_2}$ | **0** | | Settling time | 5–10 s | 30–60 s | 5–10 s | 30–60 s | | Overshoot (Δf) | Moderate | Low (with proper $K_i$) | Moderate | Low | | Tie-line oscillation | None | None | Yes (inter-area mode) | Damped out | | Cost of control | Free | Generation cost ↑ | Free | Generation cost ↑ | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often write $ACE = \Delta P_{tie} - B \cdot \Delta f$ with a **minus sign**. The correct KTU convention is **PLUS**: $ACE = \Delta P_{tie} + B \cdot \Delta f$. Always check your reference frame. ## 3.5 Lab-Oriented Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A two-area system has $B_1 = B_2 = 20$ pu/Hz, $T_{12} = 0.1$ pu/Hz. A load of 0.02 pu is suddenly added in Area 1. Find steady-state tie-line power and frequency with AGC active. **Solution (with AGC):** - By derivation 2: $\Delta f^{ss} = 0$ Hz - $\Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} = 0$ pu - Area 1 generation rises by 0.02 pu - Area 2 generation unchanged **Solution (without AGC):** - $\Delta f^{ss} = -\frac{0.02}{20+20} = -0.0005$ pu $= -0.025$ Hz - $\Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} = \frac{0 - 0.02 \cdot 20}{40} = -0.01$ pu > **Interpretation:** Without AGC, Area 2 unknowingly supplies half the load (0.01 pu flows into Area 1). With AGC, this unfair burden is eliminated. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Single Area AGC — Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph LR DL["Delta_PL
Load Disturbance"] --> SUM1(("+")) SUMREF["Delta_P_ref
Reference Setpoint"] --> SUM2(("+")) SUM2 --> GOV["Governor
1 divided by 1 plus s Tg"] GOV --> TURB["Turbine
1 divided by 1 plus s Tt"] TURB --> SUM1 SUM1 --> POWSYS["Power System
Kps divided by 1 plus s Tps"] POWSYS --> DELTAF["Delta_f
Frequency Deviation"] DELTAF --> FEED1[/"Feedback Line"/] FEED1 --> SUM2 DELTAF --> DISP["Display
Scope Output"] DELTAF --> INT["Integral Controller
Ki divided by s"] INT --> SUM2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block Diagram Reading Guide:** Start from the right (Δf) and trace the feedback path. The Δf signal is both displayed (for lab scope) and fed back to the summing junction before the governor. The integral controller is in parallel with the direct feedback path. ## 4.2 Two Area AGC — Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph AREA1["AREA 1"] DL1["Delta_PL1"] --> SUMA1(("+")) SUMREF1["Delta_P_ref1"] --> SUMB1(("+")) SUMB1 --> GOV1["Gov1"] GOV1 --> TURB1["Turb1"] TURB1 --> SUMA1 SUMA1 --> PSYS1["PowerSys1
Kps1 by 1 plus s Tps1"] PSYS1 --> DF1["Delta_f1"] DF1 --> ACE1["ACE1
DPTIE1 plus B1 times DF1"] ACE1 --> INT1["Ki by s"] INT1 --> SUMB1 end subgraph AREA2["AREA 2"] DL2["Delta_PL2"] --> SUMA2(("+")) SUMREF2["Delta_P_ref2"] --> SUMB2(("+")) SUMB2 --> GOV2["Gov2"] GOV2 --> TURB2["Turb2"] TURB2 --> SUMA2 SUMA2 --> PSYS2["PowerSys2
Kps2 by 1 plus s Tps2"] PSYS2 --> DF2["Delta_f2"] DF2 --> ACE2["ACE2
minus DPTIE1 plus B2 times DF2"] ACE2 --> INT2["Ki by s"] INT2 --> SUMB2 end DF1 --> TIEINT["Tie-line
Integrator 2 pi T12 by s"] TIEINT --> DPTIE1["Delta_P_tie1"] DPTIE1 --> ACE1 DPTIE1 --> ACE2 DF2 --> TIEINT ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Observation:** The tie-line signal is **shared** by both areas. A positive $\Delta P_{tie,1}$ (more power leaving Area 1) appears with a PLUS sign in ACE₁ and a MINUS sign in ACE₂. This sign convention enforces the physics: when Area 1 sends power out, it sees a positive ACE and ramps up generation. ## 4.3 Operational Flowchart — AGC Decision Logic (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD START(["Start: Monitor System"]) --> MEAS["Measure frequency and tie-line power"] MEAS --> COMP["Compute ACE
ACE equals DPTIE plus B times DF"] COMP --> CHK{"Is absolute ACE
greater than threshold?"} CHK --> NO["No Action
Continue Monitoring"] CHK --> YES["Compute area participation factors
alpha_i and beta_i"] YES --> ALLOC["Allocate generation change
to each generating unit"] ALLOC --> RAMP["Ramp unit setpoints
at defined rate MW per minute"] RAMP --> VERIFY["Wait for next control cycle
typically 2 to 4 seconds"] VERIFY --> MEAS NO --> WAIT["Wait for next scan"] WAIT --> MEAS ``` > [!NOTE] > **Lab Implementation Tip:** In the MATLAB/Simulink lab model, the control cycle period is set to **2–4 seconds** (matching real KSLDC practice). Setting it too fast (e.g., 0.1 s) causes numerical instability in the simulation. ## 4.4 Lab Signal Flow — Practical Wiring (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph LR SIGGEN["Signal Generator
Step Load 0.01 pu at t = 5s"] --> SUMJ(["Summing Junction"]) SETPT["Load Setpoint Display"] --> CTRL["AGC Controller
Ki by s"] CTRL --> SUMJ SUMJ --> GENMODEL["Generator Model
Governor plus Turbine"] GENMODEL --> GRID["Grid Model
Swing Equation"] GRID --> SCOPEF["Scope Channel 1
Delta_f vs t"] GRID --> SCOPEP["Scope Channel 2
Delta_P_G vs t"] GRID --> FEED["Feedback to AGC"] FEED --> CTRL ``` > [!WARNING] > **Lab Common Error:** Forgetting to connect the **feedback loop** in the Simulink model. This results in an open-loop simulation where frequency keeps drifting downward without bound — a useful diagnostic symptom for students to recognize. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Area Control Error (ACE) for a two-area power system. Why is it important in AGC?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Area Control Error for Area *i* in a two-area AGC is defined as the linear combination of frequency deviation and tie-line power deviation: > $$ACE_i = \Delta P_{tie,i} + B_i \cdot \Delta f_i$$ > where $B_i$ is the bias factor in pu/Hz. > **Significance (1.5 marks):** > - ACE is the **control input** to the supplementary integral controller. > - Driving ACE to zero simultaneously restores frequency to nominal **and** tie-line power to scheduled value. > - It represents the **un-served generation requirement** of the area. > - In India, KSLDC computes ACE every 4 seconds; persistent non-zero ACE incurs UI (Unscheduled Interchange) penalties. --- ### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the function of the integral controller in an AGC loop? Why is proportional control alone insufficient?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Function (1.5 marks):** > The integral controller $\frac{K_i}{s}$ accumulates the area control error over time and adjusts the generator setpoint $\Delta P_{ref}$ such that: > $$\Delta P_{ref} = -\frac{K_i}{s} \cdot ACE$$ > This ensures the steady-state ACE becomes zero. > **Why Proportional Alone Fails (1.5 marks):** > - Proportional control yields a non-zero steady-state error: $e_{ss} = \frac{e_{ref}}{1 + K_p K_{plant}}$. > - In AGC, this would mean a permanent frequency offset after every load change — unacceptable for grid code compliance. > - Integral action provides **infinite DC gain**, forcing steady-state error to zero. > - Integral action also has inherent **memory** — it can correct small persistent biases that proportional control ignores. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question B1 — Option A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the steady-state frequency deviation $\Delta f_{ss}$ in a single-area AGC system **without** secondary (integral) control, for a step load change of magnitude $\Delta P_L$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of **two-area AGC** with integral control. Show that the steady-state tie-line power deviation and frequency deviation both become zero when AGC is active. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Single Area Without Secondary Control **Assumptions (1 mark):** - Step load change $\Delta P_L$ at $t = 0$. - All generators identical with droop $R$. - Load damping constant $D$ (pu/Hz). - Steady-state: $s \to 0$, all transfer functions become constants. **Derivation (5 marks):** From the swing equation: $$\Delta f = \frac{1}{2Hs + D}(\Delta P_m - \Delta P_L)$$ At steady state ($s = 0$): $$\Delta f_{ss} = \frac{\Delta P_{m,ss} - \Delta P_L}{D}$$ The governor sets mechanical power based on droop: $$\Delta P_{m,ss} = -\frac{\Delta f_{ss}}{R}$$ Substituting: $$\Delta f_{ss} = \frac{-\frac{\Delta f_{ss}}{R} - \Delta P_L}{D}$$ $$D \cdot \Delta f_{ss} + \frac{\Delta f_{ss}}{R} = -\Delta P_L$$ $$\Delta f_{ss} \left( D + \frac{1}{R} \right) = -\Delta P_L$$ $$\boxed{\Delta f_{ss} = -\frac{\Delta P_L \cdot R}{1 + DR}}$$ **Physical Interpretation (1 mark):** - For $R = 0.05$, $D = 1.0$, $\Delta P_L = 0.01$ pu: - $\Delta f_{ss} = -\frac{0.01 \times 0.05}{1.05} = -4.76 \times 10^{-4}$ pu $= -0.0238$ Hz. - This is a permanent frequency drop that cannot be self-corrected. #### Part (b) — Two-Area AGC With Integral Control **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the complete two-area AGC block diagram. Each area has its own governor, turbine, power system, and integral controller. The tie-line carries the signal $\Delta P_{tie}$ between areas. **Mathematical Proof (4 marks):** With integral control active in both areas, at steady state: $$ACE_1 = \Delta P_{tie,1} + B_1 \Delta f_1 \to 0 \quad \text{(as } t \to \infty\text{)}$$ $$ACE_2 = -\Delta P_{tie,1} + B_2 \Delta f_2 \to 0$$ Since both areas are rigidly coupled via the tie-line at steady state: $$\Delta f_1 = \Delta f_2 = \Delta f$$ Adding the two ACE equations: $$(B_1 + B_2) \Delta f = 0 \implies \Delta f = 0$$ Substituting back: $$\Delta P_{tie,1} = 0 \quad \text{(from ACE}_1 = 0\text{)}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta f^{ss} = 0, \quad \Delta P_{tie}^{ss} = 0}$$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** The integral controllers of both areas jointly ensure that all local disturbances are absorbed by local generation, the frequency returns to nominal, and the tie-line power is restored to its scheduled value. --- ### Question B1 — Option B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the role of the **tie-line bias factor B** in two-area AGC. What happens if B is chosen too small or too large? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A two-area system has $B_1 = 20$ pu/Hz and $B_2 = 25$ pu/Hz. A load of 0.05 pu is suddenly added in Area 1. Compute the steady-state tie-line power deviation **and** frequency deviation (i) with AGC active, and (ii) without AGC. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Tie-Line Bias Factor B **Definition (2 marks):** The bias factor $B_i$ (also called $\beta_i$) is the area frequency response characteristic: $$B_i = D_i + \frac{1}{R_i}$$ It determines how much each area's ACE responds to frequency deviation. **Role in AGC (2 marks):** - $B$ sets the **gain** of the ACE signal: $ACE_i = \Delta P_{tie,i} + B_i \Delta f_i$. - Correctly chosen $B_i$ prevents inter-area control **interference**. - Ensures that a disturbance in Area 1 is primarily corrected by Area 1, not Area 2. **Effects of Mismatched B (3 marks):** - **B too small:** Area's response to frequency is weak. Disturbances in Area 1 are "leaked" into Area 2 (unwanted cross-coupling). Other area over-corrects. - **B too large:** Area over-reacts to frequency, causing large initial generation swings, oscillations, and slow settling. - **Optimal B:** $B_i \geq$ natural frequency response, typically $B_i = D_i + 1/R_i$ (natural setting). In Indian practice, $B$ is updated annually based on the area's load composition. #### Part (b) — Numerical Computation **Given:** $B_1 = 20$ pu/Hz, $B_2 = 25$ pu/Hz, $\Delta P_{L,1} = 0.05$ pu, $\Delta P_{L,2} = 0$. **Case (i) — With AGC (3.5 marks):** From derivation in Section 3.2: $$\Delta f^{ss} = 0 \text{ Hz}, \quad \Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} = 0 \text{ pu}$$ Area 1 generation rises by 0.05 pu; Area 2 generation unchanged. **Case (ii) — Without AGC (3.5 marks):** Combined system frequency deviation: $$\Delta f^{ss} = -\frac{\Delta P_{L,1} + \Delta P_{L,2}}{B_1 + B_2} = -\frac{0.05 + 0}{20 + 25} = -\frac{0.05}{45}$$ $$\Delta f^{ss} = -1.111 \times 10^{-3} \text{ pu} = -0.0556 \text{ Hz}$$ Tie-line power deviation: $$\Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} = \frac{-\Delta P_{L,1} B_2 + \Delta P_{L,2} B_1}{B_1 + B_2} = \frac{-0.05 \times 25 + 0}{45}$$ $$\Delta P_{tie,1}^{ss} = -0.0278 \text{ pu}$$ > **Interpretation:** Without AGC, Area 2 unknowingly supplies 0.0278 pu (55.6% of the disturbance) to Area 1 via the tie-line. The frequency drops by 0.0556 Hz. Both deviations are eliminated by AGC. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign of ACE:** Always use $\Delta P_{tie} + B \cdot \Delta f$, never minus. > 2. **Units mismatch:** $B$ is in **pu/Hz** but $\Delta f$ must be in **pu** (not Hz) for consistency. > 3. **Droop confusion:** $R$ is the **per-unit frequency change for full load change**, not the slope of the governor characteristic in Hz/MW. > 4. **Tie-line sign:** $\Delta P_{tie,1}$ is the scheduled flow *out of* Area 1; Area 2 uses $-\Delta P_{tie,1}$. > 5. **Steady-state vs transient:** Many students write transient responses for "steady-state" questions, losing 2–3 marks. > 6. **Block diagram labeling:** Each block must show its transfer function; vague labels like "Governor" alone earn only partial credit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — AGC: Single & Two Area** ### Core Concepts: - ☐ AGC = secondary control loop; **eliminates** steady-state frequency error. - ☐ Primary control (governor) **arrests** frequency drop but cannot **eliminate** it. - ☐ Single area: only frequency to control; ACE reduces to $B \cdot \Delta f$. - ☐ Two area: must control **both** frequency **and** tie-line power simultaneously. ### Key Formulas (Memorize): - ☐ $\Delta f_{ss}^{\text{primary}} = -\Delta P_L \cdot \frac{R}{1 + DR}$ (single area, no AGC) - ☐ $\Delta f_{ss}^{\text{AGC}} = 0$ (always, with proper AGC) - ☐ $ACE = \Delta P_{tie} + B \cdot \Delta f$ - ☐ $B = D + \frac{1}{R}$ - ☐ Tie-line: $\Delta P_{tie} = 2\pi T_{12} \int (\Delta f_1 - \Delta f_2)\, dt$ ### Two-Area Steady-State Facts: - ☐ With AGC: $\Delta f^{ss} = 0$ and $\Delta P_{tie}^{ss} = 0$ in **both** areas. - ☐ Each area's generation meets its **own** load (no free-riding). - ☐ Without AGC: load is shared in proportion to $\frac{1}{B_i}$. ### Lab Practical Points: - ☐ Typical parameters: $T_g = 0.2$ s, $T_t = 0.5$ s, $H = 5$ s, $D = 1.0$, $R = 0.05$. - ☐ Tie-line coefficient $T_{12} \approx 0.05$ to $0.2$ pu/Hz. - ☐ Integral gain $K_i$ choice: too high → oscillations; too low → slow response. - ☐ Use scope to observe $\Delta f$ vs time and $\Delta P_G$ vs time. - ☐ Compare plots with AGC ON vs AGC OFF. ### Real-World Connection: - ☐ KSLDC monitors ACE every **4 seconds** for Kerala grid. - ☐ India follows **tie-line bias control** with $B_i$ updated annually. - ☐ IEGC mandates frequency within **49.5 – 50.5 Hz**;
