deviations cause UI penalties. - ☐ Southern Regional Grid (SR) operates as a multi-area AGC system with 4 constituent states. > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked to "explain AGC," always structure your answer in three layers: (1) **Problem** (frequency deviation due to load-generation mismatch), (2) **Mechanism** (primary → secondary control), (3) **Result** (zero frequency error, restored tie-line schedule). Examiners reward this logical flow.

topics.title: Automatic Voltage Regulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Automatic Voltage Regulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR)** is a closed-loop feedback control system employed in synchronous generators (alternators) that continuously monitors the generator's terminal voltage, compares it against a precisely defined reference voltage, and automatically adjusts the **DC field excitation current** of the rotor to maintain the terminal voltage within prescribed statutory limits despite variations in load, power factor, speed, or ambient temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL609):** In the Power System Lab, the AVR experiment typically demonstrates the open-loop and closed-loop voltage regulation characteristics of a self-excited or separately-excited DC shunt generator / synchronous generator emulator, observing how the field current manipulates the output voltage magnitude in real-time.

Mathematically, the steady-state operating objective of the AVR can be expressed as:

$$V_{t(actual)} \;\longrightarrow\; V_{t(reference)} \pm \Delta V_{tolerance}$$

where $\Delta V_{tolerance}$ is typically **± 5 %** of the nominal rated terminal voltage as per Indian Electricity Rules and IEEE standards.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway, and your job is to maintain a **constant speed of 60 km/h**.

| Your Driving Scenario | Equivalent AVR Action |
|---|---|
| **Speedometer** shows current speed | Voltage Sensor reads terminal voltage $V_t$ |
| **Brain** compares actual speed vs. set speed (60) | Comparator produces **error signal** $V_e = V_{ref} - V_t$ |
| **Right foot on accelerator** adjusts fuel | Amplifier drives the **exciter field** |
| **Engine** produces torque to restore speed | **Synchronous generator** terminal voltage is corrected |

When you go uphill, speed drops — you press the accelerator harder (more fuel). This is exactly analogous to when the load increases on the alternator, the terminal voltage droops, and the AVR pushes **more excitation current** into the rotor field winding to push the voltage back up. The accelerator pedal in your car is the **exciter field winding** in the generator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight for KTU Exam:** Without an AVR, an alternator's terminal voltage would collapse under heavy lagging-power-factor (inductive) loads and rise dangerously under leading-power-factor (capacitive) loads. The AVR is the **"heartbeat"** of any stable power generation unit.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Generator rated terminal voltage (typical lab machines):** **415 V (3-phase)** or **220 V (single-phase)**
- **Field excitation voltage:** **110 V DC** or **220 V DC**
- **Standard steady-state voltage tolerance:** **± 5 %** of rated voltage
- **Response time (modern static AVR):** typically **< 50 ms**
- **IEEE Standard governing excitation systems:** **IEEE Std 421.1** and **IEEE Std 421.2**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block Architecture of the AVR

The AVR loop is composed of five distinct functional blocks, each with a specific transfer function. Understanding these blocks is mandatory for KTU viva and lab record writing.

**Step 1 — Voltage Sensing (Potential Transformer Step-Down)**
The three-phase terminal voltage is stepped down to a manageable level (typically **110 V AC**) using a Potential Transformer (PT). It is then rectified and filtered to produce a smooth DC signal proportional to the actual terminal voltage.

**Step 2 — Reference Voltage Setting**
A highly stable **Zener diode-based reference** provides the set-point voltage $V_{ref}$. In digital AVRs, this is a 16-bit DAC output. The reference represents the desired terminal voltage.

**Step 3 — Comparator / Error Detector**
The comparator subtracts the sensed voltage from the reference:

$$V_{error}(t) = V_{ref}(t) - V_{sense}(t)$$

This is the **heart of the feedback loop** — any deviation immediately produces a non-zero error.

**Step 4 — Amplifier and Controller (PID)**
A Proportional-Integral-Derivative controller amplifies the error. The **PI (Proportional-Integral)** action is most common because:
- **P-action** gives fast response
- **I-action** eliminates steady-state error
- **D-action** is rarely used due to noise amplification

**Step 5 — Exciter and Field Winding**
The amplified signal drives the **exciter armature** (in brushless systems) or directly feeds the **main rotor field winding** through slip rings (in static excitation systems).

---

### 2.2 Mathematical Modeling — The AVR Transfer Function Chain

Each block of the AVR has its own first-order transfer function. The total open-loop transfer function is their product.

| Block Element | Transfer Function $G(s)$ | Time Constant | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Amplifier | $G_A(s) = \dfrac{K_A}{1 + \tau_A s}$ | $\tau_A$ | **0.02 – 0.1 s** |
| Exciter | $G_E(s) = \dfrac{K_E}{1 + \tau_E s}$ | $\tau_E$ | **0.5 – 1.0 s** |
| Generator | $G_G(s) = \dfrac{K_G}{1 + \tau_G s}$ | $\tau_G$ | **1.0 – 2.0 s** |
| Sensor (PT + rectifier) | $G_S(s) = \dfrac{K_S}{1 + \tau_S s}$ | $\tau_S$ | **0.01 – 0.05 s** |
| PID Controller | $G_C(s) = K_P + \dfrac{K_I}{s} + K_D s$ | — | — |

Because the sensor time constant $\tau_S$ is very small, it is often neglected (approximated as unity gain $K_S \approx 1$) in KTU-level analysis.

The **closed-loop transfer function** of the complete AVR system is:

$$T(s) = \dfrac{G_C(s) \cdot G_A(s) \cdot G_E(s) \cdot G_G(s)}{1 + G_C(s) \cdot G_A(s) \cdot G_E(s) \cdot G_G(s) \cdot G_S(s)}$$

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants (Thermal, Hydro, Nuclear):** Every grid-connected alternator from 1 MW to 1500 MW relies on a digital AVR for voltage stability.
- **Standalone Diesel Generators:** Hospitals, data centers, and telecom towers use AVRs to protect sensitive electronics from voltage flicker.
- **Wind Turbine Generators (DFIG):** Modern doubly-fed induction generators use grid-side and rotor-side converters that mimic AVR behaviour.
- **Automotive Alternators:** The voltage regulator inside a car alternator is a compact electromechanical AVR controlling the rotor field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common viva question is *"What happens if the AVR fails?"* — The answer: The terminal voltage either rises (leading PF load → over-excitation without limit) or collapses (lagging PF load → under-excitation), triggering protective **over-voltage** or **under-voltage** relays to trip the circuit breaker.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_t$ | Terminal voltage (line-to-line) | Volts (V) | $V_t \in [0.95 V_{rated}, 1.05 V_{rated}]$ |
| $E_f$ | Induced EMF (internal) | Volts | $E_f = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T \, K_w$ |
| $V_{ref}$ | Reference (set-point) voltage | Volts | DC, derived from Zener |
| $V_e$ | Error voltage | Volts | $V_e = V_{ref} - V_{sense}$ |
| $I_f$ | Field (excitation) current | Amperes | Adjusted by AVR output |
| $K_A$ | Amplifier gain | dimensionless | **10 – 40** typical |
| $K_E$ | Exciter gain | dimensionless | **1 – 10** typical |
| $K_G$ | Generator gain | dimensionless | **0.7 – 1.0** typical |
| $\tau_A, \tau_E, \tau_G$ | Block time constants | seconds | Sum governs response |
| $K_P, K_I, K_D$ | PID parameters | varies | Tuned via Ziegler-Nichols |

> [!NOTE]
> The induced EMF equation $E_f$ in synchronous generators is the foundational link between **excitation current** and **terminal voltage** — every AVR ultimately manipulates $I_f$ to control $\phi$ (flux), which controls $E_f$.

$$E_f = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T \, K_w$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function of the AVR

**Objective:** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = \dfrac{V_t(s)}{V_{ref}(s)}$ for a simplified AVR with proportional control.

**Assumption:** For KTU-level analysis, we lump the amplifier + exciter into a single first-order block, and ignore the fast sensor dynamics. The generator is modeled as a first-order lag.

**Step 1 — Define individual block transfer functions**

The amplifier-exciter combined block:

$$G_{AE}(s) = \dfrac{K_{AE}}{1 + \tau_{AE} \, s}$$

where $K_{AE} = K_A \cdot K_E$ and $\tau_{AE} \approx \tau_A + \tau_E$ (dominant time constant approximation).

The generator block:

$$G_G(s) = \dfrac{K_G}{1 + \tau_G \, s}$$

The proportional controller gain is simply $K_P$.

**Step 2 — Write the forward path transfer function**

$$G(s) = K_P \cdot G_{AE}(s) \cdot G_G(s) = K_P \cdot \dfrac{K_{AE}}{1 + \tau_{AE} s} \cdot \dfrac{K_G}{1 + \tau_G s}$$

$$G(s) = \dfrac{K_P \, K_{AE} \, K_G}{(1 + \tau_{AE} s)(1 + \tau_G s)}$$

Let $K = K_P \, K_{AE} \, K_G$ (the total open-loop DC gain).

$$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{(1 + \tau_{AE} s)(1 + \tau_G s)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the standard negative-feedback closed-loop formula**

The general negative feedback formula:

$$T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \, H(s)}$$

For a unity-feedback voltage regulator, $H(s) = 1$.

$$T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the forward path**

$$T(s) = \dfrac{\dfrac{K}{(1 + \tau_{AE} s)(1 + \tau_G s)}}{1 + \dfrac{K}{(1 + \tau_{AE} s)(1 + \tau_G s)}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + \tau_{AE} s)(1 + \tau_G s)$**

$$T(s) = \dfrac{K}{(1 + \tau_{AE} s)(1 + \tau_G s) + K}$$

**Step 6 — Expand the denominator terms**

$$(1 + \tau_{AE} s)(1 + \tau_G s) = 1 + (\tau_{AE} + \tau_G) s + \tau_{AE} \tau_G s^2$$

Therefore:

$$T(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau_{AE} \tau_G s^2 + (\tau_{AE} + \tau_G) s + 1 + K}$$

**Step 7 — Final normalized second-order form**

$$T(s) = \dfrac{K}{(1+K)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\tau_{AE} + \tau_G}{1+K} s + \dfrac{\tau_{AE} \tau_G}{1+K} s^2}$$

This matches the canonical second-order system:

$$T(s) = \dfrac{K}{(1+K)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + 2 \zeta \tau_n s + \tau_n^2 s^2}$$

From which we can identify the **natural frequency** $\omega_n$ and **damping ratio** $\zeta$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{1+K}{\tau_{AE} \tau_G}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

$$\zeta = \dfrac{\tau_{AE} + \tau_G}{2 \sqrt{\tau_{AE} \tau_G (1+K)}}$$

These two parameters completely characterize the AVR transient response — overshoot, settling time, and steady-state error.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Voltage Regulation

**Definition:** Voltage Regulation is the percentage change in terminal voltage when the full load is removed (open-circuited) at constant speed and constant field excitation.

$$VR (\%) = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$$

For a synchronous generator, the internal phasor equation is:

$$\vec{E_f} = \vec{V_t} + \vec{I_a} (R_a + j X_s)$$

The magnitude:

$$E_f = \sqrt{(V_t \cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V_t \sin\phi \pm I_a X_s)^2}$$

The AVR's role is to ensure that $V_t$ remains constant regardless of $I_a$, by varying $I_f$ (which controls $E_f$).

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style Problem)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 5 kVA, 220 V, 50 Hz synchronous generator has a synchronous reactance $X_s = 4 \, \Omega$ per phase (star-connected), negligible armature resistance $R_a \approx 0$. It supplies a rated load at 0.8 lagging power factor. The AVR maintains terminal voltage constant at 220 V (line-to-line). Calculate the required internal EMF $E_f$ (line-to-neutral).

**Given Data:**
- $V_{LL} = 220 \text{ V}$
- $V_{ph} = \dfrac{220}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 127.02 \text{ V}$
- $S = 5000 \text{ VA}$
- $\cos\phi = 0.8 \Rightarrow \phi = 36.87°$
- $X_s = 4 \, \Omega$

**Step 1 — Compute rated phase current**

$$I_a = \dfrac{S}{3 \, V_{ph}} = \dfrac{5000}{3 \times 127.02} \approx 13.12 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose into real and reactive components**

$$I_a \cos\phi = 13.12 \times 0.8 = 10.50 \text{ A}$$
$$I_a \sin\phi = 13.12 \times 0.6 = 7.87 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Write the phasor equation component-wise**

Real axis: $E_f \cos\delta = V_t \cos\phi + I_a R_a$
Imaginary axis: $E_f \sin\delta = V_t \sin\phi + I_a X_s$

With $R_a = 0$:

$$E_f \cos\delta = 127.02 \times 0.8 = 101.62 \text{ V}$$
$$E_f \sin\delta = 127.02 \times 0.6 + 13.12 \times 4 = 76.21 + 52.49 = 128.70 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $E_f$ magnitude**

$$E_f = \sqrt{(101.62)^2 + (128.70)^2}$$

$$E_f = \sqrt{10326.6 + 16563.7} = \sqrt{26890.3} \approx 163.99 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to line-to-line**

$$E_{f(LL)} = \sqrt{3} \times 163.99 \approx 284.0 \text{ V}$$

**Conclusion:** When load increases (especially with lagging PF), the AVR must boost the excitation to increase $E_f$ from 127 V to ~164 V (phase) to keep the terminal voltage locked at 220 V line-to-line. This is precisely the dynamic action the AVR performs continuously.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Open-Loop Condition | With AVR (Closed-Loop) | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage regulation | 20 – 40 % | < 1 % | Direct measure of AVR effectiveness |
| Settling time | Slow / oscillatory | < 500 ms | Depends on $\zeta, \omega_n$ |
| Steady-state error | Large | ≈ 0 (with integral action) | PI controller removes offset |
| Sensitivity to load change | High | Very low | Robustness test |
| Overshoot | Uncontrolled | 5 – 15 % | Designed via $K_P, K_I$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop AVR System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVref["Vref Reference Setpoint DC"]
    nodeSum["Comparator Summing Junction"]
    nodeVerr["Error Signal Ve"]
    nodePID["PID Controller KP KI KD"]
    nodeAmp["Amplifier Gain KA"]
    nodeExc["Exciter Field Winding KE"]
    nodeGen["Synchronous Generator E f"]
    nodeSens["Voltage Sensor PT Rectifier"]
    nodeVt["Terminal Voltage Vt Actual"]
    nodeLoad["Three Phase Load R L C"]

    nodeVref --> nodeSum
    nodeVt --> nodeSens
    nodeSens --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeVerr
    nodeVerr --> nodePID
    nodePID --> nodeAmp
    nodeAmp --> nodeExc
    nodeExc --> nodeGen
    nodeGen --> nodeVt
    nodeVt --> nodeLoad
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU-style interpretation):**
- The **outer loop** is the negative feedback: actual terminal voltage $V_t$ is sensed, scaled, and fed back to be subtracted from $V_{ref}$.
- The **inner forward path** consists of PID → Amplifier → Exciter → Generator.
- The signal flows clockwise around the loop until the error $V_e = V_{ref} - V_{sense}$ becomes zero — at that moment, the system reaches **steady-state regulation**.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Lab Setup of AVR Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Source["DC Source for Field"]
        nodeDC["230 V AC Mains"]
        nodeRect["Diode Bridge Rectifier"]
        nodeFld["Rheostat Field Winding"]
        nodeDC --> nodeRect --> nodeFld
    end

    subgraph PrimeMover["Prime Mover Assembly"]
        nodeMtr["DC Shunt Motor"]
        nodeCoupling["Shaft Coupling"]
        nodeAlt["Synchronous Generator Alternator"]
        nodeMtr --> nodeCoupling --> nodeAlt
    end

    subgraph AVR["Automatic Voltage Regulator Unit"]
        nodePT["Potential Transformer 220 to 110 V"]
        nodeCmp["Comparator and Error Amplifier"]
        nodeTrig["Thyristor Firing Circuit"]
        nodeThyr["SCR Bank Controlled Rectifier"]
        nodePT --> nodeCmp --> nodeTrig --> nodeThyr
    end

    subgraph LoadBank["Variable Load Bank"]
        nodeL1["Resistive Load"]
        nodeL2["Inductive Load Reactor"]
        nodeL3["Capacitive Load Bank"]
    end

    nodeAlt --> nodePT
    nodeAlt --> nodeLoadBank
    nodeThyr --> nodeFld
```

---

### 4.3 Step Response Waveform — KTU Sketch Equivalent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Time t equals 0 Load Step Applied"]
    nodeB["V t drops to V min"]
    nodeC["AVR Excites Field I f Rises"]
    nodeD["V t Rises with Overshoot"]
    nodeE["V t Settles at V ref"]
    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE
```

This represents the classic AVR transient response after a sudden load application. The student should label:
- **Settling time** $t_s$
- **Peak overshoot** $M_p$
- **Steady-state value** $V_{ref}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In your KTU lab record, always sketch the response curve with the **time axis (seconds)** and **voltage axis (V)** clearly marked, and explicitly mark the overshoot $M_p$ and settling time $t_s$. Examiners reward these annotations with full marks.

---

### 4.4 Schematic — Practical Wiring of AVR Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL1["Phase A 220 V"]
    nodeL2["Phase B 220 V"]
    nodeL3["Phase C 220 V"]
    nodePT["PT 220 to 110 V Step Down"]
    nodeBridge["Full Wave Bridge Rectifier"]
    nodeCap["Filter Capacitor 100 microFarad"]
    nodeZener["Zener Reference 5.1 V"]
    nodeOpAmp["OpAmp Error Detector LM741"]
    nodeTrig["UJT or TCA785 Firing Circuit"]
    nodeSCR["SCR Bridge Feeding Field"]
    nodeField["Alternator Rotor Field Winding"]

    nodeL1 --> nodePT
    nodeL2 --> nodePT
    nodePT --> nodeBridge
    nodeBridge --> nodeCap
    nodeCap --> nodeOpAmp
    nodeZener --> nodeOpAmp
    nodeOpAmp --> nodeTrig
    nodeTrig --> nodeSCR
    nodeSCR --> nodeField
```

This practical schematic explains why your lab kit has **op-amps, Zener diodes, UJT/TCA firing chips, and an SCR bridge** — they implement the five theoretical blocks of the AVR.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR) and state its primary function in a synchronous generator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR) is a closed-loop feedback control system used in synchronous generators that automatically maintains the terminal voltage at a constant preset value. Its primary function is to **sense the terminal voltage**, **compare** it with a reference, and **adjust the DC field excitation current** of the rotor such that any deviation caused by load, power-factor, or speed variations is corrected in real-time. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sensing-comparing-correcting loop, 1 mark for excitation adjustment)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] List the main functional blocks of an AVR system and briefly explain the role of the comparator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The main functional blocks are:
1. **Voltage sensor** (PT + rectifier) — senses terminal voltage
2. **Comparator** — subtracts sensed voltage from reference to produce error signal
3. **Amplifier / PID controller** — boosts and shapes the error signal
4. **Exciter** — DC source that supplies field winding
5. **Generator field winding** — receives controlled excitation

The **comparator** computes the error voltage $V_e = V_{ref} - V_{sense}$. This error is the driving signal of the entire control loop; its magnitude and sign dictate whether excitation must increase or decrease. **(1 mark for list, 1 mark each for comparator role)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.A (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Automatic Voltage Regulator used in a synchronous generator. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The AVR consists of five functional blocks forming a negative-feedback closed loop:

1. **Voltage Sensing Block:** A potential transformer steps down the terminal voltage, and a bridge rectifier converts it to DC proportional to actual terminal voltage $V_t$.

2. **Reference Block:** A temperature-compensated Zener diode provides a stable DC reference $V_{ref}$ corresponding to the desired set-point voltage.

3. **Comparator:** An operational amplifier (LM741 or equivalent) subtracts $V_{sense}$ from $V_{ref}$, producing the error signal $V_e = V_{ref} - V_{sense}$.

4. **Amplifier with PID Controller:** The error is amplified and shaped. The proportional term gives immediate correction, the integral term eliminates steady-state offset.

5. **Exciter and Field Winding:** The amplified signal modulates the firing angle of an SCR bridge feeding the DC field winding of the rotor. Increased firing (earlier firing) → higher $I_f$ → higher flux $\phi$ → higher induced EMF $E_f$ → higher terminal voltage $V_t$.

**Working Principle:** Whenever load increases or PF becomes more lagging, $V_t$ drops momentarily, $V_e$ becomes positive, the SCR firing advances, $I_f$ increases, and $V_t$ is restored. The opposite occurs when load decreases. **(Full 7 marks: 2 for diagram description, 3 for block explanation, 2 for closed-loop working principle)**

---

**Q.A (b)** A 3-phase, 10 kVA, 400 V, 50 Hz star-connected synchronous generator has synchronous reactance $X_s = 6 \, \Omega$ per phase and negligible armature resistance. It supplies a full-load current at 0.7 power factor lagging. Calculate (i) the induced EMF per phase, and (ii) the percentage voltage regulation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $S = 10000$ VA, $V_{LL} = 400$ V, $X_s = 6 \, \Omega$, $\cos\phi = 0.7$.

**Step 1 — Phase voltage and rated current**
$$V_{ph} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V}$$
$$I_a = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL}} = \dfrac{10000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400} = 14.43 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Compute real and reactive drops**
$$V_{ph} \cos\phi = 230.94 \times 0.7 = 161.66 \text{ V}$$
$$V_{ph} \sin\phi = 230.94 \times 0.714 = 164.89 \text{ V}$$
$$I_a X_s = 14.43 \times 6 = 86.58 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Induced EMF (per phase)**
$$E_f = \sqrt{(V_{ph}\cos\phi)^2 + (V_{ph}\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$
$$E_f = \sqrt{(161.66)^2 + (164.89 + 86.58)^2}$$
$$E_f = \sqrt{26134 + 63131.6} = \sqrt{89265.6} = 298.77 \text{ V per phase}$$

**Step 4 — No-load terminal voltage (per phase, line-to-line conversion)**
Since excitation is held constant, $E_f$ becomes the no-load voltage:
$$V_{NL(LL)} = \sqrt{3} \times 298.77 = 517.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage voltage regulation**
$$VR(\%) = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100 = \dfrac{517.5 - 400}{400} \times 100 = 29.38\%$$

**Conclusion:** A regulation of **29.38 %** is unacceptably high. This is precisely why an **AVR is essential** — without it, the terminal voltage would surge by nearly 30 % the moment the load is disconnected, damaging connected equipment. **(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1-4, 1 for step 5, 1 for conclusion)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q.B (a)** Explain the role of an exciter in an AVR system. Compare brushless excitation with static (thyristor-based) excitation systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Exciter (3 marks):**
The exciter is a DC source (in older systems, a small DC generator mounted on the same shaft as the main alternator; in modern systems, a thyristor-controlled rectifier fed from the alternator output) that supplies the field winding of the **main rotor**. It is the **final actuator** in the AVR forward path. The exciter receives the amplified error signal and translates it into a proportional DC field current $I_f$. Its time constant $\tau_E$ (typically 0.5–1.0 s) is the slowest block in the AVR loop and dominates the dynamic response.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Brushless Excitation | Static (Thyristor) Excitation |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Rotating exciter + rotating diode bridge on shaft | Stationary transformer + thyristor rectifier |
| Slip rings / brushes | **Not required** | **Required** for field supply |
| Maintenance | Very low (no brush wear) | Higher (brush replacement needed) |
| Response time | Slower (field time constant dominant) | Very fast (< 50 ms) |
| Reliability | High | Moderate (thyristor failures possible) |
| Ceiling voltage | Limited by rotating diodes | High (up to 2× rated field voltage) |
| Cost | Higher initial cost | Lower cost, mature technology |
| Typical application | Large turbo-generators (> 100 MW) | Medium hydro and diesel sets |

---

**Q.B (b)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of an AVR system with proportional controller $K_P$, amplifier gain $K_A$, exciter gain $K_E$, and generator gain $K_G$, each modeled as first-order lags with time constants $\tau_E$ and $\tau_G$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Forward path**
$$G(s) = K_P \cdot K_A \cdot \dfrac{K_E}{1 + \tau_E s} \cdot \dfrac{K_G}{1 + \tau_G s} = \dfrac{K}{(1 + \tau_E s)(1 + \tau_G s)}$$

where $K = K_P \cdot K_A \cdot K_E \cdot K_G$.

**Step 2 — Closed-loop (unity feedback)**
$$T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \dfrac{K}{(1 + \tau_E s)(1 + \tau_G s) + K}$$

**Step 3 — Expand**
$$T(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau_E \tau_G s^2 + (\tau_E + \tau_G)s + (1 + K)}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to standard second-order form**
$$T(s) = \dfrac{K}{1+K} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\tau_E + \tau_G}{1+K}s + \dfrac{\tau_E \tau_G}{1+K}s^2}$$

**Step 5 — Identify natural frequency and damping ratio**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{1+K}{\tau_E \tau_G}}$$
$$\zeta = \dfrac{\tau_E + \tau_G}{2\sqrt{\tau_E \tau_G (1+K)}}$$

**Step 6 — DC gain (steady-state behavior)**
$$\lim_{s \to 0} T(s) = \dfrac{K}{1+K}$$

This shows that with proportional control alone, a **steady-state error** persists:

$$e_{ss} = 1 - \dfrac{K}{1+K} = \dfrac{1}{1+K}$$

To eliminate this, an **integral term** must be added (PI controller), which forces $e_{ss} \to 0$. **(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1-4, 1 for step 5, 1 for step 6 and conclusion)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $R_a$ drop:** Many students drop the $I_a R_a$ term or include it incorrectly in the phasor equation. Always double-check the sign convention.
> 2. **Mixing line-to-line and phase values:** Voltage regulation problems require consistent units — convert $V_{LL}$ to $V_{ph}$ using $V_{ph} = V_{LL}/\sqrt{3}$ before doing phasor arithmetic.
> 3. **Missing the feedback direction:** The AVR is a **negative feedback** system. Drawing the comparator with a '+' sign for both inputs will lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the PI vs P distinction:** A pure proportional controller cannot achieve zero steady-state error — students often forget this and lose marks on the transfer-function question.
> 5. **Lab record errors:** Always include the **ratings plate readings**, the **no-load characteristic curve**, and the **load characteristic curve** plotted in your lab record. Sketches without axis labels attract deductions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and review it the night before your exam.**

- [ ] **AVR** is a **closed-loop negative feedback** control system for synchronous generators.
- [ ] Five blocks: **Sensor → Comparator → Amplifier/PID → Exciter → Field winding**.
- [ ] The induced EMF equation $E_f = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T \, K_w$ is the foundation — flux $\phi$ is controlled via field current $I_f$.
- [ ] **Voltage Regulation** $VR(\%) = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100$. AVR reduces this from 20–40 % to < 1 %.
- [ ] **Closed-loop transfer function** is a second-order system characterized by $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$.
- [ ] **Proportional controller alone** gives steady-state error $\dfrac{1}{1+K}$ — must add **integral action**.
- [ ] **Brushless excitation** — no slip rings, slow response, high reliability; **Static excitation** — fast response, needs brushes.
- [ ] **AVR failure consequences**: under-voltage or over-voltage trip of the generator breaker.
- [ ] **Standard tolerance**: $\pm 5\%$ of rated terminal voltage as per IEEE/Indian standards.
- [ ] In the **lab experiment**, plot **Open-Circuit Characteristic (OCC)** and **Load Characteristic (LCC)**, and demonstrate voltage droop and restoration under step load changes.
- [ ] **Phasor equation to memorize**: $\vec{E_f} = \vec{V_t} + \vec{I_a}(R_a + jX_s)$.
- [ ] For lagging PF loads, AVR must **increase** excitation; for leading PF loads, AVR must **decrease** excitation.
- [ ] **Lab safety**: Always start the prime mover, build up voltage slowly, and never short-circuit the field winding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ferranti Effect and Reactive Power Compensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Ferranti Effect and Reactive Power Compensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ferranti Effect — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ferranti Effect** is the phenomenon in which the **receiving-end voltage (V_R)** of a transmission line rises above the **sending-end voltage (V_S)** when the line is operating under **no-load or very light load** conditions. This effect becomes prominent in **long, high-voltage (EHV/UHV) overhead transmission lines** because the **distributed line capacitance** draws a leading (capacitive) charging current that flows through the series inductance of the line, producing a voltage drop that *adds* to V_S rather than subtracting from it.

Mathematically, in per-unit (p.u.):

$$
V_R > V_S \quad \text{when} \quad I_L \approx 0 \quad \text{(light/no load)}
$$

The voltage rise at the receiving end is given by the classical expression:

$$
\% \text{ Voltage Rise} = \left( \frac{V_R - V_S}{V_S} \right) \times 100
$$

This effect was first observed by **Sebastian Ziani de Ferranti** in **1887** during the operation of a 10 kV AC transmission line from Deptford to London.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: The Garden Hose with a Kinked End**
> Imagine a long garden hose connected to a tap. Water flows inside the hose wall — this wall is **slightly elastic** (analogous to line inductance L). The water itself can be compressed microscopically (analogous to line capacitance C). Now, if you **close the nozzle** at the far end (no-load), the pressure at the closed end becomes *higher* than at the tap because the elastic hose wall pushes back against the trapped water. Open the nozzle (apply load), and the pressure equalizes.
>
> Similarly, in a transmission line, the **distributed capacitance** stores energy when the line is lightly loaded. The leading capacitive current produces a voltage drop **across the series inductance** that pushes the receiving-end voltage *above* the sending-end voltage. Apply a heavy inductive load, and the receiving-end voltage *falls* below sending-end voltage (the usual case).

**Key physical constants involved:**
- **System frequency**: f = **50 Hz** (India)
- **Angular frequency**: ω = **2πf = 314.159 rad/s**
- **Speed of light on line**: v ≈ **3 × 10⁵ km/s**

---

## 1.3 Reactive Power Compensation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactive Power Compensation** is the process of **managing (generating or absorbing)** reactive power (Q) in a power system using shunt or series-connected devices to **maintain voltage profile**, **improve power factor**, **reduce losses**, and **enhance system stability**. In the context of the Ferranti effect, reactive power compensation is used to **absorb the excess charging reactive power (Q_C)** of a lightly-loaded line, thereby preventing over-voltage at the receiving end.

The fundamental reactive power balance at any bus:

$$
Q_{generated} = Q_{load} + Q_{losses} - Q_{compensation}
$$

Common compensation devices used in KTU-syllabus experiments:

| Device | Type | Function |
|--------|------|----------|
| **Shunt Reactor** | Shunt | Absorbs Q (over-voltage mitigation) |
| **Shunt Capacitor Bank** | Shunt | Supplies Q (under-voltage support) |
| **Series Capacitor** | Series | Reduces effective X_L |
| **Synchronous Condenser** | Shunt | Variable Q source/sink |
| **SVC (Static VAR Compensator)** | Shunt | Fast, dynamic Q control |
| **STATCOM** | Shunt | Self-commutated, rapid response |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Distributed Capacitance Exists**: Every transmission line has capacitance between conductors and to ground. This capacitance is uniformly distributed along the line (not lumped at one point).
2. **Charging Current Flows**: Even at no-load, the line voltage drives a **leading capacitive current** through this distributed capacitance: $I_C = j\omega C V$.
3. **Voltage Drop Across Series Inductance**: This leading current passes through the line's series inductance $L$, producing a voltage drop $V_L = j\omega L \cdot I_C$.
4. **Vectorial Addition**: The phasor sum shows that this drop *raises* V_R above V_S because the drop is **in phase with V_S** (due to the 90° phase shifts canceling).
5. **Critical Length Effect**: For very short lines, capacitance is negligible → no Ferranti effect. For lines longer than approximately **80 km** at 110 kV (or **30 km** at 400 kV), the effect becomes significant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why load current masks the effect**: Under heavy load, the lagging inductive current produces a voltage drop that *lowers* V_R. This drop dominates over the Ferranti rise, so V_R < V_S. The Ferranti effect is only visible under **light load**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly usable in KTU exams** for the PCEEL609 experiment.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Line series impedance | $Z = R + jX_L = (r + j\omega L)\,\ell$ | Ω |
| 2 | Line shunt admittance | $Y = j\omega C\,\ell$ | Siemens (S) |
| 3 | Propagation constant | $\gamma = \sqrt{ZY} = \alpha + j\beta$ | per km |
| 4 | Surge impedance | $Z_c = \sqrt{Z/Y} = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Ω (≈ 400 Ω for OH line) |
| 5 | Wavelength | $\lambda = v/f = 1/(f\sqrt{LC})$ | km |
| 6 | ABCD params (nominal-π) | $A = D = 1 + ZY/2$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | ABCD params (nominal-π) | $B = Z\left(1 + ZY/4\right)$ | Ω |
| 8 | ABCD params (nominal-π) | $C = Y\left(1 + ZY/4\right)$ | S |
| 9 | Receiving-end voltage (no-load) | $V_R = V_S / A$ | V |
| 10 | Ferranti rise (lossless line) | $V_R/V_S \approx 1/\cos(\beta\ell)$ | pu |
| 11 | Charging reactive power | $Q_C = \omega C \ell V^2 = V^2/X_C$ | VAR |
| 12 | Shunt reactor rating | $Q_{reactor} = Q_C - Q_{load}$ | VAR |
| 13 | SIL (Surge Impedance Loading) | $SIL = V^2 / Z_c$ | MW |
| 14 | Voltage regulation | $\%\text{Reg} = (V_{NL} - V_{FL})/V_{FL} \times 100$ | % |
| 15 | Per-unit voltage rise | $\Delta V_{pu} \approx (X_C - X_L)/X_C$ | pu |

---

## 2.3 Practical Engineering Utility

- **EHV Grid Planning**: At **400 kV and 765 kV**, lines longer than **200 km** absolutely require **shunt reactors** to limit Ferranti rise within statutory limits (typically **+5% to +10%** of nominal).
- **Cable Systems**: Underground cables have **~20× higher capacitance** than overhead lines → Ferranti effect is severe even at **33 kV** for cables longer than a few km.
- **Reactive Power Management**: Reactive power compensation reduces $I^2R$ losses, frees up line thermal capacity, and allows real power (P) transfer closer to the thermal limit.
- **Renewable Integration**: Modern wind/solar farms use **STATCOMs** for dynamic reactive support — directly tested in advanced lab setups.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ferranti Effect Using Nominal-π Model

### Setup

Consider a **medium-length transmission line** represented by the **nominal-π equivalent circuit**:
- Total series impedance: $Z = R + jX_L$ lumped at the centre
- Total shunt admittance: $Y = jB_C$ split equally, $Y/2$ at each end

### ABCD Parameter Framework

By definition of two-port network:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
V_S \\ I_S
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
A & B \\ C & D
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
V_R \\ I_R
\end{bmatrix}
$$

For the nominal-π model, applying KVL and KCL:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_S &= V_R + Z \cdot I_{series} \\
I_{series} &= I_R + (Y/2) V_R \\
\Rightarrow V_S &= V_R + Z\left[I_R + \frac{Y}{2}V_R\right] \\
V_S &= \underbrace{\left(1 + \frac{ZY}{2}\right)}_{A}\, V_R \;+\; \underbrace{Z}_{B}\, I_R
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_S &= (Y/2) V_S + I_{series} \\
    &= (Y/2) V_S + I_R + (Y/2) V_R \\
    &= (Y/2)[V_S + V_R] + I_R
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $V_S$ and simplifying:

$$
I_S = \underbrace{\left(Y + \frac{ZY^2}{4}\right)}_{C}\, V_R + \underbrace{\left(1 + \frac{ZY}{2}\right)}_{D}\, I_R
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,A = D = 1 + \dfrac{ZY}{2}, \qquad B = Z\left(1 + \dfrac{ZY}{4}\right), \qquad C = Y\left(1 + \dfrac{ZY}{4}\right)\,}
$$

### Applying No-Load Condition ($I_R = 0$)

Under no-load (open-circuited receiving end), the receiving-end current is zero. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_S &= A \cdot V_R + B \cdot 0 = A V_R \\
\Rightarrow V_R &= \frac{V_S}{A} = \frac{V_S}{1 + ZY/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Simplifying for a Lossless Line ($R = 0$)

For an overhead EHV line, $R \ll X_L$. Taking $Z = jX_L$ and $Y = jB_C$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ZY &= (jX_L)(jB_C) = -X_L B_C \\
A &= 1 + \frac{ZY}{2} = 1 - \frac{X_L B_C}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The magnitude of A becomes:

$$
|A| = 1 - \frac{X_L B_C}{2} < 1 \quad \text{(since } X_L B_C > 0\text{)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
|V_R| = \frac{|V_S|}{|A|} = \frac{|V_S|}{1 - X_L B_C / 2} > |V_S|
$$

**This proves Ferranti Effect: $V_R > V_S$ under no-load.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Distributed Parameter (Long Line) Treatment

### Telegrapher's Equations

For an *infinitesimally short* line element $dx$ at distance $x$ from the receiving end:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{dV(x)}{dx} &= z\, I(x) = (r + j\omega L)\, I(x) \\
-\frac{dI(x)}{dx} &= y\, V(x) = (j\omega C)\, V(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating the first and substituting the second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} &= -z \frac{dI}{dx} = z y V = \gamma^2 V \\
\Rightarrow V(x) &= A_1 e^{\gamma x} + A_2 e^{-\gamma x}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\gamma = \sqrt{zy} = \alpha + j\beta$ is the **propagation constant**.

### Boundary Conditions

At $x = 0$: $V(0) = V_R$, $\frac{dV}{dx}\Big|_0 = -z I(0) = -z I_R$

Solving yields the **hyperbolic form**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(x) &= V_R \cosh(\gamma x) + I_R Z_c \sinh(\gamma x) \\
I(x) &= I_R \cosh(\gamma x) + \frac{V_R}{Z_c}\sinh(\gamma x)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Z_c = \sqrt{z/y}$ is the **surge (characteristic) impedance**.

### At No-Load ($I_R = 0$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_S &= V_R \cosh(\gamma \ell) \\
\Rightarrow \frac{V_R}{V_S} &= \frac{1}{\cosh(\gamma \ell)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **lossless line** ($\alpha = 0$, $\gamma = j\beta$):

$$
\cosh(j\beta \ell) = \cos(\beta \ell)
$$

So:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{V_R}{V_S} = \frac{1}{\cos(\beta \ell)} \quad \text{(Ferranti effect, exact)}\,}
$$

Since $0 < \beta\ell < \pi/2$ for practical lines, $\cos(\beta\ell) < 1$, confirming $V_R > V_S$.

**Approximation for short/medium lines** ($\beta\ell \ll 1$):

$$
\frac{V_R}{V_S} \approx \frac{1}{1 - (\beta\ell)^2/2} \approx 1 + \frac{(\beta\ell)^2}{2} = 1 + \frac{\omega^2 L C\, \ell^2}{2}
$$

This matches the lumped-parameter result $A = 1 - \omega^2 LC/2$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

### Problem Statement
A **three-phase, 50 Hz, 220 kV** transmission line is **250 km** long. The line parameters per phase per km are:
- $r = 0.1\ \Omega/\text{km}$
- $L = 1.2\ \text{mH/km}$
- $C = 9.5\ \text{nF/km}$

The line is energized at the sending end with **V_S = 220 kV (line-to-line)** and operates at **no-load**. Find:
1. The total Z and Y of the line (per phase)
2. ABCD parameters (nominal-π)
3. Receiving-end voltage V_R (line-to-line)
4. Percentage voltage rise
5. Charging reactive power generated by the line

### Solution

**Step 1 — Per-km parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_L &= \omega L = 2\pi(50)(1.2 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.3770\ \Omega/\text{km} \\
B_C &= \omega C = 2\pi(50)(9.5 \times 10^{-9}) = 2.985 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{S/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total parameters (per phase):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= (r + jX_L)\ell = (0.1 + j0.3770)(250) = 25.0 + j94.25\ \Omega \\
|Z| &= \sqrt{25.0^2 + 94.25^2} = 97.51\ \Omega,\quad \angle Z = 75.16^\circ \\
Y &= jB_C \ell = j(2.985 \times 10^{-6})(250) = j7.4625 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ZY &= (25.0 + j94.25)(j7.4625 \times 10^{-4}) \\
   &= j(25.0)(7.4625 \times 10^{-4}) + j^2(94.25)(7.4625 \times 10^{-4}) \\
   &= -7.034 \times 10^{-2} + j1.866 \times 10^{-2} \\
ZY/2 &= -3.517 \times 10^{-2} + j9.329 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
A = 1 + ZY/2 = 0.9648 + j9.329 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
|A| = \sqrt{0.9648^2 + 0.009329^2} \approx 0.9648,\quad \angle A = 0.554^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Receiving-end voltage (per phase):**

$$
V_R(\text{phase}) = \frac{V_S(\text{phase})}{|A|} = \frac{220/\sqrt{3}}{0.9648} = \frac{127.02}{0.9648} = 131.65\ \text{kV}
$$

**Line-to-line:**

$$
V_R(L-L) = \sqrt{3} \times 131.65 = 228.03\ \text{kV}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage voltage rise:**

$$
\%\text{Rise} = \frac{228.03 - 220.00}{220.00} \times 100 = 3.65\%
$$

**Step 6 — Charging reactive power (3-phase):**

$$
Q_C = 3 \times V_{R,\text{phase}}^2 \times B_C \times \ell = 3 \times (131.65 \times 10^3)^2 \times 7.4625 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
Q_C = 3 \times 1.733 \times 10^{10} \times 7.4625 \times 10^{-4} = 3.881 \times 10^{7}\ \text{VAR} \approx 38.81\ \text{MVAR}
$$

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Z (per phase) | $25.0 + j94.25\ \Omega$ |
| Y (per phase) | $j7.4625 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{S}$ |
| A | $0.9648 \angle 0.554^\circ$ |
| V_R (L-L) | **228.03 kV** |
| % Voltage Rise | **3.65%** |
| Q_C (3-phase) | **38.81 MVAR** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Shunt reactor rating needed** to fully compensate: $Q_{reactor} \approx 38.81$ MVAR (to bring V_R back to 220 kV exactly).
