# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Ferranti effect. Under what operating conditions is it most prominently observed?**

**Model Answer:**
> The Ferranti effect is the phenomenon in which the receiving-end voltage of a transmission line exceeds the sending-end voltage under no-load or very light load conditions. It occurs because the leading charging current drawn by the distributed line capacitance flows through the line's series inductance, producing a voltage drop that adds to (rather than subtracts from) the sending-end voltage. The effect is most prominent in **long, high-voltage (EHV/UHV) overhead lines** (>200 km at 220 kV and above) and **underground cables** (where capacitance is much higher).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three methods of reactive power compensation used to mitigate the Ferranti effect in EHV transmission lines.**

**Model Answer:**
> Three commonly used reactive power compensation methods to mitigate the Ferranti effect are:
> 1. **Shunt Reactors** — Connected at the receiving end or intermediate substations to absorb the excess charging reactive power Q_C.
> 2. **Synchronous Condensers** — Over-excited or under-excited synchronous machines that can absorb or supply reactive power dynamically.
> 3. **Static VAR Compensators (SVCs) / STATCOMs** — Power-electronic devices providing fast, continuously variable reactive power support.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 400 kV transmission line is 300 km long. The line parameters per phase per km are r = 0.03 Ω, L = 1.0 mH, and C = 11.0 nF. Using the nominal-π model, calculate:**

**(a)** The ABCD parameters of the line and the receiving-end voltage under no-load, when V_S = 400 kV (line-to-line). (7 Marks)

**(b)** The percentage Ferranti voltage rise, the charging reactive power generated by the line, and the rating of the shunt reactor required to bring V_R back to exactly 400 kV. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute per-km impedance and admittance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_L &= \omega L = 2\pi(50)(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.3142\ \Omega/\text{km} \\
B_C &= \omega C = 2\pi(50)(11.0 \times 10^{-9}) = 3.456 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{S/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Z and Y for line length ℓ = 300 km:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= (r + jX_L)\ell = (0.03 + j0.3142)(300) = 9.0 + j94.25\ \Omega \\
Y &= jB_C \ell = j(3.456 \times 10^{-6})(300) = j1.0368 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ZY:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ZY &= (9.0 + j94.25)(j1.0368 \times 10^{-3}) \\
   &= j(9.0)(1.0368 \times 10^{-3}) - (94.25)(1.0368 \times 10^{-3}) \\
   &= -9.773 \times 10^{-2} + j9.331 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — ABCD parameters (nominal-π):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 1 + \frac{ZY}{2} = 1 + \frac{-0.09773 + j0.009331}{2} \\
  &= 1 - 0.04886 + j0.004665 \\
  &= 0.95114 + j0.004665 \\
|A| &= \sqrt{0.95114^2 + 0.004665^2} \approx 0.9511,\quad \angle A = 0.281^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
B = Z\left(1 + \frac{ZY}{4}\right) = (9.0 + j94.25)\left(1 + \frac{-0.09773 + j0.009331}{4}\right)
$$

$$
1 + \frac{ZY}{4} = 1 - 0.02443 + j0.002333 = 0.97557 + j0.002333
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= (9.0 + j94.25)(0.97557 + j0.002333) \\
  &= (9.0)(0.97557) + (9.0)(j0.002333) + (j94.25)(0.97557) + (j94.25)(j0.002333) \\
  &= 8.780 + j0.0210 + j91.946 - 0.2199 \\
  &= 8.560 + j91.967 \\
B &\approx 92.36\ \angle 84.69^\circ\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
C = Y\left(1 + \frac{ZY}{4}\right) = (j1.0368 \times 10^{-3})(0.97557 + j0.002333)
$$

$$
C = j1.0115 \times 10^{-3} - 2.419 \times 10^{-6} \approx j1.011 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{S}
$$

**Step 5 — Receiving-end voltage at no-load (I_R = 0):**

$$
V_R(\text{phase}) = \frac{V_S(\text{phase})}{|A|} = \frac{400/\sqrt{3}}{0.9511} = \frac{230.94}{0.9511} = 242.82\ \text{kV/phase}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_R(L\text{-}L) = \sqrt{3} \times 242.82 = 420.57\ \text{kV}}
$$

**(b) Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Percentage voltage rise:**

$$
\%\text{Rise} = \frac{420.57 - 400.00}{400.00} \times 100 = \boxed{5.14\%}
$$

**Step 2 — Charging reactive power:**

$$
Q_C = 3 \times V_{R,\text{phase}}^2 \times B_C \times \ell
$$

$$
Q_C = 3 \times (242.82 \times 10^3)^2 \times (1.0368 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
Q_C = 3 \times 5.896 \times 10^{10} \times 1.0368 \times 10^{-3} = 1.834 \times 10^{8}\ \text{VAR}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_C \approx 183.4\ \text{MVAR}}
$$

**Step 3 — Shunt reactor rating:**

To bring V_R back to 400 kV exactly, the reactor must absorb the entire charging reactive power:

$$
\boxed{Q_{reactor} \approx 183.4\ \text{MVAR (3-phase total)}}
$$

In practice, a reactor of **~80% of Q_C (≈ 147 MVAR)** is used to allow for some natural load and maintain voltage at slightly above 400 kV.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a) Derive an expression for the receiving-end voltage of a long transmission line using the hyperbolic (distributed parameter) form. Hence, obtain the simplified expression for the Ferranti effect on a lossless line. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A 380 kV, 50 Hz, 250 km overhead line has L = 1.2 mH/km and C = 10 nF/km. Assuming a lossless line, compute the propagation constant β, the wavelength λ, and the no-load receiving-end voltage when V_S = 380 kV. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (already shown in Section 3.2 above for full credit):**

Key steps the student must reproduce:

1. Write telegrapher's equations: $-dV/dx = zI$ and $-dI/dx = yV$.
2. Combine to obtain $d^2V/dx^2 = \gamma^2 V$ where $\gamma^2 = zy$.
3. General solution: $V(x) = A_1 e^{\gamma x} + A_2 e^{-\gamma x}$.
4. Apply boundary conditions at $x = 0$: $V(0) = V_R$ and $I(0) = I_R$.
5. Obtain: $V(x) = V_R\cosh(\gamma x) + I_R Z_c \sinh(\gamma x)$ where $Z_c = \sqrt{z/y}$.
6. At sending end ($x = \ell$): $V_S = V_R\cosh(\gamma\ell) + I_R Z_c\sinh(\gamma\ell)$.
7. Under no-load $I_R = 0$: $V_S = V_R\cosh(\gamma\ell) \Rightarrow V_R/V_S = 1/\cosh(\gamma\ell)$.
8. For lossless line: $\gamma = j\beta$, $\cosh(j\beta\ell) = \cos(\beta\ell)$.
9. **Final expression:** $\boxed{V_R/V_S = 1/\cos(\beta\ell)}$

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute β:**

For a lossless line: $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$

$$
\beta = 2\pi(50)\sqrt{(1.2 \times 10^{-3})(10 \times 10^{-9})} = 314.159 \times \sqrt{1.2 \times 10^{-11}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{1.2 \times 10^{-11}} = 3.464 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\beta = 314.159 \times 3.464 \times 10^{-6} = 1.088 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{rad/km}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute wavelength:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \frac{2\pi}{1.088 \times 10^{-3}} = \boxed{5774\ \text{km}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute βℓ:**

$$
\beta\ell = (1.088 \times 10^{-3})(250) = 0.2720\ \text{rad} = 15.59^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Compute V_R:**

$$
\frac{V_R}{V_S} = \frac{1}{\cos(0.2720)} = \frac{1}{0.9632} = 1.0382
$$

$$
V_R = 1.0382 \times 380 = \boxed{394.5\ \text{kV}}
$$

**Voltage rise = 14.5 kV = 3.82%**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the √3 factor** when converting phase voltage to line-to-line (or vice versa). Always state which quantity you are computing.
> 2. **Using Z and Y as per-km values instead of total** (multiplied by line length ℓ). This error gives wrong answers by orders of magnitude.
> 3. **Confusing A with V_R/V_S** — remember: under no-load, $V_R = V_S/A$, NOT $V_R = V_S \cdot A$.
> 4. **Ignoring the angle of A** — although $|V_R/V_S| = 1/|A|$, students often wrongly compute $|1 - X_L B_C/2|$ as a complex magnitude.
> 5. **Sign error in ZY**: For lossless line, $Z = jX_L$ and $Y = jB_C$, so $ZY = -X_L B_C$ (negative real). Missing this gives $|A| > 1$, which is WRONG.
> 6. **Forgetting to multiply charging Q by 3** — Q_C is per phase; the 3-phase total needs the factor of 3.
> 7. **Shunt reactor rating confusion**: Reactor rating = Q_C only if you want zero rise; partial compensation is more practical (60–80% of Q_C).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ferranti Effect** = $V_R > V_S$ at no-load/light load; caused by line capacitance charging current through series inductance.
- **More severe in** long lines, EHV/UHV lines, and underground cables (higher C).
- **No-load condition** is the worst case — heavy loads mask the effect.
- **Exact expression (lossless long line):** $V_R/V_S = 1/\cos(\beta\ell)$
- **Approximate expression (lumped, short line):** $V_R/V_S \approx 1/(1 - \omega^2 LC\ell^2/2)$
- **Nominal-π ABCD parameters:** $A = D = 1 + ZY/2$, $B = Z(1 + ZY/4)$, $C = Y(1 + ZY/4)$
- **Charging reactive power** of a line: $Q_C = \omega C \ell V^2$ per phase (multiply by 3 for 3-phase total).
- **Shunt reactor** absorbs Q_C → most common fix for Ferranti effect in EHV grids.
- **Synchronous condenser** provides variable, controllable Q; rotating machine, slower response.
- **SVC/STATCOM** are static, fast-response devices using thyristors / IGBTs.
- **Surge Impedance Loading (SIL)**: $V^2/Z_c$ — at SIL, the line is naturally compensated; below SIL → Ferranti; above SIL → voltage drop.
- **Per-unit calculation tip:** Always work in per-unit for grid-scale problems; one base voltage simplifies Z and Y.
- **KTU lab observation**: In a transmission line simulator, V_R typically rises by **5–10%** at no-load for a 200–400 km equivalent model.
- **Mitigation in practice**: Switched shunt reactors (on/off as load varies) + fixed reactors for EHV lines; series capacitors rarely used solely for Ferranti mitigation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I-V Characteristics of PV Module and Maximum Power Point Determination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** I-V Characteristics of PV Module and Maximum Power Point Determination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Photovoltaic (PV) Module** is a packaged, interconnected assembly of multiple solar cells (typically 36 or 72 in series) that converts incident solar irradiance directly into direct current (DC) electrical energy through the **photovoltaic effect** discovered by Becquerel (1839). The **I–V (Current–Voltage) Characteristics** of a PV module describe the non-linear, weather-dependent relationship between the terminal current and terminal voltage of the module under specific irradiance (G, in W/m²) and cell temperature (T, in °C) conditions. **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** is defined as the unique operating point on the I–V curve where the product of voltage and current (and hence the output power) attains its absolute maximum value, denoted as **P_max (in Watts)**. The determination of MPP is a fundamental experimental task in PV system engineering because it directly dictates the energy harvest efficiency of the module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment requires plotting the I–V curve, identifying the short-circuit current (I_sc), open-circuit voltage (V_oc), and the MPP (V_mp, I_mp), and computing the **Fill Factor (FF)** and **Conversion Efficiency (η)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap connected to a municipal pipe**:
- The **water pressure** at the tap = **Voltage (V)** of the PV module
- The **flow rate of water** = **Current (I)** of the PV module
- The **total power delivered** = Pressure × Flow Rate (i.e., V × I)

If you **fully open the tap** (short-circuit condition, V = 0), water gushes out at maximum flow (I = I_sc), but since the pressure is zero, the useful work done is zero. Conversely, if you **completely close the tap** (open-circuit condition, I = 0), the pressure builds up to its maximum (V = V_oc), but again no water flows, so work done is zero. The **maximum power** is delivered when the tap is **partially open** at a sweet spot — this is the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** of the PV module, conceptually the "Goldilocks zone" where pressure and flow multiply to the highest value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A PV module never operates at I_sc or V_oc in practice — both extremes yield zero power. Real-world operation is always at an intermediate point where the product V × I is maximized. This is what makes MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) a critical subsystem in every solar inverter.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Value (1 kW module) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-Circuit Current** | **I_sc** | **A** | 8 – 9 A |
| **Open-Circuit Voltage** | **V_oc** | **V** | 37 – 45 V |
| **Maximum Power Voltage** | **V_mp** | **V** | 30 – 36 V |
| **Maximum Power Current** | **I_mp** | **A** | 7.5 – 8.5 A |
| **Maximum Power** | **P_max** | **W** | 250 – 400 W |
| **Fill Factor** | **FF** | dimensionless | 0.65 – 0.80 |
| **Module Efficiency** | **η** | **%** | 15 – 22 % |

The physical constants universally used in PV cell modeling are the **Boltzmann constant k = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** and the **electronic charge q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Photovoltaic Effect

When photons of energy **E_ph = hν** (where h = Planck's constant, ν = photon frequency) strike a p–n junction semiconductor (typically monocrystalline or polycrystalline silicon), the following sequence occurs:

1. **Photon Absorption:** A photon is absorbed by a valence-band electron if hν ≥ E_g (band-gap energy, 1.12 eV for Si at 300 K).
2. **Electron–Hole Pair Generation:** The absorbed energy excites the electron from the valence band to the conduction band, creating a free electron in the n-region and a free hole in the p-region.
3. **Charge Separation by the Junction Field:** The built-in electric field at the depletion region sweeps the electron toward the n-side and the hole toward the p-side, producing a **photo-generated current I_L** proportional to the incident irradiance G.
4. **Terminal Extraction:** The separated charges flow through an external load, delivering usable DC electrical power.

### 2.2 The Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit (Five-Parameter Model)

The standard electrical model used by KTU examiners for PV cell analysis is the **single-diode, five-parameter model**:

- **I_L** — Photo-generated current source (function of G and T)
- **I_D** — Diode current representing recombination losses
- **R_s** — Series resistance (representing ohmic losses in contacts, busbars, and silicon bulk)
- **R_sh** — Shunt (parallel) resistance (representing manufacturing defects and leakage paths)
- **Diode with ideality factor 'a'** — representing diffusion and recombination currents

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables / Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Output Current Equation (Single-Diode Model)** | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left( \dfrac{V + I R_s}{a \, N_s \, k T / q} \right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$ | I, I_L, I_0 in A; V in V; R_s, R_sh in Ω; a dimensionless; T in K; N_s = number of cells in series | Foundational I–V equation used to model and simulate the module |
| 2 | **Thermal Voltage** | $V_T = \dfrac{k T}{q} = \dfrac{T}{11600}$ | V_T in V; T in K (≈ 0.02585 V at 300 K) | Appears in every PV exponential term |
| 3 | **Photo-generated Current (Empirical)** | $I_L = \dfrac{G}{G_{STC}} \left[ I_{L,STC} + \mu_{I_{sc}} (T - T_{STC}) \right]$ | G in W/m²; μ in A/°C; T in °C or K | Predicts I_L change with weather |
| 4 | **Open-Circuit Voltage** | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{a \, N_s \, k T}{q} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)$ | V_oc in V | Determines V-axis intercept of I–V curve |
| 5 | **Maximum Power Point** | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | P_max in W; V_mp in V; I_mp in A | The "optimum" operating point |
| 6 | **Fill Factor** | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | FF dimensionless (0 ≤ FF ≤ 1) | Quantifies "squareness" of I–V curve |
| 7 | **Module Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \, I_{sc} \, FF}{G \cdot A}$ | η dimensionless (%); G in W/m²; A in m² | Performance benchmark for module quality |
| 8 | **Empirical Fill Factor Approximation** | $FF \approx \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$ | where $v_{oc} = V_{oc} / (N_s \, a \, kT/q)$ | Used when V_mp, I_mp are not measured |

> [!NOTE]
> **STC (Standard Test Conditions):** G = 1000 W/m², T_cell = 25 °C, AM = 1.5. All manufacturer datasheets are quoted at STC, and the experiment must be conducted as close to STC as possible.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar Inverters** continuously adjust the operating voltage of the PV array using MPPT algorithms (Perturb & Observe, Incremental Conductance) to lock the array at V_mp, boosting energy harvest by **20 – 30 %** compared to a fixed-voltage operation.
- **PV System Sizing:** Knowing V_oc helps in selecting the maximum number of modules per string such that the DC input voltage of the inverter is not exceeded at the lowest expected cell temperature (because V_oc rises sharply as T falls).
- **Performance Ratio (PR):** The FF and η obtained experimentally are inputs to the PR calculation, which is the key quality index of an installed PV plant.
- **Fault Diagnosis:** Degradation in FF over years of operation is a signature of solder-bond fatigue, PID (Potential Induced Degradation), or EVA browning — used by O&M teams to schedule maintenance.

### 2.5 Why and How of Each Step

- **Why R_s appears:** Real silicon has finite resistivity; metallization contacts and silver paste have resistance — these combine to "rob" voltage from the load.
- **Why R_sh appears:** Micro-cracks, edge shunts, and crystalline defects create parasitic current paths in parallel with the junction.
- **Why the exponential term:** The diode equation arises from Shockley's semiconductor physics — recombination of minority carriers across the junction follows the Boltzmann distribution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the PV Cell I–V Equation (KCL at the Output Node)

**Step 1 — Identify the three currents at the output node.**
Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the external terminal of the single-diode model:

$$
I_{L} = I + I_{D} + I_{sh}
$$

**Row Explanation:** The current I_L generated by the light source must exit through the load (I) and partially leak through the diode (I_D) and the shunt path (I_sh). KCL is the energy-conservation law here.

**Step 2 — Express the diode current using the Shockley Diode Equation.**

$$
I_{D} = I_0 \left[ \exp\left( \dfrac{V_{D}}{a \, V_T} \right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Row Explanation:** The diode carries a forward current driven by the voltage V_D across it, with reverse saturation current I_0. The minus one accounts for reverse saturation.

**Step 3 — Express the shunt current using Ohm's Law.**

$$
I_{sh} = \dfrac{V_{D}}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Row Explanation:** The shunt path is a pure resistor; current through it equals the voltage across it divided by R_sh.

**Step 4 — Express V_D in terms of terminal voltage and the series drop.**

The total voltage at the diode terminals equals the terminal voltage V plus the drop across the series resistance:

$$
V_{D} = V + I \, R_s
$$

**Row Explanation:** Because the series resistor is in series with the load, the diode sees a slightly higher voltage than the load terminal.

**Step 5 — Substitute Steps 2, 3, and 4 into Step 1.**

$$
I_{L} = I + I_0 \left[ \exp\left( \dfrac{V + I R_s}{a \, V_T} \right) - 1 \right] + \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to obtain the standard I–V equation.**

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left( \dfrac{V + I R_s}{a \, V_T} \right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Row Explanation:** This is the explicit, implicit (because I appears on both sides) equation that defines the I–V curve of a single PV cell. For a module with N_s cells in series, replace V_T with N_s · V_T and I with the module current.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Fill Factor (FF)

**Step 1 — Define FF as the ratio of the rectangular area of the maximum-power rectangle to the area of the I_sc × V_oc rectangle.**

$$
FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric Interpretation.**
On the I–V plot, V_oc · I_sc forms a hypothetical rectangle filling the area to the top-left corner. The actual operating rectangle (V_mp × I_mp) inscribed under the curve is always smaller. The ratio FF is the proportion of the rectangle that is "filled" by the actual MPP rectangle — hence the name **Fill Factor**.

**Step 3 — Typical Numerical Estimation.**
For a crystalline-silicon module: V_oc = 45 V, I_sc = 8.5 A, V_mp = 36 V, I_mp = 8.0 A, P_max = 288 W.

$$
FF = \dfrac{36 \times 8.0}{45 \times 8.5} = \dfrac{288}{382.5} = 0.753
$$

**Row Explanation:** A high FF ≈ 0.75 indicates a "square" I–V curve (good diode, low R_s, high R_sh). A low FF ≈ 0.50 indicates severe ohmic losses.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Module Efficiency (η)

**Step 1 — Definition of Efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\text{Electrical Power Output}}{\text{Solar Power Input}} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express P_in in terms of irradiance and module area.**

$$
P_{in} = G \cdot A \quad [\text{W}]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute P_max using FF, V_oc, I_sc.**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example.**
For the same module: A = 1.65 m², G = 1000 W/m², V_oc = 45 V, I_sc = 8.5 A, FF = 0.753.

$$
P_{in} = 1000 \times 1.65 = 1650 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{45 \times 8.5 \times 0.753}{1650} = \dfrac{288}{1650} = 0.1745 = 17.45 \,\%
$$

**Row Explanation:** A modern commercial module with 17 – 22 % efficiency is considered competitive; values below 12 % indicate a degraded or low-quality module.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Conditions at Key Operating Points

| Operating Point | Terminal Condition | Voltage | Current | Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-Circuit (SC)** | Output terminals shorted | V = 0 | I = I_sc | P = 0 |
| **Open-Circuit (OC)** | Output terminals open | V = V_oc | I = 0 | P = 0 |
| **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** | dP/dV = 0 | V = V_mp | I = I_mp | P = P_max (peak) |
| **Current Source Region (V < V_mp)** | I ≈ I_L (nearly constant) | Low V | I near I_sc | P rising |
| **Voltage Source Region (V > V_mp)** | I drops exponentially | High V | I near 0 | P falling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The condition for the MPP is mathematically **dP/dV = 0**. Since P = V·I, differentiating and using dI/dV from the I–V equation yields an implicit transcendental equation that is solved numerically in MPPT controllers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the I–V Characteristic Measurement Setup

The physical laboratory setup is mapped here as a block diagram showing the signal and power flow between the PV module, the variable load, and the measurement instruments.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SunBlock[Sun Source Class AAA Solar Simulator] --> PVBlockA[PV Module Under Test]
    PVBlockA --> WireA[DC Power Cable Pair]
    WireA --> LoadBlock[Variable Resistive Load Rheostat 0 to 1000 Ohm]
    LoadBlock --> WireB[Return Cable Pair]
    WireB --> PVBlockA
    PVBlockA --> Vmeter[Digital Voltmeter V across terminals]
    PVBlockA --> Ameter[Digital Ammeter A in series with load]
    Ameter --> DataloggerBlock[Data Acquisition System DAQ]
    Vmeter --> DataloggerBlock
    DataloggerBlock --> PCBlock[Laptop with Plotting Software Origin or MATLAB]
    TempSens[Cell Temperature Sensor PT100] --> DataloggerBlock
    IrradSens[Irradiance Meter Pyranometer] --> DataloggerBlock
    PCBlock --> PlotOut[I-V Curve and P-V Curve]
```

**Node Legend:**
- **PVBlockA:** The PV module whose I–V curve is being characterized.
- **LoadBlock:** A variable resistor (rheostat) swept from 0 Ω (short-circuit) to ∞ Ω (open-circuit).
- **Vmeter / Ameter:** Measure instantaneous V and I as the load is varied.
- **TempSens / IrradSens:** Capture the test conditions (must be reported in the lab record).
- **PCBlock:** Plots the I–V and P–V curves in real time.

### 4.2 Block Diagram of the Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit of a PV Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ILnode[Current Source I_L Photogenerated] --> TopNode
    DiodeNode[Diode D Ideality Factor a] --> TopNode
    RshNode[Shunt Resistor R_sh] --> TopNode
    TopNode --> RsNode[Series Resistor R_s]
    RsNode --> PosOut[Positive Terminal V and I]
    PosOut --> LoadExt[External Load]
    LoadExt --> NegOut[Negative Terminal]
    NegOut --> ILnode
    NegOut --> DiodeNode
    NegOut --> RshNode
```

**Node Legend:**
- **ILnode:** Photo-current source, depends linearly on G and weakly on T.
- **DiodeNode:** Models the p–n junction; carries the recombination current.
- **RshNode / RsNode:** Parasitic resistances; in an ideal cell, R_s = 0 and R_sh = ∞.
- **PosOut / NegOut:** External terminals across which the load is connected.

### 4.3 Flowchart for MPPT Determination from Experimental Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StepA[Start: Collect V and I samples] --> StepB[Compute P_k = V_k times I_k for every sample]
    StepB --> StepC{Is P_k greater than P_max so far?}
    StepC -- Yes --> StepD[Update P_max = P_k, store V_mp = V_k, I_mp = I_k]
    StepC -- No --> StepE[Skip update and continue]
    StepD --> StepF{End of all samples?}
    StepE --> StepF
    StepF -- No --> StepB
    StepF -- Yes --> StepG[Report V_mp, I_mp, P_max]
    StepG --> StepH[Compute FF = P_max divided by V_oc times I_sc]
    StepH --> StepI[Compute Efficiency eta = P_max divided by G times A]
    StepI --> StepJ[Plot I-V and P-V curves and annotate MPP]
```

**Node Legend:**
- **StepA → StepB:** Scan across all recorded samples.
- **StepC:** Decision node implementing the maximum-search logic.
- **StepG → StepI:** Final KTU-mandated calculations.
- **StepJ:** Graphical confirmation that MPP is at the peak of the P–V curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** When using a rheostat, take at least 20–30 readings, with denser sampling near the "knee" of the I–V curve (between 70 % and 90 % of V_oc) where the MPP typically lies.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]**
**Define the following terms with reference to a PV module:**
**(a) Open-circuit voltage (V_oc)**
**(b) Short-circuit current (I_sc)**
**(c) Fill Factor (FF)**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

**(a) Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc):** The terminal voltage of a PV module when the external load is disconnected (infinite resistance), i.e., when the output current I = 0. Mathematically,

$$
V_{oc} = \dfrac{a \, N_s \, k T}{q} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)
$$

**(b) Short-Circuit Current (I_sc):** The current delivered by the PV module when the output terminals are shorted (load resistance = 0), i.e., when the terminal voltage V = 0. Numerically, I_sc ≈ I_L (since the diode and shunt terms vanish at V = 0). I_sc is directly proportional to the incident solar irradiance G.

**(c) Fill Factor (FF):** The ratio of the actual maximum obtainable power (P_max = V_mp · I_mp) to the product V_oc · I_sc:

$$
FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \, I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \, I_{sc}}
$$

FF is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1, typically 0.65 – 0.80 for good crystalline-silicon modules. A higher FF indicates a "squarer" I–V curve and superior cell quality.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]**
**What are Standard Test Conditions (STC) for a PV module? Why is it essential to report I–V characteristics at STC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition of STC (1.5 marks):** Standard Test Conditions are a fixed reference set of environmental and spectral parameters under which every PV module is rated worldwide. They are:
- **Irradiance G = 1000 W/m²** (AM 1.5 global spectrum, equivalent to midday sun on a clear day)
- **Cell temperature T_cell = 25 °C** (298.15 K)
- **Air Mass AM = 1.5** (solar spectrum after passing through 1.5 atmospheres)

**Why STC is Essential (1.5 marks):**
1. **Comparability:** STC provides a uniform benchmark so that the performance of modules from different manufacturers can be compared apples-to-apples.
2. **Reproducibility:** Since I–V characteristics vary with G and T, a fixed reference allows the same module to be re-tested and verified at any laboratory worldwide.
3. **Sizing & Yield Estimation:** System designers use STC ratings to size strings and arrays, and to predict annual energy yield using translation equations (NOCT model) for field conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Confusing **I_sc** with **I_mp** — many students interchange the two; I_sc is at V = 0, I_mp is at V = V_mp.
> 2. Stating FF as a percentage. FF is a **dimensionless ratio** (0 – 1), not a percentage. Module **efficiency (η)** is the parameter expressed in %.
> 3. Forgetting the minus sign in the I–V equation — the term inside the brackets is $[\exp(\cdot) - 1]$, not $[\exp(\cdot) + 1]$.
> 4. Omitting the units (V, A, W, W/m²) in the answer — this costs easy marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat equivalent circuit, derive the **single-diode I–V equation** of a PV cell. Clearly define all the five parameters involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PV module has the following specifications at STC: V_oc = 45 V, I_sc = 8.5 A, V_mp = 36 V, I_mp = 8.0 A. The module area is 1.65 m². Calculate:
**(i)** Maximum power P_max
**(ii)** Fill Factor FF
**(iii)** Module efficiency η (take G = 1000 W/m²) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Equivalent Circuit:** Draw the single-diode model showing the photo-current source I_L in parallel with the diode D (ideality factor a) and the shunt resistor R_sh, the combination being in series with R_s and the external load. (Diagram omitted here for brevity but should be drawn in the exam.)

**Step 2 [1 mark] — KCL Application:** Applying KCL at the top node:
$$
I_L = I + I_D + I_{sh}
$$

**Step 3 [1 mark] — Diode Equation:**
$$
I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\left( \dfrac{V + I R_s}{a V_T} \right) - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 4 [1 mark] — Shunt Current Equation:**
$$
I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 5 [2 marks] — Final I–V Equation:** Substituting and rearranging:
$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left( \dfrac{V + I R_s}{a V_T} \right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 6 [1 mark] — Define the Five Parameters:**
1. **I_L** — Photo-generated current (A); linear in G.
2. **I_0** — Diode reverse saturation current (A); very small (~10⁻⁹ A).
3. **a** — Diode ideality factor (1 ≤ a ≤ 2); 1 for ideal diode, ~1.3 for Si.
4. **R_s** — Series resistance (Ω); should be as low as possible.
5. **R_sh** — Shunt resistance (Ω); should be as high as possible.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:** V_oc = 45 V, I_sc = 8.5 A, V_mp = 36 V, I_mp = 8.0 A, A = 1.65 m², G = 1000 W/m².

**(i) Maximum Power P_max [2 marks]:**
$$
P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 36 \times 8.0 = 288 \text{ W}
$$

**(ii) Fill Factor FF [2 marks]:**
$$
FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \, I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \, I_{sc}} = \dfrac{36 \times 8.0}{45 \times 8.5} = \dfrac{288}{382.5} = 0.753
$$

**(iii) Module Efficiency η [3 marks]:**
$$
P_{in} = G \cdot A = 1000 \times 1.65 = 1650 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{288}{1650} = 0.1745 = 17.45\,\%
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Regular Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain the **procedure to obtain the I–V characteristics of a PV module** in the laboratory. List the instruments used and the precautions to be observed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the effect of variation in **irradiance (G)** and **cell temperature (T)** on the I–V and P–V characteristics of a PV module. State the corresponding trends for I_sc, V_oc, V_mp, I_mp, and P_max. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Apparatus List:** PV module under test, variable resistive load (rheostat 0–1000 Ω), two digital multimeters (or a voltmeter and an ammeter), a pyranometer (irradiance meter), a cell-temperature sensor (PT100 or thermocouple), connecting leads, and a data logger.

**Step 2 [2 marks] — Circuit Connection:** Connect the PV module in series with the ammeter and the variable load. Connect the voltmeter in parallel across the module terminals. Place the pyranometer coplanar with the module surface. Attach the temperature sensor to the back of the module (using thermal paste) at the centre of one cell.

**Step 3 [2 marks] — Measurement Procedure:**
1. Set the rheostat to its maximum resistance position (≈ 1000 Ω) — this corresponds to the open-circuit condition.
2. Record V_oc (voltmeter reading) and I = 0.
3. Gradually decrease the rheostat resistance in steps of 50 Ω (or finer near the MPP region).
4. At each step, record the voltmeter and ammeter readings.
5. Take the last reading at R_load = 0 Ω, which gives I_sc.
6. Repeat the procedure 2–3 times to ensure repeatability, and average the values.

**Step 4 [1 mark] — Plotting:** Compute P = V × I for each pair of readings. Plot I vs V (I–V curve) and P vs V (P–V curve) on graph paper or using software. Identify the MPP as the peak of the P–V curve.

**Step 5 [1 mark] — Precautions:**
- Keep the module surface clean and free from shadows.
- Maintain G and T as close to STC as possible; record the actual values.
- Avoid touching the module surface during measurement (prevents heating).
- Use a high-precision ammeter for I_mp measurement (small change in I near MPP).

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

| Parameter | Effect of Increasing G (at constant T) | Effect of Increasing T (at constant G) |
|---|---|---|
| **I_sc** | Increases **linearly** with G | Increases very slightly (small positive temperature coefficient ≈ +0.05 %/°C) |
| **V_oc** | Increases **logarithmically** with G | **Decreases** strongly (≈ −0.35 %/°C per cell) |
| **V_mp** | Increases slightly | Decreases |
| **I_mp** | Increases (≈ proportionally to G) | Decreases slightly |
| **P_max** | Increases **proportionally** to G | **Decreases** (because V_oc drop dominates) |

**Step 2 [2 marks] — I–V Curve Trends:**
- As **G decreases** (e.g., cloudy weather), the I–V curve shrinks **downward** (lower I_sc) but V_oc drops only slightly — the curve becomes "shorter" vertically.
- As **T increases** (e.g., hot summer afternoon at noon), the I–V curve shifts slightly upward (I_sc rises marginally) but shrinks **leftward** (V_oc falls), and the curve becomes "narrower."

**Step 3 [1 mark] — P–V Curve Trends:**
- The peak P_max rises linearly with G and falls with T. This is why **PV modules lose ~0.4 – 0.5 %** of their power output for every 1 °C rise in cell temperature above 25 °C.

**Step 4 [1 mark] — Engineering Implications:**
- STC ratings over-predict field output in hot climates; the **NOCT (Nominal Operating Cell Temperature)** model is used to correct for this.
- MPPT controllers must re-track the MPP continuously because V_mp drifts with T throughout the day.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in This Experiment:**
> 1. **Not Reporting G and T** (–1 mark): Every I–V characteristic is invalid without the simultaneous irradiance and cell temperature values. The I–V curve drawn without these is considered incomplete.
> 2. **Confusing "Maximum Power Point" with "Knee Voltage"** (–1 mark): The MPP is at the peak of the P–V curve, not at the "knee" of the I–V curve (although they lie close to each other geometrically).
> 3. **Wrong FF Formula** (–1 to –2 marks): Students often write $FF = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} / (V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp})$ — the ratio is **inverted**. Correct: $FF = P_{max} / (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$.
> 4. **Ignoring the "−1" in the diode equation** (–1 mark): The diode current is $I_0 [\exp(\cdot) - 1]$, not just $I_0 \exp(\cdot)$.
> 5. **Efficiency Calculation Without Module Area** (–2 marks): $\eta$ is meaningless without $A$ and $G$. Always state $P_{in} = G \cdot A$ first.
> 6. **Failing to State STC Conditions** (–1 mark): Any mention of V_oc, I_sc, or P_max must reference the STC values; otherwise, the values are ambiguous.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **PV cell** is a p–n junction diode that converts light into electricity via the **photovoltaic effect**.
- The **single-diode, five-parameter model** is the KTU-standard equivalent circuit: $I_L, I_0, a, R_s, R_{sh}$.
- The **I–V equation** is **implicit** in I; iterative or numerical (Newton–Raphson) methods are used to solve it.
- **Short-Circuit Current I_sc** is at V = 0; **Open-Circuit Voltage V_oc** is at I = 0; both are endpoints of the I–V curve.
- **P_max = V_mp × I_mp** is the unique point on the curve where the product V·I is maximized.
- **Fill Factor FF = P_max / (V_oc · I_sc)** indicates the "squareness" of the I–V curve; typical range 0.65 – 0.80.
- **Efficiency η = P_max / (G · A)**; commercial modules are 15 – 22 % efficient.
- **STC** = 1000 W/m², 25 °C, AM 1.5 — the universal reference for module rating.
- **I_sc** is **linearly** proportional to G; **V_oc** varies **logarithmically** with G and is **strongly inversely** related to T.
- **P_max** rises linearly with G and falls with T — this is the single most important practical fact for field installation.
- The **MPP shifts with G and T**; therefore, **MPPT controllers** are mandatory in all grid-tied inverters.
- Methods to determine MPP experimentally: **direct measurement** (load sweep), **Perturb & Observe (P&O)**, and **Incremental Conductance** algorithms.
- **NOCT** (Nominal Operating Cell Temperature, typically 45 ± 2 °C at 800 W/m², 20 °C ambient) is the field-referenced rating used to predict real-world output.
- For lab record, always tabulate: **S. No. | V (V) | I (A) | P = V·I (W) | G (W/m²) | T (°C)** — at least 15 – 20 rows.
- The experimental V_oc, I_sc must be compared with the **datasheet STC values**, and any deviation must be justified using the **translation equations** (correcting for G and T).
- **Series resistance R_s** primarily degrades the "knee" of the I–V curve and reduces FF.
- **Shunt resistance R_sh** primarily degrades the slope near I_sc and reduces V_oc.
- An **ideal PV cell** would have **R_s = 0, R_sh = ∞, a = 1, FF = 1, and η = 100 %** — none of these are achievable in practice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Voltage Testing: Power Frequency and Impulse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** High Voltage Testing: Power Frequency and Impulse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Voltage (HV) Testing** is the controlled application of elevated electrical stresses to power system equipment (transformers, cables, insulators, bushings, switchgear) to verify its dielectric integrity, operational safety margin, and conformity with national/international standards such as **IEC 60060-1**, **IEC 60060-2**, **IEC 60060-3**, **IEEE Std 4**, and **IS 2071**.

For KTU 2024 Scheme PCEEL609 (Power System Lab), the topic is split into two complementary stress types:

| Test Type | Stress Waveform | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Frequency AC Test** | Continuous sinusoid, **50 Hz** (India) | Simulates sustained over-voltages, verifies insulation under operating frequency |
| **Impulse Voltage Test** | Double-exponential transient, **1.2 / 50 μs** standard lightning impulse | Simulates atmospheric switching / lightning transients |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a water pipe network.
> - The **power frequency test** is like cranking the water pressure to 1.5× the normal rating for **one full minute** and watching if any joint leaks. It mimics the long-term, continuous stress the pipe will endure.
> - The **impulse test** is like firing a sudden **water hammer** pulse (an extremely fast pressure spike lasting microseconds) to check whether the pipe bursts from a sudden shock — equivalent to a lightning strike hitting the system.
>
> Engineers run **both** tests because a pipe that withstands steady pressure can still crack under a sudden hammer blow, and vice-versa.

## 1.3 Why Both Tests Are Mandatory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Insulation materials exhibit **time-dependent breakdown behavior**:
> - For **short-duration impulses**, the dielectric strength appears *higher* because there is insufficient time for the **avalanche / streamer** mechanism to fully develop across the insulation gap.
> - For **long-duration AC stress**, partial discharge, thermal runaway, and tracking dominate the failure mechanism.
>
> Therefore a *single* test type is **insufficient** — both power-frequency and impulse tests are mandated by every major standard.

## 1.4 Key Standard Reference Values (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Standard Lightning Impulse:** **1.2 / 50 μs** (front time / tail time).
- **Standard Switching Impulse:** **250 / 2500 μs**.
- **Power Frequency in India:** **50 Hz**, nominal RMS.
- **Standard Atmospheric Correction Factor:** $k = \frac{k_d \cdot k_h}{k_1}$ (where $k_d$ = air density factor, $k_h$ = humidity factor).
- **Reference Atmospheric Conditions:** Temperature = **20 °C**, Pressure = **101.3 kPa**, Absolute humidity = **11 g/m³**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Frequency AC High Voltage Testing

### 2.1.1 Test Transformer Setup

A single test transformer rarely produces the **hundreds of kV** needed for EHV/UHV equipment. Therefore, several stages are cascaded:

- **Cascaded Transformer Group (Tesla/Autotransformer Cascade):** Each unit is fed from a separate winding; outputs are in series to add up the secondary voltages.
- The final output voltage $V_{\text{out}} = n \cdot V_{\text{stage}}$, where $n$ = number of stages.
- The **short-circuit impedance** must be low to maintain voltage regulation under capacitive loads (such as capacitive voltage dividers).

### 2.1.2 Why Power Frequency, Not DC?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - AC tests reproduce the **actual operating stress** experienced by AC equipment.
> - The peak value of a 50 Hz sine wave is $\sqrt{2}$ times its RMS — equipment is therefore subjected to a peak voltage **≈ 1.414 × rated RMS**, and must withstand this peak repeatedly every 10 ms.
> - DC testing is mainly used for **cables** (to avoid capacitive charging current), not for general power apparatus.

### 2.1.3 Test Categories

- **Routine / Type Tests:**
  - **One-minute withstand test:** Apply specified test voltage for **60 seconds**, no flashover allowed.
  - **Dry and wet withstand test:** Performed under dry and artificially wetted conditions for outdoor insulators.
- **Acceptance Test on site.**
- **Diagnostic / Condition Monitoring Tests:** e.g., partial discharge (PD) measurement, tan-delta, insulation resistance.

## 2.2 Impulse Voltage Testing

### 2.2.1 Need for Impulse Testing

Lightning strikes and switching operations generate voltages with rise times on the order of **microseconds**, far steeper than any AC waveform. The insulation must survive these *transient* overvoltages. The **impulse voltage test** replicates this in a controlled lab environment.

### 2.2.2 Standard Lightning Impulse Waveform — 1.2 / 50 μs

The waveform is a **double exponential** described by:

$$v(t) = V_p \, \left( e^{-\alpha t} - e^{-\beta t} \right)$$

where $\alpha$ governs the **tail decay**, $\beta$ governs the **front steepness**, and $V_p$ is the peak (crest) value.

**Definition of Front Time $T_1$ and Tail Time $T_2$:**

> [!NOTE]
> - $T_1$ (front time) = **1.67 × (t₉₀ − t₃₀)**, where $t_{30}$ and $t_{90}$ are the times at **30 %** and **90 %** of peak on the rising front. (Theoretical zero is at $t = 0 - 0.3 \, T_1$.)
> - $T_2$ (time to half value) = time from virtual origin to the instant the tail decays to **50 %** of peak.
> - **Nominal standard:** $T_1 = 1.2 \pm 30\%$ μs, $T_2 = 50 \pm 20\%$ μs.

### 2.2.3 The Marx Multiplier / Impulse Generator Circuit

The Marx circuit (1923) is the workhorse of HV impulse labs. It charges several capacitors in *parallel* from a DC source and then discharges them in *series* using sphere gaps.

- **Charging phase:** All $n$ capacitors $C$ charged to $V_c$ (typically ±100 to ±200 kV per stage).
- **Triggering phase:** First sphere gap breaks down, the resulting over-voltage triggers the next gap, and within **~ 100 ns** all $n$ gaps fire — capacitors now in series.
- **Output voltage:** $V_{\text{out}} = \pm n \, V_c$ (neglecting losses).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard stage capacitance:** $C$ is chosen so that the **discharge time constant** yields the 1.2/50 μs shape. Typically $C \approx 0.5$ to 1 μF and **wavefront / wavetail resistors** $R_f \approx$ tens of Ω, $R_t \approx$ hundreds to few kΩ.
> - **Energy stored:** $E = \frac{1}{2} \, n \, C \, V_c^{2}$ (per stage × n stages in *parallel* during charging).

### 2.2.4 Impulse Ratio and 50 % Flashover Voltage

- **Impulse Ratio:** $IR = \dfrac{V_{50\% \, \text{impulse}}}{V_{\text{flashover (rms AC)}}}$. For air gaps under positive polarity, $IR \approx 1.3$ to $1.5$.
- **50 % Flashover Voltage ($V_{50}$)**: The crest value of an impulse that causes flashover in **50 %** of the applications. Determined statistically using the **Up-and-Down Method**.

### 2.2.5 Up-and-Down Method for $V_{50}$

A series of impulses at controlled voltage levels $V_k$ is applied. The step $\Delta V$ is constant (≈ 2–3 % of expected $V_{50}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm:**
> 1. Start at $V_1 \approx$ expected $V_{50}$.
> 2. Apply impulse.
> 3. If **withstand** → raise next level by $\Delta V$.
> 4. If **flashover** → lower next level by $\Delta V$.
> 5. Repeat for at least **20 valid impulses**.
> 6. $V_{50} \approx \dfrac{\sum n_i \, V_i}{N}$ (weighted average over breakdown levels).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **cheat sheet** for KTU lab viva + ESE numericals on this experiment. Memorize the boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Peak from RMS | $V_p = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{\text{rms}}$ | $V_p$ in kV |
| 2 | Impulse ratio | $IR = V_{50\% \, \text{imp}} \, / \, V_{\text{ac flashover}}$ | Dimensionless, $\approx 1.3$–$1.5$ for air |
| 3 | Marx output | $V_{\text{out}} = n \cdot V_c$ (ideal, no load) | kV |
| 4 | Energy stored in bank | $E = \frac{1}{2} \, n \, C \, V_c^{2}$ | Joules (J) |
| 5 | Front time definition | $T_1 = 1.67 \cdot (t_{90} - t_{30})$ | μs |
| 6 | Tail time | $T_2 = $ time to 50 % peak | μs |
| 7 | Up-and-Down $V_{50}$ | $V_{50} = \sum n_i V_i \, / \, N$ | kV |
| 8 | Atmospheric correction | $V_{\text{corrected}} = V_{\text{measured}} \, / \, k$ | $k = (k_d \, k_h) \, / \, k_1$ |
| 9 | Air density factor | $k_d = \frac{p}{p_0} \cdot \frac{273 + t_0}{273 + t}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Sphere gap breakdown (approx., air) | $V_b = a \cdot d + b$ (linear empirical, $d$ in cm) | kV |
| 11 | Double-exponential waveform | $v(t) = V_p (e^{-\alpha t} - e^{-\beta t})$ | Volts |
| 12 | Withstand probability | $P_{\text{withstand}} = 1 - P_{\text{breakdown}}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** HV testing laboratories (e.g., CPRI India, BHEL, KERI Korea, CESI Italy, STRI Sweden) certify every transformer, insulator, cable, GIS module before commissioning. A failure to withstand the impulse test is the leading cause of rejection during Factory Acceptance Tests (FAT).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Marx Generator Output Voltage

**Statement:** Show that an $n$-stage Marx impulse generator charged to $V_c$ produces an open-circuit output of $n \cdot V_c$ (ideal case).

### Step-by-step

**Step 1 — Charging Phase.**
Each stage capacitor $C$ is charged through the charging resistor $R_c$ from a DC supply of magnitude $V_c$. Because all stages are connected in *parallel* during charging, every capacitor acquires the same voltage $V_c$.

$$V_C^{(k)} = V_c \quad \text{for} \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Step 2 — Initiating Trigger.**
The first sphere gap $G_1$ is forced to break down by an external trigger pulse. Upon breakdown, its resistance collapses to nearly zero. The voltage across $G_1$ suddenly drops, and the potential at the **top plate** of $C_1$ rises sharply (toward $V_c$).

**Step 3 — Cascaded Triggering.**
Once $G_1$ fires, the voltage on the next stage's lower plate rises to approximately $2 \, V_c$, exceeding the breakdown voltage of $G_2$ by a large margin. $G_2$ fires within tens of nanoseconds — propagating the breakdown through all $n$ gaps almost simultaneously.

**Step 4 — Series Reconfiguration.**
After all gaps fire, the $n$ capacitors, each charged to $V_c$, are now connected in *series* across the load. The total voltage at the output terminals is the algebraic sum.

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_C^{(1)} + V_C^{(2)} + \cdots + V_C^{(n)}$$

$$V_{\text{out}} = n \cdot V_c$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical correction:** In a real generator, internal resistors, sphere-gap arc resistance, and load current cause a small droop. The *actual* peak is typically $(0.90$ to $0.95) \cdot n \cdot V_c$. This factor is called the **efficiency** $\eta$ of the impulse generator.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Up-and-Down Method for 50 % Flashover Voltage

**Statement:** Derive the estimator $V_{50} \approx \dfrac{\sum n_i V_i}{N}$ used in HV impulse testing.

### Step-by-step

**Step 1 — Define Levels.**
Suppose the impulse voltage is varied across a finite set of levels $\{V_1, V_2, \dots, V_m\}$ separated by a fixed step $\Delta V$. At each application, the outcome is binary: **withstand (W)** or **flashover (F)**.

**Step 2 — Up-and-Down Transition Rule.**
The level transitions are governed by:

$$V_{k+1} = V_k + \Delta V \quad \text{if outcome at } k \text{ was W}$$

$$V_{k+1} = V_k - \Delta V \quad \text{if outcome at } k \text{ was F}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency Count.**
Let $n_i$ be the number of impulses applied at level $V_i$ (regardless of outcome). The total number of valid impulses is:

$$N = \sum_{i=1}^{m} n_i$$

**Step 4 — Probability Estimate.**
The empirical probability of flashover at level $V_i$ is the relative frequency. The 50 % flashover level is the level where the empirical probability of flashover is 0.5. Under the assumption that the *transition* between levels approximates this 50 % point symmetrically, the simple weighted-mean estimator follows:

$$V_{50} \approx \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} n_i \, V_i}{N}$$

**Step 5 — Example (Numerical).**
Let $\Delta V = 2$ kV, applied levels observed: $V = 60, 62, 64, 66, 68$ kV with counts $n_i = 1, 3, 6, 3, 1$ respectively (out of $N = 14$).

$$V_{50} = \frac{(1)(60) + (3)(62) + (6)(64) + (3)(66) + (1)(68)}{14}$$

$$V_{50} = \frac{60 + 186 + 384 + 198 + 68}{14} = \frac{896}{14}$$

$$V_{50} = 64.0 \text{ kV}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Atmospheric Correction Factor

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the air density factor $k_d$.

The density of air depends on both pressure and absolute temperature (in Kelvin):

$$\rho = \frac{p}{R \, T} \quad \text{where } T = 273 + t \; (^\circ \text{C}), \quad p = \text{pressure in kPa}$$

The density factor is the ratio of the actual air density to the standard density:

$$k_d = \frac{\rho}{\rho_0} = \frac{p}{p_0} \cdot \frac{T_0}{T} = \frac{p}{p_0} \cdot \frac{273 + t_0}{273 + t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference conditions:** $p_0 = 101.3$ kPa, $t_0 = 20$ °C, so $T_0 = 293$ K.

Similarly, the humidity factor $k_h$ is determined from tables (or from the absolute humidity $h$ in g/m³). The **standard test voltage at site conditions** must be corrected to standard atmospheric conditions before comparing with rated BIL (Basic Insulation Level):

$$V_{\text{standard}} = V_{\text{measured}} \cdot \frac{k_d \cdot k_h}{k_1}$$

where $k_1$ is a gap-factor (≈ 1 for uniform field).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Test Waveform

| Equipment Type | Required Waveform | Typical Test Level | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power transformer** | Lightning impulse (1.2/50) **+** Power freq | 1.5 pu BIL | Subject to both line surges and continuous stress |
| **Outdoor insulator (porcelain / composite)** | Wet power freq + Lightning impulse | As per IS 7311 / IEC 60815 | Pollution + rain + surges |
| **XLPE cable** | DC ramp + Power freq (no impulse for HV) | $3 V_0$ DC, $2.5 V_0$ AC | AC charging current too high |
| **GIS bus duct** | Lightning + Switching impulse | Per IEC 62271 | Trapped charges on disconnector operation |
| **Lightning arrester (MOA)** | 8/20 μs current impulse, square wave | Per IEC 60099-4 | Not a voltage-withstand device |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Lab viva loves the question *"Why is the lightning impulse front time defined as 1.67 × (t₉₀ − t₃₀)?"*. Answer: because the *true* virtual origin (where the tangent to the front extrapolates to zero) is hard to measure directly; the 30/90 method gives a robust, repeatable measurement aligned with the **tangent intersection** by construction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Block Architecture of a Complete HV Test Lab

> [!NOTE]
> Physical circuit drawings (transformer windings, sphere gaps with precise spatial relations) cannot be faithfully rendered in Mermaid. The block-level functional architecture below maps every interaction in an HV test lab.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains 415V 50Hz] --> B[Regulator Variac]
    B --> C[Test Transformer HT Side]
    C --> D[HV Rectifier and DC Link]
    D --> E[Charging Resistor Rc Bank]
    E --> F[Marx Capacitor Bank n Stages]
    F --> G[Sphere Gap Trigger]
    G --> H[Wave Shaping Rf and Rt]
    H --> I[Test Object DUT]
    I --> J[Capacitive Voltage Divider]
    J --> K[Digitizing Oscilloscope or Peak Voltmeter]
    K --> L[Data Acquisition and Display]
    M[Sphere Gap Calibrator] -.Calibrates.-> J
    N[Atmospheric Sensors T and P and Humidity] -.Corrects.-> L
    O[Control Desk and Safety Interlocks] -.Triggers.-> G
    P[Grounding Mat and Earth Bus] -.Safety.-> I
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **A → B → C:** Standard AC mains steps up via the regulator and test transformer.
- **D → E → F → G → H:** This is the **impulse generation chain** — DC rectification, charging, Marx storage, sphere-gap trigger, wave-shaping resistors.
- **I:** The device under test (e.g., a post insulator or transformer bushing).
- **J → K → L:** The **measurement chain** — the capacitive divider scales the HV down to a few volts for the scope; atmospheric data feeds the correction algorithm.
- **M, N, O, P:** Auxiliary and safety subsystems.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Decision Flow of the Up-and-Down Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start at initial voltage V1] --> S2[Apply single impulse]
    S2 --> Q1{Outcome}
    Q1 -->|Withstand| S3[Increase level by deltaV]
    Q1 -->|Flashover| S4[Decrease level by deltaV]
    S3 --> S5{Counter less than 20}
    S4 --> S5
    S5 -->|Yes| S2
    S5 -->|No| S6[Compute weighted mean]
    S6 --> S7[Report V50 in kV]
    S8[Discard first impulse if per IEC 60060] -.Optional.-> S1
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Marx Impulse Generator (4-stage)

```
        +HV DC Supply
            |
          [Rc]   Charging resistor (high value)
            |
    +-------+-------+-------+-------+
    |       |       |       |       |
   ---     ---     ---     ---     ---       C1, C2, C3, C4 (charged to +Vc)
    |       |       |       |       |
   (G1)    (G2)    (G3)    (G4)              Sphere gaps (normally open)
    |       |       |       |
    +---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+
              When triggered, gaps fire
              and capacitors reconfigure
              in SERIES across the load
                       |
              +--------+--------+
              |                 |
            [Rf]              [Rt]            Wave-shaping resistors
              |                 |
              +--------+--------+
                       |
                    [ DUT ]                Device under test
                       |
                      (GND)
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Power Frequency Test Setup with Sphere-Gap Measurement

```
   AC Mains
     |
   [Variac]
     |
   [LV side of Test Transformer]
     |
   [HV side of Test Transformer]
     |
   +---------+--------+
   |                  |
  [DUT]        [Sphere Gap in parallel]     Both see the same HV
   |                  |
  (GND)              (GND)

   [Voltmeter] on LV side (calibrated by sphere gap)
```
