<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Voltage Relay — Operational Logic

The voltage relay operates in a **comparator-based architecture**. Internally, a rectifier converts the AC secondary voltage to a DC quantity proportional to the RMS value. This DC voltage is compared against a **Zener-stabilized reference (pickup level)** using a comparator (Schmitt trigger / op-amp / numerical algorithm).

**Step-by-step operating sequence:**

1. **PT Step-Down:** The Potential Transformer reduces primary voltage (e.g., 11 kV, 110 V) to a standardized secondary value (110 V or 63.5 V).
2. **Rectification & Smoothing:** The AC secondary voltage is rectified to a proportional DC voltage using a full-wave bridge.
3. **Comparison:** The DC level is compared with the **pickup reference voltage** ($V_{pickup}$).
4. **Time Delay Integration:** A definite-time or inverse-time delay element (TDM) suppresses transient noise.
5. **Trip Coil Energization:** If the over-/under-condition persists beyond the time delay, the trip contact closes, energizing the circuit breaker trip coil.

**Why Voltage Relays are Insufficient Alone:**
Voltage alone cannot discriminate between **load reduction** (legal) and **fault** (illegal). A sudden load drop also causes voltage rise. Hence voltage relays are used as **back-up protection** or in conjunction with current-derived supervision (e.g., over-voltage + over-current).

### 2.2 Impedance Relay — Operational Logic

The impedance relay is the simplest member of the distance relay family. Its core principle rests on **Ohm's Law applied to the protected line**:

$$Z_{seen} = \frac{V_{relay}}{I_{relay}}$$

**Why this works for fault discrimination:**

- A fault **close** to the relay produces a **large current** and a **small voltage drop** → low $Z_{seen}$.
- A fault **far** from the relay produces a **small current** and a **large voltage drop** → high $Z_{seen}$.
- A **normal load** produces a current and voltage that result in a $Z_{seen}$ typically **higher** than the line impedance.

**Step-by-step operating sequence:**

1. **Voltage Sensing:** PT secondary voltage is applied to the relay's voltage-sensing circuit.
2. **Current Sensing:** CT secondary current is applied to the relay's current-sensing circuit (often via an auxiliary CT to standardize at 1 A or 5 A).
3. **Impedance Computation:** The relay computes the **magnitude of the apparent impedance** $\vert Z_{apparent} \vert = \vert V/I \vert$.
4. **Comparison with Pickup:** The measured magnitude is compared with the **pickup impedance** $Z_{pickup} = \dfrac{V_{pickup}}{I_{pickup}}$.
5. **Trip Decision:** If $Z_{seen} \leq Z_{pickup}$, the relay trips.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **impedance relay is inherently non-directional**. It will trip for a fault *behind* the relay (in the reverse direction) if the apparent impedance falls below the pickup. This is why, in practice, **mho relays** (directional impedance relays) are more commonly deployed on transmission lines.

### 2.3 R–X Diagram and Operating Characteristics

The **R–X plane** (or **impedance plane**) is the geometric workspace of the distance relay. The x-axis is **resistance (R)** and the y-axis is **reactance (X)**. Each fault appears as a single point whose coordinates are determined by the line impedance up to the fault.

| Characteristic | Geometric Shape on R-X Plane | Directional? |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure Impedance Relay** | Circle centered at origin | No |
| **Mho (Admittance) Relay** | Circle passing through origin | Yes |
| **Reactance Relay** | Straight line parallel to R-axis | Yes |
| **Quadrilateral Relay** | Four-sided polygon | Yes |
| **Offset Mho / Lens** | Two overlapping circles | Partial |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar character `$\vert$` is used below to denote absolute value (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE formatting rules to avoid breaking the markdown table).

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{pickup} = K_{V} \cdot V_{nominal}$ | Pickup voltage setting | V (rms) |
| 2 | $t_{op} = TDM \cdot \left(\dfrac{M - 1}{M^{2} - 1}\right)$ | IDMT operating time (TDM = Time Dial Multiplier, M = multiple of pickup) | seconds |
| 3 | $Z_{apparent} = \dfrac{V_{relay}}{I_{relay}}$ | Impedance seen by relay | Ω (secondary) |
| 4 | $Z_{pickup} = \dfrac{V_{pickup}}{I_{pickup}}$ | Pickup impedance setting | Ω |
| 5 | $Z_{primary} = Z_{secondary} \cdot CTR \cdot PTR$ | Conversion from secondary to primary impedance | Ω (primary) |
| 6 | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^{2} + X^{2}}$ | Magnitude of complex impedance | Ω |
| 7 | $\theta_{Z} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X}{R}\right)$ | Impedance angle (line angle) | degrees |
| 8 | $R = \vert Z \vert \cos\theta$ | Resistance component | Ω |
| 9 | $X = \vert Z \vert \sin\theta$ | Reactance component | Ω |
| 10 | $Reach_{primary} = Z_{pickup(sec)} \cdot CTR \cdot PTR$ | Line reach in primary ohms | Ω or km |
| 11 | $Reach_{km} = \dfrac{Z_{line(per \ km, primary)}}{Z_{pickup(primary)}}$ | Fractional reach (0 to 1) | per-unit |
| 12 | $M = \dfrac{V_{pickup}}{V_{actual}}$ (UVR) | Multiple of pickup | pu |
| 13 | $M = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{pickup}}$ (OVR) | Multiple of pickup | pu |
| 14 | $Z_{load} = \dfrac{V_{load}^{2}}{P_{load}}$ (approx.) | Apparent load impedance | Ω |

Where **CTR = CT Ratio** and **PTR = PT Ratio**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Voltage Relays** protect against sub-synchronous over-voltages in EHV lines, generator self-excitation, and load rejection scenarios.
- **Impedance Relays** form the basis of **distance protection**, the universal primary protection for HV/EHV transmission lines (66 kV to 765 kV in India).
- The KTU lab tests these relays using **secondary injection test kits** (e.g., Doble, OMICRON, or locally assembled lab test benches) that simulate PT and CT signals directly.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Impedance from Voltage and Current Phasors

We begin with the fundamental phasor relationship for a transmission line section.

**Step 1:** Apply Ohm's Law in phasor form for the protected line.

$$
V = I \cdot Z
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the line impedance $Z$.

$$
Z = \frac{V}{I}
$$

**Step 3:** Express in rectangular form. Since $Z$ is a complex number:

$$
Z = R + jX
$$

**Step 4:** Express in polar form. Let $\vert Z \vert$ be the magnitude and $\theta$ be the phase angle:

$$
Z = \vert Z \vert \angle \theta
$$

**Step 5:** Equate polar and rectangular forms.

$$
\vert Z \vert \angle \theta = R + jX
$$

**Step 6:** Use Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$ to separate real and imaginary parts.

$$
\vert Z \vert \cos\theta + j \vert Z \vert \sin\theta = R + jX
$$

**Step 7:** Equate real and imaginary components.

$$
R = \vert Z \vert \cos\theta
$$
$$
X = \vert Z \vert \sin\theta
$$

**Step 8:** Compute the magnitude by the Pythagorean identity.

$$
\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^{2} + X^{2}}
$$

**Step 9:** Compute the angle using the tangent.

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X}{R}\right)
$$

This gives us the impedance trajectory in the **R-X plane**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion of Secondary Impedance to Primary Impedance

In lab testing, we always measure $V$ and $I$ at the **relay (secondary) terminals**. However, the line impedance that the relay must "see" is a **primary** value. The conversion derivation is:

**Step 1:** Express the primary voltage in terms of secondary voltage and PT ratio.

$$
V_{primary} = V_{secondary} \cdot PTR
$$

**Step 2:** Express the primary current in terms of secondary current and CT ratio.

$$
I_{primary} = \frac{I_{secondary}}{CTR}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Ohm's Law at the primary level.

$$
Z_{primary} = \frac{V_{primary}}{I_{primary}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the relations from Steps 1 and 2.

$$
Z_{primary} = \frac{V_{secondary} \cdot PTR}{I_{secondary} / CTR}
$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the fraction.

$$
Z_{primary} = V_{secondary} \cdot PTR \cdot \frac{CTR}{I_{secondary}}
$$

**Step 6:** Group the secondary V/I ratio.

$$
Z_{primary} = \left(\frac{V_{secondary}}{I_{secondary}}\right) \cdot (CTR \cdot PTR)
$$

**Step 7:** Identify the secondary impedance.

$$
Z_{primary} = Z_{secondary} \cdot CTR \cdot PTR
$$

This is the **fundamental conversion formula** used throughout distance relay engineering.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Operating Boundary of the Pure Impedance Relay

A pure impedance relay operates when $Z_{seen}$ lies **inside a circle of radius $Z_{pickup}$** centered at the origin of the R-X plane.

**Step 1:** Define the operating condition.

$$
\text{Operate} \iff \vert Z_{seen} \vert \leq Z_{pickup}
$$

**Step 2:** Expand $Z_{seen}$ in rectangular form.

$$
\sqrt{R_{seen}^{2} + X_{seen}^{2}} \leq Z_{pickup}
$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides (to remove the square root).

$$
R_{seen}^{2} + X_{seen}^{2} \leq Z_{pickup}^{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize this is the equation of a circle.

- **Center:** $(0, 0)$ — the origin of the R-X plane.
- **Radius:** $Z_{pickup}$ — the pickup impedance.

**Step 5:** The fault must lie *inside* this circle for the relay to trip.

> [!NOTE]
> This circle passes through the **origin**, which means the relay will operate for a **three-phase bolted fault at the relay bus** (where V = 0, so Z = 0). This is the desired behavior.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A 11 kV / 110 V PT and a 400 A / 5 A CT feed an impedance relay. The relay pickup is set at **80% of the rated secondary voltage** at rated secondary current. Compute:
- (i) The secondary pickup impedance.
- (ii) The primary pickup impedance.
- (iii) The reach in km, given the line has an impedance of **0.4 + j0.5 Ω/km** (primary).
- (iv) The R and X components of the pickup point on the R-X plane, given the line angle is **60°**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the secondary pickup voltage.

$$
V_{pickup(sec)} = 0.80 \cdot 110 = 88 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the secondary pickup current (rated CT secondary).

$$
I_{pickup(sec)} = 5 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the secondary pickup impedance.

$$
Z_{pickup(sec)} = \frac{V_{pickup(sec)}}{I_{pickup(sec)}} = \frac{88}{5}
$$

$$
Z_{pickup(sec)} = 17.6 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the CT ratio.

$$
CTR = \frac{400}{5} = 80
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the PT ratio.

$$
PTR = \frac{11000}{110} = 100
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the primary pickup impedance.

$$
Z_{pickup(pri)} = Z_{pickup(sec)} \cdot CTR \cdot PTR
$$

$$
Z_{pickup(pri)} = 17.6 \cdot 80 \cdot 100
$$

$$
Z_{pickup(pri)} = 140800 \text{ Ω} = 140.8 \text{ kΩ}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute the line impedance per km (primary magnitude).

$$
\vert Z_{line} \vert = \sqrt{0.4^{2} + 0.5^{2}} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.41}
$$

$$
\vert Z_{line} \vert \approx 0.6403 \text{ Ω/km}
$$

**Step 8:** Compute the reach in km.

$$
L_{reach} = \frac{Z_{pickup(pri)}}{\vert Z_{line} \vert} = \frac{140800}{0.6403}
$$

$$
L_{reach} \approx 219867 \text{ km}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This absurdly high value illustrates why we test relays in **secondary quantities** — the primary impedance of an 11 kV line is huge because the voltage is high. The relay only "sees" the secondary equivalent.

**Step 9:** Compute R and X of the pickup point.

$$
R = \vert Z_{pickup(pri)} \vert \cos(60°) = 140800 \cdot 0.5
$$

$$
R = 70400 \text{ Ω}
$$

$$
X = \vert Z_{pickup(pri)} \vert \sin(60°) = 140800 \cdot 0.866
$$

$$
X \approx 121933 \text{ Ω}
$$

### 3.5 IDMT Characteristic — Inverse-Time Derivation

The Inverse Definite Minimum Time (IDMT) characteristic is the most widely used curve for voltage and current relays. The standard equation is:

**Step 1:** Define the multiple of pickup.

$$
M = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{pickup}} \quad \text{(for OVR)}
$$

**Step 2:** State the standard IDMT operating time formula (per IEC 60255-151).

$$
t_{op} = TDM \cdot \left(\frac{A}{M^{n} - 1} + B\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the standard IEEE/ANSI inverse constants: $A = 0.14$, $B = 0$, $n = 0.02$ (very inverse); or $A = 3.922$, $B = 0.0982$, $n = 2$ (standard inverse).

**Step 4:** Example — for standard inverse, $M = 2$, $TDM = 1$:

$$
t_{op} = 1 \cdot \left(\frac{3.922}{2^{2} - 1} + 0.0982\right)
$$

$$
t_{op} = \frac{3.922}{3} + 0.0982 = 1.3073 + 0.0982 \approx 1.4055 \text{ s}
$$

### 4.3 R-X Plane (Impedance Plane) Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    O[ORIGIN RELAY BUS] --> RAX[R AXIS RESISTANCE]
    O --> RAY[X AXIS REACTANCE]
    O --> PA[POINT A PICKUP RADIUS ZP]
    PA --> PB[POINT B FAULT INSIDE ZONE 1]
    PA --> PC[POINT C FAULT OUTSIDE ZONE]
    PA --> PD[POINT D LOAD POINT TYPICALLY OUTSIDE]
    O -. CIRCLE .-> CIRC[ZONE 1 OPERATING REGION]
```

**R-X Plane Interpretation:**

| Point | Meaning | Relay Action |
|---|---|---|
| **O (Origin)** | Relay bus location | Reference point |
| **A (Pickup)** | Threshold point at $Z_{pickup}$ | Boundary of operation |
| **B** | Fault inside Zone 1 | **TRIP** |
| **C** | Fault outside Zone 1 (e.g., Zone 2) | Delayed trip / restrain |
| **D** | Normal load point (typically outside) | Restrain (no trip) |
| **Circle** | Operating characteristic | Inside = trip, outside = restrain |

### 4.4 Lab Test Procedure Flow

```mermaid
graph TD

```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define an impedance relay. With the help of a neat sketch, explain its operating principle on the R-X plane.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **impedance relay** is a protective relay that operates when the ratio of the relay voltage to the relay current (i.e., the apparent impedance seen by the relay) is less than a pre-set **pickup impedance value**.

**Operating Principle:**

1. The relay continuously computes $Z_{apparent} = V_{relay} / I_{relay}$.
2. This measured impedance is plotted on the **R-X (impedance) plane**.
3. The relay has a **circular operating characteristic** centered at the origin with radius equal to $Z_{pickup}$.
4. **If $Z_{apparent} \leq Z_{pickup}$**, the impedance point lies **inside** the circle, and the relay issues a **trip command**.
5. If $Z_{apparent} > Z_{pickup}$, the point lies **outside** the circle, and the relay **restrains**.

A fault close to the relay produces a low impedance (inside circle → trip). A far-away fault or normal load produces a high impedance (outside circle → restrain).

*(Sketch: 1 mark, definition: 1 mark, explanation: 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between an over-voltage relay and an under-voltage relay. State two applications of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Over-Voltage Relay (OVR) | Under-Voltage Relay (UVR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operates when** | $V_{actual} > V_{pickup}$ | $V_{actual} < V_{pickup}$ |
| **Pickup setting** | 1.05 to 1.3 × $V_{nominal}$ | 0.5 to 0.9 × $V_{nominal}$ |
| **Typical use** | Over-voltage protection of generators, capacitors | Busbar under-voltage, motor protection |

**Two applications of OVR (1 mark):**

1. **Generator over-voltage protection** against sub-synchronous resonance and load rejection transients.
2. **Capacitor bank over-voltage protection** to prevent dielectric breakdown.

**Two applications of UVR (1 mark):**

1. **Induction motor protection** against sustained under-voltage causing stalling and overheating.
2. **Anti-motoring protection of generators** — detects loss of prime-mover torque.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*During the lab testing of an impedance relay, the following observations were recorded:*

| Test | $V_{secondary}$ (V) | $I_{secondary}$ (A) | Relay State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 88 | 5.0 | Just picked up (boundary) |
| 2 | 66 | 5.0 | Tripped |
| 3 | 110 | 5.0 | Restrained |
| 4 | 44 | 5.0 | Tripped |

*CT Ratio = 400/5 A. PT Ratio = 11000/110 V. Line impedance = (0.4 + j0.5) Ω/km.*

**(a)** Calculate the **secondary and primary pickup impedance** of the relay. Also compute the **reach in km** of the line protected. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the line-to-line voltage on the primary side is 11 kV and a three-phase fault occurs at 80% of the relay reach, find the **fault current** and the **apparent impedance** seen by the relay under fault conditions. State whether the relay will operate. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Pickup Impedance and Reach (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Identify the boundary point.** Test 1 is the boundary; therefore:

$$
V_{pickup(sec)} = 88 \text{ V}, \quad I_{pickup(sec)} = 5.0 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2: Compute secondary pickup impedance.**

$$
Z_{pickup(sec)} = \frac{V_{pickup(sec)}}{I_{pickup(sec)}} = \frac{88}{5} = 17.6 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 3: Compute CTR and PTR.**

$$
CTR = \frac{400}{5} = 80, \quad PTR = \frac{11000}{110} = 100
$$

**Step 4: Compute primary pickup impedance.**

$$
Z_{pickup(pri)} = Z_{pickup(sec)} \cdot CTR \cdot PTR = 17.6 \cdot 80 \cdot 100
$$

$$
Z_{pickup(pri)} = 140{,}800 \text{ Ω} = 140.8 \text{ kΩ}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the per-km line impedance magnitude.**

$$
\vert Z_{line} \vert = \sqrt{0.4^{2} + 0.5^{2}} = \sqrt{0.41} \approx 0.6403 \text{ Ω/km}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the reach.**

$$
L_{reach} = \frac{Z_{pickup(pri)}}{\vert Z_{line} \vert} = \frac{140{,}800}{0.6403} \approx 219{,}867 \text{ km}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note (1 mark awarded for recognizing this anomaly):** This huge number arises because we are computing primary impedance of an 11 kV line. In practice, engineers work in **secondary ohms** or in **per-unit**, not primary ohms. The lab is verifying the **secondary pickup**, not the primary reach.

**Reach in secondary terms (alternative interpretation):** $Z_{pickup(sec)} = 17.6 \text{ Ω}$.

**Part (b) — Fault at 80% Reach (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Find primary impedance at 80% reach.**

$$
Z_{fault(pri)} = 0.80 \cdot Z_{pickup(pri)} = 0.80 \cdot 140{,}800 = 112{,}640 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the fault current. Use $V_{LL} = 11 \text{ kV}$. Phase voltage:**

$$
V_{ph} = \frac{11{,}000}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 6350.85 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3: For a three-phase fault, $I_{fault} = V_{ph} / Z_{fault(pri)}$.**

$$
I_{fault} = \frac{6350.85}{112{,}640} \approx 0.0564 \text{ A (primary)}
$$

**Step 4: This unrealistic current confirms the test was conducted in secondary. The lab interpretation:**

In the **secondary test**, with 80% reach, the secondary voltage at 5 A would be:

$$
V_{secondary(80\%)} = 0.80 \cdot 88 = 70.4 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the apparent impedance at the fault.**

$$
Z_{apparent} = \frac{V_{secondary(80\%)}}{I_{secondary}} = \frac{70.4}{5} = 14.08 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 6: Compare with pickup.**

$$
Z_{apparent} = 14.08 \text{ Ω} < Z_{pickup(sec)} = 17.6 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 7: Decision — The relay WILL OPERATE** (impedance inside the pickup circle).

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **lab testing procedure of a static voltage relay**. Describe the method to obtain the **pickup voltage** and the **operating time characteristic** at multiple TDM (Time Dial Multiplier) settings. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **numerical distance relay** is set with the following parameters: **Zone 1 reach = 80%** of the line, **Zone 2 reach = 150%**, and **Zone 1 time delay = 0 ms (instantaneous)**, **Zone 2 time delay = 350 ms**. A fault at 90% of the line produces a fault current of **3000 A primary** and a relay voltage of **55 V secondary**. CT ratio = 600/1 A, PT ratio = 220 kV/110 V. Determine:
- (i) The apparent impedance seen by the relay.
- (ii) The primary impedance at the fault point.
- (iii) Whether the relay operates in Zone 1 or Zone 2, and the operating time. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Voltage Relay Lab Test (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The lab test setup consists of:
1. **AC Variac (0–270 V)** — to vary the input voltage smoothly.
2. **Digital Voltmeter (DVM)** — to measure RMS voltage accurately.
3. **Voltage Relay Under Test** — the DUT (Device Under Test).
4. **Pickup Indicator (LED/Lamp)** — shows when the relay coil is energized.
5. **Trip Indicator + Trip Coil** — shows the trip contact closure.
6. **Timer (Electronic Stopwatch)** — measures operating time.

**Procedure for Pickup Voltage (2 marks):**

1. Set the **TDM** to a fixed value (e.g., 1.0).
2. Apply **rated voltage** initially and observe the relay state (should be restrained).
3. **Gradually increase (OVR) or decrease (UVR)** the variac slowly.
4. Note the voltage at which the **pickup LED** first turns on → this is $V_{pickup}$.
5. Repeat three times and take the **average** to eliminate reading errors.

**Procedure for Operating Time (2 marks):**

1. With relay de-energized, set the variac to give a voltage **multiple of pickup** (e.g., 1.2×, 1.5×, 2×).
2. Simultaneously start the timer and apply the voltage.
3. Stop the timer when the trip contact closes.
4. Record the **operating time**.
5. Repeat for different TDM values (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0) and at different multiples of pickup.
6. Plot the **IDMT characteristic curve** (time vs. multiple of pickup).

**Part (b) — Distance Relay Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Compute CTR and PTR.**

$$
CTR = \frac{600}{1} = 600, \quad PTR = \frac{220{,}000}{110} = 2000
$$

**Step 2: Compute the apparent impedance (secondary).**

$$
Z_{apparent(sec)} = \frac{V_{secondary}}{I_{secondary}}
$$

The CT secondary current is:

$$
I_{secondary} = \frac{I_{primary}}{CTR} = \frac{3000}{600} = 5 \text{ A}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Z_{apparent(sec)} = \frac{55}{5} = 11 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the primary impedance at the fault.**

$$
Z_{apparent(pri)} = Z_{apparent(sec)} \cdot CTR \cdot PTR
$$

$$
Z_{apparent(pri)} = 11 \cdot 600 \cdot 2000 = 13{,}200{,}000 \text{ Ω} = 13.2 \text{ MΩ}
$$

**Step 4: Determine the operating zone.**

The fault is at **90% of the line**. 

- Zone 1 covers **0% to 80%** of the line → fault at 90% is **outside Zone 1**.
- Zone 2 covers **0% to 150%** of the line → fault at 90% is **inside Zone 2**.

**Step 5: Determine the operating time.**

The relay operates in **Zone 2** with a time delay of **350 ms**.

$$
\boxed{t_{op} = 350 \text{ ms}}
$$

**Step 6: Concluding statement.**

The relay **will NOT trip instantaneously**; it will trip after a **350 ms delay** because the fault is outside Zone 1 but within Zone 2.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing Primary and Secondary Quantities (–3 marks):** The single most common error. Always state which side (primary or secondary) the impedance, voltage, and current are on. Use the **conversion formula $Z_{primary} = Z_{secondary} \cdot CTR \cdot PTR$** explicitly.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Phase Voltage (–2 marks):** For three-phase faults, the line-to-line voltage must be divided by $\sqrt{3}$ to obtain phase voltage. Using line voltage directly gives wrong current.
> 
> 3. **Confusing OVR and UVR Multiple of Pickup (–2 marks):** For OVR, $M = V_{actual}/V_{pickup}$; for UVR, $M = V_{pickup}/V_{actual}$. Students often invert this.
> 
> 4. **Not Drawing the R-X Diagram (–2 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly look for the R-X plane sketch with the operating circle. Omitting the diagram costs **2 marks minimum**.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting Units (–1 mark):** Always write **Ω (sec)**, **Ω (pri)**, **V (rms)**, **A (sec)** etc. Unit-less answers lose marks.
> 
> 6. **IDMT Equation Mistakes (–2 marks):** Use the **standard inverse** form: $t = TDM \cdot (A/(M^n - 1) + B)$ with $A = 3.922$, $B = 0.0982$, $n = 2$. Do **not** invent constants.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Voltage Relay** operates on the **magnitude of voltage** alone; it has **no current input**.
- **Impedance Relay** operates on the **ratio V/I**; it is the **simplest form of distance relay** and is **non-directional**.
- The **R-X plane** is the impedance relay's "workspace" — each fault plots as a point.
- The **operating characteristic** of a pure impedance relay is a **circle centered at the origin** of radius $Z_{pickup}$.
- The **pickup impedance** is defined as $Z_{pickup} = V_{pickup} / I_{pickup}$ measured on the **secondary side**.
- **Conversion formula:** $Z_{primary} = Z_{secondary} \cdot CTR \cdot PTR$ — **memorize this**.
- **CTR** = CT primary current / CT secondary current (e.g., 400/5 → CTR = 80).
- **PTR** = PT primary voltage / PT secondary voltage (e.g., 11000/110 → PTR = 100).
- The **mho relay** is a directional impedance relay whose characteristic circle **passes through the origin**, making it inherently directional.
- For **three-phase fault current**, always use **phase voltage** ($V_{LL} / \sqrt{3}$), not line voltage.
- The **IDMT characteristic** is given by $t = TDM \cdot (A/(M^n - 1) + B)$ with standard IEEE constants.
- The relay **trips** if $Z_{apparent} \leq Z_{pickup}$ (inside circle) and **restrains** otherwise.
- The **R-X diagram must be drawn** in every KTU answer involving distance/impedance relays — examiners explicitly reward this.
- The **secondary injection test** is the standard KTU lab method: inject V and I at relay terminals using a test kit.
- The **pickup voltage** of an OVR is **above rated**; the **pickup voltage** of a UVR is **below rated**.
- **Lab recording must include:** pickup voltage, operating time, TDM setting, multiple of pickup, and the plotted IDMT curve.
- **The "reach"** in distance relay terminology = the **fraction of the line** covered by the relay's zone (e.g., Zone 1 = 80%, Zone 2 = 150%).
- **Time delays** in distance relays: Zone 1 = ~0 ms, Zone 2 = ~350 ms, Zone 3 = ~1 second (typical).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insulation Testing: LT and HT Cable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Insulation Testing: LT and HT Cable # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insulation Testing (also called Dielectric Withstand / Megger Test)** is a non-destructive electrical diagnostic procedure used to evaluate the **dielectric integrity** of the insulating material surrounding current-carrying conductors in **Low Tension (LT)** and **High Tension (HT)** power cables. The test quantifies the **Insulation Resistance (IR)** measured in **Mega-ohms (MΩ)** by applying a stable DC high-voltage source across the insulation barrier. ### Terminology Breakdown (KTU Standard Lexicon) | Term | Expansion | KTU Standard Meaning | |------|-----------|---------------------| | LT Cable | Low Tension Cable | Cables rated for voltages up to **1.1 kV** (typically 415 V / 240 V distribution) | | HT Cable | High Tension Cable | Cables rated for voltages from **1.1 kV** up to **33 kV** (and above) | | IR | Insulation Resistance | Opposition offered by the dielectric to leakage current, expressed in **MΩ** | | Megger | Mega-ohm-meter | Portable instrument generating **500 V / 1000 V / 2500 V / 5000 V DC** for IR measurement | | PI | Polarization Index | Ratio of IR measured at 10 min to IR measured at 1 min | | DAR | Dielectric Absorption Ratio | Ratio of IR measured at 60 s to IR measured at 30 s | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** Students must record IR values for **LT** cables (single-core, two-core, three-core) and **HT** cables between **(a) each core and earth (sheath)**, and **(b) between any two cores**, then calculate leakage current, insulation resistance per km, and analyze results. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **water pipe** carrying pressurized water (current flowing through conductor). The pipe wall is the **insulation**. Over time, hairline cracks develop in the pipe wall due to aging, heat, moisture, or chemical attack. Water starts **seeping out** (leakage current flows through insulation to ground). **Insulation testing is like pressure-testing the pipe wall from inside** — we pump a high DC voltage (pressure) through the conductor and measure how much current "leaks" through the insulation wall to earth. The lower the leakage, the healthier the insulation. - **LT cable** → thin-walled garden hose (low pressure inside) - **HT cable** → thick-walled industrial hydraulic pipe (very high pressure inside, requires tougher test voltages) > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** If the IR is **infinite (∞)**, the insulation is perfect. If IR drops to **kΩ range**, the cable is unsafe and must be discarded. For healthy LT cables, IR should be **> 1 MΩ**; for healthy HT cables, IR should be **> 100 MΩ** per km. ## 1.3 Standards & Reference Frameworks The experiment follows: - **IS 1554 (Part 1)** — PVC insulated LT cables - **IS 7098 (Part 1 & 2)** — XLPE/EPR insulated HT cables - **IS 5831** — PVC insulation sheath testing - **IEEE 43-2013** — Recommended practice for testing insulation resistance of electric machinery (extended to cables) - **IEC 60364** — Low-voltage electrical installations testing - **NEVER USE AC** — Only **DC voltage** is used because AC would cause continuous charging currents and inductive heating, masking leakage measurements. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why DC and not AC for IR measurement?** Under DC, the cable acts as a pure capacitor (no inductive reactance). The very small steady leakage current can be precisely measured. Under AC, capacitive charging current dominates and **insulation leakage cannot be distinguished** from charging current. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physics of Insulation Resistance ### Why Insulation is Not a Perfect Insulator Even the best dielectric (XLPE, EPR, PVC) has a finite resistivity $\rho$ (typically $10^{12}$ to $10^{16}$ Ω·m). When DC voltage $V$ is applied: $$I_{total} = I_{capacitive} + I_{conductive} + I_{absorption}$$ - **$I_{capacitive}$** → initial high current that decays as cable charges up - **$I_{conductive}$** → true leakage through dielectric (constant) - **$I_{absorption}$** → molecular polarization current (decays over minutes) The **steady-state** reading on the Megger corresponds to **$I_{conductive}$**, giving: $$R_{insulation} = \frac{V_{applied}}{I_{leakage}}$$ ## 2.2 Insulation Resistance of a Single-Core Cable For a single-core cylindrical cable of: - Conductor radius = $r$ (or $d/2$) - Inner sheath radius = $R$ (or $D/2$) - Length = $L$ (in km) - Material resistivity = $\rho$ $$R_{insulation} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r}\right) = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D}{d}\right) \cdot 2.303$$ Or in KTU-preferred logarithmic form: $$R = \frac{2.303 \cdot \rho}{2\pi L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D}{d}\right) \;\; \Omega$$ ### Parameters at a Glance - $\rho$ = resistivity of insulating material (Ω·m) - $D$ = inner diameter of sheath (m) - $d$ = diameter of conductor (m) - $L$ = length of cable (km) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Application | Units | Validity / Note | |---|---------|-------------|-------|-----------------| | 1 | $R = \frac{2.303\,\rho}{2\pi L}\log_{10}(D/d)$ | IR of single-core cable | $\Omega$ | Fundamental KTU derivation | | 2 | $R_{per\,km} = R_{measured} \times L$ | Normalize IR to per-km basis | $\Omega$/km | Standard for comparison | | 3 | $I_{leakage} = \dfrac{V_{test}}{R_{insulation}}$ | Calculate leakage current | A | Used in all practical problems | | 4 | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}}$ | Polarization Index | dimensionless | Healthy insulation: $\text{PI} \geq 2$ | | 5 | $\text{DAR} = \dfrac{R_{60\,s}}{R_{30\,s}}$ | Dielectric Absorption Ratio | dimensionless | Healthy insulation: $\text{DAR} \geq 1.4$ | | 6 | $k = \dfrac{\text{Megger reading (M}\Omega\text{)}}{\text{Cable length (km)}}$ | Quick health check (LT) | MΩ/km | LT healthy: $k \geq 1$ | | 7 | $k = \dfrac{\text{Megger reading (M}\Omega\text{)}}{\text{Cable length (km)}}$ | Quick health check (HT) | MΩ/km | HT healthy: $k \geq 100$ | | 8 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (2\pi L)}{2.303 \log_{10}(D/d)}$ | Cable capacitance (for time constant) | F | Used in PI calculations | | 9 | $\tau = R_{insulation} \times C_{cable}$ | Cable charging time constant | s | Useful for selecting test duration | > [!IMPORTANT] > **MN Rule for IR — KTU Standard:** IR $\times$ Length = constant (for cables of same type and cross-section). Hence to compare results from different lengths, always convert to **MΩ per km**. ## 2.4 LT vs HT Cable — Engineering Distinction | Parameter | LT Cable | HT Cable | |-----------|----------|----------| | Voltage rating | $\leq 1.1$ kV | $1.1$ kV to $33$ kV (and above) | | Insulation thickness | Thin (1–2 mm typical) | Thick (3–25 mm depending on class) | | Typical dielectric | PVC | XLPE / EPR | | Megger test voltage | **500 V DC** | **1000 V / 2500 V / 5000 V DC** | | Minimum acceptable IR | $\geq 1$ MΩ per km | $\geq 100$ MΩ per km | | Acceptance standard | IS 1554 | IS 7098 | | Number of cores (common) | 2, 3, 4 | 3 (3-core) or single-core | | Test duration | 30 s to 1 min | 1 min, 10 min (for PI) | | Electric stress grading | Not required | Required (semiconducting layers) | > [!TIP] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Insulation testing is mandatory at: > - Factory acceptance tests (FAT) before cable dispatch > - Site acceptance tests (SAT) before energizing new installations > - Periodic maintenance (annually for HT, half-yearly for critical feeders) > - Post-fault verification before re-energizing ## 2.5 Theory of Polarization Index (PI) When DC voltage is applied, the dielectric molecules realign themselves. This produces a **slow-decaying absorption current** superimposed on the steady leakage current. The IR therefore **rises** with time. - **Dry, healthy insulation** → PI $\geq$ 2.0 (current drops a lot because absorption current is large and decays) - **Wet/contaminated insulation** → PI $\approx$ 1.0 (current dominated by leakage, absorption effect small) $$\boxed{\;\text{PI} = \frac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}} \geq 2.0 \; \text{(healthy)}\;}$$ # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Insulation Resistance of a Single-Core Cable ### Step 1 — Visualize the Geometry Consider a coaxial cylindrical cable: - Inner conductor radius = $a$ (in metres) - Inner sheath radius = $b$ (in metres) - Length = $L$ (in km — convert to metres during derivation) Current leaks **radially outward** through the insulation. ### Step 2 — Consider an Elementary Shell At radius $r$ (where $a \leq r \leq b$), consider a thin cylindrical shell of thickness $dr$. The leakage current flows radially through this shell. The cross-sectional area available for current flow at radius $r$ is: $$A(r) = 2\pi r L$$ ### Step 3 — Resistivity Equation The resistance of the elementary shell is: $$dR = \frac{\rho \, dr}{A(r)} = \frac{\rho \, dr}{2\pi r L}$$ ### Step 4 — Integrate Across the Insulation Thickness Since all elementary shells are in **series** (current flows through them sequentially), we integrate from $a$ to $b$: $$R_{insulation} = \int_{a}^{b} dR = \int_{a}^{b} \frac{\rho \, dr}{2\pi r L}$$ Pulling out the constants: $$R_{insulation} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \int_{a}^{b} \frac{dr}{r}$$ ### Step 5 — Perform the Integration The integral $\int \frac{dr}{r} = \ln(r)$ evaluated between $a$ and $b$: $$R_{insulation} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \big[\ln(r)\big]_{a}^{b} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$ ### Step 6 — Convert to $\log_{10}$ for Practical Use Using the identity $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$, and substituting $b = D/2$, $a = d/2$: $$R_{insulation} = \frac{2.303 \, \rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D}{d}\right)$$ This is the **famous KTU derivation** for insulation resistance of a single-core cable. ### Step 7 — Note the Units If $\rho$ is in Ω·m and $L$ is in metres, then $R$ is in Ω. To express in **MΩ per km** for HV cable comparisons, divide by $10^6$ and multiply $L$ by $10^{-3}$ accordingly. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem #1 (KTU Standard Pattern) > **Problem:** A single-core cable has conductor diameter **15 mm** and inner sheath diameter **45 mm**. The insulation material has resistivity **5 × 10¹² Ω·m**. Calculate the insulation resistance for a **5 km** length. Test voltage used is **500 V DC**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Given:** - $d = 15$ mm $= 0.015$ m - $D = 45$ mm $= 0.045$ m - $\rho = 5 \times 10^{12}$ Ω·m - $L = 5$ km $= 5000$ m - $V_{test} = 500$ V DC **Step 1 — Substitute into the formula:** $$R = \frac{2.303 \times 5 \times 10^{12}}{2\pi \times 5000} \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.045}{0.015}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Compute the logarithmic ratio:** $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.045}{0.015}\right) = \log_{10}(3) = 0.4771$$ **Step 3 — Compute the denominator:** $$2\pi \times 5000 = 31415.93$$ **Step 4 — Numerator:** $$2.303 \times 5 \times 10^{12} = 1.1515 \times 10^{13}$$ **Step 5 — Assemble the expression:** $$R = \frac{1.1515 \times 10^{13} \times 0.4771}{31415.93}$$ **Step 6 — Final calculation:** $$R = \frac{5.494 \times 10^{12}}{31415.93} = 1.748 \times 10^{8} \, \Omega$$ **Step 7 — Convert to Mega-ohms:** $$\boxed{R \approx 174.8 \; \text{M}\Omega \text{ (for 5 km)}}$$ **Step 8 — Per km normalization (KTU requirement):** $$R_{per\,km} = \frac{174.8}{5} = 34.96 \; \text{M}\Omega / \text{km}$$ **Step 9 — Leakage current calculation:** $$I_{leakage} = \frac{V_{test}}{R_{insulation}} = \frac{500}{1.748 \times 10^{8}} = 2.86 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{A} \approx 2.86 \; \mu\text{A}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Acceptance verdict:** This LT cable is **healthy**, since IR > 1 MΩ/km (acceptance threshold for LT). ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem #2 — PI Calculation > **Problem:** For an 11 kV HT cable (3-core, 5 km), the Megger readings at standard test voltage of 5000 V DC were: > - At 1 minute: **500 MΩ** > - At 10 minutes: **1500 MΩ** > > Calculate: **(a)** PI, **(b)** IR per km, **(c)** leakage current at full test voltage. ### Solution **Step 1 — Polarization Index:** $$\text{PI} = \frac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}} = \frac{1500}{500} = 3.0$$ Since PI = 3.0 $\geq$ 2.0, **the insulation is healthy and dry**. **Step 2 — IR per km:** $$R_{per\,km} = \frac{1500 \, \text{M}\Omega}{5 \, \text{km}} = 300 \; \text{M}\Omega / \text{km}$$ Since 300 MΩ/km $\gg$ 100 MΩ/km, the cable passes the IR criterion. **Step 3 — Leakage current at 5000 V:** $$I_{leakage} = \frac{5000}{1500 \times 10^{6}} = 3.33 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{A} \approx 3.33 \; \mu\text{A}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Mark-Saver Insight:** Always state the **acceptance verdict** explicitly after numerical calculations — examiners award marks for the conclusion, not just the arithmetic. ## 3.4 Multi-Core Cable Adjustment (Buchholz / Empirical Method) For **multi-core cables**, the simple single-core formula does not apply directly. Engineers use empirical correction: $$R_{multi\,core} \approx \frac{R_{single\,core}}{n^{0.5}}$$ where $n$ = number of cores (when all cores tested together with sheath as common return). For a **3-core cable** (HT), tested with all 3 cores bundled and measured against sheath: $$R_{3\,core} = \frac{R_{single\,core}}{\sqrt{3}}$$ If single-core equivalent gives 174.8 MΩ, then: $$R_{3\,core} = \frac{174.8}{1.732} \approx 100.9 \; \text{M}\Omega$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — What Does the Reading Tell Us? | Megger Reading | PI Value | Diagnosis | Action | |----------------|----------|-----------|--------| | $\infty$ (needle stays at max) | Cannot measure | Perfect / open circuit | Continue to next phase | | > 1000 MΩ | $\geq 2.5$ | Excellent insulation | Energize cable | | 100–1000 MΩ | 2.0–2.5 | Good insulation | Energize, log reading | | 10–100 MΩ | 1.5–2.0 | Marginal insulation | Investigate, locate fault | | 1–10 MΩ | 1.0–1.5 | Poor / moist insulation | Dry out, retest | | < 1 MΩ | ~1.0 | Failed insulation | **Reject the cable** | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **megger readings** with **ohmmeter readings**. The Megger itself is a high-voltage DC generator + sensitive ammeter — it is **not** a multimeter. Never use a regular multimeter for insulation testing on HT cables. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Insulation Test Connection Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PowerSource["DC High Voltage Source (Megger Internal Generator)"] DC["500 V / 1000 V / 2500 V / 5000 V DC"] NEG["Negative Terminal (E)"] POS["Positive Terminal (L)"] end subgraph CableUnderTest["Cable Under Test (CUT)"] CORE["Conductor Core"] INS["Dielectric Insulation"] SHEATH["Metallic Sheath / Armour"] end subgraph Metering["Measurement Readout"] METER["Analog Needle / Digital Display in MΩ"] end POS --> CORE CORE --- INS INS --- SHEATH SHEATH --> NEG NEG --> DC DC --> POS POS --> METER style DC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style METER fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style INS fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style SHEATH fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Insulation Test Procedure ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Step 1: De-energize and isolate cable
Confirm zero voltage with VTVM"] --> B["Step 2: Discharge any stored charge
Short all cores to earth for 1 minute"] B --> C["Step 3: Identify test configuration
Core-Earth or Core-Core"] C --> D["Step 4: Connect Megger leads
L to conductor, E to sheath"] D --> E["Step 5: Select test voltage
500V LT, 1000-5000V HT"] E --> F["Step 6: Crank or press TEST button
Apply DC voltage steadily"] F --> G["Step 7: Read IR at 30s, 60s, 10min
Record in tabular form"] G --> H["Step 8: Disconnect, discharge cable
Short to earth before handling"] H --> I["Step 9: Compute PI, IR/km, I_leakage
Compare with IS standards"] I --> J{"Step 10: Pass or Fail?"} J -->|Pass| K["Document and energize cable"] J -->|Fail| L["Mark faulty section, replace"] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513 style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF style J fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style L fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 ``` ## 4.3 Megger Internal Architecture (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HandCrank["Hand-Cranked / Battery Source"] CRANK["Mechanical Crank or Battery 9V"] end subgraph Generator["DC-DC Boost Converter"] OSC["Oscillator Circuit"] XFRM["Step-Up Transformer"] RECT["Voltage Multiplier Rectifier"] end subgraph Regulator["Voltage Regulator"] REG["Zener / Feedback Regulator"] end subgraph OutputStage["Output and Measurement"] OUT["HV DC Output Terminals L and E"] AMM["Sensitive Moving-Coil Ammeter"] SHUNT["Current Shunt Resistor"] OUT --> SHUNT --> AMM end CRANK --> OSC --> XFRM --> RECT --> REG --> OUT style OSC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B style RECT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B style AMM fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 ``` ## 4.4 Test Configuration Schematic — Three Measurements ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Test1["Test 1: Phase R to Earth"] T1A["Megger L terminal"] T1B["Phase R Conductor"] T1C["Earth Sheath"] T1A --> T1B T1C -.-> T1A end subgraph Test2["Test 2: Phase Y to Earth"] T2A["Megger L terminal"] T2B["Phase Y Conductor"] T2C["Earth Sheath"] T2A --> T2B T2C -.-> T2A end subgraph Test3["Test 3: Phase Y to Phase B"] T3A["Megger L terminal"] T3B["Phase Y Conductor"] T3C["Phase B Conductor"] T3A --> T3B T3C -.-> T3A end Test1 ~~~ Test2 Test2 ~~~ Test3 style Test1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513 style Test2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513 style Test3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **For LT cables** the standard is **500 V DC** test. For **HT cables (11 kV)**, use **1000 V or 2500 V DC**. For **EHV cables (33 kV and above)**, **5000 V DC** is mandated per IEEE 43-2013 / IS 7098. ## 4.5 Observation Table Template (for KTU Lab Record) | S.No | Cable Type | Length (m) | Core-Earth IR (MΩ) | Core-Core IR (MΩ) | Test Voltage (V) | Leakage Current (μA) | IR/km (MΩ/km) | Result | |------|------------|------------|--------------------|--------------------|------------------|----------------------|---------------|--------| | 1 | LT 2-core | 100 | — | — | 500 | — | — | — | | 2 | LT 3-core | 150 | — | — | 500 | — | — | — | | 3 | HT 3-core | 500 | — | — | 2500 | — | — | — | | 4 | HT 3-core | 1000 | — | — | 5000 | — | — | — | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Define Insulation Resistance. Why is DC voltage used (not AC) for insulation testing of cables?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Insulation Resistance (IR) is the electrical resistance offered by the dielectric material of a cable between the conductor and earth (sheath) or between two conductors, when a DC voltage is applied. It is measured in Mega-ohms (MΩ) and indicates the ability of the insulation to oppose leakage current. **Why DC and not AC (1.5 Marks):** 1. Under DC, the cable acts purely as a capacitor with no inductive reactance, so only steady leakage current flows which can be accurately measured. 2. Under AC, the dominant capacitive charging current would mask the small leakage current, making IR measurement impossible. 3. DC testing is non-destructive and safer for both the operator and the insulation, as no continuous heating occurs. 4. The polarization index (PI) measurement requires a stable DC application over time. --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **What is Polarization Index? State its significance and the value for healthy insulation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Polarization Index (PI) is the ratio of insulation resistance measured after **10 minutes** of applied DC voltage to the insulation resistance measured after **1 minute**. $$\text{PI} = \frac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}}$$ **Significance (1.5 Marks):** - Indicates moisture content and overall condition of the insulation. - Eliminates the effect of ambient temperature and surface leakage. - A high PI means the insulation is dry, clean, and the dielectric absorption current is high — a sign of healthy insulation. - Used as a trending parameter over the life of the cable. **Healthy Value (0.5 Mark):** $$\text{PI} \geq 2.0 \;\; (\text{KTU standard — healthy insulation})$$ If PI < 1.5, the insulation is **suspected faulty** and needs drying or replacement. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the insulation resistance of a single-core cable. State the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A single-core cable has conductor diameter **20 mm**, inner sheath diameter **50 mm**, and insulation resistivity **4 × 10¹² Ω·m**. Calculate the insulation resistance per km and the leakage current when tested with a 500 V Megger. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** ### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)** **Assumptions (1 Mark):** 1. The cable is cylindrical and coaxial with uniform insulation. 2. Leakage current flows **radially outward** through the insulation. 3. The insulating material is **homogeneous and isotropic** with constant resistivity $\rho$. 4. The temperature is uniform throughout the insulation. **Derivation (6 Marks):** Consider an elementary cylindrical shell at radius $r$, thickness $dr$, and length $L$. The cross-sectional area for current flow at radius $r$ is: $$A(r) = 2\pi r L$$ The resistance of the elementary shell is: $$dR = \frac{\rho \, dr}{A(r)} = \frac{\rho \, dr}{2\pi r L}$$ Since all elemental shells are in **series**, total insulation resistance is obtained by integration between $a = d/2$ and $b = D/2$: $$R = \int_{a}^{b} \frac{\rho \, dr}{2\pi r L} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \int_{a}^{b} \frac{dr}{r}$$ Evaluating the integral: $$R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{d}\right)$$ Converting natural log to common log using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$: $$\boxed{\;R = \frac{2.303 \, \rho}{2\pi L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D}{d}\right) \; \Omega\;}$$ ### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)** **Given:** - $d = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m - $D = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m - $\rho = 4 \times 10^{12}$ Ω·m - $L = 1$ km $= 1000$ m - $V = 500$ V DC **Step 1 — Logarithmic ratio (2 Marks):** $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D}{d}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{50}{20}\right) = \log_{10}(2.5) = 0.3979$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the formula (2 Marks):** $$R = \frac{2.303 \times 4 \times 10^{12}}{2\pi \times 1000} \times 0.3979$$ **Step 3 — Compute numerically (2 Marks):** $$R = \frac{9.212 \times 10^{12} \times 0.3979}{6283.19} = \frac{3.666 \times 10^{12}}{6283.19} = 5.834 \times 10^{8} \, \Omega$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 583.4 \; \text{M}\Omega \text{ per km}}$$ **Step 4 — Leakage current (1 Mark):** $$I = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{500}{5.834 \times 10^{8}} = 8.57 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{A} \approx 0.857 \; \mu\text{A}$$ **Conclusion:** IR $> 1$ MΩ/km, so the cable is **healthy**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the procedure for conducting insulation resistance testing on an **LT 3-core cable** using a Megger. Draw the connection diagram for all three core-earth tests. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** During insulation testing of an **11 kV HT cable**, the following Megger readings (at 2500 V DC) were obtained: | Time | IR Reading | |------|------------| | 30 s | 200 MΩ | | 60 s | 400 MΩ | | 10 min | 1200 MΩ | Compute: **(i)** DAR, **(ii)** PI, **(iii)** IR per km for a 4 km cable, and **(iv)** state the condition of insulation. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** ### **Part (a) — LT 3-Core Cable Test Procedure (7 Marks)** **Apparatus (1 Mark):** - 500 V DC Megger (hand-cranked or electronic) - LT 3-core PVC cable sample - Connecting leads, screwdriver, gloves **Procedure (4 Marks):** 1. Ensure the cable is **completely de-energized** and isolated from the supply. Verify using a voltmeter. 2. **Short all three cores together** and connect to earth for 1 minute to discharge any stored charge. 3. **Test 1 — Red Core to Earth:** Connect Megger **L (Line)** terminal to the Red core, and **E (Earth)** terminal to the cable metallic sheath/armour. Operate Megger at 500 V DC and read IR after 1 minute. 4. **Test 2 — Yellow Core to Earth:** Repeat step 3 with Yellow core. 5. **Test 3 — Blue Core to Earth:** Repeat step 3 with Blue core. 6. **Test 4 — Red-Yellow Core to Core:** Connect L to Red core, E to Yellow core (both Blue and sheath grounded). Read IR. 7. **Test 5 — Yellow-Blue:** Similar procedure. 8. **Test 6 — Red-Blue:** Similar procedure. 9. After each test, **discharge the cable** by shorting to earth before handling. **Connection Diagram Description (2 Marks):** Three sequential tests are performed with Megger **L** terminal moved across R, Y, B cores sequentially while **E** terminal remains connected to the metallic sheath. The third terminal is left floating (not connected to anything) during each test. **Inference:** All IR readings should be $> 1$ MΩ per km for LT cables. ### **Part (b) — HT Cable Analysis (7 Marks)** **Given:** 11 kV cable, 4 km, 2500 V DC Megger **Step 1 — Dielectric Absorption Ratio (2 Marks):** $$\text{DAR} = \frac{R_{60\,s}}{R_{30\,s}} = \frac{400}{200} = 2.0$$ Since DAR $\geq 1.4$, insulation is in good condition. **Step 2 — Polarization Index (2 Marks):** $$\text{PI} = \frac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}} = \frac{1200}{400} = 3.0$$ Since PI $\geq 2.0$, insulation is **excellent**. **Step 3 — IR per km (2 Marks):** $$R_{per\,km} = \frac{1200 \, \text{M}\Omega}{4 \, \text{km}} = 300 \; \text{M}\Omega / \text{km}$$ Since 300 MΩ/km $\gg 100$ MΩ/km (HT threshold), the cable passes. **Step 4 — Condition of insulation (1 Mark):** The insulation is **healthy, dry, and free from contamination**. The cable can be safely energized. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** — Mixing km and m in the same formula costs 2-3 marks. > 2. **Not computing IR per km** — KTU specifically asks for normalization; raw IR values get partial credit only. > 3. **Skipping the discharge step** in the procedure — A mandatory safety step worth 1 mark. > 4. **Writing AC instead of DC** in the procedure — instant 1-mark penalty. > 5. **Not stating the acceptance verdict** — "Conclusion" line is essential. > 6. **Confusing Megger voltage rating** with cable operating voltage — Megger voltage is a **test voltage**, not the operating voltage. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST - ✅ Insulation Resistance (IR) is measured in **MΩ** using a **Megger** (DC high-voltage source + ammeter). - ✅ Always use **DC voltage** — never AC — for IR testing. - ✅ Standard Megger voltages: **500 V DC (LT)**, **1000/2500/5000 V DC (HT)**. - ✅ Fundamental formula: $\boxed{\,R = \dfrac{2.303\,\rho}{2\pi L}\log_{10}(D/d)\,}$ - ✅ Acceptance threshold: **LT** ≥ 1 MΩ/km | **HT** ≥ 100 MΩ/km. - ✅ Polarization Index (PI) = $R_{10\,min} / R_{1\,min}$ $\geq$ 2.0 for healthy insulation. - ✅ Dielectric Absorption Ratio (DAR) = $R_{60\,s} / R_{30\,s}$ $\geq$ 1.4 for healthy insulation. - ✅ Always **discharge the cable** before and after testing by shorting to earth. - ✅ Leakage current: $\boxed{\,I_{leakage} = V_{test} / R_{insulation}\,}$ - ✅ Always normalize IR to **per km** for comparison using $R_{per\,km} = R_{measured} \times L_{km}$. - ✅ For multi-core cables, use empirical correction $R_{n\,core} \approx R_{single\,core} / \sqrt{n}$. - ✅ Always perform **3 core-earth tests** + **3 core-core tests** for 3-core cables. - ✅ Standards followed: **IS 1554 (LT PVC)**, **IS 7098 (HT XLPE)**, **IEEE 43-2013**, **IEC 60364**. - ✅ PI $< 1.5$ → insulation is faulty → replace cable. - ✅ Megger is a **non-destructive test** — it does not harm the insulation.

topics.title: Testing of CT and PT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Testing of CT and PT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Instrument Transformers – The Foundation

In high-voltage power systems, directly measuring currents of thousands of amperes and voltages of hundreds of kilovolts using conventional ammeters and voltmeters is **practically impossible** and **unsafe**. The solution is the **Instrument Transformer** — a class of transformers purpose-built to step down these quantities to safe, standardized, measurable levels, while simultaneously providing **isolation** between the high-voltage primary circuit and the low-voltage measurement/relay side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Exact):** *Instrument transformers* are specially designed transformers used to isolate the secondary measurement and protection circuits from the primary high-voltage/high-current power circuit, and to provide proportional, scaled-down replicas of the primary current (CT) or voltage (PT) for metering, protection, and control.

There are two fundamental members of this family:

| Instrument Transformer | Function | Scales Down | Secondary Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Current Transformer (CT)** | Steps down current | Primary current $I_p$ → Secondary current $I_s$ | **5 A** (or 1 A) |
| **Potential Transformer (PT) / Voltage Transformer (VT)** | Steps down voltage | Primary voltage $V_p$ → Secondary voltage $V_s$ | **110 V / $\sqrt{3}$** (phase), **110 V** (line) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making Sense of CT and PT

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Camera Lens of a Power System**
> Think of a power substation as a giant, dangerous wild animal. You cannot measure its "size" (voltage) or "strength" (current) by hand. A CT is like a **current clamp** — a magnifying lens that shrinks the animal's roaring strength (kA) into a tame purr (5 A) that a small measuring device (ammeter) can handle. A PT is like a **voltage-reducing periscope** — it lets you look at a 110 kV line from a safe distance and see it as a harmless 110 V signal on your voltmeter.
>
> Both devices do *not* alter the **shape** or **timing** of the waveform — only its **magnitude**. Just as a good camera lens preserves the proportions of an image, a good CT/PT must faithfully reproduce the primary signal on the secondary side.

## 1.3 Why Do We *Test* CTs and PTs?

A CT or PT is considered "ideal" only when:
1. The **turns ratio** is **exactly** equal to the inverse of the transformation ratio.
2. The secondary current/voltage is **exactly in phase** with the primary quantity.

In reality, no transformer is ideal. Each CT/PT has:
- **Ratio Error (Amplitude Error)** — the magnitude is not exactly scaled.
- **Phase Error (Angle Error)** — the secondary phasor is slightly displaced from the primary.

These errors directly affect the **accuracy of metering** (incorrect energy billing) and the **coordination of protection relays** (false tripping or failure to trip). Therefore, **testing** is mandatory to classify the CT/PT into an **accuracy class** as per standards (e.g., **IS 2705**, **IEC 61869-2**, **IEEE C57.13**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** As per KTU 2024 lab syllabus (PCEEL609), the experiment on *Testing of CT and PT* aims to verify the **transformation ratio**, determine the **polarity**, measure the **ratio error** and **phase angle error**, and confirm the **burden** handling capability of the instrument transformers.

## 1.4 Classification of Tests (Big Picture)

The complete set of tests is logically grouped as follows:

**A. Polarity Test**
- Verifies the **relative direction** of primary and secondary windings (dot convention).
- Critical for correct relay/ammeter connection.

**B. Ratio Test (Transformation Ratio Verification)**
- Compares the **actual** ratio with the **nameplate** (rated) ratio.
- Yields the **ratio error** in percentage.

**C. Phase Angle (Phase Error) Test**
- Measures the angular displacement between primary and secondary phators.
- Expressed in **minutes** or **centi-radians**.

**D. Knee-Point Voltage Test (for Protection CTs)**
- Determines the voltage at which the CT core begins to **saturate** heavily.
- Vital for protection-class CTs (e.g., **5P20**, **10P**, **PS class**).

**E. Burden Test**
- Confirms the rated VA can be delivered without exceeding accuracy limits.
- Performed across standard burdens (e.g., 2.5 VA, 5 VA, 10 VA, 15 VA).

**F. Insulation / Dielectric Test**
- High-voltage withstand between primary–secondary, primary–earth, secondary–earth.
- Not detailed in this experiment; mentioned for completeness.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3 Mandatory KTU Experiments on CT/PT** (most common in university lab exams):
> 1. **Polarity Test** using a 1.5 V battery + DC ammeter (or neon-lamp/moving coil galvanometer).
> 2. **Ratio Test & Phase Error Test** using the **Silsbee's Method** (Deflectional Method) or **Arnold's Method** (Null Method).
> 3. **Knee-Point Voltage Test** for protection CTs.

---

## 1.5 Standard Definitions (Memorize for KTU Viva)

| Term | Definition | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Rated Primary Current / Voltage** | The nominal primary quantity the CT/PT is designed for. | $I_{1n}$ / $V_{1n}$ |
| **Rated Secondary Current / Voltage** | The standardized secondary value (**5 A** for CT, **110 V** for PT). | $I_{2n}$ / $V_{2n}$ |
| **Rated Transformation Ratio (Kn)** | $K_n = \dfrac{I_{1n}}{I_{2n}}$ for CT; $K_n = \dfrac{V_{1n}}{V_{2n}}$ for PT | $K_n$ |
| **Actual Transformation Ratio (Ka)** | $K_a = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2}$ measured during the test | $K_a$ |
| **Nominal Ratio (Kn)** | $K_n = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ (CT) or $K_n = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ (PT) | $K_n$ |
| **Ratio Error** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{K_n - K_a}{K_a} \times 100\,\%$ | $\varepsilon\,\%$ |
| **Phase Error** | Angular displacement $\delta$ between reversed secondary and primary phasors. | $\delta$ in **minutes** |
| **Knee-Point Voltage ($V_{kn}$)** | The sinusoidal rms voltage applied to the secondary (with primary open) at which a **10% increase** in voltage causes a **50% increase** in exciting current. | $V_{kn}$ |
| **Burden** | Total impedance (in VA at rated secondary) connected to the secondary. | $B$ in VA |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle Recap (Why a CT/PT Behaves the Way It Does)

A CT or PT is essentially a **transformer operating under specific loading conditions**:

- A **CT** has its **primary winding connected in series** with the power line (carrying the full line current). The secondary is connected to a near-short circuit (an ammeter or relay coil of very low impedance). It operates **near short-circuit conditions**.
- A **PT** has its **primary winding connected in parallel (shunt)** with the power line. The secondary feeds a high-impedance voltmeter or relay. It operates **near no-load (open-circuit) conditions**.

The **general transformer EMF equation** governs both:
$$E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N$$

where $\phi_m$ is the peak core flux and $N$ is the number of turns. Since $E \propto N$ for a given flux, the **ratio of induced EMFs** equals the **turns ratio**.

## 2.2 The Two Error Sources (Phasor Diagram Insight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **no-load current** ($I_0$) drawn by the core is the **root cause of all CT/PT errors**. It has two components:
> - **Magnetizing component** ($I_m$) — produces the working flux.
> - **Core-loss (working) component** ($I_w$) — supplies the hysteresis + eddy-current losses.
>
> The presence of $I_0$ means the primary MMF ($N_1 I_1$) is **not exactly balanced** by the secondary MMF ($N_2 I_2$); a small residual MMF (= $N_1 I_0$) drives the flux. This residual MMF creates both the **ratio error** and the **phase error**.

### CT Phasor Diagram Logic (Step-by-Step)
1. Draw secondary current $I_2$ as the reference phasor.
2. Add secondary induced EMF $E_2$ leading $I_2$ by the secondary load angle $\theta_2 = \cos^{-1}(R_2 / Z_2)$.
3. Subtract secondary impedance drop $I_2 (R_2 + jX_2)$ to get secondary terminal voltage $V_2$.
4. Apply $-E_2 = N_2 \cdot d\phi / dt$ — flux $\phi$ lags $E_2$ by 90°.
5. No-load current $I_0$ consists of $I_m$ (along $\phi$) and $I_w$ (in phase with $-E_2$).
6. Primary current $I_1 = I_0 + (N_2/N_1) \cdot I_2$ (reversed sign convention).
7. The angle between $-I_1'$ (reversed secondary referred to primary) and $I_1$ is the **phase error $\delta$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "*Imagine a load (R + jX) on the secondary. The primary must supply not just the load-reflected current but also a small extra component (I₀) to magnetize the iron core. This 'extra' is what causes the error.*"

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 exam-grade essentials. Memorize the symbol, the equation, and the unit.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_n = \dfrac{I_{1n}}{I_{2n}}$ (CT) or $\dfrac{V_{1n}}{V_{2n}}$ (PT) | Rated (nameplate) transformation ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $K_a = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2}$ (CT) or $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ (PT) | Actual measured transformation ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K_n = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ (CT) or $\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ (PT) | Nominal (turns) ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{K_n - K_a}{K_a} \times 100\,\%$ | **Ratio Error** | % |
| 5 | Phase Error $\delta$ (from Silsbee/Arnold method) | Angular displacement between primary and reversed secondary | **minutes** (') or centi-radians (crad) |
| 6 | $V_{kn}$ — Knee-point voltage | Voltage at which a **10%** voltage rise gives a **50%** exciting current rise | V (rms) |
| 7 | $V_{2(\text{induced})} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_m \, N_2$ | Induced secondary EMF | V |
| 8 | $E_k = \dfrac{V_{kn}}{I_e}$ | **Exciting volt-ampere** (relates core size to performance) | VA |
| 9 | Burden $B = I_{2n}^{2} \cdot Z_b = V_{2n} \cdot I_{2n}$ | Rated burden (always specified at rated secondary) | VA |
| 10 | $I_1 \cdot N_1 = I_2 \cdot N_2 + I_0 \cdot N_1$ | MMF balance (CT) | AT (ampere-turns) |
| 11 | Accuracy Class (Metering): **0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 3.0** | % ratio error at rated frequency | – |
| 12 | Accuracy Class (Protection): **5P, 10P, 15P** | Composite designation: P-class error + accuracy limit factor (ALF) | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick conversion for phase error units:**
> - 1 radian = $\dfrac{180 \times 60}{\pi}$ = **3437.75 minutes** ≈ **3438 '** .
> - 1 centi-radian = **34.38 minutes**.
> - In KTU exams, phase error is most commonly expressed in **minutes of arc**.

## 2.4 Standard Accuracy Classes (IS 2705 / IEC 61869)

### Metering CT Classes
- **Class 0.1** — Highest accuracy; used for **tariff metering** at EHV (400 kV) substation interchange points.
- **Class 0.2 / 0.5** — Standard tariff metering.
- **Class 1.0 / 3.0** — Industrial metering, indicating instruments.

### Protection CT Classes
- Designation: **5P20** means: ratio error ≤ **5%** at **20 × rated primary current** (the Accuracy Limit Factor, ALF).
- The **composite error** (which includes both ratio + phase errors at high currents) is the specifier for protection CTs.

### PT / VT Accuracy Classes
- **Class 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 3.0** — same as metering CTs, but for voltage.
- Class **3P / 6P** — for protection PTs.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters beyond the exam hall:**
> - **Energy Billing:** A 0.5% error in a 500 MVA interchange CT translates to **₹ lakhs of revenue** per day. Hence, **Class 0.2** metering CTs are mandated at power-grid interchange points by **CTU (Central Transmission Utility)**.
> - **Differential Protection of Transformers/Generators:** Even a 1° phase error in a CT can cause **false differential current**, leading to **incorrect tripping** of a 500 MVA generator. Hence, **Class PS / PX** (low-leakage, defined $V_{kn}$) CTs are mandatory.
> - **Distance Protection:** A 1° error in a PT shifts the apparent impedance locus, jeopardising **Zone-1 tripping coordination**. Hence **Class 3P** PTs are used.

---

## 2.6 Step-by-Step Logic of the Two Common Test Methods

### Method A: Silsbee's Method (Deflectional Method) for Ratio & Phase Error

**Principle:** The instrument transformer's secondary is loaded with a known burden. A standard (calibrated) CT/PT of the same ratio is connected in opposition (differential circuit). A sensitive **Null Detector** measures the difference between the two secondaries. From the magnitude and phase of the null detector reading, the errors are deduced.

**Why this method?** The reference instrument transformer is **assumed ideal**; any residual reading on the null detector is therefore the **error of the CT/PT under test**.

### Method B: Arnold's Method (Null Method with Mutual Inductometer)

**Principle:** A variable **mutual inductometer** and a **variable resistor** are connected in the secondary circuit to compensate the ratio and phase errors exactly. When the null detector reads zero, the variable dial readings directly give the **ratio error (%)** and the **phase error (minutes)**.

**Why this method?** Higher accuracy than Silsbee's; used in **National Metrology Institutes** and manufacturer test labs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Exam Tip:** The Silsbee's method is **most commonly asked** in KTU lab viva. Be ready to draw the circuit, name every component, and explain why the **standard CT/PT must be of higher accuracy class** than the CT/PT under test.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Ratio Error Formula

Let us derive the expression for the **ratio error** of a Current Transformer from first principles.

### Step 1: Write the MMF Balance Equation
For an ideal CT, the primary MMF equals the secondary MMF:
$$N_1 \cdot I_{1,ideal} = N_2 \cdot I_2$$

Solving for the ideal primary current:
$$I_{1,ideal} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right) I_2 = K_n \cdot I_2$$

### Step 2: Account for the Excitation Current
In a real CT, part of the primary MMF is consumed in magnetizing the core. Let $I_0$ be the excitation (no-load) current. Then the actual primary current is:
$$N_1 \cdot I_1 = N_2 \cdot I_2 + N_1 \cdot I_0$$

Dividing by $N_1$:
$$I_1 = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right) I_2 + I_0 = K_n \cdot I_2 + I_0$$

### Step 3: Form the Actual Transformation Ratio
The actual ratio is the ratio of the actual primary current to the actual secondary current:
$$K_a = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{K_n \cdot I_2 + I_0}{I_2} = K_n + \dfrac{I_0}{I_2}$$

### Step 4: Form the Ratio Error
By definition, ratio error $\varepsilon$ is:
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{K_n - K_a}{K_a}$$

Substituting $K_a = K_n + I_0 / I_2$:
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{K_n - \left(K_n + \dfrac{I_0}{I_2}\right)}{K_n + \dfrac{I_0}{I_2}} = \dfrac{-\dfrac{I_0}{I_2}}{K_n + \dfrac{I_0}{I_2}}$$

For a well-designed CT, $I_0 \ll K_n I_2$, so the denominator $\approx K_n$. Therefore:
$$\varepsilon \approx -\dfrac{I_0}{K_n \cdot I_2} \times 100\,\%$$

Or equivalently:
$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx -\dfrac{I_0 \cdot N_1}{I_1 \cdot N_1} \times 100\,\% = -\dfrac{\text{Excitation MMF}}{\text{Total Primary MMF}} \times 100\,\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The ratio error is **negative** for a CT — meaning the secondary current is *slightly higher* than what the nameplate ratio would suggest. This is because some primary MMF is "stolen" by the magnetizing branch, so to maintain the secondary current the same, the primary must be slightly larger.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Phase Error (using Approximate Method)

### Step 1: Resolve the Excitation Current into Components
$$I_0 = I_m + j I_w \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad I_0 = I_0 \cos \alpha + j I_0 \sin \alpha$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle of $I_0$ with respect to the secondary current phasor (i.e., $\alpha = 90° - \theta$ in CT phasor conventions).

### Step 2: Phasor Relationship
From the phasor diagram, the **phase error** $\delta$ (in radians) is approximately:
$$\delta \approx \dfrac{I_0 \cos \alpha}{I_1} \quad \text{(radians)}$$

### Step 3: Convert to Minutes
$$1 \text{ rad} = 3437.75 \text{ minutes}$$

Therefore:
$$\delta \approx \dfrac{I_0 \cos \alpha}{I_1} \times 3437.75 \quad \text{(minutes)}$$

In CT terminology, this is the angular lead of the reversed secondary with respect to the primary.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Ratio Error (Typical KTU 2-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A CT has 200 primary turns and 1000 secondary turns. When tested, primary current $I_1 = 400$ A produces secondary current $I_2 = 3.9$ A. The excitation MMF is found to be 12 AT.
>
> **Find:** (a) The actual transformation ratio, (b) The nominal ratio, (c) The ratio error.

### Solution:

**Step (a): Actual Transformation Ratio ($K_a$)**
$$K_a = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{400}{3.9} \approx 102.564$$

**Step (b): Nominal (Turns) Ratio ($K_n$)**
$$K_n = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{1000}{200} = 5$$

Wait — there is a discrepancy. The secondary turns (1000) being **more** than primary turns (200) and yet the primary current being much larger than secondary means the CT is a **step-up** in current (which is incorrect for a step-down CT). Let me re-read — yes, the **primary turns** in a bar-primary CT is just 1 turn, and the secondary has 1000 turns. So in this problem, let me assume $N_1 = 1$ (bar type) and $N_2 = 200$, so that the current steps down by 200:1.

**Re-stated problem:** A bar-primary CT ($N_1 = 1$, $N_2 = 200$). Primary $I_1 = 400$ A, Secondary $I_2 = 1.95$ A. Excitation MMF = 12 AT.

### Solution (Corrected):

**Step (a): Actual Transformation Ratio**
$$K_a = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{400}{1.95} \approx 205.128$$

**Step (b): Nominal (Turns) Ratio**
$$K_n = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{200}{1} = 200$$

**Step (c): Ratio Error**
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{K_n - K_a}{K_a} \times 100\,\% = \dfrac{200 - 205.128}{205.128} \times 100\,\%$$
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{-5.128}{205.128} \times 100\,\% \approx -2.50\,\%$$

The negative sign indicates a **negative ratio error** (typical of CTs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** Numerical problems of this type (5-mark to 7-mark) are commonly asked in the **university lab written exam**. Always carry 4 decimal places in intermediate steps.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Polarity Test Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is a high-yield KTU viva question. The examiner will likely ask: *"Why choose one method over another?"*

| Test Method | Equipment Required | Sensitivity | Speed | Accuracy | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Deflection Method** | 1.5 V dry cell + DC ammeter (0–1 mA) | Medium | Fast | ±1 winding sense | Field installation check |
| **AC Deflection Method (Volt-ammeter)** | Low-voltage AC source + voltmeter + ammeter | Low | Very fast | ±5% | Quick field test |
| **Neon-Lamp / Lamp Polarity** | Neon lamp across secondary | Visual (no instrument) | Fast | ±2% sense | On-site visual check |
| **Standard CT Comparison (Silsbee)** | Standard CT + null detector | High | Slow | ±0.1% | Laboratory / NABL |
| **Mutual Inductometer (Arnold)** | Variable MI + null detector | Very high | Slow | ±0.01% | National metrology |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Silsbee vs. Arnold Methods

| Feature | Silsbee's Method | Arnold's Method |
|---|---|---|
| Reference standard | Calibrated standard CT/PT of same ratio | Mutual inductometer + variable resistor |
| Detection principle | Deflectional (magnitude + phase read from instrument) | Null (compensated to zero) |
| Reading interpretation | Requires calculation from two deflections | Direct dial readings |
| Accuracy | ±0.1% to ±0.5% | ±0.01% to ±0.05% |
| KTU exam weight | High (most commonly asked) | Moderate (theory + circuit) |
| Burden handling | Yes (tested at multiple burdens) | Yes |
| Best for | Routine lab testing | Precision / standardisation labs |

## 3.6 Exhaustive KTU Walk-Through — Knee-Point Voltage Test

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **very common KTU 14-mark question**. Here is the entire experimental procedure in stepwise form.

**Setup:** The CT primary is left **open-circuited**. A variable low-voltage AC source (typically 0–250 V) is applied to the secondary through an **ammeter** in series. A **voltmeter** is connected across the secondary.

**Procedure (Exhaustive):**

1. Keep the autotransformer/variac at its **zero** position. Switch ON the supply.
2. Gradually increase the secondary voltage in small steps.
3. At each step, record the voltmeter reading ($V_2$) and the ammeter reading (which is the **exciting current** $I_e$).
4. Plot $V_2$ on the **Y-axis** and $I_e$ on the **X-axis** on a graph sheet.
5. The curve is initially **linear** (low-current region) — this is the unsaturated region.
6. As voltage rises, the curve **bends sharply** — this is the **knee point**.
7. The **knee-point voltage** $V_{kn}$ is the voltage at which a 10% increase in $V_2$ causes a 50% increase in $I_e$.
8. Switch OFF the supply, bring the variac to zero, and discharge any stored energy.

**Significance for Protection CTs:**
- For differential protection, the CT must not saturate up to at least $20 \times I_{1n}$.
- The **higher the $V_{kn}$**, the **larger the CT's ability** to transform high fault currents linearly.
- A typical **5P20 CT** has $V_{kn} \geq 2 \times I_{2n} \times (R_{ct} + R_{burden})$ where $R_{ct}$ is the CT secondary winding resistance.

**Calculations:**

Let the rated secondary burden be $R_b = 0.5\,\Omega$ and the secondary winding resistance be $R_{ct} = 0.3\,\Omega$.
For 5P20 class: $\text{Required } V_{kn} = 2 \times 5 \times (0.3 + 0.5) = 2 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 8\,\text{V}$

If the test reveals $V_{kn} = 9.5\,\text{V}$, the CT is **acceptable** (margin > required minimum).

## 4.2 Mermaid Circuit Schematic — Polarity Test (DC Deflection Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[1.5V DC Battery] --> SW[SPST Switch]
    SW --> P1[CT Primary Terminal P1]
    P2[CT Primary Terminal P2] --> RET[Return to Battery Negative]
    S1[CT Secondary Terminal S1] --> AM[DC Milliammeter 0-1mA]
    AM --> S2[CT Secondary Terminal S2]

    OBS[Observation] -.-> AM2[If Ammeter Deflects Positive on Switch ON:<br>Polarity is Marked S1 S2 Correct]
    OBS -.-> AM3[If Ammeter Deflects Negative:<br>Polarity is Reversed Swap S1 S2]
```

**Operating Logic:**
- The battery momentarily drives a small DC current through the primary.
- By **Lenz's Law**, the induced secondary EMF drives a current whose direction depends on winding sense.
- If the dots (or terminal labels P1–S1, P2–S2) are correctly marked, the secondary current direction matches the polarity convention; otherwise it reverses.
- The **direction of the kick** on the moving-coil ammeter tells you whether the markings are correct.

## 4.3 Mermaid Circuit Schematic — Silsbee's Ratio & Phase Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PS[High Current Source Variac Step Down Transformer] --> BUS[Primary Bus Bar]
    BUS --> XCT[CT Under Test Primary]
    BUS --> SCT[Standard CT Primary]
    XCT --> XS1[CT Under Test Secondary S1]
    XS1 --> BD1[Burden of CT Under Test]
    BD1 --> XS2[CT Under Test Secondary S2]
    XS2 --> JD1[Null Detector Junction 1]
    SCT --> SS1[Standard CT Secondary S1]
    SS1 --> BD2[Burden of Standard CT]
    BD2 --> SS2[Standard CT Secondary S2]
    SS2 --> JD2[Null Detector Junction 2]
    JD1 --> ND[Null Detector DCC Galvanometer or AC Null Meter]
    JD2 --> ND
    ND --> VR[Variable Resistor for Phase Compensation]
    VR --> MI[Mutual Inductometer for Ratio Compensation]
```

**Operating Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Energize the primary with rated (or test) current via the variac + step-down transformer.
2. Both secondaries produce currents: $I_{2X}$ (test CT) and $I_{2S}$ (standard CT).
3. Connect the two secondaries in **opposition** (differential connection).
4. The null detector reads the **vector difference** $\Delta I = I_{2S} - I_{2X}$ (or vice versa).
5. If the test CT is **ideal**, the differential is zero.
6. Any residual **magnitude** of $\Delta I$ gives the **ratio error**.
7. Any residual **phase** of $\Delta I$ gives the **phase angle error**.
8. Compute the errors using the null detector's magnitude and phase scale readings.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] Define "ratio error" of a current transformer. How is it expressed?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **ratio error** of a CT is the deviation of the actual transformation ratio ($K_a$) from the rated (nominal) ratio ($K_n$), expressed as a percentage. It arises because part of the primary MMF is consumed in magnetizing the core, leaving an unbalance between primary and secondary MMFs.

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{K_n - K_a}{K_a} \times 100\,\%$$

where $K_a = I_1 / I_2$ and $K_n = N_2 / N_1$. The error is **negative** for a CT, meaning the secondary current is slightly higher than the ideal nameplate value.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] What is "knee-point voltage" of a CT, and why is it important for protection-class CTs?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **knee-point voltage** ($V_{kn}$) of a CT is the sinusoidal rms voltage applied to the secondary winding (with the primary open-circuited) at which a **10% increase in voltage** causes a **50% increase in the exciting (magnetizing) current**.
