It is identified from the sharp bend in the secondary $V$–$I$ characteristic.

For **protection-class CTs**, $V_{kn}$ determines the maximum fault current that the CT can transform without core saturation. A higher $V_{kn}$ ensures faithful reproduction of the high primary fault current (e.g., 20 × rated) on the secondary side, so that protection relays operate correctly during short circuits. Hence $V_{kn}$ is the **primary design parameter** for classes like 5P, 10P, and PS.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, describe the **Silsbee's method** of testing a Current Transformer for ratio and phase angle errors. Explain the role of the **standard CT** and the **null detector** in the test.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A 1000/5 A current transformer has a bar primary ($N_1 = 1$) and 200 secondary turns. During a Silsbee test, the differential current magnitude is $0.08$ A and the phase angle on the null detector reads **24 minutes**. Calculate (i) the actual transformation ratio, (ii) the ratio error in %, and (iii) the phase error in minutes and in centi-radians.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Silsbee's Method — Step-by-Step Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Principle:** The CT under test is compared with a **standard CT of the same nominal ratio** but of higher accuracy (typically 0.1 class). Any residual differential current is attributed to the test CT.

2. **Circuit Connection (Sketch as per Section 4.3):**
   - The primaries of both CTs are connected in **series** to a low-voltage high-current source (variac + step-down transformer + ammeter).
   - The secondaries are connected in **opposition** (differential connection) through a sensitive **null detector** (DCC galvanometer for DC, vibration galvanometer or AC null meter for AC).
   - Both CTs carry the **same primary current** (they are in series).

3. **Procedure:**
   - Energize the primary and increase current in steps up to **120%** of rated.
   - At each step, observe the **null detector reading** (magnitude and phase).
   - From the magnitude, compute the **ratio error**.
   - From the phase, compute the **phase angle error**.

4. **Role of Standard CT:** Since it is calibrated and of a higher accuracy class, its errors are **negligible** compared to the test CT. Any differential is therefore a faithful measure of the test CT's errors.

5. **Role of Null Detector:** A high-sensitivity, low-burden detector ensures that the differential is measured without disturbing the secondary circuits. It is connected between the two S1 terminals (differential point).

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Given:**
- Rated ratio: $K_n = 1000/5 = 200$
- Bar primary: $N_1 = 1$, $N_2 = 200$
- Differential current magnitude: $\Delta I = 0.08$ A
- Phase reading: **24 minutes**

**Step 1: Actual Transformation Ratio**
The differential current $\Delta I$ represents the magnitude error. If the secondary current is $I_2 = 5$ A (rated), the actual measured $I_2$ is $5 \pm 0.08 = 4.92$ or $5.08$ A depending on sign convention.

Let us take $I_2 = 5.08$ A (i.e., the test CT over-supplies):
$$I_1 = K_n \cdot I_2 = 200 \times 5.08 = 1016 \text{ A (this is the primary current maintained)}$$

But for the actual ratio, use the **measured** $I_1 = 1000$ A and the **measured** $I_2 = 5 - 0.08 = 4.92$ A (if $\Delta I$ indicates under-supply):
$$K_a = \dfrac{1000}{4.92} \approx 203.25$$

**Step 2: Ratio Error**
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{K_n - K_a}{K_a} \times 100\,\% = \dfrac{200 - 203.25}{203.25} \times 100\,\%$$
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{-3.25}{203.25} \times 100\,\% \approx -1.60\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used in KTU Exams:** The negative sign means the CT is *over-rating* on the secondary side. Always report the sign.

**Step 3: Phase Error**
- Phase error (already given as null detector reading): **24 minutes**.
- Converting to centi-radians:
$$\delta = \dfrac{24}{34.38} \approx 0.698 \text{ crad}$$

where 1 crad = 34.38 minutes (since $\pi/2$ crad = 90° = 5400' → 1 crad = 5400/$\pi$ ≈ 34.38').

**Step 4: Final Answer (3 sub-parts in 7 marks)**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Actual transformation ratio $K_a$ | ≈ 203.25 |
| Ratio error $\varepsilon$ | ≈ **−1.60 %** |
| Phase error $\delta$ | **24 minutes** ≈ 0.698 crad |

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat phasor diagram, derive the **ratio error** and **phase error** for a current transformer. State the **MMF balance equation** and explain why errors arise.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **knee-point voltage test** for a protection-class CT. Sketch the expected $V$–$I$ curve. A 5P20 rated CT has secondary winding resistance $R_{ct} = 0.2\,\Omega$ and is connected to a burden of $0.6\,\Omega$. Find the **minimum required knee-point voltage** to meet the 5P20 class specification. If the test reveals $V_{kn} = 12$ V, is the CT acceptable?

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Phasor Diagram Approach (7 marks):**

1. **Draw the equivalent circuit** of the CT referred to the secondary side:
   - Magnetizing branch ($L_m \parallel R_c$) in parallel with the **ideal** transformer of ratio $1 : N_2/N_1$.
   - Secondary impedance $Z_2 = R_2 + jX_2$ and burden $Z_b$ in series.

2. **Define phasors:**
   - Let secondary current $I_2$ be the **reference phasor** (horizontal).
   - The secondary induced EMF $E_2$ leads $I_2$ by angle $\theta_2$ where $\cos\theta_2 = (R_2 + R_b)/Z_b$.
   - Flux $\phi$ **lags** $E_2$ by 90°.
   - The no-load current $I_0$ has two components:
     - Magnetizing component $I_m$ (along $\phi$),
     - Core-loss component $I_w$ (in phase with $E_2$).
   - Hence $I_0$ makes an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal where $\tan \alpha = I_m / I_w$.

3. **MMF balance** (per Section 3.1 derivation):
$$N_1 I_1 = N_2 I_2 + N_1 I_0$$

4. **Derive ratio error:**
The phasor difference $I_1 - (N_2/N_1) I_2 = I_0$. Drawing this in the phasor diagram, the projection of $I_0$ along the $I_2$ direction gives the **magnitude error**:
$$\varepsilon \approx -\dfrac{I_0 \sin \alpha}{I_1} \times 100\,\%$$

5. **Derive phase error:**
The projection of $I_0$ **perpendicular** to the $I_2$ direction gives the phase error:
$$\delta \approx \dfrac{I_0 \cos \alpha}{I_1} \text{ (radians)} = \dfrac{I_0 \cos \alpha}{I_1} \times 3437.75 \text{ (minutes)}$$

6. **Conclusion:** Both errors are directly proportional to the **excitation current $I_0$** and inversely proportional to the **primary current $I_1$**. To reduce errors, designers use (i) high-permeability core material (e.g., **CRGO steel**, **Permalloy**, **Nanocrystalline**), (ii) large cross-section, and (iii) keep $I_0$ minimal.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Knee-Point Voltage Test — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

1. **Procedure Recap** (per Section 3.6).
2. **Expected $V$–$I$ Curve** (refer Section 4.4 block diagram and the textual description below):

   - **Region 1 (Linear):** Low voltage, low exciting current; the core is unsaturated.
   - **Region 2 (Knee):** A sharp bend occurs; the core enters saturation.
   - **Region 3 (Saturation):** Current rises steeply for small voltage increments.

3. **Numerical Calculation:**

   **Given:**
   - CT class: **5P20** → ratio error ≤ 5% at **20 × rated secondary current**
   - $R_{ct} = 0.2\,\Omega$ (CT secondary winding resistance)
   - Burden $R_b = 0.6\,\Omega$
   - Rated secondary current: $I_{2n} = 5$ A

   **Step 1: Compute total secondary loop impedance**
$$R_{total} = R_{ct} + R_b = 0.2 + 0.6 = 0.8\,\Omega$$

**Step 2: Compute the secondary voltage at 20 × rated current**
The secondary voltage across the CT (internal EMF minus drop) at the worst-case (20 × rated) is:
$$V_{2,peak} = 20 \times I_{2n} \times R_{total} = 20 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 80 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Compute minimum required $V_{kn}$ for 5P20 class**
The empirical rule for protection CTs (per IEC 61869-2):
$$V_{kn,min} \geq 2 \times \left( V_{2,peak} \right) / \text{(number of turns correction factor)}$$

For a standard rule-of-thumb (most textbooks use):
$$V_{kn,min} \geq 2 \times I_{2n} \times R_{total} \times \text{ALF}$$
$$V_{kn,min} = 2 \times 5 \times 0.8 \times 20 = 160 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Formula Correction:** The widely-used textbook (Sawhney, *Electrical & Electronic Measuring Instruments*) formula is:
> $$V_{kn,min} = \text{ALF} \times I_{2n} \times (R_{ct} + R_b)$$
> Hence:
> $$V_{kn,min} = 20 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 80 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Compare with tested value**
Tested $V_{kn} = 12$ V.

**Conclusion:** The tested $V_{kn}$ (**12 V**) is **far less** than the minimum required (**80 V**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Verdict:** The CT is **REJECTED** for 5P20 class operation. The core saturates too early; during a 20 × fault current, the secondary waveform will be severely distorted, leading to **failure of protection relays** (e.g., the differential relay will not operate, or operate with a time delay, potentially causing catastrophic equipment damage).

**Step 5: Recommendation**
- Use a CT with **larger core cross-section**.
- Use **high-permeability material** (e.g., Permalloy or Nanocrystalline).
- Reduce the connected burden $R_b$ (use shorter leads, smaller-gauge burden resistor).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** in ratio error (CT: usually negative; PT: usually positive).
> 2. **Confusing turns ratio direction** — CT: $K_n = N_2 / N_1$; PT: $K_n = N_1 / N_2$.
> 3. **Using wrong unit for phase error** — most KTU papers expect **minutes**; mixing with radians/degrees without conversion loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Missing the open-primary condition** in knee-point test description.
> 5. **In Silsbee's method**, students often write that the standard CT's secondary is connected directly in parallel (not in opposition/differential) — this is a **critical conceptual error** worth 2 marks.
> 6. **In 5P20 numericals**, students forget the factor of 2 safety margin and directly equate $V_{kn} = I_{2n} \times R_{total} \times \text{ALF}$, missing the empirical doubling for safety.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and revise the night before your KTU Lab Exam.**

- ☐ **CT** steps down current; **PT** steps down voltage. Both provide **galvanic isolation**.
- ☐ Standard secondary: **5 A** (CT) and **110 V** (PT).
- ☐ CT operates near **short-circuit**; PT operates near **open-circuit** secondary.
- ☐ **Turns ratio direction:** CT: $K_n = N_2/N_1$; PT: $K_n = N_1/N_2$.
- ☐ **Ratio error** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{K_n - K_a}{K_a} \times 100\,\%$. **Negative for CT**, **positive for PT** (typical).
- ☐ **Phase error** in **minutes** (1 rad = 3437.75 minutes; 1 crad = 34.38 minutes).
- ☐ **Both errors are caused by the excitation current $I_0$** drawn by the core.
- ☐ **Polarity test:** Use 1.5 V battery + DC mA meter; check the **direction of kick** on switch ON.
- ☐ **Silsbee's method:** Differential connection of standard CT + null detector.
- ☐ **Arnold's method:** Use **mutual inductometer** (for phase) and **variable resistor** (for ratio) to null the detector.
- ☐ **Knee-point voltage $V_{kn}$:** Test with **primary open**, apply V to secondary, plot V–I curve.
- ☐ **5P20:** ≤ 5% ratio error at **20 × rated** primary current.
- ☐ For **differential protection**, prefer **Class PS or PX** CTs (defined $V_{kn}$ and turns).
- ☐ **Standards to remember:** IS 2705, IS 3156, IEC 61869-2 (CT), IEC 61869-3 (PT), IEEE C57.13.
- ☐ **Most common KTU viva questions:** "Why is a CT never operated with secondary open-circuited?" → *Because the entire primary MMF becomes magnetizing, causing dangerously high flux, core saturation, severe overheating, and insulation failure.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Lab Exam:** Always present your **circuit diagram first**, then **state the principle**, then **write the procedure**, then **tabulate the readings**, then **compute the result with units**, then **comment on accuracy class**. This sequence is the **highest-scoring template** validated across multiple KTU university question papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of Transformer Oil
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Testing of Transformer Oil

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Transformer Oil** is a highly refined mineral oil obtained by fractional distillation and subsequent treatment of crude petroleum. It serves a **dual purpose** in oil-immersed transformers:
> 1. **Electrical Insulation** — provides dielectric strength between live windings and the grounded tank.
> 2. **Heat Transfer (Cooling)** — circulates through windings to absorb and dissipate heat generated by $I^2R$ losses and stray/hysteresis losses.

**Dielectric Testing of Transformer Oil** refers to the standardized set of electrical, chemical, and physical tests performed on insulating oil to verify its quality, purity, and continued suitability as a dielectric medium and coolant in HV power apparatus. These tests are mandated by **IS 335 (Bureau of Indian Standards)**, **IEC 60156**, and **ASTM D877/D1816** standards.

### Key Functional Roles of Transformer Oil
| Function | Mechanism | Engineering Significance |
|----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **Dielectric Insulation** | Oil molecules resist ionization under high electric field | Prevents internal flashovers |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Convective circulation transfers heat to radiators | Keeps windings below thermal limit (typically **95 °C** for ONAN) |
| **Arc Quenching** | Decomposition products (H₂, C₂H₂) absorb arc energy | Protects OLTC and tap-changer contacts |
| **Moisture Barrier** | Hydrophobic film on cellulose (kraft paper) | Preserves winding insulation life |
| **Information Carrier** | Dissolved Gas Analysis (DGA) reflects fault gases | Enables **condition monitoring** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab experiment primarily focuses on the **Breakdown Voltage (BDV) Test** of transformer oil using a standard test cell, along with brief exposure to **tan-delta**, **moisture content**, and **flash-point** measurements.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Shield" of the Transformer

Think of transformer oil as the **life-blood of a transformer**, much like blood plasma in a human body:

- 🩸 **Blood carries oxygen and nutrients + fights infection** → Transformer oil **carries heat away + fights electrical breakdown (insulation)**.
- 🛡️ **Skin is the first defense against germs** → Oil is the **first defense against internal short-circuits and arcing**.
- 🧪 **A blood test reveals diseases** → An **oil test reveals incipient faults** (moisture, sludge, acidity, dissolved gases).
- ⚠️ **Contaminated blood makes the body sick** → **Contaminated oil causes transformer failure** — one of the costliest equipment breakdowns in a power system.

If the oil's "health" deteriorates (moisture, particles, ageing by-products), the **dielectric strength drops dramatically**, just as contaminated blood loses its oxygen-carrying capacity — leading to catastrophic **winding short-circuits or tank explosions**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Quantitative Benchmarks (KTU Board-Favorite Numbers):**
> - **Standard Breakdown Voltage (BDV) of new transformer oil:** ≥ **30 kV (minimum)** as per **IS 335**, ideally **50–70 kV** for new oil.
> - **Dielectric strength of dry, clean oil:** ≈ **120–200 kV/cm** (intrinsic).
> - **Relative permittivity ($\varepsilon_r$) of oil:** **2.1 – 2.5** (at 20 °C, 50 Hz).
> - **Density of transformer oil:** ≈ **0.85 – 0.92 g/cm³** at 15 °C.
> - **Flash Point (minimum):** **140 °C** (IS 335) — fire safety threshold.
> - **Pour Point (maximum):** **-30 °C** (low-temperature fluidity).
> - **Viscosity at 27 °C:** ≤ **27 cSt (kinematic)** for proper circulation.
> - **Moisture content (new oil):** **< 25 ppm**; in-service: **< 35 ppm**.
> - **Tan $\delta$ at 90 °C:** **< 0.005 (0.5%)** for service-aged oil.
> - **Acidity (Neutralization Number):** **< 0.2 mg KOH/g** for new oil.
> - **Dielectric dissipation factor:** Measured as **$\tan \delta$**.

---

## 1.4 Why Testing is Non-Negotiable

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A direct question — *"Why is periodic testing of transformer oil necessary?"* — carries **2–3 marks** in Part A. The answer must cite **three pillars**: safety, reliability, and economic life-extension of the transformer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Transformer Oil Tests

Transformer oil is evaluated under **three umbrella categories**, each targeting a specific degradation pathway:

### (A) Electrical Tests
| Test | Parameter Measured | Standard | KTU Weightage |
|------|-------------------|----------|---------------|
| **Breakdown Voltage (BDV)** | Dielectric strength in kV | IS 6792 / IEC 60156 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Highest) |
| **Dielectric Dissipation Factor (tan $\delta$)** | Loss angle under AC | IS 6262 / IEC 60247 | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Specific Resistance (Resistivity)** | Volume/surface resistivity | IS 6103 | ⭐⭐ |

### (B) Chemical Tests
| Test | Parameter | Standard |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Acidity (Neutralization Value)** | mg KOH/g | IS 1448 |
| **Water Content (Karl Fischer)** | ppm | IS 13567 / IEC 60814 |
| **Interfacial Tension (IFT)** | mN/m | IS 6104 / ASTM D971 |
| **Oxidation Stability** | Induction period (h) | IS 12422 |

### (C) Physical Tests
| Test | Parameter | Standard |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Flash Point** | °C (Pensky-Martens closed cup) | IS 1448 |
| **Pour Point** | °C | IS 1448 |
| **Viscosity (kinematic)** | cSt at 27 °C | IS 1448 |
| **Density** | g/cm³ at 15 °C | IS 1448 |
| **Color & Appearance** | Visual / ASTM color number | IS 1448 |

---

## 2.2 The Breakdown Voltage (BDV) Test — The Flagship Experiment

### 2.2.1 Theoretical Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Paschen's Law** governs the breakdown of oil — the breakdown voltage $V_b$ depends on the product of gas pressure (or, in oil, the effective density) and electrode gap:
> $$V_b = f(p \cdot d)$$
> where $p$ = effective pressure (or vapor pressure of oil at local temperature), $d$ = gap between electrodes.

**However**, in transformer oil, breakdown is **NOT** purely Paschen-type; it is governed by **three competing mechanisms**:

1. **Electronic (Townsend) mechanism** — field-emitted electrons cause ionization avalanches.
2. **Suspended particle / "bubble" mechanism** — moisture and fibers lower local strength.
3. **Electro-convection / streamer mechanism** — fast-rising streamers bridge the gap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why oil breaks down:**
> - **Water in oil** drastically reduces BDV: just **50 ppm** of water can halve the breakdown voltage.
> - **Particle contamination** (fibres, dust, cellulose debris) creates local field enhancement.
> - **Gas bubbles** (from thermal decomposition) initiate partial discharge (PD).

### 2.2.2 The Test Cell (Electrode Geometry)

The **standard IEC 60156 / IS 6792 test cell** uses:

- **Electrodes**: Spherical (dome-shaped) brass or stainless-steel, each of **12.5 mm – 13 mm radius**.
- **Gap between electrodes:** **2.5 mm ± 0.05 mm** (fixed by a precise spacer).
- **Volume of oil sample:** **300 – 500 mL** (covering both electrodes).
- **Test transformer:** Usually **0–60 kV** or **0–100 kV**, 50 Hz, with **short-circuit current ≥ 20 mA** at the test voltage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "**2 mm**" or "**5 mm**" for electrode gap. The KTU-correct value is **2.5 mm**. Marks are lost for this.

### 2.2.3 Test Procedure (Step-by-Step, KTU Lab Manual Standard)

1. **Clean the test cell** thoroughly with dry, lint-free tissue; rinse with the test oil sample.
2. **Fill the cell** with the oil sample, ensuring no air bubbles cling to the electrodes.
3. **Allow the oil to settle** for **5–10 minutes** to release entrapped air.
4. **Apply voltage** at a uniform rate of rise: **2 kV/s ± 0.2 kV/s** (as per IEC 60156).
5. **Record the voltage** at which a **sustained breakdown** occurs (visible arc, audible "pop", circuit-breaker trips, or current exceeds cutoff).
6. **Stir the oil gently** with a glass rod (without introducing air bubbles) and **wait 2 minutes** before the next test.
7. **Repeat the test 6 times** on the same sample.
8. **Discard the first reading** (it conditions the oil).
9. **Average the remaining 5 readings** to obtain the **Breakdown Voltage**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Trap:** Examiner may ask — *"Why is the first reading discarded?"*
> **Answer:** The first breakdown introduces gas bubbles, carbon particles, and ionic residue which artificially lower subsequent readings. The 1st reading conditions the oil, hence is not representative.

### 2.2.4 Choice of Voltage-Rise Rate (Why 2 kV/s?)

If the rate of rise is too **slow** → oil self-heats, vaporizes, and breakdown may occur at lower voltage (over-estimation of weakness).
If too **fast** → electronic breakdown mechanism is not given time to develop → **erratically high or non-representative values**.

Hence, **2 kV/s** is the IEC-recommended compromise — slow enough to mimic real-world stress ramp, fast enough to avoid thermal pre-conditioning.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam gold. Memorize and understand each.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{bd,avg} = \dfrac{1}{n}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_{bd,i}$ | Average BDV from $n$ shots | kV |
| 2 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (V_i - V_{avg})^2}{n-1}}$ | Standard deviation of BDV | kV |
| 3 | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{V_{avg}} \times 100\%$ | **Coefficient of Variation** — measures dispersion | % |
| 4 | $E_{bd} = \dfrac{V_{bd}}{d}$ | Dielectric strength (electric field at breakdown) | kV/cm |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{C_{oil}}{C_0}$ | Relative permittivity from capacitance ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{I_R}{I_C} = \dfrac{1}{\omega R_p C_p}$ | Dissipation factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{loss} = V^2 \cdot \omega C \cdot \tan \delta$ | Dielectric loss in oil | W |
| 8 | $\rho = \dfrac{R \cdot A}{l}$ | Resistivity from measured resistance | $\Omega \cdot m$ |
| 9 | $IFT = F \cdot \dfrac{L}{4\pi R}$ (du Nouy ring) | Interfacial tension | mN/m |
| 10 | $Water\ ppm = \dfrac{V_{KF} \cdot C_{KF} \cdot 18.02}{m_{sample}}$ | Karl Fischer moisture content | ppm |

**Boundary Conditions (per IS 335 / IEC 60156):**
- New dry oil: $V_{bd} \geq 30\ kV$ (minimum acceptable), preferred ≥ **50 kV**.
- In-service oil: $V_{bd} \geq 30\ kV$ (good), **20–30 kV** (needs filtration), **< 20 kV** (replace/reclaim).
- Electrode gap: $d = 2.5\ mm \pm 0.05\ mm$.
- Rate of voltage rise: $2\ kV/s \pm 0.2\ kV/s$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | How Oil Testing Helps |
|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Power Utilities (KSEB, NTPC, PGCIL)** | Periodic BDV testing prevents catastrophic transformer failures costing ₹ crores |
| **EHV/UHV Substations (400 kV, 765 kV)** | Tan-delta and partial discharge testing on oil insulates inter-winding capacitances |
| **Industrial Plants (Refineries, Steel, Cement)** | Onsite oil testing extends transformer life and avoids unplanned shutdowns |
| **OEMs (ABB, Siemens, GE, Bharat Bijlee)** | Factory acceptance tests (FAT) of oil determine shipping approval |
| **Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)** | DGA + BDV trend monitoring enables **RUL (Remaining Useful Life)** prediction |
| **Recycling Industry** | Re-refining of used oil judged by IFT, acidity, and color indices |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern smart transformers integrate **online oil sensors** (e.g., Vaisala Optimus OPT100 for moisture) and **Hydran M2** for dissolved H₂, giving continuous BDV-equivalent diagnostics — a direct evolution of the offline lab test you are performing today.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem #1 — Standard BDV Calculation

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> The following breakdown voltages were recorded for a transformer oil sample using a standard BDV test kit (2.5 mm gap, 2 kV/s rise rate, 5 valid shots after discarding the 1st):
> **42, 38, 45, 40, 41 kV**.
> Determine:
> (i) Average BDV.
> (ii) Standard deviation $\sigma$.
> (iii) Coefficient of variation $CV$.
> (iv) Dielectric strength in kV/cm.
> (v) Whether the oil is acceptable as per **IS 335**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### (i) Average Breakdown Voltage
$$
V_{avg} = \frac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + V_4 + V_5}{5}
$$
$$
V_{avg} = \frac{42 + 38 + 45 + 40 + 41}{5} = \frac{206}{5} = 41.2\ \text{kV}
$$

#### (ii) Standard Deviation
Compute deviations $d_i = V_i - V_{avg}$:
- $d_1 = 42 - 41.2 = +0.8\ kV$
- $d_2 = 38 - 41.2 = -3.2\ kV$
- $d_3 = 45 - 41.2 = +3.8\ kV$
- $d_4 = 40 - 41.2 = -1.2\ kV$
- $d_5 = 41 - 41.2 = -0.2\ kV$

Square the deviations:
- $d_1^2 = 0.64$
- $d_2^2 = 10.24$
- $d_3^2 = 14.44$
- $d_4^2 = 1.44$
- $d_5^2 = 0.04$

Sum: $\sum d_i^2 = 0.64 + 10.24 + 14.44 + 1.44 + 0.04 = 26.80\ \text{kV}^2$.

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum d_i^2}{n - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{26.80}{4}} = \sqrt{6.70} = 2.588\ \text{kV}
$$

#### (iii) Coefficient of Variation
$$
CV = \frac{\sigma}{V_{avg}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.588}{41.2} \times 100\% = 6.28\%
$$

#### (iv) Dielectric Strength
Convert gap $d = 2.5\ mm = 0.25\ cm$:
$$
E_{bd} = \frac{V_{avg}}{d} = \frac{41.2\ \text{kV}}{0.25\ \text{cm}} = 164.8\ \text{kV/cm}
$$

#### (v) Acceptability Check
As per **IS 335**, the minimum acceptable BDV for in-service transformer oil is **30 kV**.

$$
V_{avg} = 41.2\ \text{kV} \;\geq\; 30\ \text{kV} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Oil is ACCEPTABLE for service.}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus — Dispersity Check:** A **CV < 10%** is considered excellent consistency. Here $CV = 6.28\%$ → oil is **homogeneous and well-conditioned**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem #2 — Tan-Delta Loss Derivation

> **Problem Statement:**
> An oil-filled test capacitor has a plate area $A = 100\ cm^2$ and plate separation $d = 1\ mm$. At 50 Hz, the capacitance with oil is $C_{oil} = 88.5\ pF$, and the equivalent parallel resistance is $R_p = 2 \times 10^9\ \Omega$. Calculate:
> (i) $\tan \delta$,
> (ii) Dielectric loss $P$ at 11 kV (rms), 50 Hz,
> (iii) Relative permittivity of oil.

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### (i) Dissipation Factor
$$
\tan \delta = \frac{1}{\omega R_p C_{oil}}
$$

Compute $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159\ \text{rad/s}$.

$$
\omega R_p C_{oil} = 314.159 \times 2 \times 10^9 \times 88.5 \times 10^{-12}
$$
$$
= 314.159 \times 2 \times 88.5 \times 10^{-3}
$$
$$
= 314.159 \times 177 \times 10^{-3}
$$
$$
= 55{,}606.14 \times 10^{-3} = 55.606
$$

$$
\tan \delta = \frac{1}{55.606} = 0.01798 \approx 0.018
$$

#### (ii) Dielectric Loss
$$
P = V^2 \cdot \omega C \cdot \tan \delta
$$

$V = 11{,}000\ V$, $V^2 = 1.21 \times 10^8\ V^2$.

$$
P = 1.21 \times 10^8 \times 314.159 \times 88.5 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.018
$$

Compute step by step:
- $1.21 \times 10^8 \times 88.5 \times 10^{-12} = 1.21 \times 88.5 \times 10^{-4} = 107.085 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0107085$
- $\times 314.159 = 0.0107085 \times 314.159 = 3.3637$
- $\times 0.018 = 0.06055\ \text{W}$

$$
P \approx 60.55\ \text{mW}
$$

#### (iii) Relative Permittivity
Vacuum capacitance $C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$, with $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$, $A = 100 \times 10^{-4}\ m^2 = 0.01\ m^2$, $d = 1 \times 10^{-3}\ m$:
$$
C_0 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.01}{10^{-3}} = 8.854 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F} = 88.54\ \text{pF}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_r = \frac{C_{oil}}{C_0} = \frac{88.5}{88.54} \approx 2.0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.0$ is **typical for mineral transformer oil** (range 2.1–2.5). Tan-$\delta$ of 0.018 is **borderline acceptable** (limit is 0.005 at 90 °C for service-aged oil, but at room temperature it can be higher). The 60.55 mW loss is what the test cell experiences — heat that must be dissipated by the cooling system.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pure vs. Aged Oil

| Parameter | New (Pure) Oil | Service-Aged (Good) | Service-Aged (Failed) |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|-----------------------|
| **BDV (kV)** | ≥ 50 | 30 – 50 | < 20 |
| **Moisture (ppm)** | < 25 | 25 – 35 | > 50 |
| **Acidity (mg KOH/g)** | < 0.03 | 0.03 – 0.2 | > 0.5 |
| **IFT (mN/m)** | > 40 | 25 – 40 | < 18 |
| **Tan $\delta$ at 90 °C** | < 0.001 | 0.001 – 0.005 | > 0.05 |
| **Color** | Pale yellow | Amber | Dark brown/black |
| **Sludge content** | Nil | Trace | Visible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Linkage:** If BDV falls below 30 kV, the **recommended action is filtration** (using a **filter press** with absorbent Fuller’s earth). Below 20 kV, **reclamation or replacement** is mandatory.

---

## 3.4 Theoretical Linkage — Paschen's Curve for Oil

While Paschen's Law strictly applies to gases, oil's breakdown follows a **modified Paschen-like behavior** because under high field, the oil locally vaporizes to form gas bubbles — and gas breakdown follows Paschen:

$$
V_{bd} = \frac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln\left[\ln\left(1 + \frac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)\right]}
$$

where $A, B$ are Townsend coefficients, $p$ is bubble pressure, $d$ is gap, $\gamma_{se}$ is secondary emission coefficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Exam:** You are **not** expected to derive the full Paschen expression. Just **state** that oil breakdown exhibits a Paschen-type minimum at a critical $p \cdot d$ value, and the standard test fixes $d = 2.5\ mm$ to operate away from this minimum.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BDV Test Setup — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL[Control and Measurement Section]
        A1[Auto Variac 0 to 270 V]
        A2[Step Up Transformer 0 to 60 kV or 100 kV]
        A3[Voltage Divider and kV Meter]
        A4[Overcurrent Relay and Trip CB]
        A5[Timer and Ramp Controller 2 kV per s]
    end

    subgraph HV[High Voltage Test Cell Section]
        B1[Test Cup with Oil Sample 300 to 500 mL]
        B2[Top Spherical Electrode 12.5 mm radius]
        B3[Bottom Spherical Electrode 12.5 mm radius]
        B4[Gap Spacer 2.5 mm fixed]
    end

    subgraph SAFE[Safety and Grounding Section]
        C1[Interlocked Safety Cage]
        C2[Earthing Rod]
        C3[Emergency Stop Pushbutton]
        C4[Warning Lamp and Buzzer]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B2
    A3 -. measures .-> A2
    A5 --> A1
    A4 -. trips on breakdown .-> A1
    B2 --> B4
    B4 --> B3
    B3 --> C2
    B1 -. contains .-> B2
    B1 -. contains .-> B3
    C1 -. guards .-> HV
    C4 -. alarm .-> A4
    C3 -. cuts supply .-> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **auto-variac** (low-voltage side) feeds the **step-up transformer** (HV side). The HV output is applied across the **spherical electrodes** immersed in the oil-filled test cup. The **kV meter** reads the voltage through a calibrated divider. The **ramp controller** ensures 2 kV/s rise. On breakdown, the **over-current relay** trips the supply within ~10–20 ms to protect the cell.

---

## 4.2 Testing Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1([Start]) --> S2[Collect Oil Sample in Clean Dry Bottle]
    S2 --> S3[Clean Test Cell with Dry Lint Free Tissue]
    S3 --> S4[Rinse Cell with Test Oil]
    S4 --> S5[Fill Cell with 300 to 500 mL Oil]
    S5 --> S6[Let Oil Stand 5 to 10 min to Release Air Bubbles]
    S6 --> S7[Close Safety Cage and Start HV]
    S7 --> S8[Raise Voltage at 2 kV per s]
    S8 --> S9{Breakdown Occurs?}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Record BDV Voltage VkV]
    S9 -- No --> S8
    S10 --> S11[Stir Oil and Wait 2 min]
    S11 --> S12{Repeat 6 times?}
    S12 -- No --> S8
    S12 -- Yes --> S13[Discard 1st Reading]
    S13 --> S14[Average Remaining 5 Readings]
    S14 --> S15[Compute Sigma and CV]
    S15 --> S16{Acceptable per IS 335?}
    S16 -- Yes --> S17([Oil Approved for Service])
    S16 -- No --> S18[Filter and Re-test or Replace]
    S18 --> S19([End of Test])
    S17 --> S19
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Diagnosis from Oil Test Results

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oil Test Results] --> B{BDV greater than 30 kV?}
    B -- Yes --> C{IFT greater than 25 mN per m?}
    B -- No --> D[Filter and Re-test]

    C -- Yes --> E{Tan delta less than 0.005?}
    C -- No --> F[Investigate Polar Contaminants]

    E -- Yes --> G[Oil is Good Continue Service]
    E -- No --> H[Investigate Moisture and Ageing]

    D --> I{If BDV less than 20 kV?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Replace or Reclaim Oil]
    I -- No --> K[Filtration via Filter Press]

    style G fill:#90EE90
    style J fill:#FF7F7F
    style K fill:#FFD580
    style F fill:#FFD580
    style H fill:#FFD580
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Manual Note:** The decision tree above is the **expected schematic answer** if a 14-mark question asks, *"How do you decide whether transformer oil needs replacement?"*

---

## 4.4 Three-Layer Test Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    Root[Transformer Oil Testing]

    Root --> L1[Electrical Tests]
    Root --> L2[Chemical Tests]
    Root --> L3[Physical Tests]

    L1 --> L1a[BDV Test]
    L1 --> L1b[Tan Delta Test]
    L1 --> L1c[Resistivity Test]
    L1 --> L1d[Partial Discharge Test]

    L2 --> L2a[Acidity Neutralization Number]
    L2 --> L2b[Moisture Karl Fischer]
    L2 --> L2c[Interfacial Tension]
    L2 --> L2d[Oxidation Stability]

    L3 --> L3a[Flash Point]
    L3 --> L3b[Pour Point]
    L3 --> L3c[Viscosity]
    L3 --> L3d[Density and Color]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**What is the significance of testing transformer oil? List any four tests commonly performed on it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Transformer oil serves as both **insulation** and **cooling medium** in oil-immersed transformers. During service, it deteriorates due to heat, moisture, oxidation, and electrical stress. Testing is essential to:
1. Ensure **dielectric strength** is maintained (prevent winding short-circuits).
2. Detect **moisture ingress** that degrades insulation life.
3. Identify **ageing by-products** (acids, sludge) that reduce cooling efficiency.
4. Enable **condition-based maintenance**, avoiding catastrophic and costly failures.

**Four common tests:**
- **Breakdown Voltage (BDV) test** (electrical)
- **Acidity / Neutralization Number test** (chemical)
- **Flash Point test** (physical)
- **Interfacial Tension (IFT) test** (chemical)
- *(Any four of the above or moisture/tan-delta)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Why is the first BDV reading discarded, and why are spherical electrodes of 12.5 mm radius used with a 2.5 mm gap?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **first reading is discarded** because it conditions the oil — the breakdown arc produces **gas bubbles, carbon particles, and ionized residue** that artificially lower the subsequent breakdown voltages, making them non-representative of the true oil quality. The 1st reading is for **stabilization**, not evaluation.

**Spherical electrodes of 12.5 mm radius** with a **2.5 mm gap** are mandated by **IS 6792 / IEC 60156** because:
1. The large radius produces a **near-uniform electric field** between the electrodes (avoiding sharp-edge field enhancement that would cause premature breakdown).
2. The 2.5 mm gap is the standardized distance for which historical BDV benchmarks and acceptance criteria (e.g., 30 kV minimum) have been established, ensuring **reproducible and comparable** results across labs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — BDV Test: Theory, Procedure, and Computation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Breakdown Voltage (BDV) test setup** for transformer oil. State the relevant IS/IEC standard, electrode geometry, voltage rise rate, and acceptance criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a BDV test, the following 6 readings (in kV) were obtained: **28, 35, 38, 40, 42, 41**. Determine the average BDV, standard deviation, coefficient of variation, and dielectric strength. Comment on the acceptability of the oil as per **IS 335**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** above (the Mermaid functional architecture diagram).

**Explanation:**

- **Standard:** IS 6792 / IEC 60156.
- **Test Transformer:** 0–60 kV or 0–100 kV, 50 Hz, with secondary short-circuit current ≥ 20 mA.
- **Voltage rise rate:** **2 kV/s ± 0.2 kV/s** (controlled by auto-variac with ramp generator).
- **Electrode geometry:** Two **spherical (dome-shaped) brass electrodes**, each of **12.5 mm radius**, with a **fixed 2.5 mm gap** maintained by a calibrated spacer.
- **Test cell:** Transparent acrylic or glass cup holding **300–500 mL** of oil, fully covering the electrodes.
- **Measurement:** A **capacitive/resistive voltage divider** drives a digital kV meter; an **over-current trip** disconnects the supply within one cycle of breakdown to limit electrode damage.
- **Procedure:** Fill → settle 5–10 min → ramp voltage → record 6 shots (stir + 2 min wait between shots) → discard 1st → average next 5.
- **Acceptance Criterion (IS 335):**
  - New oil: $V_{bd} \geq 30\ kV$ (typically 50–70 kV).
  - In-service oil: $V_{bd} \geq 30\ kV$ is good; **20–30 kV** warrants filtration; **< 20 kV** demands replacement/reclamation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Hint:** 2 marks for the diagram, 2 marks for standards/specs, 2 marks for procedure, 1 mark for acceptance criterion.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Discard 1st reading** (conditioning shot, per KTU convention).
Valid readings: **35, 38, 40, 42, 41 kV**.

**Step 2 — Average BDV:**
$$
V_{avg} = \frac{35 + 38 + 40 + 42 + 41}{5} = \frac{196}{5} = 39.2\ \text{kV}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Deviation:**
Deviations:
- $d_1 = 35 - 39.2 = -4.2$
- $d_2 = 38 - 39.2 = -1.2$
- $d_3 = 40 - 39.2 = +0.8$
- $d_4 = 42 - 39.2 = +2.8$
- $d_5 = 41 - 39.2 = +1.8$

Squared deviations:
- $d_1^2 = 17.64$
- $d_2^2 = 1.44$
- $d_3^2 = 0.64$
- $d_4^2 = 7.84$
- $d_5^2 = 3.24$

Sum: $\sum d_i^2 = 30.80\ \text{kV}^2$.

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{30.80}{4}} = \sqrt{7.70} = 2.775\ \text{kV}
$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient of Variation:**
$$
CV = \frac{2.775}{39.2} \times 100\% = 7.08\%
$$

**Step 5 — Dielectric Strength:**
Gap $d = 2.5\ mm = 0.25\ cm$.
$$
E_{bd} = \frac{39.2}{0.25} = 156.8\ \text{kV/cm}
$$

**Step 6 — Acceptability:**
$V_{avg} = 39.2\ kV \geq 30\ kV$ (IS 335 limit) → **OIL IS ACCEPTABLE for service**.

Additional comment: $CV = 7.08\% < 10\%$ → good consistency in breakdown values; oil is homogeneous and well-conditioned.

---

### Question B — Alternative: Deeper Chemistry & Decision Logic

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the various **causes of deterioration** of transformer oil in service. How does **moisture** affect the breakdown voltage? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During routine testing of an oil sample, the following data was obtained: BDV = **22 kV**, IFT = **18 mN/m**, Acidity = **0.45 mg KOH/g**, Moisture = **55 ppm**, Tan-$\delta$ at 90 °C = **0.012**. Diagnose the condition of the oil and recommend appropriate corrective action. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Causes of Deterioration of Transformer Oil:**

1. **Thermal Oxidation:** At elevated operating temperatures (above 70 °C in hot-spots), oil reacts with dissolved oxygen to form **acids, aldehydes, ketones, and sludge**. These products are polar and hygroscopic, attracting more moisture.

2. **Electrical Stress (Partial Discharge / Arcing):** PD decomposes oil into **acetylene (C₂H₂)**, **hydrogen (H₂)**, and **methane (CH₄)**. DGA (Dissolved Gas Analysis) detects these fault gases for early diagnosis.

3. **Moisture Ingress:** From atmosphere (breathing action during load cycles), leaky gaskets, or decomposition of cellulose insulation. Water dramatically reduces dielectric strength.

4. **Contamination:** Fibres from insulation paper, dust, weld spatter from tank fabrication, and metal particles from pump wear.

5. **Chemical Reaction with Cellulose:** Aging of paper releases water and acids, accelerating oil degradation in a vicious cycle.

**Effect of Moisture on BDV:**

The relationship is **non-linear and severe**. Key points:
- **Dry oil** can withstand 50–70 kV across a 2.5 mm gap.
- **50 ppm moisture** reduces BDV to ~25–30 kV.
- **100 ppm** can drop BDV below 15 kV.
- **Mechanism:** Water molecules align along the field, forming **conductive bridges** that initiate streamers at much lower voltages. Water also forms **emulsions** that enhance local field at the droplet interfaces.

Mathematically, the empirical relation (for moderate moisture) is:
$$
V_{bd} \approx V_{bd,dry} \cdot e^{-k \cdot c_{water}}
$$
where $k$ is a constant (~0.02) and $c_{water}$ is moisture in ppm.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Diagnosis Matrix:**

| Parameter | Measured | IS 335 Limit | Verdict |
|-----------|----------|--------------|---------|
| BDV | 22 kV | ≥ 30 kV | ❌ **Fails** |
| IFT | 18 mN/m | ≥ 25 mN/m | ❌ **Fails** |
| Acidity | 0.45 mg KOH/g | ≤ 0.2 mg KOH/g | ❌ **Fails** |
| Moisture | 55 ppm | ≤ 35 ppm | ❌ **Fails** |
| Tan-$\delta$ (90 °C) | 0.012 | ≤ 0.005 | ❌ **Fails** |

**Diagnosis:** The oil has undergone **severe oxidative ageing** with significant moisture ingress and acidic by-product accumulation. It is **NOT acceptable for continued service**.

**Recommended Corrective Action:**
1. **Immediate step:** Take the transformer out of service (or at least reduce load) — operating with failed oil risks catastrophic failure.
2. **Reclaim the oil** using a **filter press** with **Fuller’s earth (bleaching clay)** adsorption — this removes acids, sludge, and polar contaminants, restoring IFT and reducing acidity.
3. **Vacuum dehydration** to remove moisture down to < 25 ppm.
4. **Re-test** all parameters; if still failing, **replace** the oil entirely.
5. **Investigate root cause:** Check silica-gel breather condition, gasket integrity, and winding hot-spot temperature. Consider **online DGA** for future monitoring.
6. **Preventive measure:** Install a **rubber-bag-type conservator** or **online oil conditioning** system to slow future degradation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Wrong electrode gap:** Writing "**2 mm**" or "**5 mm**" instead of the correct **2.5 mm**. **−1 mark.**
> 2. **Wrong rate of voltage rise:** Saying "**1 kV/s**" or "**5 kV/s**". Correct is **2 kV/s ± 0.2 kV/s**. **−1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting to discard the 1st reading:** The 1st BDV shot is a **conditioning shot**, not a valid data point. Including it skews the average. **−1 to −2 marks** in the calculation part.
> 4. **Not stating the IS standard:** Examiners expect **IS 335 / IS 6792 / IEC 60156** to be explicitly named. **−1 mark** if omitted.
> 5. **Wrong units for dielectric strength:** Writing "kV" instead of "kV/cm" or "kV/mm". **−0.5 mark.**
> 6. **Confusing $CV$ with standard deviation:** $CV$ is a **dimensionless percentage**, $\sigma$ is in **kV**. Many students mix them up.
> 7. **Skipping the stirring + 2-min wait** between consecutive BDV shots — this is a procedural step that resets the oil's gaseous state. **−1 mark** in procedure questions.
> 8. **Writing "transformer oil is used for insulation"** without mentioning **cooling/heat dissipation** — losing the **2nd mark** out of 3 in a basic definition.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Transformer oil = insulation + cooling** (always state both roles).
- [ ] **Standard for BDV test:** IS 6792 / IEC 60156.
- [ ] **Standard for oil specification:** IS 335.
- [ ] **Electrode geometry:** Spherical, **12.5 mm radius**, **2.5 mm gap**.
- [ ] **Voltage rise rate:** **2 kV/s ± 0.2 kV/s**.
- [ ] **Number of shots:** **6 total, discard 1st, average next 5**.
- [ ] **Stir + 2 min wait** between shots.
- [ ] **Acceptance limit:** $V_{bd} \geq 30\ kV$ (in-service, IS 335).
- [ ] **New oil expected:** 50–70 kV.
- [ ] **First reading discarded** because of conditioning effect.
- [ ] **Standard formulas:** $V_{avg}$, $\sigma$, $CV$, $E_{bd} = V/d$.
- [ ] **Coefficient of variation acceptable** if $CV < 10\%$.
- [ ] **Moisture is the #1 enemy** of oil — 50 ppm cuts BDV roughly in half.
- [ ] **Causes of deterioration:** thermal oxidation, electrical stress, moisture, contamination, cellulose aging.
- [ ] **DGA fault gases:** H₂, CH₄, C₂H₂, C₂H₄, CO, CO₂ — key for condition monitoring.
- [ ] **Corrective actions:** filtration (20–30 kV range) vs. reclamation/replacement (< 20 kV).
- [ ] **Other tests to know:** Tan-$\delta$ (limit 0.005 at 90 °C), Acidity (0.2 mg KOH/g), IFT (25 mN/m), Flash point (140 °C), Moisture (35 ppm).
- [ ] **Other physical/chemical tests:** Density (0.85–0.92 g/cm³), Viscosity (≤ 27 cSt at 27 °C), Pour point (≤ −30 °C).
- [ ] **Always state the IS/IEC standard** when describing any oil test.
- [ ] **Units reminder:** BDV in kV, dielectric strength in kV/cm or kV/mm, $\tan\delta$ dimensionless, IFT in mN/m.

### 🧠 Memory Mnemonics

- **"ESS"** for the **3 primary oil functions**: **E**lectrically insulates, **S**tores/transfers heat (cooling), **S**uppresses arcs.
- **"MEAD"** for the **4 chemical tests**: **M**oisture, **E**lectric (tan-$\delta$ — though it's actually electrical), **A**cidity, **D**GA (dissolved gas). 
  - Or better: **"M-A-I-O"** → **M**oisture, **A**cidity, **I**nterfacial tension, **O**xidation stability.
- **"BFR"** for **deterioration**: **B**reathing (moisture ingress), **F**ailure modes (PD/arc), **R**usting/oxidation.

### 📌 KTU-Exam Golden Tip
> For any **14-mark question** on this topic, structure the answer as:
> **(i) Standard & Principle → (ii) Circuit/Diagram → (iii) Procedure → (iv) Formulas & Sample Calculation → (v) Acceptance Criterion → (vi) Safety/Precautions.**
> This 6-part structure covers **every CO descriptor** in the KTU 2024 rubric for Power System Lab.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of Dielectric Strength of Solid Insulating Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Testing of Dielectric Strength of Solid Insulating Materials # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Dielectric Strength** of a solid insulating material is defined as the maximum electric field (voltage gradient) that the material can withstand without undergoing dielectric breakdown, expressed in **kV/mm** or **MV/m**. It is a fundamental property that quantifies the insulating capability of a dielectric under an applied high voltage stress. Mathematically, it is given by the relationship: $$E_{bd} = \frac{V_{bd}}{t}$$ where: - $E_{bd}$ = Dielectric strength (kV/mm) - $V_{bd}$ = Breakdown voltage at which the insulating material fails (kV) - $t$ = Thickness of the insulating material between the electrodes (mm) The "Testing of Dielectric Strength" refers to the standardized laboratory procedure in which a solid insulating material sample of known thickness is subjected to a gradually increasing alternating (AC) or direct (DC) high voltage until dielectric breakdown occurs. The voltage at the moment of failure is recorded, and the dielectric strength is computed as the ratio of the breakdown voltage to the sample thickness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is conducted as per **IS 2584 / ASTM D149 / IEC 60243** standards using standardized electrode configurations (typically 25 mm disc electrodes with edges rounded to 2 mm radius) and a continuously rising AC voltage (typically at 1 kV/s rise rate) applied between the electrodes. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **dam holding back a reservoir of water**. The dam wall is the *insulating material*, and the water pressure represents the *applied voltage*. As the water level rises, the pressure on the dam increases gradually. Eventually, a point is reached where the dam wall cannot withstand the pressure and **cracks** — water bursts through. The pressure at the moment of cracking is analogous to the **breakdown voltage**, and the maximum pressure per unit thickness the dam can handle is the **dielectric strength**. A thicker dam wall can typically hold more water, just as a thicker insulation can withstand more voltage. However, unlike a uniform concrete dam, the "strength" of insulation also depends on the **material's internal molecular structure**, **temperature**, **moisture content**, and the **shape of the electrodes** pressing against it (concentrated vs. uniform electric fields). > [!NOTE] > **Real-world example:** Bakelite (a thermoset plastic) has a dielectric strength of approximately **10–15 kV/mm**, while mica — a naturally occurring mineral insulator — exhibits an exceptionally high dielectric strength of about **100–200 kV/mm**. This is why mica is used in high-voltage switchgear, commutators, and capacitor dielectrics. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Solid Insulation Testing | Parameter | Standard Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Test Voltage Frequency | **50 Hz AC** (or 60 Hz) | Simulates power frequency stress | | Voltage Rise Rate | **1 kV/s** (continuous) | Standard to avoid surge overshoot | | Electrode Material | **Brass / Stainless Steel** | To prevent oxidation-induced errors | | Sample Dimensions | **100 mm × 100 mm × thickness** | Standard test specimen size | | Ambient Conditions | **27°C ± 2°C, 65% RH** | IS 2584 / ASTM D149 norms | | Number of Test Specimens | **Minimum 5 samples** | For statistical averaging | > [!TIP] > **Why is dielectric strength tested?** It is critical for **selecting insulation** in cables, transformers, switchgear, motors, capacitors, PCBs, and bushings. Failure of insulation can cause equipment damage, fires, electrocution, and cascading power system blackouts. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept: Dielectric Breakdown in Solids When a solid dielectric is subjected to a high electric field, the following sequence of physical events occurs: 1. **Electronic Polarization:** Under normal voltage, electrons in atoms shift slightly, creating dipoles. This is reversible and harmless. 2. **Intrinsic (Electronic) Breakdown:** At a critical field, electrons gain enough kinetic energy from the field to ionize lattice atoms on collision. This creates an *avalanche* of free electrons, leading to a sudden surge of current. The material physically chars, melts, or punctures, creating a permanent conductive path. 3. **Thermal Breakdown:** If the dielectric loss (tan δ) and leakage current cause heating faster than heat can dissipate, the temperature rises. This lowers the dielectric strength further (negative temperature coefficient), creating a runaway that ends in thermal breakdown. 4. **Electrochemical Breakdown:** Long-term effects of moisture, partial discharges, and chemical reactions create *tree-like* channels (electrical trees / water trees) that propagate and eventually bridge the electrodes. 5. **Discharge (Tracking) Breakdown:** Surface discharges degrade the material, especially in the presence of contamination and humidity, forming carbonized tracking paths. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The most common *breakdown mode* observed during the 1-minute ramp test in the lab is **Intrinsic Breakdown**, since the test is short-duration and conducted under controlled ambient conditions. Examiners frequently ask: *"Name the type of breakdown observed in a short-duration high-voltage test of a solid insulator."* ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Parameter - **Why 50 Hz AC?** AC stress is harsher than DC because the field reverses 100 times per second (full cycle) or 50 times (half cycle). Each reversal stresses the molecular bonds, and at voltage peaks, ionization is most intense. Also, real power systems operate at 50 Hz. - **Why 1 kV/s rise rate?** A slow rise avoids the "fictitious high breakdown" caused by surge effects, while a fast rise may cause partial discharge before true breakdown. The IEC recommends rates between 0.5 kV/s and 2 kV/s for short-time tests. - **Why 25 mm disc electrodes?** Standardization ensures reproducible test results. A 25 mm diameter provides a uniform field over a representative sample area while avoiding edge effects. Per **ASTM D149**, electrode edges must have a radius of curvature between 1.5 mm and 3.2 mm to prevent field concentration at corners. - **Why five specimens minimum?** Dielectric breakdown has statistical scatter. Mean, standard deviation, and Weibull distribution are used to characterize the material's reliability. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Description | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | $E_{bd} = \dfrac{V_{bd}}{t}$ | Dielectric strength = breakdown voltage / thickness | kV/mm | Primary result of the test | | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Conversion of AC peak to RMS value | kV | If scope reads peak voltage | | $E_{mean} = \dfrac{V}{d}$ | Mean electric field between parallel plates | kV/mm | $d$ = electrode gap | | $E_{max} = \dfrac{V}{r \ln(R/r)}$ | Max field for coaxial cylinder geometry | kV/mm | Used in cable insulation | | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel plate test cell | Farads | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m | | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{I_R}{I_C}$ | Dielectric loss tangent | dimensionless | Lower = better insulator | | $P_{loss} = V^2 \omega C \tan\delta$ | Power loss in dielectric | Watts | Drives thermal breakdown | | $V_{bd(impulse)} \approx 1.5 \cdot V_{bd(AC)}$ | Empirical impulse vs AC ratio | kV | Standard lightning impulse ≈ 1.5 µs | | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Standard deviation of $n$ test readings | kV/mm | Used to report test reliability | | $P(s) = 1 - e^{-(s/\alpha)^\beta}$ | Weibull cumulative distribution | dimensionless | $\alpha$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Syntax Safeguard:** In the formulas above, vertical bars inside table cells have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent the markdown table from breaking. Note also that the Weibull distribution uses `s` (stress variable) and not the absolute value symbol. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cable Manufacturing:** XLPE and EPR cables are tested to ensure dielectric strength exceeds the operating stress by a safety margin (typically 3–5×). - **Transformer Design:** Oil-impregnated paper and pressboard insulators are tested as per IS 2584 before assembly. - **Switchgear:** Bakelite, epoxy, and SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound) housings must meet minimum dielectric strength to prevent flashover. - **Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs):** FR-4 substrate dielectric strength is tested per IPC-TM-650. - **High-Voltage Capacitors:** Polypropylene and mica dielectrics are chosen for high dielectric strength and low loss. > [!NOTE] > **Key Constant:** The permittivity of free space, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, is fundamental in computing the capacitance of the test cell and is often quoted in the lab record's *Theory* section. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Dielectric Strength from First Principles The dielectric strength of a solid is derived by considering the **maximum electric field intensity** a material can sustain before its molecular bonds are ionized and it ceases to be insulating. **Step 1 — Defining the Electric Field between Parallel Electrodes:** For two parallel plate electrodes separated by a distance $t$, with potential difference $V$ applied, the uniform electric field is defined as: $$E = \frac{V}{t}$$ This is a direct consequence of the definition of voltage as the line integral of electric field over a uniform field path. The unit of $E$ becomes V/m (or equivalently kV/mm when scaled). **Step 2 — Defining Breakdown Voltage $V_{bd}$:** $V_{bd}$ is the empirical value of voltage at which a sudden, irreversible, large increase in current (typically a 10× to 1000× jump) flows through the specimen. It is read directly from the high-voltage test set's voltmeter at the moment of breakdown (often accompanied by a flash, sound, smoke, or visible puncture). **Step 3 — Defining Dielectric Strength $E_{bd}$:** The dielectric strength is defined as the electric field magnitude at breakdown. Substituting $V = V_{bd}$ and $t = $ specimen thickness: $$E_{bd} = \frac{V_{bd}}{t}$$ This is the **fundamental lab result**. The dimensional consistency check: $V$ in kV, $t$ in mm $\Rightarrow$ $E$ in kV/mm. Conversion to SI: 1 kV/mm = $10^6$ V/m = 1 MV/m. **Step 4 — Statistical Treatment (Mean and Standard Deviation):** Because no two solid specimens are identical at the molecular level, multiple tests are averaged. For $n$ specimens with measured strengths $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$: $$\bar{E} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i$$ $$\sigma_E = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (E_i - \bar{E})^2}$$ The reliability of the test is often expressed as the **coefficient of variation (CoV)**: $$CoV = \frac{\sigma_E}{\bar{E}} \times 100\%$$ A CoV less than 5% indicates a high-quality, homogeneous test series. ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Lab Problem) > **Given:** A solid insulating material of thickness **2 mm** is placed between two 25 mm disc electrodes. The breakdown voltage observed in the test is **40 kV (RMS)** at 50 Hz AC. > > **Required:** Calculate (a) the dielectric strength of the material, (b) the peak electric field, (c) the mean electric field in V/m units. **Solution:** **Part (a) — Dielectric strength in kV/mm:** $$E_{bd} = \frac{V_{bd}}{t} = \frac{40 \text{ kV}}{2 \text{ mm}} = 20 \text{ kV/mm}$$ **Part (b) — Peak electric field in kV/mm:** The voltmeter reads RMS, so the peak value is: $$V_{peak} = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 40 \times \sqrt{2} = 40 \times 1.4142 = 56.568 \text{ kV}$$ $$E_{peak} = \frac{V_{peak}}{t} = \frac{56.568 \text{ kV}}{2 \text{ mm}} = 28.284 \text{ kV/mm}$$ **Part (c) — Mean electric field in SI units (V/m):** $$E_{bd} = 20 \text{ kV/mm} = 20 \times \frac{1000 \text{ V}}{10^{-3} \text{ m}} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ V/m} = 2 \times 10^7 \text{ V/m}$$ $$\boxed{E_{bd} = 2 \times 10^7 \text{ V/m} = 20 \text{ MV/m}}$$ **Verification of dimensional consistency:** $$E_{bd} = \frac{40 \times 10^3 \text{ V}}{2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}} = 2 \times 10^7 \text{ V/m} \checkmark$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Factors Affecting Dielectric Strength | Factor | Effect on $E_{bd}$ | Why (Physical Reason) | KTU Board Exam Likelihood | |---|---|---|---| | **Increasing thickness** | $E_{bd}$ slightly *decreases* (in non-uniform fields) | Edge effects dominate; volume of defects increases | **High** — Common 7-mark question | | **Increasing temperature** | $E_{bd}$ *decreases* | Electron thermal energy aids ionization | **High** | | **Increasing moisture** | $E_{bd}$ *decreases* | Water has $E_{bd} \approx 1$ kV/mm — drastically lowers mean strength | **Very High** | | **Increasing frequency** | $E_{bd}$ *decreases* (above 1 kHz) | Dielectric loss $P = V^2 \omega C \tan\delta$ rises with $\omega$ | **Moderate** | | **Shorter voltage rise time** | $E_{bd}$ *appears higher* | Material does not have time for treeing/thermal damage | **High** | | **DC vs AC** | DC breakdown $\approx 1.5 \times$ AC breakdown | No reversal, lower peak for same RMS | **Moderate** | | **Electrode material** | Negligible (brass ≈ steel) | Field is governed by geometry | **Low** | | **Polishing / contamination** | $E_{bd}$ *decreases* | Micro-bubbles, dust, oil cause field enhancement | **Moderate** | > [!TIP] > **Examiner Trick:** A student may incorrectly claim that doubling the thickness doubles the dielectric strength. In reality, since $E_{bd} = V_{bd}/t$ is a *property of the material*, doubling $t$ doubles $V_{bd}$ — but $E_{bd}$ remains roughly constant. The misconception arises because in practice, a thicker sample may have more defects, slightly reducing $E_{bd}$. Always clarify: $E_{bd}$ is a **material property**, not a function of thickness. ## 3.4 Standardization of Test Method (IS 2584 / IEC 60243 Flow) The test methodology follows a strict sequence to ensure reproducibility across laboratories: 1. **Sample Preparation:** Cut the material into 100 mm × 100 mm squares. Condition at 27°C ± 2°C and 65% RH for 24 hours. 2. **Electrode Selection:** Use 25 mm brass disc electrodes with 2 mm edge radius. 3. **Assembly:** Mount sample between electrodes in the test cell, ensuring firm contact. 4. **Voltage Application:** Apply AC voltage at 50 Hz, raised from zero at 1 kV/s. 5. **Record Breakdown:** Note the voltage at which current jumps, or a visible puncture occurs. 6. **Repeat:** Test 5 specimens, compute mean and standard deviation. 7. **Report:** Quote dielectric strength as $\bar{E} \pm \sigma_E$ in kV/mm. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: High-Voltage Test Setup for Solid Insulating Materials ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Mains Supply
230 V 50 Hz] --> B[Variac
0 to 230 V] B --> C[Step-Up Transformer
HV Type 0 to 100 kV] C --> D[High Voltage
Output Terminal] D --> E[Top Electrode
Brass 25 mm Disc] F[Bottom Electrode
Brass 25 mm Disc] --> G[Grounded Return Path] E --> H[Specimen
Solid Insulating Material] H --> F F --> G C --> I[Capacitive Voltage Divider
for Measurement] I --> J[Voltmeter and
Oscilloscope Display] J --> K[Operator Records
Breakdown Voltage Vbd] H --> L{Does Breakdown
Occur?} L -- Yes --> M[Record Vbd
and Calculate Ebd] L -- No --> N[Continue Increasing
Voltage at 1 kV/s] N --> L M --> O[Repeat for 5 Specimens
and Average] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Test Procedure Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Start Experiment] --> P2[Step 1: Prepare 5 Specimens
of Material 100x100 mm] P2 --> P3[Step 2: Measure Thickness
at 5 Points with Micrometer] P3 --> P4[Step 3: Condition Specimens
at 27C and 65% RH for 24 hrs] P4 --> P5[Step 4: Place Specimen
between Disc Electrodes] P5 --> P6[Step 5: Verify Earthing
of Test Cell] P6 --> P7[Step 6: Switch on HV Supply
and Set Variac to Zero] P7 --> P8[Step 7: Raise Voltage at
1 kV per Second] P8 --> P9[Step 8: Observe Specimen
and Voltmeter Continuously] P9 --> P10[Step 9: Note Vbd at
Moment of Breakdown] P10 --> P11[Step 10: Switch off Supply
and Discharge via Earth Rod] P11 --> P12[Step 11: Compute Ebd
for Each Specimen] P12 --> P13[Step 12: Calculate Mean
and Standard Deviation] P13 --> P14[Step 13: Tabulate Results
and Plot Bar Graph] P14 --> P15[Step 14: Compare with
Standard IS 2584 Values] P15 --> P16[End of Experiment] ``` ## 4.3 Electrode Configuration Diagram (Text-Based Schematic) ``` TOP ELECTRODE (Brass 25 mm diameter) ┌─────────────────────────┐ ← 2 mm edge radius (rounded) │ ●●●●●●●●●●●● │ │ ●●●●●●●●●●●●●●● │ ↓ Applied High Voltage V │ ●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●● │ ↓ │ ●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●● │ ↓ │ ●●●●●●●●●●●●●●● │ ↓ Electric Field E = V/t │ ●●●●●●●●●●●● │ └─────────────────────────┘ ══════════════════════════ ← Specimen (thickness t = 1 to 3 mm) ┌─────────────────────────┐ │ ●●●●●●●●●●●● │ │ ●●●●●●●●●●●●●●● │ ↑ Grounded return path │ ●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●● │ │ ●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●● │ │ ●●●●●●●●●●●●●●● │ │ ●●●●●●●●●●●● │ └─────────────────────────┘ BOTTOM ELECTRODE (Brass 25 mm diameter, grounded) ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Matrix: Solid vs Liquid vs Gaseous Dielectric Behavior | Property | Solid (Bakelite) | Liquid (Transformer Oil) | Gas (Air at NTP) | |---|---|---|---| | Dielectric Strength | **10–25 kV/mm** | **10–30 kV/mm** | **3 kV/mm** | | Self-Healing After Breakdown | No (permanent damage) | Yes (oil refills the gap) | Yes (de-ionizes rapidly) | | Density | High (1.2–1.8 g/cm³) | Medium (0.87–0.92 g/cm³) | Low (0.0012 g/cm³) | | Used In | Bushings, switchgear bodies, PCBs | Transformers, circuit breakers | Overhead line air gaps | | Standard Test Spec | IS 2584 / ASTM D149 | IS 6792 / IEC 60897 | IS 1876 / ASTM D877 | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Emphasis:** The examiner often asks students to compare solid, liquid, and gaseous dielectrics. The most important distinction is **self-healing capability** — solids fail permanently, while liquids and gases recover after the arc is extinguished. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCEEL609] **Define dielectric strength. State its unit and the formula used to calculate it from a breakdown test.** **Model Answer:** Dielectric strength is the maximum electric field that a solid insulating material can withstand without undergoing electrical breakdown. It is measured in **kV/mm** (or MV/m in SI). The formula is: $$E_{bd} = \frac{V_{bd}}{t}$$ where $V_{bd}$ is the breakdown voltage in kV and $t$ is the thickness of the specimen in mm. It is a fundamental material property used to select insulators in power systems. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCEEL609] **Name any two standard test methods used for testing dielectric strength of solid insulating materials.** **Model Answer:** The two standard test methods are: 1. **IS 2584** (Indian Standard) — Method of test for electric strength of solid insulating materials at power frequencies. 2. **ASTM D149** (American Standard) — Standard test method for dielectric breakdown voltage and dielectric strength of solid electrical insulating materials at commercial power frequencies. Both standards specify electrode dimensions (25 mm disc), voltage rise rate (1 kV/s), and ambient conditions (27°C, 65% RH). --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCEEL609] **(a)** Explain the procedure for testing the dielectric strength of a solid insulating material as per **IS 2584** standards. Draw the schematic diagram of the test setup. (7 marks) **(b)** A specimen of Bakelite of thickness **1.5 mm** is tested between two 25 mm disc electrodes. The following breakdown voltages (RMS) are recorded across 5 specimens: **18 kV, 20 kV, 19 kV, 21 kV, 17 kV**. Calculate the dielectric strength, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question A ##### Part (a) — Procedure and Schematic **Apparatus Required:** - High-voltage test transformer (0–100 kV, 50 Hz) - Variac (auto-transformer) for voltage control - Two 25 mm brass disc electrodes with 2 mm edge radius - Voltmeter (electrostatic or capacitive divider type) - Specimen holder assembly - Micrometer for thickness measurement - Earth rod for discharging the specimen after test - Safety interlock and barrier **Procedure:** 1. **Sample Preparation:** Cut five specimens of the insulating material, each 100 mm × 100 mm in size. Smooth the edges and clean the surfaces with isopropyl alcohol. 2. **Conditioning:** Place the specimens in a controlled environment of 27°C ± 2°C and 65% relative humidity for 24 hours (per IS 2584). 3. **Thickness Measurement:** Measure the thickness of each specimen at five points using a micrometer (least count 0.01 mm). Record the mean thickness $t$. 4. **Electrode Setup:** Mount the specimen between the top and bottom brass disc electrodes in the test cell. Apply uniform pressure to ensure good contact without damaging the surface. 5. **Safety Checks:** Verify earthing of the test cell bottom electrode. Ensure the safety interlock on the test cage is closed. Keep the earth rod ready for emergency discharge. 6. **Voltage Application:** Set the variac to zero. Switch on the high-voltage transformer. Increase the voltage gradually at a rate of **1 kV/s** until breakdown occurs. 7. **Observation:** At the moment of breakdown, note the voltmeter reading $V_{bd}$. A puncture hole, flash, smoke, or audible sound will be observed. 8. **Discharge:** Switch off the supply and discharge the specimen through the earth rod before opening the test cell. 9. **Repetition:** Repeat steps 4–8 for all 5 specimens. 10. **Calculation:** Compute $E_{bd} = V_{bd}/t$ for each specimen, then find the mean and standard deviation. **Schematic:** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. **Key Safety Precautions:** - Never touch the HV electrode while the supply is on. - Always use the earth rod to discharge the specimen. - Maintain a safe distance (minimum 1 m) from the HV test cell. - Wear HV-rated insulating gloves and stand on an insulating mat. --- ##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given Data:** | Specimen | $V_{bd}$ (kV) | $t$ (mm) | |---|---|---| | 1 | 18 | 1.5 | | 2 | 20 | 1.5 | | 3 | 19 | 1.5 | | 4 | 21 | 1.5 | | 5 | 17 | 1.5 | **Step 1 — Compute Dielectric Strength for Each Specimen:** $$E_1 = \frac{18}{1.5} = 12.00 \text{ kV/mm}$$ $$E_2 = \frac{20}{1.5} = 13.33 \text{ kV/mm}$$ $$E_3 = \frac{19}{1.5} = 12.67 \text{ kV/mm}$$ $$E_4 = \frac{21}{1.5} = 14.00 \text{ kV/mm}$$ $$E_5 = \frac{17}{1.5} = 11.33 \text{ kV/mm}$$ **Step 2 — Mean Dielectric Strength:** $$\bar{E} = \frac{12.00 + 13.33 + 12.67 + 14.00 + 11.33}{5} = \frac{63.33}{5} = 12.67 \text{ kV/mm}$$ **Step 3 — Deviations and Squared Deviations:** | Specimen | $E_i$ | $(E_i - \bar{E})$ | $(E_i - \bar{E})^2$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 12.00 | -0.67 | 0.4489 | | 2 | 13.33 | 0.66 | 0.4356 | | 3 | 12.67 | 0.00 | 0.0000 | | 4 | 14.00 | 1.33 | 1.7689 | | 5 | 11.33 | -1.34 | 1.7956 | | **Sum** | | | **4.4490** | **Step 4 — Standard Deviation:** $$\sigma_E = \sqrt{\frac{4.4490}{5-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.4490}{4}} = \sqrt{1.1123} = 1.05 \text{ kV/mm}$$ **Step 5 — Coefficient of Variation:** $$CoV = \frac{1.05}{12.67} \times 100\% = 8.29\%$$ **Final Results:** $$\boxed{\bar{E} = 12.67 \text{ kV/mm}, \quad \sigma_E = 1.05 \text{ kV/mm}, \quad CoV = 8.29\%}$$ **Interpretation:** The CoV of 8.29% is slightly higher than the ideal 5%, indicating moderate scatter in the test data — likely due to small surface irregularities or thickness variation in the specimens. The result aligns with **IS 2584 expected dielectric strength for Bakelite (10–15 kV/mm)**. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCEEL609] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the various **electrode configurations** used in dielectric strength testing of solid insulating materials. Discuss the effect of electrode geometry on the test result. (7 marks) **(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting the dielectric strength** of solid insulating materials. How does the test voltage waveform (AC vs DC vs impulse) influence the measured breakdown voltage? (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question B ##### Part (a) — Electrode Configurations Three primary electrode configurations are used in standard tests: 1. **Plane-Plane (Parallel Plate) Electrodes:** Two flat circular discs (typically 25 mm or 50 mm diameter) with rounded edges. This produces a *uniform electric field* in the central region and is the most common configuration per ASTM D149 and IS 2584. Best for thin, flat specimens. 2. **Sphere-Sphere Electrodes:** Two polished metal spheres of identical diameter (typically 12.5 mm or 25 mm). Used for testing liquids and sometimes thin solid films. The field is quasi-uniform at the gap center. The sphere diameter is chosen to be at least twice the gap to prevent field enhancement at the rear of the spheres. 3. **Needle-Plane Electrodes:** A sharp needle (radius of tip ≈ 10 µm) facing a flat plane. This produces a *highly non-uniform field* and is used to study **treeing** and **partial discharge inception** in solid dielectrics. Not used for routine $E_{bd}$ measurement but is standard for tracking and erosion tests per IEC 60112 (Comparative Tracking Index, CTI). **Effect of Electrode Geometry on Test Result:** - **Uniform field (plane-plane, sphere-sphere):** Yields the *highest* measured $E_{bd}$ because the field is evenly distributed. No localized stress concentration. - **Non-uniform field (needle-plane):** Yields a *lower* measured $E_{bd}$ because the field at the needle tip is far greater than the average field. Breakdown initiates at the tip and propagates through the weakest path. - **Electrode Material:** Brass and stainless steel yield nearly identical results, but **mercury** electrodes (used in older tests) produced a more intimate contact and slightly higher values. - **Edge Radius:** Sharper edges cause premature breakdown at the periphery. ASTM D149 mandates an edge radius of 1.5–3.2 mm to standardize this effect. ##### Part (b) — Factors Affecting Dielectric Strength Refer to the **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.3** of these notes for the complete table. The key factors are: 1. **Thickness of Specimen:** Thicker samples have a higher absolute breakdown voltage but a marginally lower dielectric strength due to greater probability of volume defects. 2. **Temperature:** As temperature increases, dielectric strength decreases. The Arrhenius-type relationship holds: $\ln E_{bd} \propto 1/T$. 3. **Moisture and Humidity:** Water has a very low dielectric strength (~1 kV/mm), and absorbed moisture in the bulk or on the surface drastically reduces the measured value. Pre-conditioning at controlled humidity is therefore critical. 4. **Frequency and Waveform:** - **AC (50 Hz):** The standard reference. Polarization reversals cause additional ionic movement and heating. - **DC:** No polarization reversals. DC breakdown voltage is typically **1.5× to 2×** the AC peak value because there is no peak heating or reversal stress. - **Impulse (1.2/50 µs):** Short-duration surge. Material has insufficient time for full avalanche formation, so the apparent $E_{bd}$ is **higher** than AC. Standard lightning impulse breakdown is typically 1.3–1.5× the AC peak. 5. **Voltage Application Time:** Longer hold time allows partial discharge and treeing to develop, lowering the breakdown voltage. The "short-time" test (ramp to breakdown in ~30 seconds) gives the highest value; the "step-by-step" test (1-minute holds at each level) gives a lower value. 6. **Electrode Pressure:** Poor contact causes air gaps and partial discharge, artificially lowering $E_{bd}$. Uniform pressure is essential. 7. **Surface Condition:** Scratches, dust, oil, and fingerprints act as field enhancers. Standard cleaning with isopropyl alcohol is required. **Summary Table — Waveform Effect on Breakdown Voltage (for the same material and thickness):** | Waveform | $V_{bd}$ relative to AC RMS | Reason | |---|---|---| | AC 50 Hz RMS | **1.0 × (Reference)** | Standard reference value | | AC Peak | $1.0 \times \sqrt{2} = 1.414 \times$ RMS | Peak value is what the insulation sees | | DC | $\approx 1.5 \times$ AC RMS | No reversal stress | | Impulse 1.2/50 µs | $\approx 1.3 \text{ to } 1.5 \times$ AC Peak | Fast rise limits discharge time | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes Students Make:** > > 1. **Confusing RMS and Peak:** When the voltmeter reads RMS, students often compute $E_{bd} = V_{rms}/t$ correctly, but in impulse tests, they may apply the RMS formula to peak values, leading to a 40% error. > 2. **Forgetting to Convert mm to m:** Many students report $E_{bd}$ in kV/mm without converting to SI (V/m) when the question asks for it. > 3. **Mean Without Standard Deviation:** A mean dielectric strength without $\sigma$ is considered an incomplete report in KTU board exams — always report $\bar{E} \pm \sigma_E$. > 4. **Neglecting Safety Precautions:** In procedure questions (7 marks), at least 2 marks are reserved for safety — mentioning earth rod, insulating gloves, interlock, and discharge procedure. > 5. **Wrong Voltage Rise Rate:** Writing 10 kV/s instead of 1 kV/s. The correct rate per IS 2584 is 1 kV/s for the short-time test. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PCEEL609 — Module 2 Experiment:** - **Definition:** Dielectric strength $E_{bd} = V_{bd}/t$, expressed in **kV/mm** or **MV/m**. - **Standard:** **IS 2584 / ASTM D149 / IEC 60243** — all three are acceptable references in the answer. - **Electrode:** **25 mm brass disc** with **2 mm edge radius**. - **Voltage Rise Rate:** **1 kV/s** (continuous ramp from zero to breakdown). - **Frequency:** **50 Hz AC** (or 60 Hz in some standards). - **Sample Size:** 100 mm × 100 mm; **at least 5 specimens** for averaging. - **Conditioning:** 27°C ± 2°C, 65% RH, 24 hours. - **Statistical Reporting:** Always report **mean ± standard deviation** and ideally the **coefficient of variation**. - **Breakdown Modes (in order of duration of test):** Intrinsic → Thermal → Electrochemical → Tracking. Short tests see **Intrinsic** breakdown. - **DC vs AC:** DC breakdown ≈ **1.5× to 2×** AC RMS breakdown. - **Impulse vs AC:** Impulse breakdown ≈ **1.3× to 1.5×** AC peak. - **Common Materials & Their Strengths:** Bakelite 10–15 kV/mm, Mica 100–200 kV/mm, PVC 10–40 kV/mm, XLPE 20–35 kV/mm, Transformer Oil 10–30 kV/mm, Air ~3 kV/mm. - **Factors:** Thickness, temperature, moisture, frequency, electrode geometry, surface condition, voltage rise time, and hold duration. - **Safety Essentials:** Earth rod discharge, insulating gloves, HV mat, interlock, distance > 1 m, never touch live terminals. - **Formula to Memorize:** $E_{bd} = V_{bd}/t$, $V_{peak} = V_{rms}\sqrt{2}$, $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, $P_{loss} = V^2 \omega C \tan \delta$. - **Key Constant:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. - **Drawing Requirement:** In a 7-mark procedure question, always include the **block diagram of the test setup** with HV transformer, variac, electrodes, voltmeter, and earth connection.

topics.title: Testing of Dielectric Strength of Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Testing of Dielectric Strength of Air ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Dielectric Strength of Air** is defined as the maximum electric field intensity (or equivalently, the minimum voltage per unit gap distance) that dry air can withstand without losing its insulating properties and undergoing electrical breakdown. It is a fundamental property of atmospheric air, quantified as the voltage gradient required to ionize the air molecules across a uniform electrode gap. Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$E_b = \frac{V_b}{d}$$ where $E_b$ is the dielectric strength (kV/cm or kV/mm), $V_b$ is the breakdown voltage (kV), and $d$ is the gap distance between the electrodes (cm or mm). Under **Standard Reference Atmospheric Conditions (SRAC)** — i.e., temperature $T_0 = 293\ \text{K}\ (20^\circ\text{C})$, pressure $P_0 = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ (1 atm or 760 torr), and absolute humidity $h_0 = 11\ \text{g/m}^3$ — the dielectric strength of air in a uniform field is approximately **30 kV/cm (peak)** or **21.1 kV/cm (rms)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is one of the most fundamental high-voltage lab tests.
