It demonstrates the ionization phenomena in gases, validates **Paschen's Law**, and helps in designing external insulation for transmission lines, bushings, and switchgear. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine a crowded staircase in a stadium. As long as people (electrons) walk orderly (low voltage), there is no problem. But the moment a stampede begins — i.e., too many people pushing too hard (high voltage) — the staircase railing breaks (the air ionizes and conducts). The threshold at which the railing breaks is the **dielectric strength of the staircase (air)**. A simpler real-world example: when you see a **lightning bolt** or hear a **crackling spark** when removing a woollen sweater, you are witnessing the breakdown of air — air that normally is an excellent insulator becomes a momentary conductor because the applied electric field exceeded its dielectric strength. ### 1.3 Why is This Experiment Important? - **Insulation Coordination:** Engineers must know the dielectric strength of air to design the air clearances in substations, overhead lines, and bushings. - **Calibration of HV Test Equipment:** Sphere gaps are calibrated using the known dielectric strength of air. - **Standardization:** It provides a reproducible reference for testing high-voltage equipment since air is universally available. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Atmosphere (as per IS/IEC 60060-1):** > - Pressure $P_0$ = **101.3 kPa** > - Temperature $T_0$ = **293 K (20 °C)** > - Absolute humidity $h_0$ = **11 g/m³** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mechanism of Breakdown in Air The breakdown of air is a sequential, three-stage electron multiplication process, best described by **Townsend's Theory**: **Stage 1 — Primary Ionization (α-process):** An initial electron, accelerated by the electric field $E$, gains kinetic energy. When it collides with a neutral air molecule ($N_2$ or $O_2$), it may ionize the molecule, releasing a new electron. This forms an electron avalanche. The rate of ionization per unit length is the **Townsend first ionization coefficient $\alpha$** (ionizations per metre). **Stage 2 — Secondary Ionization (γ-process):** When the positive ions generated in stage 1 strike the cathode, they may liberate additional electrons. The **Townsend secondary ionization coefficient $\gamma$** accounts for this (typically $\gamma \approx 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$). **Stage 3 — Self-Sustaining Discharge:** When the total electron multiplication becomes self-sustaining (i.e., each avalanche generates just enough secondary electrons to produce another avalanche), breakdown occurs. The condition is the **Townsend Breakdown Criterion**: $$\gamma \left( e^{\alpha d} - 1 \right) = 1$$ ### 2.2 Paschen's Law — The Cornerstone of Gas Breakdown Paschen's Law states that the breakdown voltage $V_b$ of a gas in a uniform field is a function of the product of gas pressure $P$ and electrode gap distance $d$, i.e., $V_b = f(Pd)$. $$V_b = \frac{B \cdot P \cdot d}{\ln\!\left(A \cdot P \cdot d\right) - \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)\right]}$$ where: - $A$ = saturation ionization constant (depends on gas; for air $A \approx 11.0\ (\text{m·Pa})^{-1}$ or $112.5\ (\text{cm·torr})^{-1}$) - $B$ = excitation/ionization energy constant (for air $B \approx 273.8\ \text{V/(m·Pa)}$ or $27.4\ \text{V/(cm·torr)}$) - $\gamma_{se}$ = secondary emission coefficient at the cathode - $P$ = gas pressure - $d$ = electrode gap distance The Paschen curve has a characteristic **U-shape** with a minimum voltage called the **Paschen minimum** ($V_{b,\min}$ occurs at $Pd \approx 0.7\ \text{torr·cm}$ for air, with $V_{b,\min} \approx 330\ \text{V}$). ### 2.3 Factors Affecting the Dielectric Strength of Air 1. **Electrode Geometry:** Uniform-field (Rogowski/profile) electrodes give the highest strength; non-uniform (point-plane) lowers it drastically. 2. **Gap Distance:** For uniform fields, $V_b$ increases roughly linearly with $d$. 3. **Air Pressure:** Higher pressure $\Rightarrow$ denser gas $\Rightarrow$ higher dielectric strength (Paschen's Law). 4. **Air Temperature:** Higher temperature $\Rightarrow$ lower density $\Rightarrow$ lower dielectric strength. 5. **Humidity:** Higher humidity slightly **increases** dielectric strength because water vapour (electronegative) captures free electrons. 6. **Waveform & Polarity:** DC, AC (50 Hz), and impulse (1.2/50 µs) breakdown voltages differ significantly. 7. **Electrode Material & Surface Condition:** Rough or contaminated cathodes lower $\gamma$. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below contains every high-yield formula tested in KTU exams. All expressions use \vert and \mid — never the raw pipe — to preserve Markdown table integrity. | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---------|-------------------| | 1 | $E_b = \dfrac{V_b}{d}$ | Basic definition of dielectric strength | | 2 | $V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot P d}{\ln(A P d) - \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)\right]}$ | Paschen's Law (general form) | | 3 | $\gamma \left( e^{\alpha d} - 1 \right) = 1$ | Townsend breakdown criterion | | 4 | $\dfrac{\alpha}{P} = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B P}{E}\right)$ | Empirical relation: $\alpha / P$ vs $E / P$ | | 5 | $V_{b,\min} \approx 2.718 \cdot \dfrac{B}{A} \cdot \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)$ | Paschen minimum voltage | | 6 | $(Pd)_{\min} = \dfrac{2.718}{A} \cdot \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)$ | Paschen minimum (Pd) location | | 7 | $\delta = \dfrac{P}{P_0} \cdot \dfrac{T_0}{T}$ | **Air Density Factor** (relative density) | | 8 | $V_b = \delta \cdot V_{b0}$ | Corrected breakdown voltage for actual conditions | | 9 | $k_h = 1 + 0.012 \left( \dfrac{h}{11} - 1 \right)$ | Humidity correction factor (approx.) | | 10 | $V_b^{\text{DC}} \approx 1.41 \cdot V_b^{\text{AC(rms, uniform)}}$ | Approx. DC vs AC relationship for uniform fields | | 11 | $V_b^{\text{impulse}} \approx 1.10 \text{ to } 1.20 \cdot V_b^{\text{AC(peak, uniform)}}$ | Impulse vs AC-peak relationship | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Sphere-Gap Voltmeters:** Used as primary reference standards to calibrate AC/DC/impulse voltmeters up to 2 MV, based on the known dielectric strength of air. - **Transmission Line Tower Design:** Minimum air clearances between conductors and tower body are determined from impulse dielectric strength. - **Surge Arrester Selection:** Rating of arresters is matched to the basic insulation level (BIL) of substations, which depends on air breakdown. - **High-Voltage Bushing Creepage:** External insulation paths through air must withstand switching surges. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Paschen's Law from Townsend Criterion **Step 1 — Start with Townsend's first ionization coefficient relation.** The number of electrons generated per unit length by an electron moving in the direction of the field is $\alpha$. Empirical fit to experimental data gives: $$\frac{\alpha}{P} = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{E/P}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Express the electric field in terms of voltage and gap.** In a uniform field, $E = V / d$. Substituting: $$\frac{\alpha}{P} = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{B \cdot P \cdot d}{V}\right)$$ Therefore: $$\alpha = A \cdot P \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{B \cdot P \cdot d}{V}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Apply Townsend Breakdown Criterion.** For self-sustaining breakdown: $$\gamma \left( e^{\alpha d} - 1 \right) = 1$$ **Step 4 — Approximate for large $\alpha d$ (typical near breakdown).** When $\alpha d \gg 1$, $\left(e^{\alpha d} - 1\right) \approx e^{\alpha d}$: $$\gamma \cdot e^{\alpha d} = 1$$ $$\alpha d = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{\gamma}\right) = -\ln(\gamma)$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the expression for $\alpha$ from Step 2:** $$\left[ A \cdot P \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{B P d}{V_b}\right) \right] d = -\ln(\gamma)$$ $$A \cdot P \cdot d \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{B P d}{V_b}\right) = -\ln(\gamma)$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $V_b$ by taking the natural logarithm of both sides:** $$\ln(A P d) - \frac{B P d}{V_b} = \ln\!\left[-\ln(\gamma)\right]$$ $$\frac{B P d}{V_b} = \ln(A P d) - \ln\!\left[-\ln(\gamma)\right]$$ $$\boxed{V_b = \frac{B \cdot P \cdot d}{\ln(A P d) - \ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma}\right)\right]}}$$ Note: Using $\ln[-\ln(\gamma)] = \ln[\ln(1/\gamma)] = \ln[\ln(1 + 1/\gamma)]$ yields the standard textbook form of Paschen's Law. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Paschen Minimum **Step 1 — Differentiate $V_b$ with respect to $(Pd)$ and set to zero.** Let $x = A \cdot P \cdot d$. Then: $$V_b = \frac{B}{A} \cdot \frac{x}{\ln(x) - \ln[\ln(1 + 1/\gamma)]}$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate $V_b / (B/A)$ with respect to $x$:** $$\frac{d V_b'}{dx} = \frac{[\ln(x) - c] \cdot 1 - x \cdot (1/x)}{[\ln(x) - c]^2} = \frac{\ln(x) - c - 1}{[\ln(x) - c]^2}$$ where $c = \ln[\ln(1 + 1/\gamma)]$. **Step 3 — Set the numerator to zero:** $$\ln(x) - c - 1 = 0 \implies \ln(x) = c + 1 = \ln(e) + c$$ $$x = e \cdot \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\gamma}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Paschen minimum location:** $$\boxed{(Pd)_{\min} = \frac{e}{A} \cdot \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\gamma}\right)}$$ **Step 5 — Substituting back gives the minimum voltage:** $$\boxed{V_{b,\min} = \frac{e \cdot B}{A} \cdot \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\gamma}\right)}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical Breakdown Voltages in Air (Uniform Field, 50 Hz AC rms) | Gap $d$ (cm) | Breakdown Voltage $V_b$ (kV, rms) | Approx. Field Strength (kV/cm) | |---|---|---| | 0.5 | 11.0 | 22.0 | | 1.0 | 21.1 | 21.1 | | 2.0 | 41.0 | 20.5 | | 3.0 | 60.5 | 20.2 | | 5.0 | 100.0 | 20.0 | | 10.0 | 195.0 | 19.5 | > [!IMPORTANT] > For practical engineering estimations in **uniform fields**, the commonly used approximation is **30 kV (peak)/cm ≈ 21.1 kV (rms)/cm** under standard conditions. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A pair of uniform-field electrodes has a gap of 2 cm in air at 1 atm and 27 °C. Using Paschen's Law with $A = 112.5\ (\text{cm·torr})^{-1}$, $B = 27.4\ \text{V/(cm·torr)}$, and $\gamma = 10^{-4}$, compute the DC breakdown voltage. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert pressure to torr:** $1\ \text{atm} = 760\ \text{torr}$. So $P = 760\ \text{torr}$. **Step 2 — Compute $Pd$:** $Pd = 760 \times 2 = 1520\ \text{torr·cm}$ **Step 3 — Compute the denominator of Paschen's Law:** $$\ln(1 + 1/\gamma) = \ln(1 + 10000) = \ln(10001) \approx 9.2104$$ $$\ln\!\left[\ln(1 + 1/\gamma)\right] = \ln(9.2104) \approx 2.2207$$ **Step 4 — Compute $\ln(A P d)$:** $A P d = 112.5 \times 1520 = 171{,}000$ $\ln(171{,}000) \approx 12.0496$ **Step 5 — Compute $V_b$:** $$V_b = \frac{27.4 \times 1520}{12.0496 - 2.2207} = \frac{41{,}648}{9.8289} \approx 4237\ \text{V} \approx 4.24\ \text{kV}$$ **Step 6 — Apply air density correction (optional).** At 27 °C, $\delta = (1)(293/300) = 0.977$. $V_b^{\text{corrected}} = 0.977 \times 4.24 = 4.14\ \text{kV}$. **Step 7 — Compare with the experimental rule (uniform field, 30 kV/cm peak):** Expected $\approx 60\ \text{kV}$ peak $\approx 42\ \text{kV}$ rms. The Paschen calculation gives DC value, which should be lower than the AC rms. Result is reasonable. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Experimental Block Diagram of Dielectric Strength Test ```mermaid flowchart TD A[AC HV Transformer
0 to 100 kV] --> B[Variable Autotransformer
Variac 0 to 240 V] B --> C[Step-up HV Transformer
Ratio 1 to 500] C --> D[Protective Resistor
100 kiloohm Water] D --> E[Sphere Gap Electrodes
Brass Copper Polished] E --> F[Test Gap
Air Dielectric 0.5 to 5 cm] F --> G[Breakdown Detector
Sphere Gap Voltmeter] G --> H[Oscilloscope CRO
Waveform Capture] E --> I[High Voltage Probe
Capacitance Divider] I --> J[Digital Voltmeter
kV Display] F --> K[Environment Sensors
Pressure Temperature Humidity] K --> L[Data Logger
Apply Correction Factor delta] J --> M[Final Breakdown Voltage
Corrected to Standard Atmosphere] H --> M G --> M L --> M ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Paschen's Law and Breakdown Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Free Electron
in Gap] --> P2[Accelerate in
Electric Field E] P2 --> P3[Collide with
Air Molecule N2 O2] P3 --> P4{Ionization
Occurred?} P4 -- Yes --> P5[Release New
Electron] P4 -- No --> P6[Elastic
Collision] P5 --> P7[Electron
Avalanche] P6 --> P2 P7 --> P8[Positive Ions
Strike Cathode] P8 --> P9{Secondary
Emission gamma} P9 -- Yes --> P10[New Electron
from Cathode] P10 --> P2 P9 -- No --> P11[Stable
Non-conducting] P7 --> P12{Townsend
Criterion Met?} P12 -- gamma exp alpha d equals 1 --> P13[BREAKDOWN
occurs] P12 -- No --> P11 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid — Correction Factor Application Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD M1[Measure Vb at Test
Conditions P T h] --> M2[Compute Air
Density delta] M2 --> M3[delta equals P over P0
times T0 over T] M3 --> M4[Compute Humidity
Factor kh] M4 --> M5[Vb standard equals
Vb test times delta times kh] M5 --> M6[Compare with
Standard Tables] M6 --> M7[Pass or Fail
Compliance Check] ``` ### 4.4 Apparatus List (Standard Lab Setup) | Sl. No. | Equipment | Specification | |---|---|---| | 1 | HV Testing Transformer | 100 kV / 5 kVA, 50 Hz, single phase | | 2 | Variable Autotransformer (Variac) | 0 – 240 V, 5 A | | 3 | Sphere-Sphere Electrode Set | Brass spheres, 5 cm / 10 cm / 25 cm diameter | | 4 | Protective Water Resistor | 100 kΩ | | 5 | Capacitance Voltage Divider | 1000:1 ratio | | 6 | Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) | 100 MHz, 1 GS/s | | 7 | Barometer / Digital Pressure Gauge | 0 – 1000 mbar | | 8 | Thermometer / Hygrometer | –10 to 100 °C, 0 – 100 % RH | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define dielectric strength of air. State its value under standard atmospheric conditions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) **Definition:** The dielectric strength of air is the minimum value of the electric field intensity (or voltage per unit length) at which a gas-air gap loses its insulating property and breaks down to become a conductor. - (1 mark) **Units:** kV/cm or kV/mm. - (1 mark) **Standard value:** Approximately **30 kV/cm (peak)** or **21.1 kV/cm (rms)** at standard conditions (20 °C, 1 atm, 11 g/m³ humidity). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] State Paschen's Law. What is the Paschen minimum?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1.5 marks) **Statement:** Paschen's Law states that the breakdown voltage $V_b$ of a gas in a uniform field is a function only of the product $Pd$ (gas pressure × gap distance), i.e., $V_b = f(Pd)$. - (1.5 marks) **Paschen minimum:** The $V_b$ vs $Pd$ curve is U-shaped. The minimum point of this curve is called the Paschen minimum, occurring at $Pd \approx 0.7\ \text{torr·cm}$ for air, with $V_{b,\min} \approx 330\ \text{V}$. Below this product, breakdown voltage rises sharply again. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **[Question A — 14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Q.A.(a) [7 marks]** Explain the Townsend mechanism of breakdown in air with the help of primary ($\alpha$) and secondary ($\gamma$) ionization processes. Derive the Townsend breakdown criterion. **Model Answer:** **(i) Primary ionization — $\alpha$ process [2 marks]:** - When a voltage is applied across the gap, free electrons accelerate toward the anode under the electric field $E$. - These electrons collide with neutral air molecules; if the energy gained between collisions exceeds the ionization energy, an electron is liberated, leaving a positive ion. - $\alpha$ = number of ionizing collisions per unit length travelled by one electron (typically $\alpha \approx 10$ to $10^4\ \text{m}^{-1}$ depending on $E/P$). - Empirically: $\alpha / P = A \exp(-B P / E)$. **(ii) Secondary ionization — $\gamma$ process [2 marks]:** - Positive ions, moving toward the cathode, strike the cathode surface. - On impact, they may release one or more secondary electrons (also photons striking cathode release photoelectrons; metastable atoms hitting cathode do likewise). - $\gamma$ = average number of secondary electrons released per positive ion impact (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$). **(iii) Derivation of Townsend breakdown criterion [3 marks]:** Let $n_0$ = initial electrons emitted from cathode by external means. After traversing distance $x$, they become $n_0 e^{\alpha x}$. These reach the anode, releasing $n_0(e^{\alpha d} - 1)$ positive ions to travel back to the cathode. At the cathode, these ions release $\gamma n_0(e^{\alpha d} - 1)$ secondary electrons, which in turn become $n_0 + \gamma n_0(e^{\alpha d} - 1)$ electrons at the cathode. For a self-sustaining avalanche: $$n_0 = n_0 + \gamma n_0\left(e^{\alpha d} - 1\right)$$ $$\boxed{\gamma \left( e^{\alpha d} - 1 \right) = 1}$$ This is the Townsend breakdown criterion. --- **Q.A.(b) [7 marks]** A uniform-field spark gap has $d = 1\ \text{cm}$ in air at 1 atm, 25 °C. Using Paschen's Law ($A = 112.5\ \text{cm·torr}^{-1}$, $B = 27.4\ \text{V cm}^{-1}\text{torr}^{-1}$, $\gamma = 10^{-4}$), compute the DC breakdown voltage. Apply air density correction. **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Convert pressure to torr.** $P = 1\ \text{atm} = 760\ \text{torr}$. **Step 2: Compute $Pd$.** $Pd = 760 \times 1 = 760\ \text{torr·cm}$. **Step 3: Compute $APd$.** $A P d = 112.5 \times 760 = 85{,}500$. $\ln(85{,}500) \approx 11.356$. **Step 4: Compute $\ln[\ln(1 + 1/\gamma)]$.** $\ln(1 + 1/10^{-4}) = \ln(10{,}001) \approx 9.2104$. $\ln(9.2104) \approx 2.2207$. **Step 5: Paschen's Law.** $V_b = \dfrac{27.4 \times 760}{11.356 - 2.2207} = \dfrac{20{,}824}{9.1353} \approx 2279\ \text{V} \approx 2.28\ \text{kV}$. **Step 6: Air density correction.** $\delta = \dfrac{P}{P_0} \cdot \dfrac{T_0}{T} = 1 \times \dfrac{293}{298} \approx 0.983$. **Step 7: Corrected breakdown voltage.** $V_b^{\text{corrected}} = \delta \times V_b = 0.983 \times 2.28 \approx 2.24\ \text{kV}$. **Final Answer: $V_b \approx 2.24\ \text{kV}$ (DC) at the test conditions.** --- > **[Question B — 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Q.B.(a) [7 marks]** With a neat schematic, describe the experimental procedure to determine the dielectric strength of air in the laboratory. List the apparatus required. **Model Answer:** **(i) Schematic description [2 marks]:** A variac (0–240 V) feeds the low-voltage winding of a step-up HV transformer (e.g., 100 kV / 5 kVA). The HV output passes through a protective water resistor (100 kΩ) and connects to two sphere electrodes forming an adjustable air gap. A capacitance voltage divider (1000:1) feeds a digital HV voltmeter and a CRO. Barometer, thermometer, and hygrometer monitor ambient conditions. **(ii) Procedure [3 marks]:** 1. Polish the sphere electrodes with fine emery paper, then clean with acetone. 2. Set the gap distance $d$ using a micrometer/spacer. 3. Set the variac to zero, switch on the supply. 4. Slowly raise the voltage at a uniform rate (about 2 kV/s) until breakdown occurs (audible crack + flash). 5. Note the breakdown voltage $V_b$ from the voltmeter/CRO. 6. Switch off, wait 2 minutes for de-ionization, repeat 5 times, take the mean. 7. Repeat for several gap distances (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, …, 5.0 cm). 8. Record atmospheric pressure, temperature, and humidity throughout. 9. Apply air density correction $\delta$ and humidity correction $k_h$. **(iii) Apparatus list [2 marks]:** HV transformer, variac, water resistor, sphere electrodes (5/10/25 cm dia.), capacitance divider, DSO, voltmeter, barometer, thermometer, hygrometer. --- **Q.B.(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the factors that affect the dielectric strength of air. Explain the effect of humidity on the breakdown voltage with the standard correction formula. **Model Answer:** **(i) Factors affecting dielectric strength [4 marks]:** 1. **Electrode geometry:** Uniform-field (Rogowski/profile) gaps give the highest breakdown voltage. Non-uniform (point-plane) geometries drastically lower it due to field intensification near sharp points. 2. **Gap distance:** $V_b$ increases roughly linearly with $d$ in uniform fields (from Paschen's Law in the high-$Pd$ regime). 3. **Air pressure:** Higher pressure means denser gas, more frequent collisions, higher $V_b$ (until Paschen minimum is reached). 4. **Temperature:** Higher temperature reduces gas density, reducing $V_b$. 5. **Humidity:** Increases $V_b$ slightly because water molecules are electronegative and capture free electrons, reducing the avalanche growth rate. 6. **Waveform:** Impulse > AC-peak > DC > AC-rms (typically) for the same gap. 7. **Polarity:** Negative polarity gives higher breakdown than positive for non-uniform gaps. 8. **Surface condition:** Polished, clean electrodes give reproducible results. **(ii) Humidity correction [3 marks]:** The standard humidity correction factor (IEC 60060-1) is: $$k_h = 1 + 0.014 \left( \frac{h}{\delta} - 11 \right) \quad \text{(approx.)}$$ where $h$ is absolute humidity in g/m³ and $\delta$ is the relative air density. The corrected breakdown voltage is: $$V_b^{\text{corrected}} = \frac{V_b^{\text{measured}}}{k_h}$$ Higher humidity $\Rightarrow$ $k_h > 1$ $\Rightarrow$ corrected $V_b$ is slightly lower than the measured value (since measured $V_b$ was artificially higher due to humidity). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing peak vs rms:** The "30 kV/cm" rule refers to **peak** value. In AC rms, it is **21.1 kV/cm**. Forgetting this conversion loses up to 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to apply air density correction:** Examiners explicitly test this. Always compute $\delta = (P/P_0)(T_0/T)$ and use $V_b^{\text{corrected}} = \delta V_b$. > 3. **Confusing Paschen's Law region:** Students apply Paschen's Law at low $Pd$ values (left of minimum) and get absurd results — Paschen's Law is valid only on the right branch (high $Pd$) for engineering HV work. > 4. **Mixing up $A$ and $B$ units:** The constants $A$ and $B$ depend on the unit system (torr·cm vs Pa·m). Always confirm units before plugging values. > 5. **Writing Paschen's Law without the natural log:** A common error is omitting the $\ln[\ln(1 + 1/\gamma)]$ term in the denominator. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Dielectric strength of air in uniform field** ≈ **30 kV/cm (peak)** or **21.1 kV/cm (rms)** at standard conditions. - **Standard conditions (IS/IEC 60060-1):** $P_0 = 101.3\ \text{kPa}$, $T_0 = 293\ \text{K}$, $h_0 = 11\ \text{g/m}^3$. - **Townsend breakdown criterion:** $\gamma (e^{\alpha d} - 1) = 1$. - **Paschen's Law:** $V_b = f(Pd)$, the cornerstone of gas breakdown theory. - **Paschen minimum for air:** $(Pd)_{\min} \approx 0.7\ \text{torr·cm}$, $V_{b,\min} \approx 330\ \text{V}$. - **Air density correction:** $\delta = (P/P_0)(T_0/T)$, and $V_b^{\text{corrected}} = \delta \cdot V_b^{\text{measured}}$. - **Humidity correction:** Water vapour is electronegative and slightly increases $V_b$. - **Electrode shapes (highest to lowest $V_b$):** Rogowski profile > sphere-sphere > rod-rod > point-plane. - **Factors:** gap distance, pressure, temperature, humidity, waveform, polarity, electrode material — all affect the result. - **Applications:** sphere-gap voltmeters, transmission-line clearances, insulation coordination, surge arrester design. - **Typical lab result for 1 cm uniform gap at standard conditions:** AC rms $V_b \approx 21\ \text{kV}$; DC $V_b \approx 25$ – $30\ \text{kV}$. - **Always state units explicitly** in exam answers (kV, kV/cm, torr·cm, etc.) — losing unit marks is a common KTU failure pattern. - **For sphere-gap voltmeters**, only sphere gaps ≥ 1 cm diameter give reliable results; below this, field non-uniformity dominates.

topics.title: Power Factor Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL609 - POWER SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Power Factor Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Power Factor (pf)** is defined as the cosine of the angle between the supply voltage and the line current drawn by an AC load. Mathematically, it is the ratio of **Real Power (P)** in Watts (or kW) to the **Apparent Power (S)** in Volt-Amperes (or kVA).

$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{\text{Real Power (kW)}}{\text{Apparent Power (kVA)}}$$

**Power Factor Improvement** is the process of increasing the power factor of a load (typically from a low lagging value closer to unity) by supplying part of the required reactive power locally, using devices such as **shunt capacitors**, **synchronous condensers**, or **phase advancers** connected across (in parallel with) the inductive load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Board Wording):**
> "Power factor improvement refers to the technique of compensating for the reactive component of current drawn by an inductive load by connecting a leading-reactive device (usually a shunt capacitor) in parallel, thereby reducing the line current, I²R losses, and improving the voltage regulation of the system."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Beer Mug" Model

Imagine a glass mug of beer. The **liquid beer** is your **Real Power (P)** — the actual useful energy doing work (turning a motor, lighting a lamp). The **foam on top** is the **Reactive Power (Q)** — it occupies space, weighs nothing useful, and you still have to "carry" it. The **total contents of the mug** is the **Apparent Power (S)**.

> **Low Power Factor = A Mug Full of Foam**
> Your glass is mostly foam, very little beer. You are paying the bar (utility) for the *entire mug*, but you only *drink* (use) the liquid portion.

> **Power Factor Correction = Adding a "Foam Skimmer" (Capacitor)**
> A capacitor acts like a foam skimmer — it locally generates the "foam" (reactive kVAR) that the inductive load would otherwise demand from the supply. The bar still fills your mug, but now most of it is real beer, less is wasted foam.

### 1.3 Standard Power System Metrics (Must be Memorized)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| Real Power | P | Watts (W) or kW | Does useful mechanical/thermal work |
| Reactive Power | Q | VAR or kVAR | Sustains magnetic/electric fields |
| Apparent Power | S | VA or kVA | Vector sum: $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ |
| Power Factor | $\cos\phi$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Ratio P/S |
| Supply Frequency | f | **50 Hz** (India) | Fixed by grid |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega$ | rad/s | $\omega = 2\pi f$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Specific Note:** Almost all lab experiments in KTU power system labs operate at **3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz**. Always verify the rating of the capacitor bank on your lab kit before connecting it. Exceeding the voltage rating will rupture the dielectric.

### 1.4 Why Improve Power Factor? — The "Real Engineering" Reasons

1. **Reduction in Line Current** — For the same real power P, a lower pf means higher current. $I = \frac{P}{V \cos\phi}$. Improving pf reduces I, which reduces copper losses $I^2R$ in feeders.
2. **Reduced kVA Demand** — Utility bills are often based on kVA (or maximum demand in kVA), not just kWh.
3. **Improved Voltage Regulation** — Lower current means lower voltage drop $\Delta V = IR$ in transmission/distribution lines.
4. **Increased System Capacity** — The same cable and transformer can deliver more useful kW.
5. **Avoidance of Penalty Tariff** — Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) and most DISCOMs levy a **low-pf penalty** if industrial consumers operate below **0.85 lagging** (or 0.9, as per latest norms).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Power Triangle — The Heart of Power Factor Analysis

For a single-phase inductive load drawing current $I$ at voltage $V$ with phase angle $\phi$ (lagging):

$$P = VI\cos\phi \quad (\text{kW})$$

$$Q = VI\sin\phi \quad (\text{kVAR})$$

$$S = VI = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} \quad (\text{kVA})$$

For a **3-phase balanced** load, all three quantities are multiplied by **$\sqrt{3}$** for line values:

$$P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$$

$$Q_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$$

$$S_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\sqrt{3}$ appears in 3-phase:** It is the ratio of total 3-phase power to per-phase power for a balanced star or delta load, arising from the 120° phase separation of voltages and currents.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Capacitor-Based Improvement

When a capacitor of reactive rating $Q_C$ (in kVAR) is connected in parallel with the inductive load:

1. **The load still draws the same real power P and the same reactive power Q_L** (these are determined by the mechanical load on the motor, the illumination level of the lamp, etc.).
2. **The capacitor locally supplies a leading reactive power $Q_C$**, which **subtracts vectorially** from $Q_L$.
3. **The new net reactive power** drawn from the supply is $Q_{\text{new}} = Q_L - Q_C$.
4. **The new (improved) power factor angle** is $\phi_2$ where:
$$\tan\phi_2 = \frac{Q_{\text{new}}}{P} = \frac{Q_L - Q_C}{P}$$
5. **The new (lower) line current** is:
$$I_{\text{new}} = \frac{S_{\text{new}}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L} = \frac{\sqrt{P^2 + (Q_L - Q_C)^2}}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L}$$

### 2.3 Capacitor Sizing — The "Compensation Formula" (Most Important in KTU)

To improve the pf from an initial value $\cos\phi_1$ to a target value $\cos\phi_2$ for a load consuming real power $P$ (in kW), the **total three-phase leading kVAR** required from the capacitor bank is:

$$\boxed{Q_C = P \left(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2\right) \quad \text{kVAR}}$$

This is the **single most tested formula** in KTU power system and power electronics papers.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. It will appear in at least one of your Part A or Part B questions.**

| # | Quantity | Single-Phase Formula | Three-Phase Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Real Power | $P = VI\cos\phi$ | $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | W or kW |
| 2 | Reactive Power | $Q = VI\sin\phi$ | $Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | VAR or kVAR |
| 3 | Apparent Power | $S = VI$ | $S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$ | VA or kVA |
| 4 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = P/S$ | $\cos\phi = P/S$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Required Capacitor kVAR | $Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | $Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | kVAR (3-φ total) |
| 6 | Capacitance (Star) | $C = \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f V^2}$ | $C_{\text{ph}} = \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f V_L^2}$ per phase | Farads |
| 7 | Capacitance (Delta) | $C = \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f V^2}$ | $C_{\text{ph}} = \frac{Q_C}{6\pi f V_L^2}$ per phase | Farads |
| 8 | New Line Current | $I_2 = \frac{I_1 \cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}$ | $I_2 = \frac{I_1 \cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}$ | Amperes |
| 9 | Reduction in Current | $\Delta I = I_1 - I_2$ | $\Delta I = I_1 - I_2$ | Amperes |
| 10 | Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Ohms |
| 11 | Inductive Reactance | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | Ohms |

> **Note on Items 6 and 7 — Star vs. Delta:** The factor of 3 difference arises because in delta, each phase sees the line voltage $V_L$, while in star, each phase sees $V_L/\sqrt{3}$. Delta-connected capacitors need **1/3 the capacitance** of star-connected, but must be rated for **$\sqrt{3}$ times the voltage**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Plants:** Large induction motor installations (textile mills, steel plants, cement factories) routinely have pf correction capacitor banks at the bus bar.
- **Distribution Transformers:** KSEB and other utilities often install pole-mounted capacitor banks on LT distribution feeders to keep feeder pf above 0.9.
- **Power Electronics:** APFC (Automatic Power Factor Correction) panels with thyristor-switched capacitor banks are used in industries to dynamically maintain pf ≈ 1.
- **Renewable Integration:** Solar inverters and wind farms increasingly provide pf correction as an ancillary service to the grid.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Compensation Formula $Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$

We start from the power triangle and consider the load before and after correction.

**Step 1 — Before correction (initial state):**
The load has real power $P$ and reactive power $Q_1$, with pf angle $\phi_1$.

$$\tan\phi_1 = \frac{Q_1}{P} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad Q_1 = P \tan\phi_1$$

**Step 2 — Add the capacitor (which supplies $Q_C$ leading):**
The new net reactive power from the supply side is $Q_2 = Q_1 - Q_C$. The load's real power $P$ is unchanged.

**Step 3 — Target state (after correction):**
The new pf angle is $\phi_2$, so:

$$\tan\phi_2 = \frac{Q_2}{P} = \frac{Q_1 - Q_C}{P}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $Q_C$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P \tan\phi_2 &= Q_1 - Q_C \\
Q_C &= Q_1 - P \tan\phi_2 \\
Q_C &= P \tan\phi_1 - P \tan\phi_2 \\
\therefore \quad Q_C &= P \left( \tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Physical interpretation:** $P\tan\phi_1$ is the *original* reactive power being drawn. $P\tan\phi_2$ is the *reduced* reactive power we now want from the supply. Their difference is the leading kVAR the capacitor must locally supply.

### 3.2 Derivation of the New Line Current After Correction

**Step 1 — Initial line current:**

$$I_1 = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L \cos\phi_1}$$

**Step 2 — Real power $P$ is conserved** (the motor still does the same mechanical work). After correction, the new pf is $\cos\phi_2$, so:

$$I_2 = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L \cos\phi_2}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio $I_1/I_2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_1}{I_2} &= \frac{\frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L \cos\phi_1}}{\frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L \cos\phi_2}} = \frac{\cos\phi_2}{\cos\phi_1} \\
\therefore \quad I_2 &= I_1 \cdot \frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Insight:** This is a beautifully simple result. Since $\cos\phi_2 > \cos\phi_1$, the new current $I_2$ is *less* than $I_1$. The ratio of the pfs directly gives the current reduction.

### 3.3 Derivations for Star and Delta Connected Capacitor Banks

**Star-Connected Capacitor Bank:**

Each phase of the star has phase voltage $V_{\text{ph}} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$. The total 3-phase kVAR is $Q_C$, so **per phase kVAR** is $Q_C/3$.

$$Q_{C,\text{ph}} = \frac{Q_C}{3} = \frac{V_{\text{ph}}^2}{X_{C,\text{ph}}} = V_{\text{ph}}^2 \cdot (2\pi f C_Y)$$

Solving for $C_Y$ (capacitance per phase, star):

$$\begin{aligned}
C_Y &= \frac{Q_C/3}{2\pi f (V_L/\sqrt{3})^2} \\
    &= \frac{Q_C/3}{2\pi f \cdot V_L^2/3} \\
    &= \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f V_L^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Delta-Connected Capacitor Bank:**

Each phase of the delta has $V_{\text{ph}} = V_L$. Per phase kVAR is again $Q_C/3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_\Delta &= \frac{Q_C/3}{2\pi f V_L^2} \\
         &= \frac{Q_C}{6\pi f V_L^2} = \frac{1}{3}\,C_Y
\end{aligned}$$

> **Key engineering takeaway:** Delta-connected capacitors need only **1/3 the capacitance** of star, but each capacitor must be insulated for $V_L$ instead of $V_L/\sqrt{3}$ — a $\sqrt{3}$× higher voltage rating. Industrial capacitor banks are usually delta-connected for this reason.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Question (Typical KTU 14-mark problem):**
> A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz induction motor draws a line current of 25 A at a power factor of 0.7 lagging. A delta-connected capacitor bank is used to improve the power factor to 0.95 lagging.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the rating of the capacitor bank in kVAR and the value of capacitance per phase.
> **(b)** Find the new line current after correction and the percentage reduction in line current.

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the real power $P$ consumed by the motor (3-phase):**

$$P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi_1 = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 25 \times 0.7$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 1.732 \times 400 \times 25 \times 0.7 \\
  &= 1.732 \times 400 \times 17.5 \\
  &= 12{,}124\ \text{W} \approx 12.124\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the initial and target pf angles:**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.7) = 45.57° \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tan\phi_1 = \tan(45.57°) = 1.020$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19° \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tan\phi_2 = \tan(18.19°) = 0.3287$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the required capacitor kVAR:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_C &= P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) \\
    &= 12.124 \times (1.020 - 0.3287) \\
    &= 12.124 \times 0.6913 \\
    &= 8.382\ \text{kVAR}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Capacitance per phase for delta connection:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_\Delta &= \frac{Q_C}{6\pi f V_L^2} \\
         &= \frac{8.382 \times 10^3}{6 \pi \times 50 \times (400)^2} \\
         &= \frac{8382}{6 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 160000} \\
         &= \frac{8382}{150{,}796{,}447} \\
         &= 5.558 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{F} \\
         &= 55.58\ \mu\text{F per phase}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — New line current after correction:**

$$I_2 = I_1 \cdot \frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2} = 25 \times \frac{0.7}{0.95} = 18.42\ \text{A}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage reduction in line current:**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{I_1 - I_2}{I_1} \times 100 = \frac{25 - 18.42}{25} \times 100 = 26.3\%$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Real Power | P | 12.124 kW |
| Initial pf angle | $\phi_1$ | 45.57° |
| Target pf angle | $\phi_2$ | 18.19° |
| Capacitor bank rating | $Q_C$ | **8.38 kVAR** |
| Capacitance per phase (delta) | $C_\Delta$ | **55.58 μF** |
| New line current | $I_2$ | 18.42 A |
| Current reduction | $\Delta I$ | 6.58 A (26.3%) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
**Define power factor. Why is a low power factor considered disadvantageous in an industrial power system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80–100 words):**

> **Power factor** is the ratio of real power (kW) to apparent power (kVA) in an AC circuit, expressed as $\cos\phi = P/S$. It indicates how effectively the supplied current is being converted into useful work.
>
> A **low power factor is disadvantageous** because:
> 1. It increases the line current for the same real power, leading to higher $I^2R$ copper losses in feeders and transformers.
> 2. It causes poor voltage regulation due to larger voltage drops.
> 3. It reduces the effective capacity of the distribution system (transformers and cables carry more current but deliver less useful power).
> 4. It attracts **low-pf penalty** from utilities like KSEB, increasing the electricity bill.

**Mark distribution:** Definition 1M + any 2 disadvantages 1M each.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, S8 EE]**
**List any three methods of power factor improvement and state the most commonly used method in industrial practice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~60–80 words):**

> The three principal methods of power factor improvement are:
> 1. **Static Capacitor Bank** — Shunt capacitors connected across the load.
> 2. **Synchronous Condenser** — An over-excited synchronous motor running without mechanical load.
> 3. **Phase Advancer** — An AC exciter mounted on the induction motor shaft to supply exciting ampere-turns.
>
> The **most commonly used** method in industrial practice is the **static shunt capacitor bank**, because it is low-cost, virtually maintenance-free, has low losses, and can be installed in modular switchable units (APFC panels).

**Mark distribution:** 3 methods 1M each OR 1.5M for naming all + 1.5M for justifying the choice.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, S8 EEE]**
**(A) Question A — 14 Marks**

> A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz induction motor draws a line current of **30 A at 0.65 power factor lagging**. A star-connected capacitor bank is connected in parallel to improve the power factor to **0.9 lagging**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the rating of the capacitor bank in kVAR and the capacitance per phase.
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Determine the new line current after correction and the reduction in kVA demand. Comment on the size of conductor required before and after correction.
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Capacitor Bank Sizing**

**Step 1 — Real power consumed by motor:**

$$P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi_1 = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 30 \times 0.65$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= 1.732 \times 400 \times 30 \times 0.65 \\
  &= 1.732 \times 400 \times 19.5 \\
  &= 13{,}510\ \text{W} \approx 13.51\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute pf angles:**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.65) = 49.46° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\phi_1 = 1.169$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.9) = 25.84° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\phi_2 = 0.4843$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor bank kVAR:**

$$Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) = 13.51 \times (1.169 - 0.4843) = 13.51 \times 0.6847 = 9.252\ \text{kVAR}$$

**Step 4 — Capacitance per phase for STAR connection:**

$$C_Y = \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f V_L^2} = \frac{9.252 \times 10^3}{2\pi \times 50 \times (400)^2}$$

$$C_Y = \frac{9252}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 160000} = \frac{9252}{50{,}265{,}482} = 184.1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{F} = 184.1\ \mu\text{F per phase}$$

> **Answer (a):** Capacitor bank = **9.25 kVAR (3-phase)**; Capacitance per phase (star) = **184.1 μF**

**Part (b) — New Current and Conductor Sizing**

**Step 5 — New line current after correction:**

$$I_2 = I_1 \times \frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2} = 30 \times \frac{0.65}{0.9} = 21.67\ \text{A}$$

**Step 6 — Initial and final apparent power:**

$$S_1 = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_1 = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 30 = 20{,}784\ \text{VA} \approx 20.78\ \text{kVA}$$

$$S_2 = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_2 = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 21.67 = 15{,}013\ \text{VA} \approx 15.01\ \text{kVA}$$

**Step 7 — Reduction in kVA demand:**

$$\Delta S = S_1 - S_2 = 20.78 - 15.01 = 5.77\ \text{kVA}$$

$$\%\ \text{Reduction} = \frac{5.77}{20.78} \times 100 = 27.78\%$$

**Step 8 — Conductor size comment:**

The current-carrying capacity of a conductor is proportional to its cross-sectional area for the same allowable temperature rise. Since the current reduces from 30 A to 21.67 A (a 27.78% reduction), a **smaller cross-section conductor** can be used after correction. Specifically, if the original cable was rated for 30 A continuous, a cable rated for only 22 A can be selected post-correction, resulting in **material cost savings of roughly 25–30%** on the cabling.

> **Answer (b):** New line current = **21.67 A**; kVA demand reduces by **5.77 kVA (27.78%)**; conductor cross-section can be reduced by approximately the same factor.

---

**Q3. (B) Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Choice**

> A single-phase, **230 V, 50 Hz** motor draws a current of **20 A at 0.6 power factor lagging**. A capacitor is connected in parallel to improve the power factor to **0.85 lagging**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the value of capacitance required and the kVAR rating of the capacitor.
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** If a second identical motor is added in parallel (drawing the same 20 A at 0.6 pf lagging), find the **additional** capacitance needed to maintain the overall pf at 0.85 lagging. Comment on whether the **original capacitor alone** would be sufficient.
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Single Motor Capacitor Sizing**

**Step 1 — Real power of one motor:**

$$P_1 = V I_1 \cos\phi_1 = 230 \times 20 \times 0.6 = 2760\ \text{W} = 2.76\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — pf angles:**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.6) = 53.13° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\phi_1 = 1.3333$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.85) = 31.79° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan\phi_2 = 0.6197$$

**Step 3 — Required kVAR:**

$$Q_{C1} = P_1(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) = 2.76 \times (1.3333 - 0.6197) = 2.76 \times 0.7136 = 1.970\ \text{kVAR}$$

**Step 4 — Capacitance:**

$$C_1 = \frac{Q_{C1}}{2\pi f V^2} = \frac{1970}{2\pi \times 50 \times (230)^2} = \frac{1970}{16{,}619{,}000} = 118.5\ \mu\text{F}$$

> **Answer (a):** $C_1 = \mathbf{118.5\ \mu F}$, kVAR rating = **1.97 kVAR**

**Part (b) — Two Motors in Parallel**

**Step 5 — Total real power with two motors:**

$$P_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 2.76 = 5.52\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 6 — Total reactive power drawn by two motors (each at $\phi_1 = 53.13°$):**

$$Q_{\text{load}} = 2 \times P_1 \tan\phi_1 = 2 \times 2.76 \times 1.3333 = 7.36\ \text{kVAR}$$

**Step 7 — Total kVAR needed to bring overall pf to 0.85:**

$$Q_{C,\text{total}} = P_{\text{total}}(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) = 5.52 \times 0.7136 = 3.94\ \text{kVAR}$$

**Step 8 — Additional capacitance required:**

$$Q_{C,\text{add}} = Q_{C,\text{total}} - Q_{C1} = 3.94 - 1.97 = 1.97\ \text{kVAR}$$

$$C_{\text{add}} = \frac{1970}{16{,}619{,}000} = 118.5\ \mu\text{F}$$

**Step 9 — Comment on sufficiency of original capacitor:**

The **original capacitor alone (1.97 kVAR) is NOT sufficient**. With the second motor added, the load's reactive power demand doubles (from 3.68 kVAR to 7.36 kVAR), so the compensating kVAR must also double. An **additional 118.5 μF** must be switched in parallel.

> **Answer (b):** Additional capacitance = **118.5 μF**; Original capacitor alone is **insufficient** — must be doubled.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks Every Semester:**
>
> 1. **Confusing single-phase and three-phase formulas** — A 3-phase load uses $\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$, NOT $V I \cos\phi$. This is the #1 mark-loser.
> 2. **Using $V_L$ instead of $V_{\text{ph}}$ (or vice versa) in capacitor formulas** — Star uses $V_L/\sqrt{3}$, Delta uses $V_L$. Forgetting the factor of 3 in $C$ calculation is fatal.
> 3. **Mixing up $\tan\phi$ with $\cos\phi$** — The compensation formula uses $\tan$, not $\cos$. Many students write $Q_C = P(\cos\phi_1 - \cos\phi_2)$ — this is **completely wrong** and will fetch zero.
> 4. **Forgetting units** — Always convert W to kW before computing kVAR, and μF to F (or vice versa) carefully in capacitor formulas.
> 5. **Not specifying star vs. delta** in the final answer — Examiners explicitly look for "**per phase, star connected**" or "**per phase, delta connected**". A bare "55 μF" loses 1 mark.
> 6. **In lab record books, forgetting to plot graphs** — pf vs. C and $I_L$ vs. C are mandatory. Skipping the graph = 2 marks lost in the viva-cum-record evaluation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density 30-second revision — go through this the night before the exam.**

- [ ] Power factor = $\cos\phi = P/S = P/\sqrt{P^2+Q^2}$ — **dimensionless, between 0 and 1**
- [ ] For 3-phase: $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$, $Q = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \sin\phi$, $S = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L$
- [ ] For 1-phase: $P = VI \cos\phi$, $Q = VI \sin\phi$, $S = VI$
- [ ] **Golden Formula:** $Q_C = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ — must be memorized verbatim
- [ ] New line current: $I_2 = I_1 \cdot \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}$ — independent of voltage
- [ ] Star capacitor: $C_Y = \dfrac{Q_C}{2\pi f V_L^2}$ per phase
- [ ] Delta capacitor: $C_\Delta = \dfrac{Q_C}{6\pi f V_L^2} = \dfrac{C_Y}{3}$ per phase
- [ ] Delta needs **1/3 the capacitance** of star, but **$\sqrt{3}$× the voltage rating**
- [ ] Most common industrial method = **shunt capacitor bank** (static, low cost, low loss)
- [ ] Other methods: **synchronous condenser** (over-excited synchronous motor) and **phase advancer** (for slip-ring induction motors)
- [ ] Disadvantages of low pf: higher $I^2R$ loss, poor voltage regulation, low cable/transformer utilization, utility penalty
- [ ] KSEB penalty threshold: typically below **0.85–0.9 lagging** in industrial tariff
- [ ] Standard KTU lab values: **3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz** — memorize these
- [ ] Real power $P$ is **conserved** before and after pf correction; only $Q$ and $I$ change
- [ ] In lab viva: always state "**P remains the same, Q is reduced by $Q_C$, S is reduced, pf improves**"
- [ ] The pf cannot be improved beyond **unity (1.0)**; attempting to over-correct creates a **leading** pf, which is also undesirable (causes resonance, over-voltage on switching off)
- [ ] Always tabulate readings: $C$, $V_L$, $I_L$, $P$, pf calculated = $P/(\sqrt{3} V_L I_L)$ vs. pf meter reading
- [ ] Standard graph plots: (i) pf vs. $C$ (rising curve, asymptotic to 1.0), (ii) $I_L$ vs. $C$ (falling curve, then rises again past unity — over-correction zone)
